KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion blunders** — convert all lengths to **metres** and areas to **m²** before substituting. Marks deducted silently if cm² or mm are mixed. > 2. **Logarithm argument error** — $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ is **dimensionless**, but ensure $r_2 > r_1$ (numerator > denominator) to avoid negative resistance. > 3. **Forgetting the convective resistance** — KTU problems routinely couple conduction with external convection. The total resistance must include $1/(hA)$ or $1/(h 2\pi r_2 L)$. > 4. **Linear vs logarithmic profile confusion** — never apply a plane-wall formula to a cylinder or vice versa. > 5. **Spherical critical radius trap** — spheres have **no** critical radius paradox; insulation always reduces heat loss. > 6. **Sign convention slip** — always write $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$ (positive value) when computing $q$. > 7. **Interface temperature mis-step** — many students compute $q$ correctly but fail the subsequent "find interface temperature" part, costing 2-3 marks. --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Explain the physical significance of the negative sign.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > *Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous body is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to the direction of heat flow and to the temperature gradient in that direction.* Mathematically: $q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ The **negative sign** indicates that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature** — i.e., from a hotter region to a colder region, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. If heat flowed in the opposite direction (against the gradient), entropy would spontaneously decrease, which is impossible in a passive system. The proportionality constant $k$ is the **thermal conductivity**, which depends on the material and, for most engineering materials, weakly on temperature. For metals $k$ is large (free-electron transport dominates); for insulators it is small (lattice vibrations / phonons dominate). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] **Define thermal resistance. Draw the thermal resistance network for a composite plane wall consisting of three different materials with convective boundary conditions on both sides.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > *Thermal resistance is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across a body to the steady-state heat transfer rate through it.* It is the **thermal analogue of electrical resistance** and has SI units of **K/W**. For a plane wall: $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ The thermal resistance network for a 3-layer composite plane wall with convection on both sides is: $$\boxed{R_{total} = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{h_i A}}_{\text{inner convection}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A}}_{\text{wall 1}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A}}_{\text{wall 2}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A}}_{\text{wall 3}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{h_o A}}_{\text{outer convection}}}$$ The heat rate is then: $$q = \dfrac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{R_{total}}$$ Each resistor is **in series** because the same heat flux passes through every layer sequentially. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (Choice 1) — Composite Plane Wall with Convection [KTU Model Question, Module 1 — ESE Pattern] #### (a) Derive an expression for steady-state heat conduction through a composite plane wall consisting of three layers in perfect thermal contact, exposed to convective fluids on both sides. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state conditions (no energy storage, $\partial T/\partial t = 0$). 2. One-dimensional heat flow (heat flows only in the $x$-direction). 3. Constant thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and convective coefficients $h_i, h_o$. 4. Perfect thermal contact at all interfaces (no contact resistance). 5. Constant fluid temperatures $T_{\infty,1}$ and $T_{\infty,2}$. 6. No internal heat generation in any layer. **Derivation:** For the inner convection layer: $$q = h_i A \, (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1})$$ For each conduction layer (using Fourier's law integrated across the layer): $$q = \dfrac{k_1 A (T_{s,1} - T_{s,2})}{L_1}, \quad q = \dfrac{k_2 A (T_{s,2} - T_{s,3})}{L_2}, \quad q = \dfrac{k_3 A (T_{s,3} - T_{s,4})}{L_3}$$ For the outer convection layer: $$q = h_o A \, (T_{s,4} - T_{\infty,2})$$ Recognizing that each term has the form $q R_i = \Delta T$, summing all temperature drops across the series network: $$T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2} = q\!\left(\dfrac{1}{h_i A} + \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A}\right)$$ Solving for $q$: $$\boxed{q = \dfrac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{\dfrac{1}{h_i A} + \sum\limits_{j=1}^{3} \dfrac{L_j}{k_j A} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A}}}$$ Or equivalently, $q = \Delta T_{overall} / R_{total}$, where $R_{total}$ is the sum of all five thermal resistances. --- #### (b) A furnace wall is made of three layers as follows (from inside to outside): (7 Marks) | Layer | Material | Thickness $L$ (mm) | $k$ (W/m·K) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fireclay brick | 115 | 1.05 | | 2 | Insulating brick | 230 | 0.21 | | 3 | Red brick | 115 | 0.70 | The inside and outside air temperatures are $T_{\infty,1} = 1200^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $T_{\infty,2} = 60^{\circ}\text{C}$ respectively. The inside and outside convection coefficients are $h_i = 35 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$ and $h_o = 12 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The wall area is $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate: (i) The steady-state heat loss per unit area. (ii) The temperature at the interface between fireclay and insulating brick. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert units** All thicknesses are in mm — convert to **metres**: $L_1 = 0.115$ m, $L_2 = 0.230$ m, $L_3 = 0.115$ m. **Step 2: Compute each thermal resistance (per unit area, dividing by $A = 1$)** $$R_{conv,i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{35} = 0.02857 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$R_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.115}{1.05} = 0.10952 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} = \dfrac{0.230}{0.21} = 1.09524 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \dfrac{L_3}{k_3} = \dfrac{0.115}{0.70} = 0.16429 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$R_{conv,o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{12} = 0.08333 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 3: Total Resistance** $$R_{total} = 0.02857 + 0.10952 + 1.09524 + 0.16429 + 0.08333 = 1.48095 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 4: Heat Loss Per Unit Area** $$\Delta T = 1200 - 60 = 1140 \text{ K}$$ $$q'' = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1140}{1.48095} = \boxed{769.78 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **Step 5: Interface Temperature** The temperature drop across the first convective resistance (inner surface temperature): $$T_{s,1} = T_{\infty,1} - q'' R_{conv,i} = 1200 - (769.78)(0.02857) = 1200 - 22.0 = 1178.0^{\circ}\text{C}$$ Temperature drop across fireclay brick: $$T_{interface} = T_{s,1} - q'' R_1 = 1178.0 - (769.78)(0.10952) = 1178.0 - 84.32 = \boxed{1093.7^{\circ}\text{C}}$$ **Verification:** The interface temperature lies between the inner surface ($1178^{\circ}\text{C}$) and the next layer's outer surface — physically consistent. ✓ --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — Hollow Cylinder with Critical Radius [KTU Model Question, Module 1 — ESE Pattern] #### (a) Derive an expression for steady-state radial heat conduction through a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$. Show that the temperature distribution is logarithmic. (7 Marks) **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Coordinate selection** Use cylindrical coordinates with radial coordinate $r$. Temperature varies only with $r$: $T = T(r)$. **Step 2: Apply Fourier's Law** At radius $r$, the area through which heat flows is $A(r) = 2\pi r L$. Heat rate in the radial (outward) direction: $$q = -k A(r) \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -k (2\pi r L) \dfrac{dT}{dr}$$ **Step 3: Steady-state condition** Since $q$ is constant with respect to $r$ (no accumulation): $$\dfrac{dq}{dr} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left[-k (2\pi r L) \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right] = 0$$ $$-2\pi k L \dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$ **Step 4: Integrate once** $$r \dfrac{dT}{dr} = C_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dT}{dr} = \dfrac{C_1}{r}$$ **Step 5: Integrate again** $$T(r) = C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$$ **Step 6: Apply boundary conditions** $T(r_1) = T_1 \Rightarrow T_1 = C_1 \ln(r_1) + C_2$ $T(r_2) = T_2 \Rightarrow T_2 = C_1 \ln(r_2) + C_2$ Subtracting: $$T_1 - T_2 = C_1 \ln(r_1/r_2) = -C_1 \ln(r_2/r_1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 = -\dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ $$C_2 = T_1 - C_1 \ln(r_1) = T_1 + \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)\ln(r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ **Step 7: Final Temperature Distribution** $$\boxed{T(r) = T_1 - \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_1}\right)}$$ This is the **logarithmic profile** characteristic of cylindrical conduction. **Step 8: Heat Rate** Substituting $C_1$ back into $q = -k(2\pi r L)(C_1/r)$: $$q = -2\pi k L \, C_1 = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ The result is **independent of $r$**, confirming steady state. ✓ --- #### (b) A steam pipe of outer diameter 80 mm is to be insulated with an insulating material of thermal conductivity $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K. The outer surface temperature of the bare pipe is $T_s = 250^{\circ}\text{C}$, and the surrounding air is at $T_\infty = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$ with a convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 10 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. For a pipe length of $L = 10$ m, calculate: (7 Marks) (i) The critical radius of insulation. (ii) The heat loss rate per metre of pipe length if the insulation outer radius is $r_2 = 60$ mm. Comment on whether the insulation is effective. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Given:** $r_1 = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m, $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K, $T_s = 250^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h = 10$ W/m²·K, $L = 10$ m, $r_2 = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m. **Step (i): Critical Radius** $$\boxed{r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h} = \dfrac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}}$$ Since $r_2 = 60$ mm $\gg r_{cr} = 5$ mm, the insulation is **operating in the effective regime** — heat loss will decrease as insulation is added. ✓ **Step (ii): Heat Loss for the Insulated Pipe** Total thermal resistance per unit length is: $$R_{total}' = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_2 h}$$ Compute the conduction resistance: $$\dfrac{\ln(60/40)}{2\pi (0.05)} = \dfrac{\ln(1.5)}{0.3142} = \dfrac{0.4055}{0.3142} = 1.2907 \text{ m·K/W}$$ Compute the convection resistance: $$\dfrac{1}{2\pi (0.060)(10)} = \dfrac{1}{3.7699} = 0.2653 \text{ m·K/W}$$ Sum: $$R_{total}' = 1.2907 + 0.2653 = 1.5560 \text{ m·K/W}$$ Heat loss per metre length: $$q' = \dfrac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}'} = \dfrac{250 - 30}{1.5560} = \dfrac{220}{1.5560} = \boxed{141.4 \text{ W/m}}$$ **Total heat loss for 10 m pipe:** $$q = q' \times L = 141.4 \times 10 = 1414 \text{ W}$$ **Comment:** Since $r_2 (60 \text{ mm}) > r_{cr} (5 \text{ mm})$, the insulation is **effective** — it reduces the heat loss below that of a bare pipe (where only the convection resistance at $r = 40$ mm would be present, yielding much higher losses). This illustrates that for industrial insulation thicknesses (typically tens of mm), the critical radius is essentially never a practical concern. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Print This and Memorize:** - ✅ **Fourier's Law:** $q = -kA \, dT/dx$ — the cornerstone of all conduction analysis. - ✅ **1-D Steady State, No Generation → Laplace equation:** $d^2 T/dx^2 = 0$. - ✅ **Plane wall:** linear temperature profile, $R_{th} = L/(kA)$. - ✅ **Hollow cylinder:** logarithmic profile, $R_{th} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$. - ✅ **Hollow sphere:** hyperbolic profile, $R_{th} = (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$. - ✅ **Composite walls:** add **thermal resistances in series**; same $q$ flows through each. - ✅ **Convective boundary:** $R_{conv} = 1/(hA)$; convection + conduction = series network. - ✅ **Critical radius of insulation (cylinder only):** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h$. Insulation is effective only when $r_2 > r_{cr}$. - ✅ **Plane wall with heat generation:** parabolic profile, max temperature at the **midplane**, equal heat outflow at both surfaces. - ✅ **Thermal contact resistance:** adds an extra series resistor $R_{c}$ (units m²·K/W). - ✅ **Always convert to SI units before substituting:** mm → m, cm² → m². - ✅ **Interface temperature in series networks:** $T_{interface} = T_{known} - q \cdot R_{upstream}$. - ✅ **KTU-typical question patterns:** (1) composite plane wall with convection, (2) lagged cylinder, (3) critical radius with effective/ineffective regime, (4) interface temperature determination. - ✅ **Examiner's trick:** Students often forget to include **both convective resistances** (inner + outer) — losing 2-3 marks per occurrence.

topics.title: Conduction Through Plane Walls, Cylinders and Spherical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conduction Through Plane Walls, Cylinders and Spherical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Steady-State, One-Dimensional Heat Conduction** through simple geometries is the fundamental mode of thermal energy transfer in solids wherein heat flows under a *constant temperature gradient*, with no energy storage within the medium, governed by the **Fourier Law of Heat Conduction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT503) explicitly tests the ability of students to derive and apply conduction equations for **Plane Walls, Hollow Cylinders**, and **Hollow Spheres** — both as *single-layer* and *multi-layer composite* systems.

The governing differential equation (in rectangular, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates respectively) for steady, 1-D conduction is:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine three everyday objects sitting in a cold room:

1. **A flat glass window (Plane Wall):** Heat flows straight through like water seeping through a flat sponge. The area *A* is constant.
2. **A hot tea cup (Hollow Cylinder):** Heat from the hot tea must travel *outward radially*. As the radius grows, the heat gets "spread out" over a larger and larger area — like ripples on a pond.
3. **A hot metal ball bearing (Hollow Sphere):** Heat escapes in *all radial directions* simultaneously, again through an area that increases with the square of the radius — like heat radiating from the sun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** As geometry changes from a plane wall → cylinder → sphere, the cross-sectional area available for heat flow *changes with position*, which fundamentally alters the heat-transfer equation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **k** | Thermal Conductivity | **W/(m·K)** |
| **h** | Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient | **W/(m²·K)** |
| **α** | Thermal Diffusivity | **m²/s** |
| **R** (or R_th) | Thermal Resistance | **K/W** |
| **q** | Heat Flux (rate per unit area) | **W/m²** |
| **Q** | Heat Transfer Rate | **W** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **plane walls**, the area is **constant**. For **cylinders**, area scales with $r$ (i.e., $A = 2\pi r L$). For **spheres**, area scales with $r^2$ (i.e., $A = 4\pi r^2$). This geometric fact is the *single most important* concept behind all derivations that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction — The Foundation

Fourier's Law states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous medium is **directly proportional** to the area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient across the medium.

$$Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that heat flows from higher to lower temperature (in the direction of *decreasing* temperature).

## 2.2 Plane Wall — Single Layer Analysis

**Step-by-step logic for derivation:**
1. Steady-state → $\frac{dT}{dt} = 0$
2. One-dimensional → temperature varies only in *x*
3. Constant k and A → no position dependence
4. Integrate the Fourier equation between $x = 0$ (temperature $T_1$) and $x = L$ (temperature $T_2$)

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\frac{Q}{kA}\int_{0}^{L} dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_1 - T_2 = \frac{QL}{kA}$$

**Solving for Q:**

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th,wall}}$$

The temperature distribution is **linear**: $T(x) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}x$

## 2.3 Plane Wall — Composite (Multi-Layer) Systems

### 2.3.1 Layers in Series (heat flows perpendicular to layers)

For *n* layers with perfect interfacial contact:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A_i}} = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total,series}}$$

### 2.3.2 Layers in Parallel (heat flows parallel to layers)

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{k_i A_i (T_1 - T_2)}{L_i}$$

### 2.3.3 Contact Resistance

At the interface of two solids, microscopic air gaps create an additional resistance:

$$R_{contact} = \frac{1}{h_c A}$$

## 2.4 Hollow Cylinder — Single Layer

For radial conduction through a cylinder of length $L$, inner radius $r_1$, and outer radius $r_2$:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

Temperature distribution is **logarithmic**:
$$T(r) = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln(r/r_1)$$

## 2.5 Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder)

When a cylinder is insulated, adding insulation *reduces* the outside surface area's convective loss *until* a critical radius is reached. Beyond this point, adding more insulation *increases* heat loss.

$$r_c = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outside}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $r_c$:** If the **outer radius** of insulation $r_2 > r_c$, heat loss **decreases** with more insulation (as expected). If $r_2 < r_c$, heat loss **increases** with more insulation — a counterintuitive result caused by the increased outer surface area dominating over the increased conductive resistance.

The complete expression for heat loss from an insulated cylinder (with $T_i$ at the inner surface and $T_\infty$ in the ambient fluid):

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_i - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}}$$

## 2.6 Composite Hollow Cylinder (Layers in Series)

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}}$$

## 2.7 Hollow Sphere — Single Layer

$$Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}$$

Temperature distribution:
$$T(r) = \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}\right) + T_2$$

## 2.8 Composite Hollow Sphere (Layers in Series)

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k_i}\left(\frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_{i+1}}\right)}$$

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Geometry | Heat Transfer Rate (Q) | Thermal Resistance (R_th) | Area Behavior |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| **Plane Wall** | $\frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $\frac{L}{kA}$ | **Constant** |
| **Composite Plane Wall (Series)** | $\frac{A \Delta T_{overall}}{\sum L_i/k_i}$ | $\sum \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$ | **Constant** |
| **Hollow Cylinder** | $\frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $A \propto r$ |
| **Composite Cylinder (Series)** | $\frac{2\pi L \Delta T}{\sum \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}}$ | $\sum \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ | $A \propto r$ |
| **Hollow Sphere** | $\frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ | $\frac{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}{4\pi k}$ | $A \propto r^2$ |
| **Composite Sphere (Series)** | $\frac{4\pi \Delta T}{\sum \frac{(1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})}{k_i}}$ | $\sum \frac{(1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})}{4\pi k_i}$ | $A \propto r^2$ |
| **Critical Radius (Cyl.)** | $r_c = \frac{k}{h}$ | N/A (geometric) | N/A |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plane Wall Analysis:** Furnace walls, building insulation panels, double-glazed windows, refrigeration cold-room walls.
- **Cylinder Analysis:** Steam pipes, insulated pipelines, boiler tubes, nuclear reactor fuel rods, electrical resistance wires.
- **Sphere Analysis:** Storage tanks for cryogenic liquids (LNG spheres), nuclear reactor containment vessels, catalytic converter substrates, pharmaceutical reactors.
- **Critical Radius Concept:** Selection of insulation thickness for electrical cables (wire gauge matters), steam piping, and prevention of heat *gain* in chilled-water lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Conduction Through a Plane Wall

**Starting Point (Fourier's Law):**

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 1 — Steady-state and 1-D assumptions:**
With $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$ and temperature varying only in $x$, the heat rate $Q$ and flux $q$ are constants along $x$.

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $dx$ and integrate:**

$$Q\,dx = -kA\,dT$$

$$Q \int_{0}^{L} dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$Q \cdot L = -kA(T_2 - T_1) = kA(T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q$:**

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Temperature distribution (re-integrate with separation of variables):**

$$T(x) = T_1 - \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}\right) x$$

This confirms a **linear** profile, characteristic of constant-area, constant-$k$ conduction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Radial Conduction Through a Hollow Cylinder

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law in cylindrical coordinates (radial):**

$$Q = -kA(r)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

where $A(r) = 2\pi r L$ is the cylindrical surface area at radius $r$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $A(r)$:**

$$Q = -k(2\pi r L)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{Q\,dr}{r} = -2\pi k L\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between $r_1$ at $T_1$ and $r_2$ at $T_2$:**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2}\frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Q$:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 7 — Temperature distribution:**
Re-integrating the separated equation gives:

$$T(r) = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)$$

This is the **logarithmic temperature profile**, which is *not* linear because the area grows with $r$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical Geometry)

**Step 1 — Write the total thermal resistance for an insulated cylinder:**

Insulation extends from inner radius $r_1$ to outer radius $r_2$, with outside convection to ambient at $T_\infty$.

$$R_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}}_{\text{Conduction in insulation}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)}}_{\text{Convection at outer surface}}$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_i - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}}$$

**Step 3 — Condition for maximum heat transfer:**
$Q$ is maximum when $R_{total}$ is minimum. Differentiate $R_{total}$ with respect to $r_2$ and set to zero.

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k L r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi h L r_2^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = \frac{k}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Critical radius of insulation:**

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outside}}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- If $r_2 < r_c$: Heat loss **increases** as insulation is added (counterintuitive!).
- If $r_2 > r_c$: Heat loss **decreases** as more insulation is added (expected behavior).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Radial Conduction Through a Hollow Sphere

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law in spherical coordinates:**

$$Q = -kA(r)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

where $A(r) = 4\pi r^2$ is the spherical surface area at radius $r$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$Q = -k(4\pi r^2)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**

$$Q\frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $r_1$ (at $T_1$) to $r_2$ (at $T_2$):**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2}\frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$Q\left(-\frac{1}{r}\right)\Big|_{r_1}^{r_2} = -4\pi k(T_2 - T_1)$$

$$Q\left(-\frac{1}{r_2} + \frac{1}{r_1}\right) = 4\pi k(T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Q$:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}}$$

**Step 7 — Temperature distribution:**

$$T(r) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Plane Wall | Hollow Cylinder | Hollow Sphere |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Area function | $A = $ const | $A = 2\pi r L$ | $A = 4\pi r^2$ |
| Temperature profile | Linear | Logarithmic | Hyperbolic $(1/r)$ |
| Governing ODE | $\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = 0$ | $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}(r \frac{dT}{dr}) = 0$ | $\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}) = 0$ |
| Critical radius? | No | **Yes** $(r_c = k/h)$ | Yes (analogous expression) |
| Resistance formula | $L/(kA)$ | $\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $\frac{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}{4\pi k}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Conduction System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steady-State 1D Heat Conduction] --> B[Constant Area Geometry]
    A --> C[Variable Area Geometry]

    B --> B1[Plane Wall]
    B1 --> B1a[Single Layer]
    B1 --> B1b[Composite Series]
    B1 --> B1c[Composite Parallel]

    C --> C1[Hollow Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Hollow Sphere]

    C1 --> C1a[Single Layer]
    C1 --> C1b[Composite Series]
    C1 --> C1c[Critical Radius of Insulation]

    C2 --> C2a[Single Layer]
    C2 --> C2b[Composite Series]

    B1a --> D[Q equals dT divided by R_th]
    B1b --> D
    B1c --> D
    C1a --> D
    C1b --> D
    C1c --> D
    C2a --> D
    C2b --> D

    D --> E[Thermal Resistance Network Analogy]
    E --> E1[Plane Wall: R equals L over kA]
    E --> E2[Cylinder: R equals ln r2/r1 over 2 pi k L]
    E --> E3[Sphere: R equals 1/r1 minus 1/r2 over 4 pi k]
```

## 4.2 Thermal Resistance Network — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneWallResistor["PLANE WALL THERMAL RESISTANCE NETWORK"]
        P1((T_hot)) --> R1["R1 = L1 over k1A"]
        R1 --> R2["R2 = L2 over k2A"]
        R2 --> R3["R3 = L3 over k3A"]
        R3 --> P2((T_cold))

        R1 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalP["R_total = Sum of L_i over k_i A"]
        R2 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalP
        R3 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalP
    end

    subgraph CylinderResistor["HOLLOW CYLINDER RESISTANCE NETWORK"]
        C1((T_inner)) --> RC1["R_cond1 = ln r2 over r1 over 2 pi k1 L"]
        RC1 --> RC2["R_cond2 = ln r3 over r2 over 2 pi k2 L"]
        RC2 --> Rconv["R_conv = 1 over h A_out"]
        Rconv --> C2((T_ambient))

        RC1 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalC["R_total = ln r2/r1 over 2 pi k L + 1 over h A_out"]
        RC2 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalC
        Rconv -. Series Addition .-> RtotalC
    end

    subgraph SphereResistor["HOLLOW SPHERE RESISTANCE NETWORK"]
        S1((T_inner)) --> RS1["R1 = 1 over r1 minus 1 over r2 over 4 pi k1"]
        RS1 --> RS2["R2 = 1 over r2 minus 1 over r3 over 4 pi k2"]
        RS2 --> S2((T_outer))

        RS1 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalS["R_total = Sum of 1 over r_i minus 1 over r_i+1 over 4 pi k_i"]
        RS2 -. Series Addition .-> RtotalS
    end
```

## 4.3 Critical Radius of Insulation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Insulated Cylinder: T_inner at r1, T_ambient outside]) --> Step1[Step 1: Identify k of insulation and h of ambient]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Compute r_c equals k divided by h]
    Step2 --> Decision{Compare r_outer with r_c}

    Decision -->|r_outer LESS THAN r_c| Branch1[Q INCREASES with more insulation]
    Decision -->|r_outer EQUALS r_c| Branch2[Q is MAXIMUM]
    Decision -->|r_outer GREATER THAN r_c| Branch3[Q DECREASES with more insulation]

    Branch1 --> Result1[Counterintuitive Regime: Insulating Actually Increases Heat Loss]
    Branch2 --> Result2[Critical Condition Reached]
    Branch3 --> Result3[Standard Regime: Insulation Works as Expected]

    Result1 --> EngUse1[Engineering Implication: Bare Wires of Small Radius May Overheat if Insulated Poorly]
    Result3 --> EngUse2[Engineering Implication: Add Insulation Beyond r_c to Reduce Heat Loss Effectively]
```

## 4.4 Geometric Area Evolution — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneGeo["PLANE WALL"]
        PShape["Area = A (constant)"]
        PShape --> PEx["Heat flux q = Q/A = constant"]
    end

    subgraph CylGeo["HOLLOW CYLINDER"]
        CShape["Area = 2 pi r L (linear in r)"]
        CShape --> CEx["Heat flux q = Q/(2 pi r L) decreases with r"]
    end

    subgraph SphGeo["HOLLOW SPHERE"]
        SShape["Area = 4 pi r squared (quadratic in r)"]
        SShape --> SEx["Heat flux q = Q/(4 pi r squared) decreases sharply with r"]
    end

    PlaneGeo --> Comparison{Geometric Comparison Summary}
    CylGeo --> Comparison
    SphGeo --> Comparison

    Comparison --> Insight["Insight: Sphere loses heat flux fastest as r grows; Plane wall keeps flux uniform."]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define thermal conductivity. Explain its physical significance with reference to Fourier's law.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal conductivity ($k$) is a **material property** that quantifies a substance's ability to conduct heat. Its SI unit is **W/(m·K)**.

From Fourier's law $Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$, the thermal conductivity $k$ is the **proportionality constant** linking heat flux to the temperature gradient. Physically:

- A **high $k$** material (e.g., copper, $k \approx 400$ W/m·K) conducts heat readily — used in heat sinks.
- A **low $k$** material (e.g., fiberglass, $k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K) resists heat flow — used in insulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always quote the **numerical range** of $k$ for solids, liquids, and gases. Students often forget that *thermal conductivity depends weakly on temperature*, which matters in composite wall problems.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is meant by critical radius of insulation? Write its expression for a cylindrical pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical radius of insulation** ($r_c$) is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from an insulated cylinder reaches a **maximum value**. For cylindrical insulation:

$$r_c = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{ambient}}$$

**Key insight:** When the actual outer radius $r_2 < r_c$, adding insulation paradoxically **increases** heat loss because the gain in external surface area (which enhances convection) outweighs the gain in conductive resistance. Once $r_2 > r_c$, additional insulation reduces heat loss as expected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Example:** Electrical cables with **small radius** relative to $r_c$ may overheat if poorly insulated. Always choose insulation thickness greater than $r_c$ for effective insulation.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts **(a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks**. Module-level internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A — OPTION 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the steady-state heat transfer rate through a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, thermal conductivity $k$, and length $L$, with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ at the inner and outer surfaces respectively. Also write the temperature distribution equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steam pipe of outer diameter 10 cm is covered with an insulating layer of thermal conductivity 0.18 W/(m·K) and thickness 5 cm. The outer surface temperature of insulation is 40 °C and it is exposed to ambient air at 25 °C with convection coefficient $h = 10$ W/(m²·K). Calculate:

1. The critical radius of insulation.
2. The rate of heat loss per metre length of the pipe.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation**

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates:
$$Q = -kA(r)\frac{dT}{dr} = -k(2\pi r L)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:
$$Q\frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L\,dT$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $r_1$ (at $T_1$) to $r_2$ (at $T_2$):
$$Q\int_{r_1}^{r_2}\frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$
$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 4:** Final expression:
$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Step 5:** Temperature distribution:
$$T(r) = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**
- Outer diameter of pipe = 10 cm → $r_1 = 0.05$ m
- Insulation thickness = 5 cm → $r_2 = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10$ m
- $k = 0.18$ W/(m·K)
- Surface temperature of insulation $T_s = 40$ °C, $T_\infty = 25$ °C
- $h = 10$ W/(m²·K)
- $L = 1$ m (per metre length)

**(i) Critical radius of insulation:**
$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.18}{10} = 0.018 \text{ m} = 1.8 \text{ cm}$$

Since $r_2 = 10$ cm $> r_c = 1.8$ cm, the insulation thickness is **greater than critical**, so heat loss will *decrease* with more insulation.

**(ii) Rate of heat loss per metre:**

Total resistance per metre length:
$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)}$$

Compute each term:
$$\frac{\ln(0.10/0.05)}{2\pi \cdot 0.18 \cdot 1} = \frac{\ln(2)}{1.131} = \frac{0.693}{1.131} = 0.613 \text{ K/W}$$

$$\frac{1}{10 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.10 \cdot 1} = \frac{1}{6.283} = 0.159 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.613 + 0.159 = 0.772 \text{ K/W}$$

Heat loss:
$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{40 - 25}{0.772} = 19.43 \text{ W per metre}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add thickness to get $r_2$** — A 5 cm insulation on a 5 cm pipe radius gives $r_2 = 10$ cm, NOT 5 cm. This is the **#1 mistake** in this question.
> 2. **Mixing units** — All dimensions must be in metres before substituting into SI formulas.
> 3. **Confusing $r_1$ (pipe outer radius)** with the *inner radius of insulation*. In this problem, they are the same since the pipe itself is not hollow.

---

### **Question B — OPTION 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for steady-state heat conduction rate through a hollow sphere with inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, thermal conductivity $k$, and inner and outer surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite spherical wall consists of two layers. The inner layer has an inner radius of 5 cm, outer radius of 10 cm, and thermal conductivity of 50 W/(m·K). The outer layer has thermal conductivity 0.05 W/(m·K) and outer radius 15 cm. The inner surface temperature is 600 °C and the outer surface temperature is 50 °C. Calculate:

1. The rate of heat loss.
2. The temperature at the interface between the two layers.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation**

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law in spherical coordinates:
$$Q = -kA(r)\frac{dT}{dr} = -k(4\pi r^2)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:
$$Q\frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k\,dT$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $r_1$ (at $T_1$) to $r_2$ (at $T_2$):
$$Q\int_{r_1}^{r_2}\frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$Q\left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$
$$Q\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 5:** Final expression:
$$\boxed{Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**
- Layer 1 (inner): $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 50$ W/(m·K)
- Layer 2 (outer): $r_3 = 0.15$ m, $k_2 = 0.05$ W/(m·K)
- $T_1 = 600$ °C, $T_3 = 50$ °C
- $\Delta T_{overall} = 600 - 50 = 550$ °C

**(i) Rate of heat loss using composite sphere formula:**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_3)}{\frac{1}{k_1}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{1}{k_2}\left(\frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{r_3}\right)}$$

Compute each resistance term:

**Conduction resistance of Layer 1:**
$$R_1 = \frac{1}{50}\left(\frac{1}{0.05} - \frac{1}{0.10}\right) = \frac{1}{50}(20 - 10) = \frac{10}{50} = 0.200 \text{ K/W}$$

**Conduction resistance of Layer 2:**
$$R_2 = \frac{1}{0.05}\left(\frac{1}{0.10} - \frac{1}{0.15}\right) = 20 \times (10 - 6.667) = 20 \times 3.333 = 66.667 \text{ K/W}$$

**Total resistance:**
$$R_{total} = 0.200 + 66.667 = 66.867 \text{ K/W}$$

**Heat loss:**
$$Q = \frac{4\pi \cdot 550}{66.867} = \frac{6911.5}{66.867} = 103.36 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Interface temperature $T_2$:**

Heat flux through Layer 1:
$$Q = \frac{4\pi k_1 (T_1 - T_2)}{1/r_1 - 1/r_2}$$

Solving for $T_2$:
$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}{4\pi k_1} = \frac{103.36 \times 10}{4\pi \times 50} = \frac{1033.6}{628.32} = 1.645 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_2 = 600 - 1.645 = 598.35 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Formula mix-up:** Some students mistakenly use the **cylindrical** formula for spherical problems — the denominator is $(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$, NOT $\ln(r_2/r_1)$.
> 2. **Interface temperature check:** In a composite system, the temperature drop occurs **mostly across the high-resistance layer**. Here, layer 2 has $R = 66.67$ K/W (insulator), so almost the entire drop (≈ 548.4 °C) occurs there, and $T_2 \approx T_1$.
> 3. **Order of radii:** Always list $r_1 < r_2 < r_3 < \ldots < r_n$ in the direction of heat flow.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

- ✅ **Fourier's Law** is the foundation: $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$. Always begin derivations here.
- ✅ **Plane Wall:** Heat rate $Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ → **linear** temperature profile.
- ✅ **Hollow Cylinder:** Heat rate $Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ → **logarithmic** temperature profile.
- ✅ **Hollow Sphere:** Heat rate $Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ → **hyperbolic (1/r)** temperature profile.
- ✅ **Composite systems (series):** Resistances add directly: $R_{total} = \sum R_i$.
- ✅ **Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder):** $r_c = k/h$. If $r_2 < r_c$, adding insulation **increases** heat loss — a classic KTU conceptual question.
- ✅ **Units are non-negotiable:** Use SI (metres, watts, kelvin). Wrong unit conversions are the most common reason for losing 1–2 marks per sub-question.
- ✅ **Direction of heat flow:** Always from higher to lower temperature. Verify signs consistently.
- ✅ **For interface temperature problems:** Compute heat rate first, then work backward through any one layer to find $T_{interface}$.
- ✅ **Distinguish the three geometries by their area dependence:** constant (plane), linear $r$ (cylinder), quadratic $r^2$ (sphere).
- ✅ **Thermal Resistance Analogy:** The conduction problem reduces to Ohm's-law form: $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$. Use this universally.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Conduction Through Plane Walls, Cylinders and Spherical Systems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Composite Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Composite System? In heat transfer, a **composite system** is a physical body or structure that is made up of **two or more distinct materials (or layers) of different thermal conductivities arranged in series, parallel, or a combination of both**, through which heat is transferred under steady-state, one-dimensional conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** > *"A composite system consists of multiple layers of different materials joined together to form a single structural unit. Heat conduction through such systems is analyzed using the principle of superposition of thermal resistances, analogous to electrical resistance networks."* The three principal geometries of composite systems studied in KTU Module 1 are: | Geometry | Typical Engineering Example | |---|---| | **Plane Composite Wall** | Building walls (brick + plaster + insulation), furnace walls, refrigerator walls | | **Composite Cylinder** | Insulated steam pipes, boiler tubes, underground steam lines | | **Composite Sphere** | Storage tanks, spherical pressure vessels, cryogenic containers | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Woolen Sweater Inside a Raincoat" Imagine you are standing in the cold rain wearing: - An **inner cotton shirt** (low thermal conductivity, $k \approx 0.05$ W/m·K), - A **middle woolen sweater** ($k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K), - An **outer raincoat** ($k \approx 0.2$ W/m·K). This is your **composite system**. Body heat must pass through *each* layer in **sequence (series)** before escaping. Each layer "resists" the heat flow differently. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Insight:** > Just as a thicker woolen sweater provides *more resistance* to heat loss, a thicker insulation layer increases *thermal resistance*. The total resistance is the **sum of individual resistances** — the fundamental basis of composite system analysis. --- ## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Arrangements **A. Series Arrangement** (Heat flows perpendicular to layers — most common): - Heat passes through one layer, then the next, then the next. - **Total Resistance:** $R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots$ - **Heat flux** is *constant* through every layer. - **Temperature drop** is *largest* across the layer with highest resistance (lowest $k$). **B. Parallel Arrangement** (Heat flows along layers, side-by-side): - Heat splits into multiple parallel paths. - **Total Resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dots$ - **Temperature difference** is *common* across all paths. - Used in composite walls where different sections are side-by-side (e.g., wall with window + wall with door). > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A *plane wall* with area $A$ and layers of different $k$ is analyzed using **series resistance** if the heat flow is normal to the wall. If the wall is built with **side-by-side sections** (e.g., half brick + half concrete), it is a **parallel** resistance problem. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference) - **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)**: W/(m·K) — material property indicating heat conduction ability. - **Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)**: W/(m²·K) — typically ranges from **5 (natural air)** to **25,000 (boiling water)**. - **Thermal Resistance ($R$)**: (m²·K)/W — opposition to heat flow. - **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)**: W/(m²·K) — inverse of total resistance per unit area. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 The Thermal Resistance Concept — The Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Gold Standard:** Every composite system in steady-state, 1-D conduction is solved using the **thermal resistance network approach**. This is non-negotiable for Module 1 board problems. The thermal resistance $R$ is the *opposition* a layer offers to heat flow, defined as the **temperature drop per unit heat flow rate**. For a plane wall: $$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{L}{kA}$$ For convection at a surface: $$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}$$ For radiation (occasionally relevant): $$R_{rad} = \frac{1}{h_r A}$$ --- ## 2.2 Composite Plane Wall — Step-by-Step Logic **Setup:** A wall of total thickness made of $n$ layers. Hot fluid at $T_{\infty,1}$ on left, cold fluid at $T_{\infty,2}$ on right. Heat flows from left to right. **Step 1: Identify the resistance network (series):** $$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} + \dots + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$ **Step 2: Apply Fourier + Newton combined law:** $$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{R_{total}}$$ **Step 3: Define Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** $$\frac{1}{UA} = R_{total} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_1} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + \frac{1}{h_2}$$ **Step 4: Heat flux ($q''$ = $Q/A$):** $$q'' = U (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})$$ **Step 5: Find intermediate interface temperatures (often asked in KTU):** $$T_{interface} = T_{preceding} - Q \cdot R_{layer}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** Under steady-state, the same $Q$ passes through every resistance in series. The temperature drop is proportional to the magnitude of each resistance. So the layer with the **lowest $k$ (or largest $L$)** experiences the **largest temperature drop**. --- ## 2.3 Composite Cylindrical System (Coaxial Cylinders) A pipe of inner radius $r_1$ carries hot fluid; insulation (or another metal layer) extends to $r_2$, $r_3$, etc. The surrounding fluid is at $T_{\infty,2}$. **Resistances in series:** $$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_1 (2\pi r_1 L)} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L} + \frac{1}{h_2 (2\pi r_3 L)}$$ **Heat transfer rate:** $$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{R_{total}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})}{\frac{1}{h_1 r_1} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_2 r_3}}$$ --- ## 2.4 Composite Spherical System For concentric spherical shells (rare in exams, but syllabus-relevant): $$\frac{Q}{4\pi} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\frac{1}{h_1 r_1^2} + \frac{1}{k_1}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{1}{k_2}\left(\frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{r_3}\right) + \frac{1}{h_2 r_3^2}}$$ --- ## 2.5 Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical) A **counterintuitive phenomenon**: Adding insulation to a *bare* small-diameter pipe can **increase** heat loss up to a point. **Critical radius of insulation** for a cylinder: $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_{outside}}$$ **Behavior:** - If $r_2 < r_{cr}$: Adding insulation **increases** heat loss. - If $r_2 > r_{cr}$: Adding insulation **decreases** heat loss (normal behavior). - If $r_2 = r_{cr}$: Heat loss is **maximum**. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Plane wall resistance | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | K/W | | 2 | Convective resistance | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | K/W | | 3 | Cylindrical resistance (radial) | $R_{cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | K/W | | 4 | Spherical resistance | $R_{sph} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | K/W | | 5 | Total series resistance | $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$ | K/W | | 6 | Heat rate (series) | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{tot}}$ | W | | 7 | Plane wall $U$ | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_1} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_2}$ | m²K/W | | 8 | Cylindrical $U$ (based on $r_1$) | $\dfrac{1}{U_1 r_1} = \dfrac{1}{h_1 r_1} + \sum \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 r_{n+1}}$ | m·K/W | | 9 | Critical radius (cylinder) | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_{out}}$ | m | | 10 | Parallel resistance (walls) | $\dfrac{1}{R_{tot}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | W/K | | 11 | Interface temperature | $T_2 = T_1 - Q R_1$ | K | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Refrigeration & AC industry:** Insulated copper tubes carrying refrigerant. 2. **Power plants:** Boiler tubes with refractory + insulation layers. 3. **Building design:** Cavity walls with brick, air-gap, plaster for thermal comfort. 4. **Cryogenics:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) for liquid nitrogen/oxygen storage. 5. **Process industries:** Jacketed reactors, double-pipe heat exchangers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Transfer Through a 3-Layer Plane Composite Wall (with surface convection) **System Description:** A plane wall consists of three solid layers (thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$; conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$). A hot fluid at $T_{\infty,1}$ flows past the left surface with coefficient $h_1$, and a cold fluid at $T_{\infty,2}$ flows past the right surface with coefficient $h_2$. Area of wall = $A$. Steady-state, 1-D conduction. **Step 1 — Energy balance at the hot fluid surface (left boundary):** Convective heat arriving from fluid = heat conducted into layer 1. $$h_1 A (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1}) = \frac{k_1 A (T_{s,1} - T_2)}{L_1}$$ where $T_{s,1}$ is the left surface temperature and $T_2$ is the temperature at the interface between layer 1 and layer 2. **Step 2 — Steady-state conduction through layer 1:** $$Q = \frac{k_1 A (T_{s,1} - T_2)}{L_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{s,1} - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}$$ **Step 3 — Conduction through layer 2:** $$T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A}$$ **Step 4 — Conduction through layer 3:** $$T_3 - T_{s,2} = \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A}$$ **Step 5 — Convection at right surface:** $$T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2} = \frac{Q}{h_2 A}$$ **Step 6 — Sum all temperature drops:** $$ \begin{aligned} (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}) &= (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1}) + (T_{s,1} - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_{s,2}) + (T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2}) \\[6pt] &= \frac{Q}{h_1 A} + \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A} + \frac{Q}{h_2 A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Factor out $Q/A$:** $$ T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2} = \frac{Q}{A}\left[\frac{1}{h_1} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_2}\right] $$ **Step 8 — Solve for $Q$:** $$ \boxed{\,Q = \frac{A (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})}{\dfrac{1}{h_1} + \dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3} + \dfrac{1}{h_2}}\,} $$ This is the **KTU-standard** form for a multi-layer plane wall with convection on both sides. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Composite Cylinder (Coaxial Pipe with Two Layers) **Setup:** Inner pipe of radius $r_1$ and conductivity $k_1$. Outer insulation from $r_2$ to $r_3$ with conductivity $k_2$. Inner convection $h_1$ inside pipe; outer convection $h_2$ in surrounding air. Length $L$. **Step 1 — Steady-state radial conduction in a hollow cylinder (Fourier's Law in cylindrical coordinates):** $$Q = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate between $r_1$ and $r_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} &= -2\pi k_1 L \int_{T_{s,1}}^{T_2} dT \\[4pt] Q \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) &= 2\pi k_1 L (T_{s,1} - T_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for temperature drop across layer 1:** $$T_{s,1} - T_2 = \frac{Q \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L}$$ **Step 4 — Similarly for insulation layer ($r_2$ to $r_3$):** $$T_2 - T_{s,2} = \frac{Q \ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L}$$ **Step 5 — Convection inside pipe (area = $2\pi r_1 L$):** $$T_{\infty,1} - T_{s,1} = \frac{Q}{h_1 (2\pi r_1 L)}$$ **Step 6 — Convection outside (area = $2\pi r_3 L$):** $$T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,2} = \frac{Q}{h_2 (2\pi r_3 L)}$$ **Step 7 — Add all temperature drops and factor out $Q/(2\pi L)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2} &= \frac{Q}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{h_1 r_1} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_2 r_3}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Final form for $Q$:** $$ \boxed{\,Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})}{\dfrac{1}{h_1 r_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 r_3}}\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder) **Setup:** Bare cylinder of outer radius $r_1$, conductivity $k_c$ (pipe), covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and conductivity $k_{ins}$. Surrounding fluid coefficient = $h$. **Step 1 — Total resistance from inner surface ($T_1$) to ambient ($T_\infty$):** $$ R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)} $$ > For a *bare* pipe (no insulation), $R_{tot} = \dfrac{1}{h (2\pi r_1 L)}$. **Step 2 — Express heat loss in terms of $r_2$:** $$ Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h r_2}} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate the denominator $D(r_2) = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h r_2}$ with respect to $r_2$:** $$ \frac{dD}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k_{ins} r_2} - \frac{1}{h r_2^2} $$ **Step 4 — Set derivative = 0 to find minimum denominator (i.e., maximum $Q$):** $$ \frac{1}{k_{ins} r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = \frac{k_{ins}}{h} $$ **Step 5 — Critical radius result:** $$ \boxed{\,r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_{outside}}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - For **small diameter pipes** (e.g., electrical wires, $r_1 < r_{cr}$): Adding insulation *increases* heat loss up to $r_{cr}$. > - This is why **electrical heating wires** often have a *thin insulation* — too much insulation can cause overheating! > - For **large pipes** (steam mains, $r_1 > r_{cr}$): Insulation always reduces heat loss. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What | Geometry | Heat Flow Direction | Key Variable | Resistance Form | |---|---|---|---| | Plane wall | 1-D, perpendicular | Area $A$ constant | $L/(kA)$ | | Cylinder | 1-D, radial | Area changes with $r$ | $\ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ | | Sphere | 1-D, radial | Area $\propto r^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question) **Problem:** A furnace wall is made of 10 cm of fireclay brick ($k = 1.0$ W/m·K), 5 cm of insulating brick ($k = 0.2$ W/m·K), and 8 cm of red brick ($k = 0.7$ W/m·K). The inner surface temperature is 800°C and the outer surface is at 60°C. Calculate: (a) Heat loss per m² of wall area (b) Temperature at the interface between fireclay and insulating brick. **Solution:** Given: $L_1 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 1.0$ W/m·K; $L_2 = 0.05$ m, $k_2 = 0.2$ W/m·K; $L_3 = 0.08$ m, $k_3 = 0.7$ W/m·K; $T_{s,1} = 800°C$, $T_{s,2} = 60°C$. **(a) Heat flux:** $$ \begin{aligned} q'' &= \frac{T_{s,1} - T_{s,2}}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{800 - 60}{\dfrac{0.10}{1.0} + \dfrac{0.05}{0.2} + \dfrac{0.08}{0.7}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{740}{0.10 + 0.25 + 0.1143} \\[8pt] &= \frac{740}{0.4643} = 1593.6 \text{ W/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Interface temperature $T_2$ (between layer 1 and layer 2):** The heat flowing through layer 1 drops temperature from $T_{s,1} = 800°C$ to $T_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} q'' &= \frac{k_1 (T_{s,1} - T_2)}{L_1} \\[6pt] T_2 &= T_{s,1} - \frac{q'' L_1}{k_1} = 800 - \frac{1593.6 \times 0.10}{1.0} = 800 - 159.36 = 640.6°C \end{aligned} $$ > **Insight:** Notice the temperature drop across the insulating brick ($k = 0.2$) is **1593.6 × 0.25 = 398.4°C** — by far the largest drop, because it has the highest resistance. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thermal Resistance Network for Composite Plane Wall ```mermaid graph LR A[Hot Fluid
T_infinity_1]:::hot -->|R_conv1 = 1/h1A| B[Left Surface

Layers, k values, h, T_inf]:::input GEOM{Geometry Type?}:::decision PLANE[Plane Wall Solver

1 by U A = R_total]:::module T_INT[Interface Temp Calculator
T_i = T_prev minus Q times R_i]:::module OUTPUT[Final Results
Q, U, T at interfaces]:::output INPUT --> GEOM GEOM -->|Plane| PLANE GEOM -->|Cylinder| CYL GEOM -->|Sphere| SPH PLANE --> SUMMER CYL --> SUMMER SPH --> SUMMER SUMMER --> Q_CALC SUMMER --> U_CALC Q_CALC --> T_INT U_CALC --> OUTPUT T_INT --> OUTPUT classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef module fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic: Heat Loss vs. Insulation Radius (Critical Radius Phenomenon) ```mermaid graph LR X[Insulation Outer Radius r2]:::axis Y[Heat Loss Q]:::axis L1[Low Q initially
bare pipe r1 small]:::point P[Maximum Q at r_cr = k_ins by h]:::peak L2[Q decreases for r2 greater than r_cr]:::point X --> P Y --> P L1 --> P --> L2 classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef point fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef peak fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` > **Key Takeaway:** Heat loss first *increases* with added insulation (up to $r_{cr}$), reaches a **peak** at $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h$, and *then decreases* as more insulation is added. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define thermal resistance. Derive the expression for thermal resistance of a plane wall and a hollow cylinder.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Thermal resistance is the opposition offered by a medium (solid, fluid interface, or combination) to the flow of heat. It is the ratio of temperature difference across the medium to the rate of heat transfer through it. $$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} \quad \text{(units: K/W or °C/W)}$$ **(a) Plane Wall Resistance:** Applying Fourier's law of conduction to a plane wall of area $A$, thickness $L$, and conductivity $k$: $$ Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} $$ Rearranging: $$ \boxed{R_{wall} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{Q} = \frac{L}{kA}} $$ **(b) Hollow Cylinder Resistance:** For radial conduction through a cylinder of length $L$, inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, conductivity $k$: Integrating Fourier's law $Q = -k(2\pi r L)\dfrac{dT}{dr}$ between $r_1$ and $r_2$: $$ \boxed{R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}} $$ > The cylindrical resistance is *logarithmic* in radius, while the plane wall resistance is *linear* in thickness. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **What is the critical radius of insulation? Explain why heat transfer from a small-diameter pipe first increases and then decreases as insulation thickness is increased.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The critical radius of insulation is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from a cylindrical pipe (or wire) to the surroundings is **maximum**, given by: $$ \boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outside}}} $$ **Explanation:** The total resistance from the pipe surface to ambient is: $$ R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)} $$ Two competing effects occur as $r_2$ increases: 1. **Conduction resistance** $\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L}$ **increases** (good — reduces $Q$). 2. **Convection resistance** $\dfrac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)}$ **decreases** (bad — increases $Q$) because the surface area $2\pi r_2 L$ grows. Initially, for small $r_2$, the *increase in outer surface area dominates*, so $Q$ rises. After $r_2$ exceeds $r_{cr}$, the *conduction resistance dominates* and $Q$ falls. > **Engineering Implication:** For small wires (e.g., thermocouple wires, $r_1 = 0.5$ mm, $k_{ins} = 0.1$, $h = 10$), $r_{cr} = 0.01$ m = 10 mm. Insulating below 10 mm actually *worsens* heat dissipation. --- ## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** A composite wall consists of three layers in series. The first layer is 5 cm thick brick ($k_1 = 0.7$ W/m·K), the second is 3 cm thick plaster ($k_2 = 0.5$ W/m·K), and the third is 8 cm thick wood ($k_3 = 0.15$ W/m·K). The hot side air is at 100°C with $h_1 = 10$ W/m²·K, and the cold side air is at 20°C with $h_2 = 25$ W/m²·K. Calculate: 1. The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$. 2. The heat flux through the wall. 3. The temperature at the brick–plaster interface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steam pipe of 5 cm outer diameter is covered with 4 cm of insulation ($k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K). The pipe surface is at 200°C, and the ambient air is at 25°C with $h = 10$ W/m²·K. Find: 1. The heat loss per metre length of pipe. 2. The outer surface temperature of the insulation. **(7 marks)** --- ### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Composite Plane Wall **Given:** - $L_1 = 0.05$ m, $k_1 = 0.7$ W/m·K - $L_2 = 0.03$ m, $k_2 = 0.5$ W/m·K - $L_3 = 0.08$ m, $k_3 = 0.15$ W/m·K - $T_{\infty,1} = 100°C$, $h_1 = 10$ W/m²·K - $T_{\infty,2} = 20°C$, $h_2 = 25$ W/m²·K **(1) Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{h_1} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.05}{0.7} + \frac{0.03}{0.5} + \frac{0.08}{0.15} + \frac{1}{25} \\[6pt] &= 0.100 + 0.0714 + 0.0600 + 0.5333 + 0.0400 \\[6pt] &= 0.8047 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{U = \frac{1}{0.8047} = 1.243 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}} $$ **(2) Heat flux $q''$:** $$ \boxed{q'' = U(T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}) = 1.243 \times 80 = 99.4 \text{ W/m}^2} $$ **(3) Brick–plaster interface temperature $T_2$:** Sum of resistances up to layer 1 from hot side (per unit area): $$ R_{up\ to\ 2} = \frac{1}{h_1} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 0.10 + 0.0714 = 0.1714 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$ $$ \boxed{T_2 = T_{\infty,1} - q'' \cdot R_{up\ to\ 2} = 100 - 99.4 \times 0.1714 = 100 - 17.04 = 82.96°C} $$ --- #### Part (b) — Insulated Cylindrical Pipe **Given:** - $r_1 = 0.025$ m (outer radius of bare pipe), $T_{s,1} = 200°C$ - $r_2 = 0.025 + 0.04 = 0.065$ m - $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K - $T_\infty = 25°C$, $h = 10$ W/m²·K, $L = 1$ m **(1) Heat loss per metre length:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{total} &= \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\ln(0.065/0.025)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1} + \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.065 \times 1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\ln(2.6)}{0.3142} + \frac{1}{4.084} \\[6pt] &= \frac{0.9555}{0.3142} + 0.2449 \\[6pt] &= 3.041 + 0.245 = 3.286 \text{ K/W (per m)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{Q = \frac{T_{s,1} - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{200 - 25}{3.286} = 53.26 \text{ W/m}} $$ **(2) Outer surface temperature of insulation $T_{s,2}$:** $$ \boxed{T_{s,2} = T_\infty + Q \cdot \frac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)} = 25 + 53.26 \times 0.245 = 25 + 13.05 = 38.05°C} $$ > **Verification:** Temperature drop across insulation = $200 - 38.05 = 161.95°C$. Check: $Q = \frac{161.95}{3.041} = 53.26$ W/m ✓ --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice). [KTU University Exam - June 2023] **(a)** A furnace wall made of firebrick ($k = 1.4$ W/m·K, 15 cm thick) is lined with insulating material ($k_{ins} = 0.25$ W/m·K, 8 cm thick). The inside surface of the firebrick is at 1100°C, and the outside of the insulation is at 80°C. Calculate: 1. The heat loss per m² of wall. 2. The temperature at the firebrick–insulation interface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of the **critical radius of insulation** for a cylindrical pipe. An electric heating wire of 2 mm diameter ($k = 200$ W/m·K) is to be insulated with a material having $k_{ins} = 0.1$ W/m·K. The surrounding air has $h = 50$ W/m²·K. Determine: 1. The critical radius of insulation. 2. The maximum thickness of insulation recommended so that heat loss is *not increased* beyond the bare-wire value. **(7 marks)** --- ### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Two-Layer Plane Wall **Given:** $L_1 = 0.15$ m, $k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K, $L_2 = 0.08$ m, $k_2 = 0.25$ W/m·K, $T_{s,1} = 1100°C$, $T_{s,2} = 80°C$. **(1) Heat flux:** $$ \begin{aligned} q'' &= \frac{T_{s,1} - T_{s,2}}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2}} = \frac{1100 - 80}{\dfrac{0.15}{1.4} + \dfrac{0.08}{0.25}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1020}{0.1071 + 0.32} = \frac{1020}{0.4271} = 2388.1 \text{ W/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(2) Interface temperature $T_2$:** $$ T_2 = T_{s,1} - q'' \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 1100 - 2388.1 \times 0.1071 = 1100 - 255.7 = 844.3°C $$ --- #### Part (b) — Critical Radius of Insulation **Given:** Bare wire radius $r_1 = 0.001$ m (1 mm), $k_{ins} = 0.1$ W/m·K, $h = 50$ W/m²·K. **(1) Critical radius:** $$ \boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h} = \frac{0.1}{50} = 0.002 \text{ m} = 2 \text{ mm}} $$ **(2) Maximum insulation thickness:** Since $r_1 = 1$ mm is *less* than $r_{cr} = 2$ mm, the wire is in the regime where adding insulation increases heat loss. To prevent heat loss exceeding the bare-wire value, the insulation outer radius must be: $$ r_2 \geq r_{cr} = 2 \text{ mm} $$ So the **maximum thickness** is when $r_2 = r_{cr}$: $$ \boxed{t_{max} = r_{cr} - r_1 = 2 - 1 = 1 \text{ mm}} $$ > **Practical Engineering Note:** For an electric heating element (small wire), insulation should be *carefully chosen* — too much insulation is counterproductive. This is why nichrome wires in toasters often appear *bare*. --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > **1. Forgetting to convert cm to m:** 90% of KTU students lose 1–2 marks by plugging thickness in cm without conversion. **Always write units explicitly.** > > **2. Mixing up $h$ values:** If $h_1$ and $h_2$ are swapped in a composite wall problem, the answer is wrong by a small but significant amount. **Hot side = $h_1$**, **Cold side = $h_2$**. > > **3. Using $L$ instead of $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ for cylinders:** A plane wall formula applied to a cylinder gives a wrong answer. **Memorize the log form.** > > **4. Confusing $r_{cr}$ with the actual outer radius:** The critical radius is a *property of the insulation + ambient*, not the wire. **Always $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h$.** > > **5. Not calculating interface temperatures:** KTU frequently asks for intermediate temperatures (T₂, T₃). Many students stop after finding $Q$. **Always complete the full solution.** --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ### **Key Concepts** - [x] A composite system is **multiple layers of different $k$** in series, parallel, or combined. - [x] Heat flow direction is **1-D and steady-state** (assumed in Module 1). - [x] **Thermal resistance network** is the universal method for solution. - [x] Series resistance **adds**: $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$. - [x] Parallel resistance **harmonic adds**: $1/R_{tot} = \sum 1/R_i$. ### **Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)** - [x] Plane wall: $R = L/(kA)$ - [x] Cylinder: $R = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ - [x] Sphere: $R = (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$ - [x] Convection: $R = 1/(hA)$ - [x] Overall $U$: $1/U = 1/h_1 + \sum L_i/k_i + 1/h_2$ (plane wall) - [x] Cylinder $U$: $1/(U_1 r_1) = 1/(h_1 r_1) + \sum \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)/k_i + 1/(h_2 r_{n+1})$ - [x] Critical radius: $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h$ ### **Physical Insights** - [x] Layer with **lowest $k$** → **largest temperature drop**. - [x] Adding insulation to a **small wire** can *increase* heat loss. - [x] In a composite cylinder, **heat flux varies with radius** (unlike plane wall). - [x] **Heat rate $Q$ is constant** in series; **temperature drop is constant** in parallel. ### **Solved Pattern (Always Follow)** 1. Draw the geometry, label $T$'s, $k$'s, $L$'s, $h$'s. 2. Write the **resistance network** explicitly. 3. Sum resistances: $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$. 4. Compute $Q = \Delta T / R_{tot}$ or $q'' = U \cdot \Delta T$. 5. Find **interface temperatures** using $T_i = T_{prev} - Q \cdot R_{up\ to\ i}$. ### **KTU-Preferred Units** - $k$ in **W/(m·K)** - $L$, $r$ in **metres** (not cm or mm — convert!) - $h$ in **W/(m²·K)** - $Q$ in **W** (or kW) - $T$ in **°C or K** (differences are identical) ### **Common Mistake Avoidance** - ✅ Convert all dimensions to SI **first**. - ✅ Use $\ln$ (natural log), not $\log_{10}$. - ✅ Check whether convection is present at boundaries. - ✅ For a cylinder, use $r$, not diameter $D$. - ✅ The outer radius of insulation = bare radius + thickness. --- > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Board Exam Strategy:** > 1. Always start with a **labeled sketch**. > 2. Write the **resistance equation first**, then plug in values. > 3. For 14-mark questions, **show all 4–6 resistance terms** to score full marks. > 4. End with a **physical interpretation sentence** (1 line) — examiners award bonus marks for clarity.

topics.title: Conduction with Internal Heat Generation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conduction with Internal Heat Generation

# 1. Conduction With Internal Heat Generation — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Internal Heat Generation** (also called *volumetric heat source* or *heat generation rate per unit volume*) refers to the uniform production of thermal energy **within** the body of a solid, rather than being supplied only at its boundaries. Mathematically, it is denoted as $\dot{q}_g$ and has SI units of **W/m³** (watts per cubic metre).

When conduction occurs in such a body, the governing Fourier heat equation in one-dimensional steady state becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0 \quad \text{(Plane Wall)}$$

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0 \quad \text{(Cylinder)}$$

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^{2} \frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0 \quad \text{(Sphere)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The constant $\dot{q}_g$ is assumed **uniform**, **time-invariant (steady state)**, and **isotropic** (same in all directions). For non-uniform $\dot{q}_g(r)$ the equation becomes analytically intractable and falls under numerical (FDM/FEM) syllabus — covered in later modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you switch on an **electric room heater (rod type)**. The nichrome wire inside doesn't just absorb heat from its surface — it actually **creates heat inside itself** because electricity flowing through its resistance dissipates as Joule heat. Now compare it to a steel rod lying in a furnace — the steel rod only receives heat from outside; nothing happens inside it.

| System Type | Where heat comes from | Governing Term |
|---|---|---|
| Heater rod, nuclear fuel pellet, curing concrete, chemical reactor | **Inside** the body | $+\dot{q}_g$ source term |
| Wall of an oven, boiler tube outer shell | **Outside** the body only | Pure Laplace equation ($\nabla^2 T = 0$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise for KTU:**
> - $\dot{q}_g$ = volumetric rate of heat generation, **W/m³**
> - $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material, **W/(m·K)**
> - $L$ or $R$ = half-thickness / radius, **m**
> - $T_{max}$ always occurs at the **centre** (plane wall, cylinder, sphere) because heat must travel maximum distance to escape.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Logic — Step by Step

The governing differential equation is obtained by performing an energy balance on a differential element of the solid:

1. **Energy In** at face $(x)$ → $-kA\,(dT/dx)\big|_x$
2. **Energy Out** at face $(x+dx)$ → $-kA\,(dT/dx)\big|_{x+dx}$
3. **Energy Generated** inside volume $A\,dx$ → $\dot{q}_g\, A\, dx$
4. At steady state: Energy In + Generation = Energy Out

This produces the **Poisson equation** form (versus Laplace's equation which is for no-generation cases):

$$\nabla^{2}T + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Geometry | Surface Condition | Temperature Distribution $T(x)$ or $T(r)$ | Centre-to-Surface Difference $\Delta T_{max}$ | Heat Rate $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plane Wall** (thickness $2L$, both surfaces $T_s$) | Symmetric (insulated at centre plane) | $T(x) = T_s + \dfrac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}(L^{2} - x^{2})$ | $T_0 - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k}$ | $q = \dot{q}_g (2L) A$ |
| 2 | **Plane Wall** (asymmetric, surfaces $T_{s1}, T_{s2}$) | $T(-L)=T_{s1},\; T(L)=T_{s2}$ | $T(x) = T_{s1} + \dfrac{(T_{s2}-T_{s1})}{2L}(x+L) + \dfrac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\left[L^{2}-x^{2}\right]$ | Solve for $x^*$ where $dT/dx = 0$ | $q = \dot{q}_g (2L) A + \text{conduction term}$ |
| 3 | **Solid Cylinder** (radius $R$, surface $T_s$) | $T(R) = T_s$, finite at $r=0$ | $T(r) = T_s + \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k}\left[1-\left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | $T_0 - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k}$ | $q = \dot{q}_g \pi R^{2} L$ |
| 4 | **Solid Sphere** (radius $R$, surface $T_s$) | $T(R) = T_s$, finite at $r=0$ | $T(r) = T_s + \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}\left[1-\left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | $T_0 - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}$ | $q = \dot{q}_g \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ |
| 5 | **Hollow Cylinder** (inner $r_1$, outer $r_2$, $\dot{q}_g$ in fuel only) | $T(r_1)=T_{s1},\; T(r_2)=T_{s2}$ | $T(r) = C_1 \ln r + C_2 - \dfrac{\dot{q}_g}{4k} r^{2}$ | $T_0 - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g}{4k}(r_2^{2} - r_1^{2})$ at centre if $r_1 \to 0$ | $q = \dot{q}_g \pi (r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}) L$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examinee Trap:** The denominator **changes** with geometry: $2k$ for plane wall, $4k$ for cylinder, $6k$ for sphere. KTU valuation keys strictly deduct $1$ mark per wrong denominator.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Engineering:** $\dot{q}_g$ in $\text{UO}_2$ fuel pellets reaches $10^{8}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$ — centreline melting must be prevented by cladding and coolant design.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Current-carrying conductors, transformers, semiconductor chips.
- **Civil Engineering:** Heat of hydration in mass concrete dams (thermal cracking control).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Exothermic reactions in packed-bed catalytic reactors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations (Poisson Equation → Temperature Profile)

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plane Wall with Uniform Generation (Symmetric Case)

**Setup:** Wall thickness $= 2L$, surface temperature $= T_s$ on both sides, uniform $\dot{q}_g$, steady state, 1-D.

### Step A — Governing Equation

From the energy balance on a differential slice of area $A$ and thickness $dx$:

$$\text{Input at } x + \text{Generated} = \text{Output at } x + dx$$

$$-kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x} + \dot{q}_g A\,dx = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx}$$

### Step B — Rearrange to Standard Form

Divide through by $A\,dx$ and take the limit $dx \to 0$:

$$k\,\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} + \dot{q}_g = 0$$

$$\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}$$

### Step C — First Integration (yields temperature gradient)

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}\,x + C_1$$

### Step D — Apply Symmetry Boundary Condition

By symmetry about the centre plane $x = 0$, the gradient must vanish there because no heat can flow across the insulated mid-plane:

$$\left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0$$

### Step E — Second Integration

$$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\,x^{2} + C_2$$

### Step F — Apply Surface Boundary Condition

$$T(L) = T_s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k}$$

### Step G — Final Temperature Distribution

$$\boxed{\,T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\left(L^{2} - x^{2}\right)\,}$$

### Step H — Maximum Temperature Location & Value

Maximum occurs at $x = 0$ (centre) where $dT/dx = 0$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k}\,}$$

### Step I — Heat Rate Leaving Either Surface

$$q = -kA\left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = -kA\left(-\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}L\right) = \dot{q}_g\,L\,A$$

Total heat rate from both surfaces:

$$q_{total} = 2\,q = \dot{q}_g\,(2L)\,A = \dot{q}_g\,V$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check:** Total heat generated in volume $V$ must equal total heat leaving — energy conservation confirmed. This is a frequent KTU Part-A question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solid Cylinder with Uniform Generation

**Setup:** Radius $R$, length $L$, surface temperature $T_s$, uniform $\dot{q}_g$.

### Step A — Governing Equation in Cylindrical Co-ordinates

For 1-D radial conduction with volumetric source:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0$$

### Step B — Multiply by $r$ to Make Exact

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}\,r$$

### Step C — First Integration

$$r\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\,r^{2} + C_1$$

### Step D — Apply Finite-Temperature Condition at Centre

At $r = 0$, $\,dT/dr$ must be finite. Therefore:

$$\lim_{r\to 0}\, r\frac{dT}{dr} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0$$

### Step E — Divide by $r$ and Integrate Again

$$\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\,r$$

$$T(r) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{4k}\,r^{2} + C_2$$

### Step F — Apply Surface Condition $T(R) = T_s$

$$C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k}$$

### Step G — Final Distribution

$$\boxed{\,T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]\,}$$

### Step H — Centreline Temperature

At $r = 0$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k}\,}$$

### Step I — Total Heat Rate

Integrating over the surface or using energy balance:

$$q = \dot{q}_g \cdot \pi R^{2} \cdot L = \dot{q}_g V$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solid Sphere with Uniform Generation

**Setup:** Radius $R$, surface $T_s$, uniform $\dot{q}_g$.

### Step A — Governing Equation

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0$$

### Step B — First Integration

Multiply by $r^{2}$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}\,r^{2}$$

$$r^{2}\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{3k}\,r^{3} + C_1$$

### Step C — Apply Finite Condition at $r = 0$

$$C_1 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{3k}\,r$$

### Step D — Second Integration

$$T(r) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{6k}\,r^{2} + C_2$$

### Step E — Apply $T(R) = T_s$

$$C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}$$

### Step F — Final Distribution

$$\boxed{\,T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]\,}$$

### Step G — Centreline Temperature

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}\,}$$

### Step H — Heat Rate

$$q = \dot{q}_g \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = \dot{q}_g V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Pattern:** $T_{max} - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R_{c}^{2}}{C\,k}$ where $C = 2$ (plane), $4$ (cylinder), $6$ (sphere). This is the **single most-tested formula** in KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Problem-Solving Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Identify Geometry]) --> nodeB{Plane / Cylinder / Sphere?}
    nodeB -->|Plane| nodeP1[Write 1D Poisson eqn]
    nodeB -->|Cylinder| nodeC1[Write radial Poisson eqn]
    nodeB -->|Sphere| nodeS1[Write radial Poisson eqn]

    nodeP1 --> nodeP2[Integrate twice -> T x expression]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Apply BC: dT dx at x=0 equals 0]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Apply BC: T L equals T sub s]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Output: T x and Tmax minus Ts equals q dot g L squared over 2k]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Multiply by r then integrate]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Apply finiteness BC at r equals 0]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4[Apply BC: T R equals T sub s]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5[Output: T r and Tmax minus Ts equals q dot g R squared over 4k]

    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[Multiply by r squared then integrate]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Apply finiteness BC at r equals 0]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Apply BC: T R equals T sub s]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Output: T r and Tmax minus Ts equals q dot g R squared over 6k]

    nodeP5 --> nodeZ([Energy balance: q equals q dot g times V])
    nodeC5 --> nodeZ
    nodeS5 --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeY([End: Numerical substitution])
```
