## 4.2 Schematic — Geometry Comparison (Text Block Diagram)

```
                PLANE WALL (2L)            CYLINDER (R)            SPHERE (R)
                ┌───────────┐
                │   ↑ q_gen │              ╭─────╮                  .─────.
                │   │   │   │            ╱  ↑q_gen ╲              ╱  ↑q_gen  ╲
                │   │   ↓   │           │   │  │   │            │   │   │    │
                │   │   │   │            ╲  │  ↓  ╱              ╲  │   ↓   ╱
                └───────────┘              ╰─────╯                  `─────`
              T_s  |   |  T_s           T_s                T_s
                   x=0 (max)              r=0 (max)            r=0 (max)
        Quadratic profile           Parabolic profile       Parabolic profile
        (downward parabola)         (downward parabola)     (downward parabola)
```

> [!NOTE]
> All three temperature profiles are **second-order polynomials** opening **downward** because $\dot{q}_g > 0$ implies $d^{2}T/dx^{2} < 0$. The maximum is always at the geometric centre, and the curves meet $T_s$ at the outer boundary.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "volumetric heat generation rate" $\dot{q}_g$. Give two engineering examples where this assumption is realistic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Volumetric heat generation rate $\dot{q}_g$ is the rate at which thermal energy is produced **per unit volume** of a medium due to an internal source. It has units of **W/m³**.
>
> **Examples (1 mark):**
> 1. Electric resistance heating in a current-carrying nichrome wire.
> 2. Nuclear fission in a $\text{UO}_2$ fuel rod of a nuclear reactor.
> *(Acceptable: chemical reaction in a catalytic packed bed, curing concrete.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A plane wall of thickness $2L = 50$ mm with uniform $\dot{q}_g = 4 \times 10^{5}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$ and $k = 20\;\text{W/(m·K)}$ has both surfaces at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. Find the centre-plane temperature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using the symmetric plane-wall formula:

$$T_0 - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{5})(0.025)^{2}}{2(20)}$$

$$= \frac{(4 \times 10^{5})(6.25 \times 10^{-4})}{40} = \frac{250}{40} = 6.25\;\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_0 = 100 + 6.25 = 106.25^{\circ}\text{C}\,}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern, ESE Module 1]**

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Derive the steady-state, one-dimensional temperature distribution for a **plane wall** of thickness $2L$ with uniform volumetric heat generation $\dot{q}_g$ and both surfaces maintained at $T_s$. State the location and value of maximum temperature clearly. **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Governing equation (2 marks):**
Starting from energy balance on a differential slice:

$$\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}$$

**Step 2 – Integration (2 marks):**

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{k}x + C_1$$

**Step 3 – Boundary condition at insulated mid-plane $x = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$\left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x=0}=0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0$$

**Step 4 – Second integration and surface BC (1 mark):**

$$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}x^{2} + C_2$$

Applying $T(L)=T_s$: $\;\;C_2 = T_s + \dfrac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k}$

**Step 5 – Final expression and maximum (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k}\!\left(L^{2}-x^{2}\right); \quad T_{max}\big|_{x=0} = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g L^{2}}{2k}\,}$$

---

**(b)** *A **nuclear fuel rod** of diameter 20 mm, length 1 m, with uniform heat generation $\dot{q}_g = 6 \times 10^{8}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$, surface temperature $T_s = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $k = 30\;\text{W/(m·K)}$. Determine:*
*(i) The centreline temperature,*
*(ii) The heat rate leaving the rod. **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 0.010\;\text{m}$, $\dot{q}_g = 6\times10^{8}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$, $k = 30\;\text{W/(m·K)}$, $L = 1\;\text{m}$, $T_s = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$

**(i) Centreline temperature (4 marks):**

Using cylinder formula:

$$T_0 - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k} = \frac{(6 \times 10^{8})(0.010)^{2}}{4(30)}$$

$$= \frac{(6 \times 10^{8})(10^{-4})}{120} = \frac{6 \times 10^{4}}{120} = 500\;\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_0 = 200 + 500 = 700^{\circ}\text{C}\,}$$

**(ii) Heat rate (3 marks):**

Energy balance — all generated heat leaves through the surface:

$$q = \dot{q}_g \cdot \pi R^{2} L = (6\times10^{8})\pi(0.010)^{2}(1)$$

$$\boxed{\,q = 6\times10^{8}\times\pi\times10^{-4} = 1.885 \times 10^{5}\;\text{W} \approx 188.5\;\text{kW}\,}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *Derive the steady-state radial temperature distribution $T(r)$ in a **solid sphere** of radius $R$ with uniform heat generation $\dot{q}_g$ and surface temperature $T_s$. Mention the maximum temperature explicitly. **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Governing equation (2 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0$$

**Step 2 – Multiply by $r^{2}$ and integrate once (2 marks):**

$$r^{2}\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{3k}r^{3} + C_1$$

**Step 3 – Finiteness at centre: $C_1 = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{3k}r$$

**Step 4 – Integrate again + apply $T(R) = T_s$ (1 mark):**

$$T(r) = -\frac{\dot{q}_g}{6k}r^{2} + T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}$$

**Step 5 – Final result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}\left[1-\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]; \quad T_{max}\big|_{r=0} = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{6k}\,}$$

---

**(b)** *A long **solid stainless-steel cylinder** ($k = 15\;\text{W/(m·K)}$) of 50 mm diameter carries electric current that produces uniform heat generation of $5 \times 10^{6}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$. The surface is held at $80^{\circ}\text{C}$. Calculate:*
*(i) Centreline temperature,*
*(ii) Temperature at $r = 10$ mm from the axis,*
*(iii) Heat dissipation per metre length. **[7 marks]***

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 0.025\;\text{m}$, $\dot{q}_g = 5\times10^{6}\;\text{W/m}^{3}$, $k = 15$, $T_s = 80^{\circ}\text{C}$, $L = 1\;\text{m}$

**(i) Centreline temperature (3 marks):**

$$T_0 - T_s = \frac{\dot{q}_g R^{2}}{4k} = \frac{(5\times10^{6})(0.025)^{2}}{4(15)} = \frac{3125}{60} = 52.08\;\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_0 = 132.08^{\circ}\text{C}\,}$$

**(ii) Temperature at $r = 10$ mm = 0.010 m (3 marks):**

$$\frac{r}{R} = \frac{0.010}{0.025} = 0.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2} = 0.16$$

$$T(0.010) = 80 + 52.08(1 - 0.16) = 80 + 52.08(0.84) = 80 + 43.75$$

$$\boxed{\,T = 123.75^{\circ}\text{C}\,}$$

**(iii) Heat per metre length (1 mark):**

$$q' = \frac{q}{L} = \dot{q}_g \cdot \pi R^{2} = (5\times10^{6})\pi(0.025)^{2} = 9817.5\;\text{W/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,q' \approx 9.82\;\text{kW per metre length}\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator** ($2k$ vs $4k$ vs $6k$): KTU key deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the symmetry BC** $dT/dx = 0$ at the centre plane — this is the most common mistake in plane-wall derivations. Causes the integration constant to be wrong → cascading 3-mark loss.
> 3. **Writing $T_s$ instead of $T_s$ — corrections in numerical substitution**: many students copy the wrong surface temperature.
> 4. **Units mismatch**: $\dot{q}_g$ in $\text{W/m}^{3}$ but $L$ in mm — always convert to metres FIRST.
> 5. **Missing the energy-conservation check** $q = \dot{q}_g V$ — a quick sanity test worth free marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Governing equation** changes from Laplace ($\nabla^{2}T=0$) to **Poisson** ($\nabla^{2}T + \dot{q}_g/k = 0$) when generation exists.
- [ ] **Two boundary conditions** are required: one on each surface (or a symmetry/finiteness condition at the centre).
- [ ] **Symmetry condition**: $dT/dx = 0$ at $x = 0$ for plane wall — and $dT/dr$ is **finite** at $r = 0$ for cylinder/sphere.
- [ ] **Universal maximum-temperature formula**: $\;T_{max} - T_s = \dfrac{\dot{q}_g R_{c}^{2}}{C\,k}\;$ with $C = 2$ (plane), $4$ (cylinder), $6$ (sphere).
- [ ] **Energy conservation**: total heat generated ($\dot{q}_g \cdot V$) = total heat leaving through all surfaces.
- [ ] **Temperature profile shape**: downward parabola (2nd-order polynomial in $x$ or $r$).
- [ ] **Units discipline**: $\dot{q}_g$ in $\text{W/m}^{3}$, lengths in **metres**, $k$ in $\text{W/(m·K)}$ → result in K or °C.
- [ ] **Heat flux at surface** = $\dot{q}_g \cdot$ (half-thickness or radius) — independent of $k$ for plane wall.
- [ ] For **asymmetric plane wall**, maximum shifts towards the **cooler** surface; locate it by setting $dT/dx = 0$.
- [ ] **Hollow cylinder with generation**: use superposition — pure conduction + pure generation parts.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions**: steady state, 1-D, constant $k$, uniform $\dot{q}_g$, isotropic, no contact resistance.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Conduction with Internal Heat Generation**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extended Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extended Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Extended Surfaces (Fins)** are solid components of high thermal conductivity material that are attached to a primary surface (the wall or base) from which heat is to be exchanged with the surrounding fluid, in order to **increase the effective surface area** available for convective heat transfer. They are also termed **Fins of uniform cross-sectional area** when their cross-section does not vary along their length.

In KTU board parlance, an extended surface is defined as:
> A surface that protrudes from a primary heat-transferring wall into a fluid medium of lower temperature, thereby augmenting the overall rate of heat dissipation by convection.

The governing parameter is the dimensionless group known as the **fin parameter** $m$, defined as:

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA}}$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient ($\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$)
- $P$ = perimeter of the fin cross-section ($\text{m}$)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of fin material ($\text{W/mK}$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the fin ($\text{m}^2$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 1 mandates a complete treatment of (i) fin equation derivation, (ii) solution under different boundary conditions, (iii) fin effectiveness, and (iv) fin efficiency. Every derivation MUST be committed to memory; the 14-mark question in Part B is almost always a derivation + numerical combination.

### 1.2 Real-World Intuition — Plain English Analogy

Imagine you are boiling water using a steel ladle. The flat bottom of the ladle touches the flame, but only that tiny circle can receive heat. If you weld long thin steel strips (like the petals of a sunflower) onto that bottom, suddenly **a lot more metal surface** is in contact with the surrounding hot air and cooler water. Heat now has many more "highways" to escape. Those strips are **fins**.

Three engineering truths follow:
1. Adding more surface area $\Rightarrow$ more heat loss for the same temperature difference.
2. The fin material MUST conduct heat quickly to its tip — otherwise the tip will be uselessly cold.
3. A fin that is too long is **wasted metal** because its tip stays near fluid temperature and contributes nothing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Examples of Fins**:
> - Motorcycle engine cylinder fins (air-cooling)
> - Refrigerator condenser coils
> - CPU heat sinks (aluminium fins on processors)
> - Transformer cooling radiators
> - Air-cooled heat exchangers

### 1.3 Need for Extended Surfaces

Newton's law of cooling gives:
$$q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$$

For a given $h$, $T_s$, and $T_\infty$, increasing $A$ is the *only* design lever. Fins geometrically multiply $A$ without enlarging the primary wall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fins

Fins are classified along two primary axes:

**(a) By Geometry**
- **Straight rectangular fin** (uniform rectangular cross-section)
- **Pin fin** (circular cross-section, e.g., spine of a heat sink)
- **Annular fin** (circular ring on a tube — used in pipes, condenser tubes)
- **Triangular fin** (trapezoidal profile)

**(b) By Thermal Behaviour at Tip**
- **Fin with convective tip** (heat lost from the tip end)
- **Fin with insulated tip** (adiabatic tip — tip heat loss = 0)
- **Long fin** (infinitely long; tip temperature $\to T_\infty$)
- **Fin with specified tip temperature** ($T_t$ given)

### 2.2 Assumptions for Fin Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard KTU board assumptions (memorize verbatim):
> 1. **Steady-state**, one-dimensional heat conduction (along $x$).
> 2. Constant thermal conductivity $k$.
> 3. Constant convective coefficient $h$ along the entire surface.
> 4. No heat generation inside the fin.
> 5. Surrounding fluid is at uniform temperature $T_\infty$.
> 6. Base temperature $T_b$ is uniform across the fin root.
> 7. Fin cross-section $A$ and perimeter $P$ are constant (uniform fin).

### 2.3 General Heat Balance on a Fin Element

Consider an elementary disc of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the base.

**Energy in (left face)** = $q_x$
**Energy out (right face)** = $q_{x+dx}$
**Convection lost from sides** = $h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$

At steady state: $\;q_x = q_{x+dx} + h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$

Using Fourier's law: $q_x = -kA\,dT/dx$

Substituting and simplifying yields the **General Fin Equation**:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$$

where the **excess temperature** is defined as $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$, and $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Fin parameter | $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA)}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Excess temperature | $\theta = T - T_\infty$ | KTU shorthand |
| 3 | General fin ODE | $d^2\theta/dx^2 - m^2\theta = 0$ | Linear, homogeneous |
| 4 | Long fin temp. dist. | $\theta/\theta_b = e^{-mx}$ | Infinitely long |
| 5 | Insulated tip temp. | $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh\!\left[m(L-x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)}$ | Adiabatic tip |
| 6 | Heat loss — long fin | $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b$ | Length-independent |
| 7 | Heat loss — insulated tip | $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$ | Most common case |
| 8 | Convective tip | $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b\,\dfrac{\sinh(mL) + (h/mk)\cosh(mL)}{\cosh(mL) + (h/mk)\sinh(mL)}$ | True physical tip |
| 9 | Fin effectiveness | $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b}$ | Ratio vs bare surface |
| 10 | Fin efficiency | $\eta_f = \dfrac{q_f}{q_{\max}} = \dfrac{q_f}{hA_f\theta_b}$ | $A_f$ = total fin area |
| 11 | Corrected length (rectangular) | $L_c = L + t/2$ | $t$ = thickness |
| 12 | Corrected length (pin) | $L_c = L + D/4$ | $D$ = diameter |
| 13 | Corrected length (annular) | $L_c = L + (r_2 - r_1)/2$ | $r_1, r_2$ = inner/outer radii |
| 14 | Overall surface efficiency | $\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_t}(1 - \eta_f)$ | $A_t$ = total extended area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule** for KTU board: Whenever the problem statement says "tip is insulated" or just gives length without mentioning tip conditions, ALWAYS use the **insulated tip** solution. The phrase "long fin" forces the **infinite-length** solution.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Power plants**: Pin fins on boiler tubes enhance steam-side convection.
- **Electronics**: Heat sinks on microprocessors are arrays of parallel-plate fins.
- **Aerospace**: Turbine blades are internally finned for cooling.
- **HVAC**: Condenser and evaporator coils are finned-tube arrangements.
- **Cryogenics**: Fins are avoided because of high conduction losses; understanding *when NOT to use a fin* is equally important.

### 2.6 Fin Effectiveness Criterion

A fin is **worth installing** only if $\varepsilon_f > 1$ (i.e., fin dissipates more heat than the same base area alone). Practical designs target $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Fin Equation

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup.**
Consider a straight fin of uniform cross-sectional area $A$ and perimeter $P$ attached to a base at $x = 0$. Let $T(x)$ be the temperature at distance $x$ from the base, and $T_\infty$ the ambient fluid temperature.

**Step 2 — Heat Flux at Section $x$.**
By Fourier's law of conduction, heat flowing into the elementary section at $x$ is:

$$q_x = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x}$$

**Step 3 — Heat Flux at Section $x+dx$.**
Heat flowing out of the section at $x + dx$ is:

$$q_{x+dx} = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx}$$

**Step 4 — Side Convection Loss.**
The lateral surface area of the elemental slab of thickness $dx$ is $P\,dx$. Heat lost by convection to the surroundings is:

$$q_{\text{conv}} = h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 5 — Energy Balance (Steady State).**

$$q_x = q_{x+dx} + h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Rearrange.**

$$-kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x} = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} + h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$$

Bringing terms to one side:

$$kA\left[\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} - \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x}\right] = h\,P\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the Second Derivative.**
By the definition of the differential:

$$\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} - \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx = \frac{d^2T}{dx^2}\,dx$$

**Step 8 — Final Form.**

$$kA\frac{d^2T}{dx^2}\,dx = hP(T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

Dividing by $kA\,dx$ and introducing $\theta = T - T_\infty$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{kA}\,\theta = 0\;}$$

This is the **general fin differential equation**, with $m^2 = hP/(kA)$.

---

### 3.2 Solution for Fin with Insulated Tip

**Boundary Conditions:**
- At $x = 0$: $\theta = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$
- At $x = L$: $d\theta/dx = 0$ (no heat flow across the tip)

**Step 1 — General Solution of the ODE.**
The characteristic equation $r^2 - m^2 = 0$ gives $r = \pm m$. Therefore:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

**Step 2 — Apply BC1 at $x = 0$.**

$$\theta_b = C_1 + C_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b$$

**Step 3 — Apply BC2 at $x = L$.**

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = mC_1 e^{mL} - mC_2 e^{-mL} = 0$$

Hence $C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL}$, giving:

$$C_2 = C_1 e^{2mL}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_1$ and $C_2$.**
Substitute into BC1:

$$C_1 + C_1 e^{2mL} = \theta_b \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = \frac{\theta_b}{1 + e^{2mL}}$$

Similarly:

$$C_2 = \frac{\theta_b\,e^{2mL}}{1 + e^{2mL}}$$

**Step 5 — Express via Hyperbolic Functions.**
Using $1 + e^{2mL} = e^{mL}(e^{-mL} + e^{mL}) = 2e^{mL}\cosh(mL)$, we obtain:

$$C_1 = \frac{\theta_b}{2e^{mL}\cosh(mL)}, \qquad C_2 = \frac{\theta_b\,e^{mL}}{2\cosh(mL)}$$

**Step 6 — Final Temperature Distribution.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh\!\left[m(L-x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Heat Transfer Rate from the Fin.**
Using Fourier's law at the base ($x = 0$):

$$q_f = -kA\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\theta_b m\,\frac{\sinh\!\left[m(L-x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

At $x = 0$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = -m\theta_b\tanh(mL)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Solution for Long Fin ($L \to \infty$)

For $L \to \infty$, $\cosh(mL) \to \infty$, and from the insulated-tip expression:

$$\frac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh\!\left[m(L-x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)} \xrightarrow{L\to\infty} e^{-mx}$$

Thus the temperature distribution is:

$$\frac{\theta}{\theta_b} = e^{-mx}$$

The heat transfer rate becomes:

$$q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b \quad\text{(independent of length)}$$

---

### 3.4 Fin Effectiveness — Derivation

Fin effectiveness is defined as the ratio of fin heat dissipation to the heat that would be transferred from the base area $A_c$ alone:

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b}$$

For an insulated-tip fin:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_f = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA}}\,\tanh(mL)\;}$$

For a long fin:

$$\varepsilon_f = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA}}$$

---

### 3.5 Fin Efficiency — Derivation

Fin efficiency is the ratio of actual heat dissipation to the *maximum possible* heat dissipation (which would occur if the entire fin were at base temperature $T_b$):

$$\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{q_{\max}} = \frac{q_f}{hA_f\theta_b}$$

Substituting $q_f$ for the insulated-tip fin:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\;}$$

This elegant formula is one of the most frequently quoted results in KTU exams.

---

### 3.6 Corrected Length for Non-Adiabatic Tip

The true fin with convective tip behaves like an insulated-tip fin of a slightly **longer** length, called the **corrected length** $L_c$:

$$L_c = L + \frac{A_t}{P}\cdot\frac{h}{km}\cdot(\text{correction factor}) \;\Rightarrow\; L_c \approx L + \frac{t}{2}\;(\text{rectangular}),\;L+\frac{D}{4}\;(\text{pin}),\;L+\frac{r_2-r_1}{2}\;(\text{annular})$$

This correction is widely used in **NH3 and refrigerant condenser** design calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fin Heat-Transfer Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Primary Wall at T_b] --> B{Fins Required?}
    B -- No --> C[Bare Surface q = h A T_b - T_infinity]
    B -- Yes --> D[Attach Extended Surface: Fin]
    D --> E[Heat Conducts Along Fin: Fourier Law]
    E --> F[Side Convection Loss: h P dx T - T_infinity]
    F --> G[Steady State Energy Balance]
    G --> H[Fin ODE: d2 theta over dx2 - m squared theta = 0]
    H --> I{Which Tip Condition?}
    I -- Long fin --> J[theta over theta_b = exp - m x]
    I -- Insulated tip --> K[theta over theta_b = cosh m L - x over cosh mL]
    I -- Convective tip --> L[Hyperbolic ratio with h over m k]
    J --> M[Heat Rate: q_f = sqrt h P k A times theta_b]
    K --> N[Heat Rate: q_f = sqrt h P k A times theta_b tanh mL]
    L --> O[Heat Rate: Hyperbolic expression with tip term]
    M --> P[Compute Effectiveness epsilon_f and Efficiency eta_f]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{epsilon_f greater than 1?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Fin is Justified: Use in Design]
    Q -- No --> S[Remove Fin: Waste of Material]
    R --> T[Output: q_total = q_fins + q_unfinned]
    S --> C
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Common Fin Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extended Surfaces] --> B[Straight Rectangular Fin]
    A --> C[Pin Fin or Spine]
    A --> D[Annular Fin]
    A --> E[Triangular or Trapezoidal Fin]
    B --> B1[Use: Heat sinks, transformer radiators]
    C --> C1[Use: Cylinder heads, motor housings]
    D --> D1[Use: Condenser tubes, boiler headers]
    E --> E1[Use: Aero turbine blade trailing edges]
```

### 4.3 Boundary-Condition Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Given Fin Problem] --> T1{Tip Temperature T_t Specified?}
    T1 -- Yes --> R1[Dirichlet BC at x = L]
    T1 -- No --> T2{Tip Heat Loss Specified?}
    T2 -- Yes --> R2[Convection BC: -k dT over dx = h T_t - T_infinity]
    T2 -- No --> R3[Insulated Tip BC: dT over dx = 0 at x = L]
    R1 --> F1[Use linear combination: theta = C1 cosh mx + C2 sinh mx]
    R2 --> F2[Modified hyperbolic solution]
    R3 --> F3[Standard cosh over cosh solution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **fin parameter** $m$ and state its physical significance. Write its dimensional formula.

**Model Answer:**
The fin parameter is defined as:
$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA}}$$
It has units of $\text{m}^{-1}$. The product $mL$ is the dimensionless fin length, which governs fin performance. Physically, $1/m$ represents the characteristic **thermal penetration distance** beyond which heat conduction along the fin is negligible and the fin becomes ineffective.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **fin effectiveness** and **fin efficiency**. When is a fin said to be "100% efficient"?

**Model Answer:**
- **Fin effectiveness** $\varepsilon_f$ = ratio of fin heat dissipation to heat that would be transferred from the bare base area. It quantifies *whether to install the fin*.
- **Fin efficiency** $\eta_f$ = ratio of actual fin heat dissipation to the maximum possible (when the entire fin is at base temperature). It quantifies *how well the fin utilises its material*.
- A fin is **100% efficient** when its tip temperature equals $T_b$, which mathematically requires $mL = 0$ (an infinitesimally short fin).

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the general fin differential equation starting from an energy balance on a differential fin element. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A stainless-steel pin fin ($k = 16 \,\text{W/mK}$) of diameter $D = 6 \,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 40 \,\text{mm}$ is exposed to air at $T_\infty = 30^\circ\text{C}$ with $h = 75 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. The fin base is at $T_b = 180^\circ\text{C}$. Assuming the tip is insulated, calculate:
   1. The fin parameter $m$.
   2. The fin efficiency $\eta_f$.
   3. The heat dissipation rate $q_f$.
   4. The fin effectiveness $\varepsilon_f$.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**
[Students write the 8-step derivation from Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps to mention for full marks:

1. State the 7 assumptions (steady state, 1-D, constant k, constant h, no heat generation, uniform $T_b$, uniform $T_\infty$).
2. Apply Fourier's law at sections $x$ and $x+dx$.
3. Side convection loss: $hP\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$.
4. Energy balance: $q_x = q_{x+dx} + hP\,dx\,(T - T_\infty)$.
5. Substitute Fourier law and identify the second derivative.
6. Define excess temperature $\theta = T - T_\infty$.
7. Arrive at $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$ with $m^2 = hP/(kA)$.]

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Given:* $k = 16 \,\text{W/mK}$, $D = 0.006 \,\text{m}$, $L = 0.040 \,\text{m}$, $T_\infty = 30^\circ\text{C}$, $h = 75 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $T_b = 180^\circ\text{C}$.

*Step 1 — Cross-section and perimeter.*

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.006)^2}{4} = 2.827\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$P = \pi D = \pi (0.006) = 0.01885\,\text{m}$$

*Step 2 — Fin parameter.*

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA}} = \sqrt{\frac{75\times 0.01885}{16\times 2.827\times10^{-5}}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{1.4138}{4.523\times10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{3126.3} = 55.91\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

*Step 3 — Fin efficiency.*

$$mL = 55.91 \times 0.040 = 2.236$$

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{\tanh(2.236)}{2.236} = \frac{0.9768}{2.236} = 0.4369 \;(\approx 43.7\%)$$

*Step 4 — Heat dissipation rate.*

$$\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty = 180 - 30 = 150^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\sqrt{hPkA} = \sqrt{75 \times 0.01885 \times 16 \times 2.827\times10^{-5}} = \sqrt{6.397\times10^{-4}} = 0.2529\,\text{W/K}$$

$$q_f = 0.2529 \times 150 \times \tanh(2.236) = 0.2529 \times 150 \times 0.9768 = 37.05\,\text{W}$$

*Step 5 — Fin effectiveness.*

$$A_c = A = 2.827\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b} = \frac{37.05}{75 \times 2.827\times10^{-5}\times 150} = \frac{37.05}{0.3180} = 116.5$$

The fin effectiveness is huge — the pin fin dissipates **116.5 times** more heat than the bare base alone. Fin is highly justified.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the temperature distribution $\theta/\theta_b$ and the heat transfer rate $q_f$ for a fin with an **insulated tip**. Clearly mention the boundary conditions used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An annular fin is fitted on a tube of outer radius $r_1 = 25 \,\text{mm}$. The fin has outer radius $r_2 = 50 \,\text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 5 \,\text{mm}$. The fin is made of aluminium ($k = 205 \,\text{W/mK}$) and is exposed to air at $T_\infty = 35^\circ\text{C}$ with $h = 50 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. The base temperature is $T_b = 200^\circ\text{C}$. Treating the fin as a straight rectangular fin using the corrected length, calculate the heat dissipation rate per unit depth.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**
[Outline the 7-step derivation from Section 3.2:
- BC1: $\theta(0) = \theta_b$
- BC2: $d\theta/dx|_{x=L} = 0$
- General solution: $\theta = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$
- Apply BC1 and BC2 to obtain $C_1, C_2$.
- Convert to hyperbolic form.
- Final result:
$$\theta/\theta_b = \cosh[m(L-x)]/\cosh(mL)$$
$$q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)$$]

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

*Step 1 — Geometry.*

$$r_1 = 0.025\,\text{m}, \quad r_2 = 0.050\,\text{m}, \quad L = r_2 - r_1 = 0.025\,\text{m}$$

Rectangular approximation: thickness $t = 0.005\,\text{m}$, length $L = 0.025\,\text{m}$.

*Step 2 — Corrected length.*

$$L_c = L + \frac{t}{2} = 0.025 + 0.0025 = 0.0275\,\text{m}$$

*Step 3 — Cross-section and perimeter.*

$$A = L_c \times t = 0.0275 \times 0.005 = 1.375\times10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$P = 2(L_c + t) = 2(0.0275 + 0.005) = 0.065\,\text{m}$$

*Step 4 — Fin parameter.*

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA}} = \sqrt{\frac{50\times 0.065}{205\times 1.375\times10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.25}{0.02819}} = \sqrt{115.3} = 10.74\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

*Step 5 — Heat dissipation rate.*

$$\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty = 200 - 35 = 165^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\sqrt{hPkA} = \sqrt{50\times 0.065\times 205\times 1.375\times10^{-4}} = \sqrt{0.9166} = 0.9574\,\text{W/K}$$

$$mL = 10.74 \times 0.025 = 0.2685$$

$$\tanh(0.2685) = 0.2624$$

$$q_f = 0.9574 \times 165 \times 0.2624 = 41.45\,\text{W per unit depth}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks (Module 1 — Extended Surfaces):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $L$, $D$, $t$ MUST be in metres before computing $m$. A common mistake is leaving $L = 40\,\text{mm}$ in the equation directly.
>
> 2. **Wrong tip boundary condition** — If the problem says "long fin" but you apply the insulated-tip solution, you will lose 3 marks instantly. Read the tip condition carefully.
>
> 3. **Mixing $A$ and $A_c$** — In the effectiveness formula, the denominator uses the **base (cut) area** $A_c$, NOT the entire fin area $A_f$. In efficiency, the denominator uses $A_f$.
>
> 4. **Dropping constants like $\pi$ or $4$** — In pin-fin geometry, $A = \pi D^2/4$ and $P = \pi D$. Writing $A = \pi D^2$ costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Skipping assumption statements** — A 7-mark derivation question that omits the seven assumptions loses 1.5 marks.
>
> 6. **Not defining $\theta$ and $m^2$ explicitly** — Always write: *"Let excess temperature $\theta = T - T_\infty$, and let $m^2 = hP/(kA)$"*.
>
> 7. **Sign errors in Fourier's law** — Use $q_x = -kA\,(dT/dx)$ consistently.

---

### Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] Fin equation: $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$, with $m^2 = hP/(kA)$.
- [ ] Boundary conditions for insulated tip: $\theta(0) = \theta_b$, $d\theta/dx|_L = 0$.
- [ ] Insulated-tip temperature distribution: $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$.
- [ ] Insulated-tip heat rate: $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)$.
- [ ] Long-fin heat rate: $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b$ (independent of length).
- [ ] Fin effectiveness: $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b}$; criterion $\varepsilon_f > 1$.
- [ ] Fin efficiency (insulated tip): $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$.
- [ ] Corrected length — rectangular: $L_c = L + t/2$; pin: $L_c = L + D/4$; annular: $L_c = L + (r_2 - r_1)/2$.
- [ ] Maximum possible heat transfer: $q_{\max} = hA_f\theta_b$ (entire fin at $T_b$).
- [ ] Pin fin geometry: $A = \pi D^2/4$, $P = \pi D$.
- [ ] Overall surface efficiency: $\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_t}(1 - \eta_f)$.
- [ ] Always check units: $h$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $k$ in $\text{W/mK}$, lengths in $\text{m}$.
- [ ] Hyperbolic identities to remember: $\cosh(x) = (e^x + e^{-x})/2$, $\sinh(x) = (e^x - e^{-x})/2$, $\tanh(x) = \sinh(x)/\cosh(x)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "EIGHT"**: **E**ffectiveness compares to bare wall, **e**fficiency compares to maximum, **G**eometry determines $A$ and $P$, **H**eat transfer scales with $\sqrt{hPkA}\,\theta_b$, **T**anh(mL) is the standard insulated-tip factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convection: Basic Concepts - Convective Heat Transfer Coefficients - Boundary Layer Concept - Types of Convection - Forced Convection - Dimensional Analysis - Useful Non Dimensional Numbers - External Flow - Flow Over Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convection: Basic Concepts - Convective Heat Transfer Coefficients - Boundary Layer Concept - Types of Convection - Forced Convection - Dimensional Analysis - Useful Non Dimensional Numbers - External Flow - Flow Over Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Convection?

**Convection** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in motion, involving the combined mechanisms of **conduction** (diffusion of heat through the fluid at the molecular level) and **advection** (bulk transport of fluid parcels carrying their thermal energy). Mathematically, convection is described empirically by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where:
- $q''$ = convective heat flux (W/m²)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $T_\infty$ = bulk fluid temperature far from the surface (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Convection is the *only* mode of heat transfer that requires the *physical movement of matter*. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is convection absent in solids?"* — Answer: Solids have a rigid lattice that prevents bulk fluid motion.

---

## 1.2 Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)

The **convective heat transfer coefficient** $h$ is a proportionality constant that quantifies the rate of heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid per unit area per unit temperature difference. It is *not* a property of the fluid alone — it depends on the fluid's thermophysical properties, the geometry, the flow velocity, and the temperature difference itself.

**Order of magnitude of $h$ (must memorize for KTU):**

| Mechanism | Approximate $h$ (W/m²·K) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Natural convection (air) | **5 – 25** |
| Natural convection (water) | **100 – 1000** |
| Forced convection (air) | **10 – 200** |
| Forced convection (water) | **500 – 20,000** |
| Boiling/Condensation | **2,500 – 100,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $h$ is a *system* parameter (not a fluid property), unlike $k$ (thermal conductivity). A single fluid can have many different $h$ values depending on the situation — for example, water boiling in a kettle vs. water flowing in a smooth pipe.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Blower Fan
Imagine a hot CPU heat sink. Without a fan (still air), heat leaves the fins slowly — that's a low $h$. The moment you switch on the blower, the same fin surface transfers heat 5–10 times faster — that's a much higher $h$. The *fin material* and *fluid* (air) didn't change; only the **fluid motion** increased $h$. This is precisely why forced convection gives higher heat transfer rates.

---

## 1.3 The Boundary Layer Concept

The **boundary layer** is a thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid surface where the effects of viscosity (velocity boundary layer) and thermal conductivity (thermal boundary layer) are confined. Outside this layer, the fluid behaves as an *inviscid* (frictionless) and *isothermal* bulk.

This concept was first formalized by **Ludwig Prandtl (1904)** and is the foundation of modern fluid mechanics and convective heat transfer.

**Two simultaneous boundary layers develop over a heated plate:**

1. **Velocity (Hydrodynamic) Boundary Layer** $\delta(x)$ — region where fluid velocity rises from 0 at the wall ($u=0$, no-slip) to 99% of the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$.
2. **Thermal Boundary Layer** $\delta_t(x)$ — region where fluid temperature drops from $T_s$ at the wall to 99% of $T_\infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Fact:** The relative thickness of $\delta_t$ to $\delta$ is governed by the **Prandtl Number (Pr)**:
> - If $\text{Pr} = 1$ → $\delta_t = \delta$
> - If $\text{Pr} \gg 1$ (oils) → $\delta_t \ll \delta$ (velocity layer is thicker)
> - If $\text{Pr} \ll 1$ (liquid metals) → $\delta_t \gg \delta$ (thermal layer is thicker)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Riverbank
When a river flows past a vertical bank, the water right at the bank is almost still (friction with soil), but as you move away, the velocity increases until it reaches the main stream speed. That thin slow region is the *velocity boundary layer*. Now imagine the bank is warm — heat only "feels" the river through a similar thin zone called the *thermal boundary layer*. The thicker the river current (higher $U_\infty$), the thinner these layers become, and the faster heat gets carried away.

---

## 1.4 Types of Convection

There are **three classifications** of convection based on the *origin of the fluid motion*:

| Type | Driving Force | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forced Convection** | External agent (pump, fan, blower, wind) | Car radiator, aircraft wing, heat exchanger |
| **Natural (Free) Convection** | Buoyancy forces due to density difference ($\Delta \rho$) caused by $\Delta T$ | Room heating by a radiator, atmospheric winds, solar chimneys |
| **Mixed (Combined) Convection** | Both buoyancy and external forcing act with comparable magnitude | Electronic cooling in low-speed cross-flow |

The dominance of one over the other is judged by the **Richardson Number (Ri)** or equivalently the **Grashof-to-Reynolds-Squared Ratio**:

$$\text{Ri} = \frac{\text{Gr}}{\text{Re}^2}$$

- $\text{Ri} \ll 1$ → Forced convection dominates
- $\text{Ri} \gg 1$ → Natural convection dominates
- $\text{Ri} \approx 1$ → Mixed convection

---

## 1.5 Forced Convection — Overview

**Forced convection** refers to all convective situations where the fluid motion is induced by an *external mechanical means* (pump, compressor, fan) rather than by buoyancy. It is industrially the most important type because it provides **predictable, controllable, and high** heat transfer rates.

**Two geometric classifications (very important for KTU Module 2):**

- **External Flow:** The fluid flows over an *unbounded* surface (plate, cylinder, sphere). The boundary layer develops freely without constraints. *Example:* Wind over a building.
- **Internal Flow:** The fluid is *confined* within walls (pipes, ducts). A *fully developed* region exists where the velocity profile is no longer changing. *Example:* Water in a heat-exchanger tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Focus:** This module focuses exclusively on **external forced flow**, specifically **flow over a flat plate**, which is the canonical geometry from which all other correlations are derived.

---

## 1.6 Dimensional Analysis & Useful Dimensionless Numbers

**Dimensional analysis** is a mathematical technique used to reduce the number of variables in a physical problem by grouping them into a smaller number of *dimensionless groups*. It is essential in convection because the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ depends on many variables (velocity, viscosity, thermal conductivity, specific heat, etc.).

The most rigorous method is the **Buckingham $\Pi$ Theorem**:

> *"If a physical problem involves $n$ variables in $k$ fundamental dimensions, it can be reduced to $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups."*

For external flow over a flat plate, the governing variables are:

$$h = f(x, U_\infty, \rho, \mu, k, c_p, L)$$

This 7-variable problem reduces to just **3 dimensionless groups**: Re, Pr, and Nu — which is the foundation of the famous **Reynolds Analogy**.

### The Key Dimensionless Numbers (must memorize)

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning | Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{Re}$ | Reynolds Number | Inertia $\div$ Viscous forces | $\dfrac{\rho U_\infty L}{\mu}$ |
| $\text{Pr}$ | Prandtl Number | Momentum diffusivity $\div$ Thermal diffusivity | $\dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ |
| $\text{Nu}$ | Nusselt Number | Convection $\div$ Conduction at the wall | $\dfrac{h L}{k}$ |
| $\text{Gr}$ | Grashof Number | Buoyancy $\div$ Viscous forces (natural conv.) | $\dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ |
| $\text{Pe}$ | Peclet Number | Advection $\div$ Conduction | $\text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr} = \dfrac{U_\infty L}{\alpha}$ |
| $\text{St}$ | Stanton Number | Heat transfer $\div$ Thermal capacity rate | $\dfrac{h}{\rho U_\infty c_p} = \dfrac{\text{Nu}}{\text{Re}\,\text{Pr}}$ |
| $\text{Ri}$ | Richardson Number | Natural $\div$ Forced convection | $\dfrac{\text{Gr}}{\text{Re}^2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "*ReNu Pr St Pe* are your best friends in convection." Know the formula AND the physical meaning — examiners will ask both.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Cooling — The Foundation

Newton's Law is *empirical*, meaning it is an observation rather than a derivation. It states that the rate of convective heat transfer is directly proportional to the temperature difference:

$$\boxed{Q = h A_s (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

**Why does it work?**
- $T_s$ is measured at the wall; $T_\infty$ is the temperature of fluid undisturbed by the surface.
- $A_s$ is the surface area exposed to convection.
- $h$ is the engineering "shortcut" — by lumping conduction + advection into a single coefficient, we avoid solving the Navier-Stokes + Energy equation for every problem.

---

## 2.2 The Three Sub-Layers of the Velocity Boundary Layer

For a flat plate starting at $x=0$, the velocity boundary layer grows downstream. Within it, three distinct sub-regions are identified:

| Sub-layer | Location | Turbulence | Heat Transfer Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Laminar Sub-layer** | $0 < y < y^+$ (very thin) | None (viscous, smooth flow) | Heat transfer is purely by **conduction** (linear T-profile) |
| **Buffer Layer** | Above laminar sub-layer | Transitional | Mixed conduction + eddy mixing |
| **Turbulent Core** | Bulk of turbulent BL | Chaotic eddies | Heat transfer is dominated by **eddy diffusion** |

For laminar flow, the velocity profile is smooth (analytical solution exists). For turbulent flow, we use empirical profiles like the **1/7th power law**:

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

---

## 2.3 Critical Reynolds Number & Transition

Flow over a flat plate remains **laminar** until the local Reynolds number reaches a critical value:

$$\text{Re}_{x,cr} = \frac{\rho U_\infty x_{cr}}{\mu} \approx 5 \times 10^5$$

- $x < x_{cr}$: **Laminar regime**
- $x_{cr} < x < L$: **Turbulent regime** (assuming transition is "natural" — not artificially tripped)

If the plate has a **turbulator** (e.g., a wire at the leading edge) the transition is forced and $\text{Re}_{x,cr} \approx 3 \times 10^5$ or even lower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Frequency:** At least 1 problem in every KTU ESE asks you to compute $x_{cr}$ given $U_\infty$ and fluid properties. **Memorize** $\text{Re}_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$.

---

## 2.4 Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient for External Flow Over a Flat Plate

### 2.4.1 Local Convective Coefficient $h_x$

The local coefficient $h_x$ varies with position $x$ along the plate because the boundary layer thickness $\delta(x)$ grows.

**Laminar local correlation (analytical, exact for $P \to \infty$):**

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x x}{k} = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \quad \text{(valid for Pr} \geq 0.6\text{)}$$

**Turbulent local correlation (empirical):**

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x x}{k} = 0.0296 \, \text{Re}_x^{4/5} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

### 2.4.2 Average Convective Coefficient $\bar{h}$

The average is computed by integrating $h_x$ from $0$ to $L$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx$$

**Laminar over the entire plate ($L < x_{cr}$):**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \frac{\bar{h} L}{k} = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

**Mixed (laminar + turbulent) regime — for a plate of length $L$ with natural transition at $x_{cr}$:**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \left(0.037 \, \text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 871\right) \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

**Turbulent over the entire plate (from the leading edge):**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037 \, \text{Re}_L^{4/5} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \quad \text{(for Re}_L \geq 5 \times 10^5, \text{Pr} \geq 0.6\text{)}$$

---

## 2.5 Total Heat Transfer from a Flat Plate

For a plate of width $W$ and length $L$:

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot (W \cdot L) \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** The "flat plate" formula is the *workhorse* of convective heat transfer. It is used as the building block for: (1) fins, (2) leading edges of turbine blades, (3) wind-loaded solar panels, (4) aircraft wings, and (5) heat exchangers with flat headers.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for revision.** It covers ~85% of KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | All convection |
| 2 | Laminar boundary layer thickness | $\delta(x) = \dfrac{5x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$ | Flat plate, $\text{Re} < 5\times 10^5$ |
| 3 | Turbulent BL thickness | $\delta(x) = \dfrac{0.37\, x}{\text{Re}_x^{1/5}}$ | $\text{Re} > 5\times 10^5$ |
| 4 | Thermal BL thickness (laminar) | $\delta_t(x) = \dfrac{\delta(x)}{\text{Pr}^{1/3}}$ | $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$ |
| 5 | Local $\text{Nu}_x$ (laminar) | $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | $\text{Re}_x < 5\times 10^5$ |
| 6 | Local $\text{Nu}_x$ (turbulent) | $\text{Nu}_x = 0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | $\text{Re}_x > 5\times 10^5$ |
| 7 | Avg $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L$ (laminar) | $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Entirely laminar |
| 8 | Avg $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L$ (turbulent) | $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037\,\text{Re}_L^{4/5}\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Entirely turbulent |
| 9 | Avg $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L$ (mixed) | $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = (0.037\,\text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 871)\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Laminar up to $x_{cr}$, then turbulent |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | External flow |
| 11 | Prandtl Number | $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Fluid property |
| 12 | Nusselt Number | $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Convection measure |
| 13 | Stanton Number | $\text{St} = \dfrac{h}{\rho U c_p} = \dfrac{\text{Nu}}{\text{Re}\,\text{Pr}}$ | Dimensionless $h$ |
| 14 | Grashof Number | $\text{Gr} = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Natural convection |
| 15 | Peclet Number | $\text{Pe} = \text{Re}\,\text{Pr}$ | Bulk transport / conduction |
| 16 | Shear stress (laminar plate) | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.664}{\text{Re}_x^{1/2}}$ | Skin friction coeff. |
| 17 | Average friction (mixed) | $\bar{C}_{f,L} = \dfrac{0.074}{\text{Re}_L^{1/5}} - \dfrac{1742}{\text{Re}_L}$ | Transition at $5\times 10^5$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buckingham $\Pi$ Theorem Applied to External Convection

**Problem:** Find the dimensionless groups governing forced convection over a flat plate.

**Step 1 — Identify all variables and their dimensions:**

We assume the local heat transfer coefficient $h$ depends on the following 7 variables:

$$h = \phi(x,\ U_\infty,\ \rho,\ \mu,\ k,\ c_p,\ g)$$

Wait — for *forced* convection, $g$ drops out. We get $n = 7$ variables.

| Variable | Symbol | Dimension (M-L-T-$\Theta$-K) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat transfer coefficient | $h$ | $M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$ |
| Distance along plate | $x$ | $L$ |
| Free-stream velocity | $U_\infty$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | $M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$ |
| Specific heat | $c_p$ | $L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}$ |

**Step 2 — Number of fundamental dimensions:** $k = 4$ (M, L, T, $\Theta$).

**Step 3 — Number of dimensionless groups:** By Buckingham Pi Theorem,

$$\text{Number of }\Pi\text{ groups} = n - k = 7 - 4 = 3$$

So we expect **3 dimensionless groups**.

**Step 4 — Choose repeating variables:** Select $U_\infty$, $\rho$, $k$, and $x$ (one per dimension). These cannot form a $\Pi$ group on their own.

**Step 5 — Form the $\Pi$ groups:**

**$\Pi_1$ (contains $h$):**

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot x^a \cdot \rho^b \cdot U_\infty^c \cdot k^d$$

Dimensions:

$$[M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}][L]^a [M L^{-3}]^b [L T^{-1}]^c [M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]^d = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$$

Equating powers:

- $M$: $1 + b + d = 0$
- $L$: $0 + a - 3b + c + d = 0$
- $T$: $-3 - c - 3d = 0$
- $\Theta$: $-1 - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -1$

Substituting $d = -1$:
- $M$: $1 + b - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$
- $T$: $-3 - c + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow c = 0$
- $L$: $a + 0 + 0 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1$

So:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{h x}{k} = \text{Nu}_x \quad \checkmark \text{ (Nusselt Number)}$$

**$\Pi_2$ (contains $\mu$):**

$$\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot x^a \cdot \rho^b \cdot U_\infty^c \cdot k^d$$

Repeating the same procedure yields:

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\rho U_\infty x}{\mu} = \text{Re}_x \quad \checkmark \text{ (Reynolds Number)}$$

**$\Pi_3$ (contains $c_p$):**

$$\Pi_3 = c_p \cdot x^a \cdot \rho^b \cdot U_\infty^c \cdot k^d$$

Solving gives:

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \text{Pr} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Prandtl Number)}$$

**Final Result (functional relationship):**

$$\boxed{\text{Nu}_x = \phi(\text{Re}_x,\ \text{Pr})}$$

This is the **fundamental dimensionless form** of all forced-convection external flow correlations. Every correlation in the formula sheet is just a *specific functional form* of this general equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Analogy (Stanton–Prandtl Analogy)

The Reynolds Analogy establishes a relationship between **skin friction** (drag) and **heat transfer** in turbulent flow.

**Starting premises:**
1. In the turbulent core, both momentum and heat are transported by the same eddies.
2. Therefore, the *eddy viscosity* $\epsilon_M$ and *eddy thermal diffusivity* $\epsilon_H$ are equal: $\epsilon_M = \epsilon_H$.

From the boundary layer momentum equation integrated to the wall, the wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \rho U_\infty^2 \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

where $\theta$ is the momentum thickness.

By the analogy, the wall heat flux is:

$$q''_w = \rho c_p U_\infty (T_s - T_\infty) \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{Pr}_t}$$

Dividing $q''_w$ by $\tau_w c_p (T_s - T_\infty)$:

$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p U_\infty} = \frac{C_f}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{St} \equiv \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

where:
- $\text{St} = \dfrac{h}{\rho c_p U_\infty}$ (Stanton Number)
- $C_f = \dfrac{2 \tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2}$ (Friction Coefficient)

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitations of the original Reynolds Analogy:**
> 1. Valid only for **Pr = 1** (e.g., gases).
> 2. Valid only for **turbulent** flow.
> 3. Assumes $\epsilon_M = \epsilon_H$ throughout the boundary layer.

**Extended Reynolds Analogy (for Pr $\neq 1$):**

$$\boxed{\text{St}\,\text{Pr}^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

This is called the **Chilton–Colburn Analogy**, and it is the most widely used in engineering practice.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Order-of-Magnitude Estimate of Laminar Boundary Layer Thickness

We can obtain a scaling relation for $\delta(x)$ using a *control volume* approach.

**Mass conservation (2D, steady):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**x-momentum equation (boundary layer approximation):**

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Order of magnitude estimates:**

$$u \sim U_\infty, \quad x \sim x, \quad v \sim ?, \quad y \sim \delta(x)$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \sim \frac{U_\infty}{x}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \sim \frac{U_\infty}{\delta}$$

**From continuity:**

$$\frac{U_\infty}{x} \sim \frac{v}{\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v \sim \frac{U_\infty \delta}{x}$$

**Inertia terms (left side of momentum):**

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \sim \frac{U_\infty^2}{x}, \quad v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \sim \frac{U_\infty^2 \delta}{x \delta} = \frac{U_\infty^2}{x}$$

**Viscous term (right side):**

$$\nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \sim \frac{\nu U_\infty}{\delta^2}$$

**Equating inertia and viscous terms (boundary layer edge where they balance):**

$$\frac{U_\infty^2}{x} \sim \frac{\nu U_\infty}{\delta^2}$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta^2 \sim \frac{\nu x}{U_\infty} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{\delta}{x} \sim \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}}$$

The exact (analytical Blasius) result is:

$$\delta(x) = \frac{5.0\, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

confirming the order-of-magnitude scaling.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Drag and Heat Transfer from a Mixed Boundary Layer

**Setup:** Plate of length $L$, transition at $x_{cr}$, where $\text{Re}_{x,cr} = 5 \times 10^5$.

**Total heat transfer:**

$$Q = W \int_0^L h_x (T_s - T_\infty) \, dx = W (T_s - T_\infty) \int_0^L h_x \, dx$$

We split the integral into two parts:

$$Q = W (T_s - T_\infty) \left[ \int_0^{x_{cr}} h_{x,\text{lam}} \, dx + \int_{x_{cr}}^L h_{x,\text{turb}} \, dx \right]$$

Using the local correlations:
- $h_{x,\text{lam}} = \dfrac{k}{x}(0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3})$
- $h_{x,\text{turb}} = \dfrac{k}{x}(0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3})$

After performing the integration (a standard KTU long-answer step), the final **average** Nusselt number is:

$$\boxed{\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \left( 0.037\, \text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 871 \right) \text{Pr}^{1/3}}$$

The constant **871** originates from $0.037\,\text{Re}_{cr}^{4/5} - 0.664\,\text{Re}_{cr}^{1/2}$ evaluated at $\text{Re}_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$:

$$0.037 \times (5 \times 10^5)^{0.8} - 0.664 \times (5 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \approx 871$$

This is the **standard form** taught in every KTU-approved Heat Transfer textbook.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Air at $T_\infty = 27^\circ$C and $U_\infty = 10$ m/s flows over a flat plate of length $L = 2$ m, maintained at $T_s = 77^\circ$C. Determine the local and average heat transfer coefficients at the trailing edge. Take $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $k = 0.026$ W/m·K, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number at $L$:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{10 \times 2}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.25 \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 — Determine the regime:**
Since $\text{Re}_L = 1.25 \times 10^6 > 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **mixed** (laminar up to $x_{cr}$, then turbulent).

**Step 3 — Find $x_{cr}$:**

$$x_{cr} = \frac{\text{Re}_{cr}\, \nu}{U_\infty} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{10} = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Local Nusselt at $L$ (turbulent):**

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.0296 \times (1.25 \times 10^6)^{0.8} \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.0296 \times 55330 \times 0.8925 \approx 1461$$

**Step 5 — Local $h_L$:**

$$h_L = \frac{\text{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{1461 \times 0.026}{2} \approx 19 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 6 — Average Nusselt (mixed):**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \left(0.037 \times (1.25 \times 10^6)^{0.8} - 871\right) \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \left(0.037 \times 55330 - 871\right) \times 0.8925 = (2047 - 871) \times 0.8925 \approx 1050$$

**Step 7 — Average $\bar{h}$:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\bar{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{1050 \times 0.026}{2} \approx 13.65 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 8 — Total heat transfer (assuming $W = 1$ m):**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot W \cdot L \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 13.65 \times 1 \times 2 \times (77 - 27) = 1365 \text{ W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Convection Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Convection Heat Transfer] --> B[Forced Convection]
    A --> C[Natural Free Convection]
    A --> D[Mixed Combined Convection]
    B --> E[External Flow]
    B --> F[Internal Flow]
    E --> G[Flow over Flat Plate]
    E --> H[Flow over Cylinder Sphere]
    F --> I[Flow in Pipes Ducts]
    G --> J[Laminar BL]
    G --> K[Turbulent BL]
    J --> L[Blasius Exact Solution]
    K --> M[Empirical 1/7 Power Law]
    C --> N[Vertical Plates]
    C --> O[Horizontal Plates]
    C --> P[Enclosed Cavities]
    D --> Q[Gr vs Re Squared Check]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the root "Convection" and follow a branch. The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus focuses specifically on the **Forced → External → Flat Plate → Laminar/Turbulent BL** branch.

---

## 4.2 Boundary Layer Schematic (Mermaid Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream Uinfinity] --> B[Leading Edge x = 0]
    B --> C[Laminar Velocity BL delta x]
    C --> D{Recr = 5e5}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Turbulent Velocity BL]
    E --> F[Trailing Edge x = L]
    G[Wall Ts] --> H[Thermal BL delta t]
    H --> I[Bulk Tinfinity]
    J[delta t by delta ratio] --> K{Prandtl Number Pr}
    K -->|Pr greater than 1| L[delta t smaller than delta]
    K -->|Pr equal 1| M[delta t = delta]
    K -->|Pr smaller than 1| N[delta t larger than delta]
```

---

## 4.3 Dimensional Analysis Process (Buckingham Pi Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[List all variables n = 7] --> B[Count fundamental dimensions k = 4]
    B --> C[Compute number of Pi groups n minus k = 3]
    C --> D[Choose repeating variables]
    D --> E[Form Pi1 with h]
    D --> F[Form Pi2 with mu]
    D --> G[Form Pi3 with cp]
    E --> H[Pi1 = Nux]
    F --> I[Pi2 = Rex]
    G --> J[Pi3 = Pr]
    H --> K[Final relation Nux = phi Rex Pr]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Empirical Closure via Experiments]
    L --> M[Correlation table of KTU module 2]
```

---

## 4.4 Boundary Layer Profile Visualization (Block Architecture)

Because Mermaid cannot render a true 2-D physical sketch of a velocity profile, we present a **functional block-architecture** representation of the physical picture, suitable for revision:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BL[Boundary Layer Region over Flat Plate]
        A1[Wall y = 0, u = 0, T = Ts] --> A2[Laminar Sublayer]
        A2 --> A3[Buffer Layer]
        A3 --> A4[Turbulent Core]
        A4 --> A5[BL Edge y = delta, u = 0.99 Uinfinity]
    end
    subgraph TBL[Thermal Boundary Layer Region]
        B1[Wall T = Ts] --> B2[Conduction Dominant]
        B2 --> B3[Convection Dominant]
        B3 --> B4[BL Edge T = 0.99 Tinfinity]
    end
    BL --- TBL
```

---

## 4.5 Heat Transfer Coefficient Behavior (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Position x along plate] --> B[Local hx decreases with x in laminar]
    A --> C[Local hx decreases faster in turbulent]
    D[Average hbar] --> E[Constant for uniform Ts]
    F[Laminar Entire Plate] --> G[bar hlam from 0.664 Re^0.5]
    H[Turbulent Entire Plate] --> I[bar hturb from 0.037 Re^0.8]
    J[Mixed Regime] --> K[bar hmix from 0.037 Re^0.8 minus 871]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define *convective heat transfer coefficient*. Why is it called a "system property" rather than a "fluid property"?

**Model Answer:**

The convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is defined as the proportionality constant in Newton's law of cooling, $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$, and has units of W/m²·K.

> $h$ is called a **system property** because its value depends on:
> 1. The fluid's thermophysical properties ($\mu$, $k$, $c_p$, $\rho$).
> 2. The flow velocity $U_\infty$ and the geometry of the surface.
> 3. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ and the boundary layer state (laminar/turbulent).

A change in velocity or geometry — even with the same fluid — yields a different $h$. Hence, $h$ characterizes a *fluid-surface-flow system*, not the fluid alone.

*(Examiner tip: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the "system property" justification.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the physical significance of the **Prandtl number**. What does a high Prandtl number fluid look like physically?

**Model Answer:**

The Prandtl number $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ represents the ratio of **momentum diffusivity** to **thermal diffusivity** of a fluid.

- $\text{Pr} \gg 1$ (e.g., oils, $\text{Pr} \sim 100$–$10^4$): Heat diffuses *much slower* than momentum. The thermal boundary layer is **much thinner** than the velocity boundary layer. Examples: engine oil, glycerol.
- $\text{Pr} \ll 1$ (e.g., liquid metals, $\text{Pr} \sim 0.01$): Heat diffuses *much faster* than momentum. The thermal boundary layer is **much thicker** than the velocity boundary layer. Examples: mercury, molten sodium.
- $\text{Pr} \approx 1$ (e.g., gases, $\text{Pr} \sim 0.7$): The two boundary layers have nearly equal thickness.

*(Examiner tip: 1 mark formula, 2 marks physical interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the dimensionless groups governing forced convection over a flat plate using Buckingham's $\Pi$ theorem. State the final functional relationship. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Atmospheric air at $T_\infty = 30^\circ$C with free-stream velocity $U_\infty = 5$ m/s flows over a flat plate maintained at $T_s = 90^\circ$C. The plate is 3 m long. Calculate the average heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transferred per meter width of the plate. Given: $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $k = 0.026$ W/m·K, $\text{Pr} = 0.71$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1**. The expected board answer is:

Step 1: List 7 variables: $h$, $x$, $U_\infty$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $k$, $c_p$ with their dimensions.

Step 2: $n = 7$, $k = 4$ (M, L, T, $\Theta$) → **3 dimensionless groups**.

Step 3: Choose repeating variables: $x$, $U_\infty$, $\rho$, $k$.

Step 4: Form $\Pi_1$ (with $h$) → $\Pi_1 = \dfrac{h x}{k} = \text{Nu}_x$

Step 5: Form $\Pi_2$ (with $\mu$) → $\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\rho U_\infty x}{\mu} = \text{Re}_x$

Step 6: Form $\Pi_3$ (with $c_p$) → $\Pi_3 = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \text{Pr}$

**Final functional relationship:**

$$\boxed{\text{Nu}_x = \phi(\text{Re}_x, \text{Pr})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to state the *number* of fundamental dimensions.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Choosing a *non-repeating* variable such as $h$ itself (it depends on what we are solving for).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing the formula but not solving for the *exponents* of the repeating variables.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Re}_L$:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 3}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.375 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Determine the regime:**

Since $\text{Re}_L = 9.375 \times 10^5 > 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer is **mixed** (laminar up to $x_{cr}$, then turbulent).

**Step 3 — Compute the average Nusselt number (mixed correlation):**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = \left( 0.037\, \text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 871 \right) \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

Compute $\text{Re}_L^{4/5}$:

$$(9.375 \times 10^5)^{0.8} = (9.375)^{0.8} \times (10^5)^{0.8} = 6.13 \times 10^4 = 61300$$

Compute the bracket:

$$0.037 \times 61300 - 871 = 2268 - 871 = 1397$$

Compute $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$:

$$(0.71)^{1/3} = 0.8925$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 1397 \times 0.8925 \approx 1247$$

**Step 4 — Average heat transfer coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\bar{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{1247 \times 0.026}{3} \approx 10.81 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat transfer per meter width ($W = 1$ m):**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 10.81 \times (1 \times 3) \times (90 - 30) = 1946 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 10.8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}, \quad Q \approx 1.95 \text{ kW per meter width}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using the *laminar* correlation $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ when $\text{Re}_L > 5\times10^5$. Always check the regime first.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert $T_\infty$ and $T_s$ to a consistent unit (K or °C — both work, but be consistent). Only the *difference* $\Delta T$ matters.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Omitting the width $W$ in the total heat transfer calculation. If width is not given, state "per meter width" explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **velocity** and **thermal** boundary layer concepts for flow over a heated flat plate. Discuss the variation of these layers with the Prandtl number. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Engine oil at $60^\circ$C flows over a 5 m long flat plate with velocity 0.05 m/s. The plate surface is at $30^\circ$C. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and total heat loss from one side of the plate (per m width). Given: $\nu = 8.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $k = 0.144$ W/m·K, $\text{Pr} = 1080$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

- A velocity boundary layer of thickness $\delta(x)$ forms on the plate, where velocity rises from 0 at the wall to $0.99 U_\infty$ at the edge.
- A thermal boundary layer of thickness $\delta_t(x)$ forms simultaneously, where temperature drops from $T_s$ at the wall to $0.99 T_\infty$ at the edge.
- For laminar flow, the analytical Blasius solution gives: $\delta(x) = \dfrac{5.0\,x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$, and the velocity profile is $u/U_\infty = f(y/\delta)$.
- **Variation with Prandtl number:** $\delta_t = \delta / \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ for laminar flow.
  - For engine oil ($\text{Pr} = 1080$): $\delta_t$ is about 10 times smaller than $\delta$.
  - For liquid metal ($\text{Pr} = 0.01$): $\delta_t$ is about 4.6 times larger than $\delta$.
- Sketch should show both layers, marking $\delta$, $\delta_t$, $U_\infty$, $T_\infty$, $T_s$, and the no-slip condition at the wall.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Re}_L$:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{0.05 \times 5}{8.5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2941$$

**Step 2 — Determine the regime:**

$\text{Re}_L = 2941 < 5 \times 10^5$ → Flow is **entirely laminar**.

**Step 3 — Apply laminar average Nusselt correlation:**

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\, \text{Re}_L^{1/2}\, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

$$\text{Re}_L^{1/2} = \sqrt{2941} \approx 54.23$$

$$\text{Pr}^{1/3} = (1080)^{1/3} \approx 10.26$$

$$\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times 54.23 \times 10.26 \approx 369.4$$

**Step 4 — Average heat transfer coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\bar{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{369.4 \times 0.144}{5} \approx 10.64 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat loss per meter width:**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 10.64 \times (1 \times 5) \times (60 - 30) = 1596 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 10.6 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}, \quad Q \approx 1.60 \text{ kW per meter width}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Confusing the direction of heat flow. Here, oil is *hotter* than the plate, so heat flows from oil to plate — the sign of $\Delta T$ matters when computing $Q$. Magnitudes are usually asked.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Using the *turbulent* correlation by mistake (the problem clearly says "engine oil" with high $\text{Pr}$, so $\text{Re}_L$ will be small).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the problem states "from one side of the plate" — only one side is considered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section as your final 5-minute revision pass before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- [x] **Convection** is the only mode of heat transfer that *requires motion of matter*. Newton's law: $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$.
- [x] **Convective heat transfer coefficient $h$** is a *system property*, not a fluid property. Order of magnitude: 5 to 20,000 W/m²·K depending on fluid and flow type.
- [x] **Boundary Layer Concept** (Prandtl 1904): A thin region near the wall where viscous and thermal effects are confined. Outside it, the fluid is inviscid and isothermal.
- [x] **Two simultaneous boundary layers**: Velocity $\delta$ and Thermal $\delta_t$. Ratio: $\delta_t = \delta / \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ (laminar).
- [x] **Types of Convection**: Forced (external agency), Natural (buoyancy), Mixed (comparable). Decided by Richardson Number $\text{Ri} = \text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2$.
- [x] **Critical Reynolds Number for a flat plate**: $\text{Re}_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$ (laminar to turbulent transition). **MEMORIZE this value.**
- [x] **Dimensional Analysis**: Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem: $n - k$ dimensionless groups from $n$ variables in $k$ dimensions.
- [x] **Three Key Dimensionless Numbers for Forced Convection**:
  - $\text{Re} = \rho U L / \mu$ — Inertia vs Viscous
  - $\text{Pr} = \mu c_p / k$ — Momentum vs Thermal diffusivity
  - $\text{Nu} = h L / k$ — Convection vs Conduction
- [x] **Master Equation (derived from Buckingham $\Pi$)**: $\text{Nu}_x = \phi(\text{Re}_x, \text{Pr})$.
- [x] **Laminar correlations (flat plate)**:
  - Local: $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332 \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
  - Average: $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
- [x] **Turbulent correlations (flat plate)**:
  - Local: $\text{Nu}_x = 0.0296 \text{Re}_x^{4/5} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
  - Average: $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037 \text{Re}_L^{4/5} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$
- [x] **Mixed regime**: $\bar{\text{Nu}}_L = (0.037 \text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 871)\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ — uses transition at $5 \times 10^5$.
- [x] **Reynolds Analogy (Chilton-Colburn)**: $\text{St}\,\text{Pr}^{2/3} = C_f / 2$ — links drag and heat transfer in turbulent flow.
- [x] **All correlations require** $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$ and $\text{Re}_x < 10^9$ (assumed isothermal plate, constant properties).
- [x] **KTU Pitfall**: Always check if the flow is laminar, turbulent, or mixed *before* selecting a correlation. **+1 mark "regime identification" is often awarded in ESE valuation.**
- [x] **Prandtl number magnitudes to remember**: Gases $\sim 0.7$, water $\sim 7$, light oils $\sim 100$–$1000$, liquid metals $\sim 0.01$.
- [x] **Final functional form** of every KTU problem in this module: Identify geometry → Identify regime → Compute Re and Pr → Select correlation → Solve for $h$ → Compute $Q$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
