topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cylinders and Spheres - Internal Flow - Laminar and Turbulent Flow - Combined Laminar and Turbulent Flow - Flow Over Bank of Tubes - Free Convection - Dimensional Analysis - Flow Over Vertical Plates

# **MODULE 2: CONVECTIVE HEAT TRANSFER – INTERNAL & EXTERNAL FLOWS**

## **1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW**

### **1.1 Convection Heat Transfer – The Governing Paradigm**

Convection is the transfer of thermal energy between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas). It is governed by Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²K), $T_s$ is the surface temperature (K), and $T_\infty$ is the bulk fluid temperature (K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is *not* a fluid property — it is a **strong function** of geometry, flow regime (laminar/turbulent), surface conditions, and thermophysical properties. The entire objective of this module is to **determine $h$** for various configurations.

---

### **1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy**

Imagine a garden hose carrying hot water. The water inside (internal flow) loses heat to the surroundings. If the water moves slowly — in smooth, parallel layers — the flow is **laminar** (like thick honey gliding in one direction). If you turn the tap harder, the water becomes chaotic, with eddies and swirls — this is **turbulent** flow, and it transfers heat *much faster* because the chaotic mixing continuously brings fresh hot fluid to the pipe wall.

Now imagine not one hose, but a *bundle* of tubes in a steam boiler — that's a **bank of tubes**. And if no fan or pump is forcing the water, but the fluid moves simply because it was heated (warm air rising from a radiator), that's **free (natural) convection** — the fluid itself becomes the engine of motion.

---

### **1.3 Classification of Convection Problems**

| **Type** | **Forcing Mechanism** | **Typical Configuration** |
|---|---|---|
| Forced Convection – Internal | Pump / Fan | Flow inside pipes, ducts |
| Forced Convection – External | Pump / Fan | Flow across tubes, plates |
| Free (Natural) Convection | Buoyancy | Heated plates, cylinders, rooms |
| Mixed Convection | Both | Industrial heat exchangers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focal Areas in Module 2:** (1) Internal flow in cylinders, (2) Flow over bank of tubes, (3) Dimensional analysis (universal tool), (4) Free convection, (5) Flow over vertical plates.

---

### **1.4 Critical Dimensionless Numbers — The Language of Convection**

| **Number** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds | $Re$ | $\dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Inertia / Viscous forces |
| Nusselt | $Nu$ | $\dfrac{hL}{k}$ | Convection / Conduction |
| Prandtl | $Pr$ | $\dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Momentum / Thermal diffusivity |
| Grashof | $Gr$ | $\dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy / Viscous forces |
| Rayleigh | $Ra$ | $Gr \cdot Pr$ | Combined buoyancy-diffusion |
| Stanton | $St$ | $\dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p}$ | Heat transfer efficiency |
| Péclet | $Pe$ | $Re \cdot Pr$ | Advection / Diffusion |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# **2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET**

## **2.1 Internal Flow Through Cylinders (Tubes)**

### **2.1.1 Flow Regimes in Tubes**

The transition from **laminar → turbulent** in circular tubes occurs at a **critical Reynolds number**:

$$Re_{D,crit} = \dfrac{\rho V_{mean} D}{\mu} \approx 2300$$

- $Re_D \le 2300$ → **Laminar** (parabolic velocity profile)
- $2300 < Re_D < 4000$ → **Transitional**
- $Re_D \ge 4000$ → **Turbulent** (flat velocity profile)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The exact transition is a probabilistic phenomenon. Some texts use $Re_{crit} = 2300$ (lower limit) and others use $Re_{crit} = 10{,}000$ (fully turbulent). KTU usually accepts **2300** as the lower critical value.

---

### **2.1.2 Hydrodynamic & Thermal Entry Lengths**

Before the flow becomes fully developed, it passes through **entry regions**:

$$L_{h} = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot D \quad \text{(Hydrodynamic entry length)}$$

$$L_{t} = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot D \quad \text{(Thermal entry length)}$$

For laminar flow in tubes, a useful constant is:
$$L_{t} = \begin{cases} 0.05 \, Re_D \, Pr \, D & Pr \ge 0.7 \text{ (heating)} \\ \dfrac{0.03 \, Re_D \, Pr \, D}{1 + 0.04 \, (Re_D \, Pr)^{2/3}} & \text{(cooling, liquid metals)} \end{cases}$$

---

### **2.1.3 Constant Heat Flux & Constant Wall Temperature Conditions**

Two classical thermal boundary conditions:

1. **Constant Surface Heat Flux** ($q''_s$ = const) — e.g., electrical heater
2. **Constant Surface Temperature** ($T_s$ = const) — e.g., condenser

For **thermally and hydrodynamically fully developed laminar flow** ($Re_D \le 2300$):

| **Geometry** | **Nu (T_s = const)** | **Nu (q''_s = const)** |
|---|---|---|
| Circular tube | $3.66$ | $4.36$ |
| Concentric annulus | varies with $D_i/D_o$ | varies |

---

### **2.1.4 Sieder–Tate Correlation (Entry Region, Laminar)**

For developing flow with property variations:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 1.86 \left( \dfrac{Re_D \, Pr}{L/D} \right)^{1/3} \left( \dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_s} \right)^{0.14}$$

Valid for: $0.48 < Pr < 16{,}700$, $0.0044 < \mu_b/\mu_s < 9.75$, and **all** $L/D$.

---

## **2.2 Turbulent Flow in Tubes**

### **2.2.1 Dittus–Boelter Equation (1930)**

The most famous turbulent-flow correlation. **Re_D ≥ 10,000**, $0.7 \le Pr \le 160$, $L/D \ge 10$:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^n$$

- $n = 0.4$ → heating ($T_s > T_m$)
- $n = 0.3$ → cooling ($T_s < T_m$)

### **2.2.2 Sieder–Tate Variant (with property correction)**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.027 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \left( \dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_s} \right)^{0.14}$$

### **2.2.3 Gnielinski Correlation (1976) – More Accurate**

Valid for $2300 \le Re_D \le 5 \times 10^6$ and $0.5 \le Pr \le 2000$:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = \dfrac{(f/8)(Re_D - 1000) Pr}{1 + 12.7 (f/8)^{0.5} (Pr^{2/3} - 1)}$$

where the **Petukhov friction factor** is:

$$f = (0.790 \ln Re_D - 1.64)^{-2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The Gnielinski correlation *bridges* the laminar and turbulent regions smoothly. It is preferred over Dittus–Boelter for higher accuracy and is often used in industrial heat-exchanger design codes (e.g., TEMA standards).

---

## **2.3 Combined Entry Length (Laminar + Turbulent in Same Tube)**

When fluid enters a tube in laminar regime and **transitions to turbulence** along the length:

$$L_{entry} = L_{laminar} + L_{turbulent}$$

The **average Nusselt number** over the *entire* tube length is computed using a weighted average (McAdams):

$$\overline{Nu} = \dfrac{\overline{Nu}_{lam} \cdot L_{lam} + \overline{Nu}_{turb} \cdot L_{turb}}{L_{total}}$$

This concept is critical in **chemical reactor tubes**, **nuclear fuel rod bundles**, and **solar thermal receivers**.

---

## **2.4 Flow Over a Bank of Tubes**

### **2.4.1 Definition**

A **tube bank** consists of $N$ tubes arranged in a geometric array, immersed in cross-flow. Applications: **boilers, condensers, air-cooled heat exchangers, radiators**.

### **2.4.2 Geometric Arrangements**

| **Type** | **Description** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Inline** | Tubes aligned in rows & columns | High heat transfer per unit area |
| **Staggered** | Alternate rows offset by $S_D/2$ | Better mixing, higher $h$ |

Geometric parameters:
- $S_L$ = longitudinal pitch (flow direction)
- $S_T$ = transverse pitch (perpendicular to flow)
- $D_o$ = outer tube diameter
- $N_L, N_T$ = number of tubes in longitudinal/transverse direction

### **2.4.3 Maximum Velocity in Bank of Tubes**

The flow area decreases as it passes through the bank; **maximum velocity** occurs at the diagonal of a staggered bank:

$$V_{max} = V \cdot \dfrac{S_T}{S_T - D_o} \quad \text{(inline, minimum area)}$$

For **staggered**:

$$V_{max} = V \cdot \dfrac{S_T}{2(S_D - D_o)} \quad \text{if } 2(S_D - D_o) < (S_T - D_o)$$

### **2.4.4 Zukauskas Correlation (KTU Favorite!)**

For staggered or inline tube banks:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = C \, Re_D^{m} \, Pr^{0.36} \left( \dfrac{Pr}{Pr_s} \right)^{0.25}$$

| **Re_D Range** | **C** | **m** |
|---|---|---|
| 10 – 100 | 0.71 | 0.5 |
| 100 – 1000 | 0.80 (inline) / 0.71 (staggered) | 0.5 |
| 1000 – 2×10⁵ | 0.27 (inline) / 0.35 (staggered) | 0.6 |
| 2×10⁵ – 2×10⁶ | 0.021 (inline) / 0.022 (staggered) | 0.84 |

Where $Re_D$ is based on **maximum velocity** $V_{max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **validity range**, arrangement (inline/staggered), and whether $Re$ is based on $V$ or $V_{max}$. Marks are routinely lost on these three checkpoints.

---

## **2.5 Free (Natural) Convection**

### **2.5.1 Physical Mechanism**

In free convection, fluid motion is driven by **buoyancy forces** caused by density differences in the gravity field:

$$F_{buoy} = (\rho_\infty - \rho) \, g$$

Using Boussinesq approximation: $\rho_\infty - \rho = \rho_\infty \beta (T_s - T_\infty)$, where $\beta$ is the thermal expansion coefficient.

### **2.5.2 Dimensional Analysis Outcome for Free Convection**

$$\overline{Nu} = f(Gr, Pr)$$

**Laminar vs. Turbulent in Free Convection:**

| **Geometry** | **Laminar** | **Turbulent** |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical plates | $Gr \le 10^9$ | $Gr \ge 10^9$ |
| Horizontal cylinders | $Gr \le 10^9$ | — |
| Horizontal plates | $Gr \le 10^7$ | $Gr \ge 10^7$ |

---

## **2.6 Free Convection Correlations (KTU High-Yield Sheet)**

### **2.6.1 Vertical Plates**

| **Correlation** | **Form** | **Validity** |
|---|---|---|
| **Churchill–Chu** | $\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$ | All $Ra_L$ |
| Simplified laminar | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.59 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^9$ |
| Turbulent | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.10 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Ra_L \le 10^{13}$ |

### **2.6.2 Horizontal Cylinders (Cross-Section)**

**Churchill–Chu correlation:**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = \left\{ 0.60 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra_D^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.559/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

Valid for $Ra_D \le 10^{12}$ (entire laminar + turbulent transition).

### **2.6.3 Spheres**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 2 + 0.43 \, (Ra_D)^{0.25} \quad ; \quad 1 \le Ra_D \le 10^7$$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 2 + 0.50 \, (Ra_D)^{0.25} \quad ; \quad 10^7 \le Ra_D \le 10^{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant "2" represents pure conduction in the limit $Ra \to 0$ — a fundamental theoretical result for spheres in stagnant fluid.

### **2.6.4 Vertical Plates – Heated/Cooled Facing Up or Down**

| **Configuration** | **Correlation** |
|---|---|
| Hot surface up / Cold down (turbulent) | $Nu = 0.15 \, Ra^{1/3}$ |
| Cold surface up / Hot down (laminar) | $Nu = 0.52 \, Ra^{1/5}$ |

---

## **2.7 Flow Over Vertical Plates (Forced Convection)**

The **local Nusselt number** at distance $x$ from leading edge of an isothermal vertical plate:

$$Nu_x = \dfrac{h_x \cdot x}{k} = 0.0292 \, Re_x^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \quad ; \quad Re_x \le 5 \times 10^5 \text{ (turbulent)}$$

For **laminar** ($Re_x \le 5 \times 10^5$):

$$Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

The **average** Nusselt over a plate of length $L$:

| **Regime** | **$\overline{Nu}_L$** |
|---|---|
| Laminar (isothermal) | $0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ |
| Turbulent (isothermal) | $0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$ |
| Mixed (laminar + turbulent) | $(0.037 Re_L^{0.8} - 870) Pr^{1/3}$ |

The transition Reynolds number for boundary layer flow is:
$$Re_{x,c} = 5 \times 10^5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the **mixed boundary-layer** case, the laminar portion goes from $0$ to $x_c$, and the turbulent portion from $x_c$ to $L$. Marks are deducted if students fail to use the *correct combined* formula or use $870$ incorrectly.

---

## **2.8 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Print-Friendly Table)**

| **Scenario** | **Governing Equation** | **Validity** |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar tube, $T_s$ = const | $Nu = 3.66$ | $Re \le 2300$, fully developed |
| Laminar tube, $q''$ = const | $Nu = 4.36$ | $Re \le 2300$, fully developed |
| Sieder–Tate (developing) | $Nu = 1.86 (Re Pr D/L)^{1/3} (\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ | Laminar, viscous dissipation negligible |
| Dittus–Boelter | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^n$ | Turbulent, $L/D \ge 10$ |
| Gnielinski | $Nu = \dfrac{(f/8)(Re-1000)Pr}{1 + 12.7(f/8)^{1/2}(Pr^{2/3}-1)}$ | 2300 ≤ Re ≤ 5×10⁶ |
| Staggered bank | $Nu = C Re^m Pr^{0.36} (Pr/Pr_s)^{0.25}$ | Zukauskas table |
| Vertical plate (laminar) | $Nu = 0.59 Ra^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra \le 10^9$ |
| Vertical plate (turbulent) | $Nu = 0.10 Ra^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Ra \le 10^{13}$ |
| Horizontal cylinder | $Nu = \{0.60 + 0.387 Ra^{1/6}/[1+(0.559/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}\}^2$ | Churchill–Chu |
| Sphere | $Nu = 2 + 0.43 Ra^{1/4}$ | $1 \le Ra \le 10^7$ |

---

## **2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications**

| **Configuration** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Internal flow in tube | Power plant condensers, solar thermal collectors, oil pipelines |
| Bank of tubes | Steam generators, HVAC coils, process industry heat exchangers |
| Free convection – vertical plates | Building façade ventilation, electronic cooling fins |
| Free convection – horizontal cylinder | Storage tanks, overhead transmission cables, nuclear rod bundles |
| Free convection – sphere | LPG bullets, cryogenic storage vessels, weather balloons |
| Flow over vertical plate | Aerodynamic heating, cooling of electronic chips on PCB edges |

# **3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX**

## **3.1 Dimensional Analysis of Forced Convection in a Tube (Buckingham Pi Theorem)**

### **3.1.1 Problem Setup**

We wish to relate the **Nusselt number** to the relevant physical variables. The functional dependence is:

$$h = f(V, D, \rho, \mu, k, c_p)$$

> Total variables $n = 7$: $\{h, V, D, \rho, \mu, k, c_p\}$
> Fundamental dimensions $m = 3$: $\{M, L, T, \Theta\}$ → wait, we need $4$ dimensions because $c_p$ brings in temperature. Let $m = 4$ (M, L, T, Θ).
> Number of $\Pi$ groups: $n - m = 7 - 4 = 3$.

### **3.1.2 Step-by-Step Application**

**Step 1: Express the dimensional form of each variable in M, L, T, Θ**

| **Variable** | **Symbol** | **Dimensions** |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient | $h$ | $M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$ |
| Velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Diameter | $D$ | $L$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | $M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}$ |
| Specific heat | $c_p$ | $L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}$ |

**Step 2: Select repeating variables**

Choose $V, D, \rho$ (they collectively contain M, L, T but not Θ).

**Step 3: Form Π₁ using $h$**

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^a \cdot D^b \cdot \rho^c$$

$$M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0 = (M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}) (L T^{-1})^a (L)^b (M L^{-3})^c$$

Equating powers:

- M: $0 = 1 + c \Rightarrow c = -1$
- Θ: $0 = -1 \Rightarrow$ wait, we need $\Theta^0$ on LHS, so $\Theta$ exponent: $0 = -1 \Rightarrow$ impossible!

We need to **add $k$ or $c_p$** to the repeating variables. Choose $V, D, k$ as repeating (no Θ issue). 

Let us re-choose repeating variables as $\rho, V, D$ and treat $h$ carefully. Better approach: pick $V, D, k$ as repeating.

**Recompute with repeating variables** $V, D, k$:

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{a_1} D^{b_1} k^{c_1}$$

Dimensions: $(M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}) \cdot (L T^{-1})^{a_1} \cdot L^{b_1} \cdot (M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1})^{c_1}$

$$M^{1+c_1} \, L^{a_1+b_1+c_1} \, T^{-3-a_1-3c_1} \, \Theta^{-1-c_1} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$$

- M: $1 + c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1$
- Θ: $-1 - c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1$ ✓
- L: $a_1 + b_1 + c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 + b_1 = 1$
- T: $-3 - a_1 - 3c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow -3 - a_1 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$

Therefore $b_1 = 1$. So:

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{h D}{k} = Nu \quad \text{(Nusselt number)}$$

**Step 4: Form Π₂ using $\mu$**

$$\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot V^{a_2} D^{b_2} k^{c_2}$$

$$(M L^{-1} T^{-1})(L T^{-1})^{a_2}(L)^{b_2}(M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1})^{c_2} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$$

- M: $1 + c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = -1$
- Θ: $-c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = 0$ ✗ Contradiction!

This means $k$ cannot be a repeating variable if $\mu$ is non-repeating. The cleanest selection is to use $V, D, \rho, \mu$ as repeating or $D, V, \rho, k$. The classical approach uses $\{D, V, k, \mu\}$.

Let us re-select repeating variables as $D, V, k, \mu$ (4 repeating variables, leaving 3 non-repeating: $h, c_p, \rho$). Then number of $\Pi$ groups = $7 - 4 = 3$. 

**Re-attempt with repeating variables** $D, V, k, \mu$:

**$\Pi_1$ (with $h$):**

$$\Pi_1 = h D^{a_1} V^{b_1} k^{c_1} \mu^{d_1}$$

Dimension equation: $(M T^{-3}\Theta^{-1})(L)^{a_1}(L T^{-1})^{b_1}(M L T^{-3}\Theta^{-1})^{c_1}(M L^{-1} T^{-1})^{d_1} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$

- M: $1 + c_1 + d_1 = 0$
- Θ: $-1 - c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1$
- So M: $1 - 1 + d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow d_1 = 0$
- L: $a_1 + b_1 + c_1 - d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 + b_1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 + b_1 = 1$
- T: $-3 - b_1 - 3c_1 - d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow -3 - b_1 + 3 - 0 = 0 \Rightarrow b_1 = 0$
- Therefore $a_1 = 1$

$$\boxed{\Pi_1 = \dfrac{h D}{k} = Nu_D}$$

**$\Pi_2$ (with $c_p$):**

$$\Pi_2 = c_p D^{a_2} V^{b_2} k^{c_2} \mu^{d_2}$$

$(L^2 T^{-2} \Theta^{-1})(L)^{a_2}(L T^{-1})^{b_2}(M L T^{-3}\Theta^{-1})^{c_2}(M L^{-1} T^{-1})^{d_2} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$

- M: $c_2 + d_2 = 0$
- Θ: $-1 - c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = -1$
- M: $-1 + d_2 = 0 \Rightarrow d_2 = 1$
- L: $2 + a_2 + b_2 - 1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 + b_2 = 0$
- T: $-2 - b_2 + 3 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow b_2 = 0$
- Therefore $a_2 = 0$

$$\boxed{\Pi_2 = \dfrac{c_p \mu}{k} = Pr}$$

**$\Pi_3$ (with $\rho$):**

$$\Pi_3 = \rho D^{a_3} V^{b_3} k^{c_3} \mu^{d_3}$$

$(M L^{-3})(L)^{a_3}(L T^{-1})^{b_3}(M L T^{-3}\Theta^{-1})^{c_3}(M L^{-1} T^{-1})^{d_3} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \Theta^0$

- M: $1 + c_3 + d_3 = 0$
- Θ: $-c_3 = 0 \Rightarrow c_3 = 0$
- M: $1 + d_3 = 0 \Rightarrow d_3 = -1$
- L: $-3 + a_3 + b_3 - d_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_3 + b_3 + 1 - 3 = 0$... let me re-do: $-3 + a_3 + b_3 + 0 - (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow -3 + a_3 + b_3 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_3 + b_3 = 2$
- T: $-b_3 - d_3 = 0 \Rightarrow b_3 = -d_3 = 1$
- Therefore $a_3 = 1$

$$\boxed{\Pi_3 = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = Re_D}$$

### **3.1.3 Final Functional Relationship**

$$\boxed{Nu_D = \phi(Re_D, Pr)}$$

> This is the cornerstone of convective heat transfer. The Buckingham Pi theorem proves — from *first principles of dimensional consistency* — that convection can be reduced to two dimensionless groups. This is why heat transfer correlations take the form $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$.

---

## **3.2 Dimensional Analysis of Free Convection (Vertical Plate)**

### **3.2.1 Problem Setup**

$$h = f(T_s - T_\infty, L, g, \beta, \rho, \mu, k, c_p)$$

That's 8 variables, with dimensions {M, L, T, Θ}, so 4 fundamental dimensions.

Number of $\Pi$ groups = $8 - 4 = 4$.

### **3.2.2 Choosing Repeating Variables**

Choose $L, g, \mu, k$ (or alternatively $L, \beta \Delta T, g, k$).

We form:

- $\Pi_1 = hL/k = Nu$ (as derived before)
- $\Pi_2 = c_p \mu / k = Pr$
- $\Pi_3 = (T_s - T_\infty) g \beta L^3 / \nu^2 = Gr$ (Grashof)
- $\Pi_4 = c_p (T_s - T_\infty) g \beta L^3 / (\nu k / \rho) = Gr \cdot Pr$ — but this is dependent on the others.

Typically we conclude:

$$Nu = f(Gr, Pr) = f(Ra, Pr)$$

where $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ is the **Rayleigh number**.

---

## **3.3 Derivation of Reynolds Analogy**

### **3.3.1 Starting Point**

For a fluid with $Pr = 1$, the **Reynolds Analogy** relates friction and heat transfer:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2} \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \text{const}$$

$$\boxed{St = \dfrac{C_f}{2} \cdot Pr^{-1/3}}$$

> **Reynolds Analogy (Chilton–Colburn):** $j_H = j_M$ where $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ and $j_M = (C_f/2)$.

### **3.3.2 Derivation Logic**

For $Pr = 1$, the velocity and thermal boundary layers have **identical thickness**. The friction coefficient (momentum) and Stanton number (heat) become equal:

$$\dfrac{C_f}{2} = St \quad (\text{when } Pr = 1)$$

For $Pr \ne 1$, modify by $Pr^{2/3}$ (Chilton–Colburn analogy).

---

## **3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)**

### **3.4.1 Problem**

Water flows through a 25 mm diameter tube at 0.6 kg/s. The water enters at 25 °C and is heated to 65 °C by saturated steam at 110 °C condensing on the outer surface. Tube length is 3 m. Estimate the exit temperature and the overall heat transfer coefficient.

> **Given:**
> - $D = 0.025$ m, $m = 0.6$ kg/s
> - $T_{m,i} = 25$ °C, $T_s = 110$ °C
> - $L = 3$ m

### **3.4.2 Solution Steps**

**Step 1: Bulk mean temperature** $T_m = (25 + 65)/2 = 45$ °C

At $T_m = 45$ °C, water properties (from steam tables):

$\rho = 990$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4178$ J/kg·K, $\mu = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/m·s, $k = 0.640$ W/m·K, $Pr = 3.91$

**Step 2: Reynolds number**

$$Re_D = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.6}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 50{,}926$$

> Flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3: Check entry length**

$$L_t = 10 D = 0.25 \text{ m} \ll 3 \text{ m}$$

> Fully developed flow for most of the tube.

**Step 4: Dittus–Boelter (heating, $n = 0.4$)**

$$Nu = 0.023 \times (50{,}926)^{0.8} \times (3.91)^{0.4}$$

$$\log(50{,}926) = 4.707, \quad (50{,}926)^{0.8} = 10^{0.8 \times 4.707} = 10^{3.766} = 5830$$

$$(3.91)^{0.4} = 10^{0.4 \times 0.592} = 10^{0.237} = 1.725$$

$$Nu = 0.023 \times 5830 \times 1.725 = 231.3$$

**Step 5: Heat transfer coefficient**

$$h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D} = \dfrac{231.3 \times 0.640}{0.025} = 5921 \text{ W/m²K}$$

> **Answer: $h \approx 5921$ W/m²K**

---

## **3.5 Combined Entry Length Example**

A fluid enters a tube at $Re_1 = 1500$ (laminar). It transitions to turbulent at $x = 0.5$ m. Tube diameter is 0.02 m, length is 2 m. Find the average Nusselt number.

**Laminar portion:** $L_{lam} = 0.5$ m. Use Sieder–Tate: $Nu_{lam} = 1.86 (Re Pr D/L)^{1/3}$ (approximate).

**Turbulent portion:** $L_{turb} = 1.5$ m. Use Dittus–Boelter at $Re_2$ (say, $Re_2 = 8000$).

**Average:**

$$\overline{Nu} = \dfrac{Nu_{lam} \cdot 0.5 + Nu_{turb} \cdot 1.5}{2}$$

# **5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP**

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the Nusselt number and explain its physical significance in convective heat transfer. How is it related to the Biot number?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nusselt number** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer at a fluid–solid interface:

$$Nu = \dfrac{h L}{k} = \dfrac{\text{Convection}}{\text{Conduction}}$$

- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of fluid (W/m·K)

**Physical Significance:** A high $Nu$ implies convection dominates over conduction through the fluid layer; $Nu = 1$ indicates pure conduction (no convection).

**Relation to Biot Number:** While $Bi$ uses the **solid's conductivity** ($Bi = hL/k_{solid}$), $Nu$ uses the **fluid's conductivity** ($Nu = hL/k_{fluid}$). Both quantify the relative importance of surface convection vs. internal conduction but in different media.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Explain the concept of hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths in internal flow. Why are they important in designing heat exchangers?**

**Model Answer:**

When a fluid enters a tube, the **velocity profile** develops from a uniform inlet over a **hydrodynamic entry length**:

$$L_h = 0.05 \, Re_D \, D$$

The **temperature profile** similarly develops from the inlet condition over a **thermal entry length**:

$$L_t = 0.05 \, Re_D \, Pr \, D$$

**Importance in Heat Exchanger Design:**

1. If $L < L_t$, the flow is in the *developing* region — use Sieder–Tate or Hausen correlation.
2. If $L \gg L_t$, the flow is *fully developed* — use $Nu = 3.66$ (laminar) or Dittus–Boelter (turbulent).
3. In short tubes (compact heat exchangers), the entry region contributes significantly to the total heat transfer and cannot be neglected.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

### **Question Choice 1 (A) — Internal Flow Correlations** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Water enters a 50 mm diameter tube at 30 °C with a velocity of 0.5 m/s. The tube wall is maintained at 90 °C. Calculate the heat transfer coefficient using: (i) Dittus–Boelter, (ii) Sieder–Tate. Properties of water at mean bulk temperature (60 °C): $\rho = 983$ kg/m³, $\mu_b = 4.71 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m·s, $\mu_s = 3.15 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m·s, $k = 0.651$ W/m·K, $c_p = 4184$ J/kg·K, $Pr = 3.02$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of combined entry length in internal flow. Show how the average Nusselt number is computed for a tube where the flow transitions from laminar to turbulent.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Reynolds number**

$$Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu_b} = \dfrac{983 \times 0.5 \times 0.05}{4.71 \times 10^{-4}} = 52{,}177$$

> Flow is **turbulent** ($Re > 4000$).

**Step 2: Dittus–Boelter (heating → $n = 0.4$)**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times (52{,}177)^{0.8} \times (3.02)^{0.4}$$

Compute $(52{,}177)^{0.8}$:
- $\log(52{,}177) = 4.7174$
- $0.8 \times 4.7174 = 3.774$
- $10^{3.774} = 5942$

Compute $(3.02)^{0.4}$:
- $\log(3.02) = 0.4800$
- $0.4 \times 0.4800 = 0.192$
- $10^{0.192} = 1.556$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times 5942 \times 1.556 = 212.6$$

$$h_{D-B} = \dfrac{212.6 \times 0.651}{0.05} = 2768 \text{ W/m²K}$$

**Step 3: Sieder–Tate variant**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.027 \times (52{,}177)^{0.8} \times (3.02)^{1/3} \times \left(\dfrac{4.71 \times 10^{-4}}{3.15 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{0.14}$$

- $(3.02)^{1/3} = 1.444$
- $(1.495)^{0.14} = 1.0585$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.027 \times 5942 \times 1.444 \times 1.0585 = 245.0$$

$$h_{S-T} = \dfrac{245.0 \times 0.651}{0.05} = 3190 \text{ W/m²K}$$

> The Sieder–Tate value is higher because it accounts for viscosity variation near the wall.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Combined Entry Length Concept:**

A schematic showing:
- Section 1: Laminar developing flow (length $L_1$)
- Transition point at $Re = 2300$ (location $x^*$)
- Section 2: Turbulent fully developed flow (length $L_2$)

**Total tube length:** $L = L_1 + L_2$

**Average Nusselt Number (McAdams method):**

$$\overline{Nu}_{total} = \dfrac{\overline{Nu}_{lam} \cdot L_1 + \overline{Nu}_{turb} \cdot L_2}{L}$$

Where:
- $\overline{Nu}_{lam}$ is obtained from Sieder–Tate for the laminar entry
- $\overline{Nu}_{turb}$ is obtained from Dittus–Boelter or Gnielinski for the turbulent section

**Physical Reasoning:** Heat transfer is more effective in the turbulent region (higher $h$), so the local Nusselt number "jumps up" at the transition point. The McAdams formula weights both regions by their respective lengths.

**Applications:** Common in chemical reactors, nuclear fuel rod cooling channels, and refinery preheaters.

---

### **Question Choice 1 (B) — Free Convection** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A vertical plate 0.5 m tall is maintained at 100 °C in air at 20 °C. Calculate the average heat transfer coefficient using the Churchill–Chu correlation. Properties of air at film temperature (60 °C): $\nu = 1.896 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.707$, $k = 0.02808$ W/m·K, $\beta = 1/(T_f)$ in K⁻¹, where $T_f = 333$ K.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the physical mechanism of free convection on a vertical plate. Discuss the difference between laminar and turbulent boundary layers in this geometry.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute Grashof and Rayleigh numbers**

$$T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2 = (100 + 20)/2 = 60 \text{ °C} = 333 \text{ K}$$

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{T_f} = \dfrac{1}{333} = 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$Gr_L = \dfrac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times (0.5)^3}{(1.896 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Gr_L = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 0.125}{3.595 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Gr_L = \dfrac{0.2946}{3.595 \times 10^{-10}} = 8.196 \times 10^{8}$$

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 8.196 \times 10^8 \times 0.707 = 5.795 \times 10^8$$

**Step 2: Churchill–Chu correlation**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

Compute $Ra_L^{1/6}$:
- $\log(5.795 \times 10^8) = 8.763$
- $8.763 / 6 = 1.460$
- $Ra_L^{1/6} = 10^{1.460} = 28.84$

Compute denominator:
- $(0.492/0.707)^{9/16} = (0.6959)^{0.5625}$
- $\log(0.6959) = -0.1573$
- $0.5625 \times (-0.1573) = -0.0885$
- $(0.6959)^{0.5625} = 10^{-0.0885} = 0.8167$

- $[1 + 0.8167]^{8/27} = (1.8167)^{0.2963}$
- $\log(1.8167) = 0.2593$
- $0.2963 \times 0.2593 = 0.0768$
- $(1.8167)^{0.2963} = 10^{0.0768} = 1.195$

So:
$$\dfrac{0.387 \times 28.84}{1.195} = \dfrac{11.16}{1.195} = 9.341$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = (0.825 + 9.341)^2 = (10.166)^2 = 103.4$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer coefficient**

$$h = \dfrac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \dfrac{103.4 \times 0.02808}{0.5} = 5.808 \text{ W/m²K}$$

> **Answer: $h \approx 5.81$ W/m²K**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Free Convection Mechanism on a Vertical Plate:**

A schematic is essential. The fluid in contact with the hot plate is heated, becomes less dense, and rises due to buoyancy. This upward flow creates a boundary layer that grows with height. A cooler fluid from the surroundings flows in to replace the rising warm fluid.

**Laminar vs. Turbulent Boundary Layer:**

- **Laminar** ($Gr < 10^9$): The flow rises in smooth, parallel layers. Velocity and temperature gradients are smooth, and $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$.
- **Transitional** ($Gr \approx 10^9$): Onset of irregular fluctuations.
- **Turbulent** ($Gr > 10^9$): Chaotic eddies dominate; $\delta \propto x^{1/5}$. A thin **viscous sublayer** persists next to the wall, where most of the temperature drop occurs.

> The transition Grashof number is approximately $Gr \sim 10^9$ for vertical plates.

---

### **Question Choice 2 (A) — Dimensional Analysis** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** By Buckingham's Pi theorem, derive the dimensionless groups for forced convection heat transfer in a tube. State the final functional form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylinder of 50 mm diameter is exposed to air at 30 °C. The surface is maintained at 200 °C. Estimate the heat loss per unit length using the Churchill–Chu correlation. Properties of air at film temperature (115 °C = 388 K): $k = 0.0337$ W/m·K, $\nu = 2.59 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.69$, $\beta = 1/388$ K⁻¹.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

(Refer to Section 3.1.2 for full derivation. Summary answer:)

$$h = f(V, D, \rho, \mu, k, c_p)$$

- $n = 7$ variables, $m = 4$ dimensions, hence $n - m = 3$ Pi groups.
- Repeating variables: $D, V, k, \mu$ (no temperature singularity).
- Three Pi groups obtained:
  - $\Pi_1 = hD/k = Nu$ (Nusselt)
  - $\Pi_2 = c_p \mu / k = Pr$ (Prandtl)
  - $\Pi_3 = \rho V D / \mu = Re$ (Reynolds)

**Final functional form:**

$$\boxed{Nu = \phi(Re, Pr)}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Gr and Ra**

$$T_f = (30 + 200)/2 = 115 \text{ °C} = 388 \text{ K}$$

$$\beta = 1/388 = 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$Gr_D = \dfrac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) D^3}{\nu^2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 \times (0.05)^3}{(2.59 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Gr_D = \dfrac{9.81 \times 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{6.708 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Gr_D = \dfrac{5.373 \times 10^{-4}}{6.708 \times 10^{-10}} = 8.01 \times 10^5$$

$$Ra_D = Gr_D \cdot Pr = 8.01 \times 10^5 \times 0.69 = 5.527 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2: Churchill–Chu (horizontal cylinder)**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = \left\{ 0.60 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra_D^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.559/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

Compute $Ra_D^{1/6}$:
- $\log(5.527 \times 10^5) = 5.7427$
- $5.7427/6 = 0.9571$
- $Ra_D^{1/6} = 10^{0.9571} = 9.057$

Compute $(0.559/0.69)^{9/16}$:
- $(0.8101)^{0.5625}$
- $\log(0.8101) = -0.0915$
- $0.5625 \times (-0.0915) = -0.0515$
- $= 10^{-0.0515} = 0.8885$

- $[1 + 0.8885]^{8/27} = (1.8885)^{0.2963}$
- $\log(1.8885) = 0.2760$
- $0.2963 \times 0.2760 = 0.0818$
- $= 10^{0.0818} = 1.208$

$$\dfrac{0.387 \times 9.057}{1.208} = 2.901$$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = (0.60 + 2.901)^2 = (3.501)^2 = 12.26$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer coefficient**

$$h = \dfrac{12.26 \times 0.0337}{0.05} = 8.26 \text{ W/m²K}$$

**Step 4: Heat loss per unit length**

$$q' = h \cdot (\pi D) \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 8.26 \times \pi \times 0.05 \times 170$$

$$q' = 220.5 \text{ W/m}$$

> **Answer: $q' \approx 220.5$ W per metre length**

---

### **Question Choice 2 (B) — Bank of Tubes & Flow Over Vertical Plates** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Engine oil at 60 °C flows across a bank of 6 rows of 8 tubes (inline) with transverse pitch $S_T = 30$ mm, longitudinal pitch $S_L = 35$ mm, and outer tube diameter $D_o = 20$ mm. The approach velocity is 0.8 m/s. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient using the Zukauskas correlation. Properties of oil at 60 °C: $\rho = 870$ kg/m³, $c_p = 2130$ J/kg·K, $\mu = 0.08$ kg/m·s, $k = 0.137$ W/m·K, $Pr = 1240$. Wall temperature correction: $Pr_s = 1100$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at 27 °C flows over a vertical plate 1 m long at 5 m/s. The plate is maintained at 127 °C. Calculate the average heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transfer rate from the plate. Properties of air at film temperature (77 °C = 350 K): $k = 0.030$ W/m·K, $Pr = 0.7$, $\nu = 2.09 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $c_p = 1009$ J/kg·K, $\rho = 0.998$ kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Maximum velocity in the bank**

$$V_{max} = V \cdot \dfrac{S_T}{S_T - D_o} = 0.8 \times \dfrac{0.030}{0.030 - 0.020} = 0.8 \times 3 = 2.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Reynolds number**

$$Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V_{max} D_o}{\mu} = \dfrac{870 \times 2.4 \times 0.02}{0.08} = 522$$

> Range: $100 \le Re \le 1000$ (inline, $C = 0.80$, $m = 0.5$).

**Step 3: Zukauskas correlation**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.80 \times (522)^{0.5} \times (1240)^{0.36} \times (1240/1100)^{0.25}$$

- $(522)^{0.5} = 22.85$
- $(1240)^{0.36}$: $\log(1240) = 3.0934$; $0.36 \times 3.0934 = 1.114$; $10^{1.114} = 12.99$
- $(1.127)^{0.25}$: $\log(1.127) = 0.0520$; $0.25 \times 0.0520 = 0.013$; $10^{0.013} = 1.030$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.80 \times 22.85 \times 12.99 \times 1.030 = 244.6$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer coefficient**

$$h = \dfrac{244.6 \times 0.137}{0.020} = 1675 \text{ W/m²K}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Reynolds number at $L$**

$$Re_L = \dfrac{V L}{\nu} = \dfrac{5 \times 1}{2.09 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.39 \times 10^5$$

> Since $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer is **entirely laminar**.

**Step 2: Average Nusselt number (laminar)**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \times (2.39 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times (0.7)^{1/3}$$

- $(2.39 \times 10^5)^{0.5} = 489$
- $(0.7)^{1/3} = 0.8879$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \times 489 \times 0.8879 = 288.3$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer coefficient**

$$h = \dfrac{288.3 \times 0.030}{1.0} = 8.65 \text{ W/m²K}$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer rate (per unit width, $W = 1$ m assumed)**

$$q' = h \cdot L \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 8.65 \times 1.0 \times (127 - 27) = 865 \text{ W/m}$$

> **Answer: $h \approx 8.65$ W/m²K, $q' \approx 865$ W/m**

---

## **5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Deducting Mistakes in Convection Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong characteristic length:** Use $D$ for cylinders, $L$ for plates, $D_o$ for tube banks. Mixing them up can produce 20–30% errors.
> 2. **Using bulk properties at the wrong temperature:** In free convection, use **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$. In forced convection, use **bulk mean temperature** $T_m$. Confusion here leads to **incorrect $Pr$ and $Gr$ values**.
> 3. **Forgetting the property correction factor** $(\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ in Sieder–Tate: this is a *signature* term and KTU expects it.
> 4. **Using $V$ instead of $V_{max}$** in tube-bank problems: examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **Wrong exponent $n$ in Dittus–Boelter:** Use $n = 0.4$ for heating, $n = 0.3$ for cooling. A small mistake here costs full marks.

---

## **5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — High-Density Revision Checklist**

### **A. Internal Flow (Tubes)**

- Critical $Re_D = 2300$ (laminar–turbulent transition).
- Fully developed laminar: $Nu = 3.66$ ($T_s$ constant) or $Nu = 4.36$ ($q''$ constant).
- Dittus–Boelter: $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ (heating).
- Gnielinski is **more accurate** and bridges the laminar-turbulent range.
- Sieder–Tate: includes property correction $(\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$.
- Hydrodynamic entry length $L_h = 0.05 ReD$, thermal $L_t = 0.05 Re Pr D$.

### **B. Flow Over Bank of Tubes**

- $V_{max}$ based on minimum flow area, *not* approach velocity.
- Zukauskas: $\overline{Nu} = C Re^{m} Pr^{0.36} (Pr/Pr_s)^{0.25}$.
- Staggered > Inline for heat transfer.
- Inline: $V_{max} = V \cdot S_T / (S_T - D_o)$.
- Staggered: $V_{max} = V \cdot S_T / (2(S_D - D_o))$ if applicable.

### **C. Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham Pi)**

- Forced convection: $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$ — always 2 Pi groups.
- Free convection: $Nu = f(Gr, Pr)$ or $f(Ra, Pr)$ — 2 Pi groups.
- Reynolds analogy: $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \approx C_f/2$.

### **D. Free Convection**

- $Gr$ = buoyancy/viscous; $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$.
- Vertical plate laminar: $Nu = 0.59 Ra^{1/4}$ (simplified).
- Vertical plate turbulent: $Nu = 0.10 Ra^{1/3}$.
- Churchill–Chu: universal correlation (vertical plates and horizontal cylinders).
- Spheres: $Nu = 2 + 0.43 Ra^{1/4}$ (conduction limit = 2).
- Transition: $Gr \sim 10^9$ (vertical plates), $Ra \sim 10^7$ (horizontal plates).

### **E. Flow Over Vertical Plates (Forced Convection)**

- Local: $Nu_x = 0.332 Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ (laminar); $0.0292 Re_x^{0.8} Pr^{1/3}$ (turbulent).
- Average: $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 Re_L^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ (laminar).
- Transition at $Re_x = 5 \times 10^5$.
- Mixed: $\overline{Nu}_L = (0.037 Re_L^{0.8} - 870) Pr^{1/3}$.

### **F. Engineering Numbers to Memorize**

| **Quantity** | **Value/Range** |
|---|---|
| $Re_{crit}$ for tube | 2300 |
| $Re_x$ for plate transition | $5 \times 10^5$ |
| $Gr$ for plate transition | $10^9$ |
| $Nu$ for fully developed laminar | 3.66 or 4.36 |
| $Nu$ for sphere (no convection) | 2 |
| Dittus–Boelter $n$ (heating) | 0.4 |
| Dittus–Boelter $n$ (cooling) | 0.3 |
| Zukauskas exponent on $Pr$ | 0.36 |

### **G. Property-Selection Rules**

- Forced convection: $T_m$ (bulk mean).
- Free convection: $T_f$ (film temperature).
- Sieder–Tate: $\mu_b$ at $T_m$, $\mu_s$ at $T_s$.

### **H. Energy Equation (Final Note)**

All correlations in this module are essentially closed-form solutions of the **boundary-layer energy equation**:

$$\rho c_p \left( u \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y} \right) = k \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

solved under various boundary conditions and flow assumptions.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PCAGT503 Heat and Mass Transfer**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — NEP 2020 Aligned — B.Tech Examination Standard*

topics.title: Horizontal Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Horizontal Plate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Convection over a Horizontal Plate:** Free (natural) convection heat transfer from a flat horizontal surface where the buoyancy-driven fluid motion develops parallel to the plate. The plate may transfer heat either through its **upper surface** (heated from below) or its **lower surface** (heated from above), each governed by distinctly different Nusselt–Rayleigh correlations.

The configuration forms part of the **free convection from plates** category in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus, sitting alongside vertical plates, inclined surfaces, and cylinders. It is the **physically simplest geometry** that exhibits the directional asymmetry of buoyancy — making it a high-yield topic for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Two Distinct Physical Orientations

| Orientation | Geometry | Flow Direction vs. Buoyancy |
|-------------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Upper Surface** of a heated plate | Hot fluid rises above the plate | Buoyancy aids flow → **Unstable / Enhanced** |
| **Lower Surface** of a heated plate | Hot fluid trapped below the plate | Buoyancy opposes flow → **Stable / Suppressed** |
| Upper Surface of a cooled plate | Cold fluid sinks above the plate | Stable |
| Lower Surface of a cooled plate | Cold fluid below the plate | Unstable / Enhanced |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Principle:** "Hot-facing-up" is physically equivalent to "Cold-facing-down" because both generate a destabilising density gradient that assists the boundary layer.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Plate vs. Cold Floor" Intuition

Imagine placing a **hot sizzler plate** on a dining table:
- The **top surface** (hot facing up): hot air rises violently — you feel the warmth rising toward your face within seconds. This is the **destabilised / unstable** case.
- The **underside** of the same plate (hot facing down against the table): heat transfer is sluggish because hot air is "trapped" — there is no buoyancy to push it away.

If you instead place an **ice slab** face-down on the table, the cold air wants to fall away from the plate — so the underside acts exactly like the hot-top surface. This 1:1 mapping between hot-top and cold-bottom is the **Galileo-style symmetry** that gives the correlations their factor-of-2 difference.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

- **Gravity** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Film (Mean) Temperature** $T_f = \dfrac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$ — used to evaluate all fluid properties
- **Volumetric Thermal Expansion Coefficient** $\beta \approx \dfrac{1}{T_f}\ \text{K}^{-1}$ for ideal gases
- **Characteristic Length for Horizontal Plates** $L_c = \dfrac{A_s}{P}$ — ratio of plate area to wetted perimeter (analogous to hydraulic diameter for non-circular ducts)
- **Critical Rayleigh Number** $\text{Ra}_{cr} \approx 2 \times 10^7$ — separates laminar from turbulent regimes

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physics — Why Two Different Correlations?

When a horizontal plate is heated from below, the fluid in immediate contact becomes lighter and is **buoyantly driven upward**, replacing it with denser cold fluid from the surroundings. The boundary layer grows **radially outward** from the leading edges, accelerating as it moves across the plate (similar to an axisymmetric stagnation flow).

When heated from above, the heated fluid is **already above** the cold fluid — a stable density stratification. The boundary layer, if it forms at all, grows slowly and remains attached to the underside. Heat transfer here is essentially **conductive through a quasi-stagnant layer**, hence roughly **half** the value of the destabilised case.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Establish the driving potential:**
$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate fluid properties at the film temperature** $T_f$ to capture the average buoyancy state of the boundary layer.

**Step 3 — Compute the Grashof Number** (ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces):
$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{g\,\beta\,\Delta T\, L_c^{\,3}}{\nu^2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Prandtl Number to obtain Rayleigh Number** (ratio of buoyancy to diffusion):
$$\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \text{Pr} = \frac{g\,\beta\,\Delta T\, L_c^{\,3}\, \text{Pr}}{\nu^2}$$

**Step 5 — Choose regime based on Ra:**
- $\text{Ra}_L < 10^5$ → conduction dominates (use $h \approx k/L_c$)
- $10^5 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 2 \times 10^7$ → **laminar convection**
- $\text{Ra}_L > 2 \times 10^7$ → **turbulent convection**

**Step 6 — Apply the appropriate correlation** for orientation and regime.

**Step 7 — Back-calculate h and Q:**
$$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L_c} \quad ; \quad Q = h\, A_s\, \Delta T$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every equation** you need for horizontal-plate convection. Memorise this — KTU examiners frequently combine 2–3 of these in a single 14-mark problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Film temperature | $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | K | Property evaluation point |
| 2 | Thermal expansion (ideal gas) | $\beta = 1/T_f$ | K⁻¹ | Valid for perfect gases |
| 3 | Characteristic length | $L_c = A_s / P$ | m | Area / Wetted perimeter |
| 4 | Grashof number | $\text{Gr}_L = g\beta\Delta T L_c^{\,3}/\nu^2$ | dimensionless | Buoyancy vs. viscous |
| 5 | Rayleigh number | $\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \text{Pr}$ | dimensionless | Buoyancy vs. diffusivity |
| 6 | Nusselt number | $\text{Nu}_L = h L_c / k$ | dimensionless | Convection vs. conduction |
| 7 | Upper surface, laminar | $\text{Nu}_L = 0.54\,\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4}$ | — | $10^5 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 2\times 10^7$ |
| 8 | Upper surface, turbulent | $\text{Nu}_L = 0.15\,\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/3}$ | — | $2\times 10^7 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 3\times 10^{10}$ |
| 9 | Lower surface, laminar | $\text{Nu}_L = 0.27\,\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4}$ | — | $3\times 10^5 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 3\times 10^{10}$ |
| 10 | Convective coefficient | $h = \text{Nu}_L \cdot k / L_c$ | W/m²·K | From Nu correlation |
| 11 | Total heat transfer | $Q = h\, A_s\, \Delta T$ | W | Final answer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficient Ratio Insight:** Notice the 0.54 / 0.27 = **2.0** in the laminar regime and 0.15 / 0.07 ≈ **2.14** in the turbulent regime. This near 2:1 ratio is the mathematical fingerprint of the orientation asymmetry — a favourite **conceptual short-answer** in KTU exams.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Electronic chip cooling:** Horizontal PCB-mounted heat sinks (hot facing up).
- **Building physics:** Heat loss from ceilings (lower surface of slab, hot facing down).
- **Solar thermal collectors:** Absorber plate (upper surface hot facing up).
- **Refrigeration:** Cold ceiling panels (cold facing down → enhanced convection).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Characteristic Length for a Rectangular Plate

For a rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b$ lying horizontally:

$$L_c = \frac{A_s}{P} = \frac{a \cdot b}{2(a+b)}$$

For a **square plate** of side $L$:

$$L_c = \frac{L \cdot L}{2(L+L)} = \frac{L^2}{4L} = \frac{L}{4}$$

For a **circular disk** of diameter $D$:

$$L_c = \frac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\pi D} = \frac{D}{4}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A square horizontal plate of dimensions $0.5\,\text{m} \times 0.5\,\text{m}$ is maintained at a uniform temperature of $80^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is exposed to quiescent air at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$. Estimate the rate of heat transfer from the **upper surface** of the plate.

**Properties of air at film temperature** ($T_f$ to be evaluated):
- $k = 0.029\,\text{W/m·K}$
- $\nu = 19.21 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\text{Pr} = 0.687$
- $\beta = 1/T_f\ \text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Film Temperature**

$$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} = \frac{80 + 20}{2} = 50^{\circ}\text{C} = 323\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal Expansion Coefficient**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{323} = 3.096 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic Length** (square plate, upper surface)

$$L_c = \frac{L}{4} = \frac{0.5}{4} = 0.125\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Temperature Difference**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 80 - 20 = 60\,\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Grashof Number**

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{g\,\beta\,\Delta T\, L_c^{\,3}}{\nu^2}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L_c^{\,3} = 9.81 \times (3.096 \times 10^{-3}) \times 60 \times (0.125)^3$$

$$(0.125)^3 = 1.953 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$

$$9.81 \times 3.096 \times 10^{-3} = 0.03037$$

$$0.03037 \times 60 = 1.822$$

$$1.822 \times 1.953 \times 10^{-3} = 3.559 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator evaluation:

$$\nu^2 = (19.21 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.690 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{3.559 \times 10^{-3}}{3.690 \times 10^{-10}} = 9.646 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 6 — Rayleigh Number**

$$\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \text{Pr} = 9.646 \times 10^{6} \times 0.687 = 6.627 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 7 — Regime Check**

$$10^5 \leq \text{Ra}_L = 6.627 \times 10^6 \leq 2 \times 10^7$$

Regime is **LAMINAR** ✓

**Step 8 — Apply Upper-Surface Laminar Correlation**

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.54\, \text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4}$$

Compute $\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4}$:

$$\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4} = (6.627 \times 10^6)^{0.25} = (6.627)^{0.25} \times (10^6)^{0.25}$$

$$(6.627)^{0.25} \approx 1.604 \quad ; \quad (10^6)^{0.25} = 10^{1.5} = 31.623$$

$$\text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4} = 1.604 \times 31.623 = 50.72$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.54 \times 50.72 = 27.39$$

**Step 9 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient**

$$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L_c} = \frac{27.39 \times 0.029}{0.125} = \frac{0.7943}{0.125} = 6.354\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 10 — Total Heat Transfer Rate**

$$Q = h \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T = 6.354 \times (0.5 \times 0.5) \times 60$$

$$Q = 6.354 \times 0.25 \times 60 = 95.31\,\text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $Q_{\text{upper}} \approx 95.3\,\text{W}$ — Heat transfer from the upper surface.

**Step 11 — Bonus: Lower Surface Calculation**

If the question also asked for the lower surface (same regime since Ra > 10⁵):

$$\text{Nu}_{L,\text{lower}} = 0.27\, \text{Ra}_L^{\,1/4} = 0.27 \times 50.72 = 13.69$$

$$h_{\text{lower}} = \frac{13.69 \times 0.029}{0.125} = 3.176\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

$$Q_{\text{lower}} = 3.176 \times 0.25 \times 60 = 47.64\,\text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of 2:1 Ratio:** $Q_{\text{upper}} / Q_{\text{lower}} = 95.31 / 47.64 \approx 2.00$ ✓ — confirms the theoretical factor of two between the two orientations.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Regime Condition | Upper Surface | Lower Surface | Governing Exponent |
|------------------|---------------|---------------|--------------------|
| $\text{Ra} < 10^5$ | Conduction-dominated | Conduction-dominated | Use $k/L_c$ directly |
| $10^5 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 2\times 10^7$ | $\text{Nu} = 0.54\,\text{Ra}^{1/4}$ | $\text{Nu} = 0.27\,\text{Ra}^{1/4}$ | $n = 1/4$ (laminar) |
| $\text{Ra} > 2 \times 10^7$ | $\text{Nu} = 0.15\,\text{Ra}^{1/3}$ | $\text{Nu} = 0.07\,\text{Ra}^{1/3}$ | $n = 1/3$ (turbulent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Horizontal Plate Setup"] --> B["Identify Orientation"]
    B --> C{"Hot Surface Faces UP?"}
    C -- "Yes - Upper Hot" --> D["Buoyancy Aids Flow"]
    C -- "No - Lower Hot" --> E["Buoyancy Opposes Flow"]
    D --> F["Destabilised Layer - Nu = 0.54 Ra^0.25"]
    E --> G["Stabilised Layer - Nu = 0.27 Ra^0.25"]
    F --> H["Evaluate Ra using Lc = A_s divided by P"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Ra less than 2e7?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Laminar - exponent 0.25"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Turbulent - exponent 0.33"]
    J --> L["Compute h = Nu k divided by Lc"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Compute Q = h A_s Delta T"]
```

### 4.2 Boundary Layer Growth Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Cold Air Inflow at Edge"] --> P2["Plate Leading Edge"]
    P2 --> P3["Thermal Boundary Layer Grows"]
    P3 --> P4["Velocity Boundary Layer Follows"]
    P4 --> P5["Plume Detachment at Trailing Edge"]
    P5 --> P6["Hot Plume Rises Vertically"]
```

### 4.3 Dimensional Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0["Heat Transfer Coefficient h"] --> Q1["Depends on k nu rho c_p beta g Delta T L"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Form Buckingham Pi Groups"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Pi1 = Nu = hL divided by k"]
    Q2 --> Q4["Pi2 = Ra = g beta Delta T L cubed divided by nu alpha"]
    Q3 --> Q5["Functional Relation Nu = f of Ra"]
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6["Empirical Fit with Constants C and n"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the characteristic length used for natural convection over a horizontal plate. Why is it taken as $A_s/P$ and not simply the plate length?*

**Model Answer:**
For a horizontal plate, the characteristic length is defined as $L_c = A_s/P$, where $A_s$ is the surface area of the plate and $P$ is the wetted perimeter. This definition arises from the boundary-layer similarity solution where $L_c$ represents the effective length over which the convective flow develops. Using only the plate length fails to account for the **perimeter effect** in non-square geometries. For example, for a square plate of side $L$, $L_c = L/4$, whereas the simple plate length would give $L$, overestimating the convective coefficient by a factor of 4.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Why is the Nusselt number for natural convection from the upper surface of a heated horizontal plate approximately twice that from its lower surface?*

**Model Answer:**
The factor of two arises because buoyancy aids the flow in the upper-surface case (hot fluid rises in the direction of natural convection) while opposing it in the lower-surface case (hot fluid trapped below the plate). The destabilised flow develops a thinner, more vigorously mixed boundary layer, doubling the convective transport. Mathematically, the correlations $\text{Nu} = 0.54\,\text{Ra}^{1/4}$ (upper) and $\text{Nu} = 0.27\,\text{Ra}^{1/4}$ (lower) differ exactly by a factor of 2 in the laminar regime.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently **forget to divide plate length by 4** for the characteristic length, leading to a 4× error in h. Always confirm whether the given dimension is $L$ or $L_c$. Another common mistake is using the surface temperature instead of film temperature for property evaluation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified 2024 Scheme]**

A circular horizontal disk of diameter $D = 0.4\,\text{m}$ is heated electrically to a uniform temperature of $90^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is exposed to ambient air at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute:
**(a)** The average convective heat transfer coefficient from the upper surface of the disk. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The total heat transfer rate from both upper and lower surfaces. **(7 marks)**

**Properties of air at film temperature:** $k = 0.030\,\text{W/m·K}$, $\nu = 20.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\text{Pr} = 0.70$, $\beta = 1/T_f\ \text{K}^{-1}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Upper Surface h:**

$T_f = (90+25)/2 = 57.5^{\circ}\text{C} = 330.5\,\text{K}$
$\beta = 1/330.5 = 3.026 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$
$\Delta T = 90 - 25 = 65\,\text{K}$

For a circular disk: $L_c = D/4 = 0.4/4 = 0.1\,\text{m}$
$A_s = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (0.4)^2/4 = 0.1257\,\text{m}^2$

Grashof number:
$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.026 \times 10^{-3})(65)(0.1)^3}{(20.0 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 3.026 \times 10^{-3} \times 65 \times 10^{-3} = 1.929 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $4.0 \times 10^{-10}$

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{1.929 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.82 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \text{Pr} = 4.82 \times 10^{6} \times 0.70 = 3.374 \times 10^{6}$$

Since $10^5 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 2 \times 10^7$, regime is **laminar**.

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.54 \times (3.374 \times 10^6)^{0.25} = 0.54 \times 42.93 = 23.18$$

$$h_{\text{upper}} = \frac{23.18 \times 0.030}{0.1} = 6.95\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Part (b) — Total Q from Both Surfaces:**

For lower surface (laminar): $\text{Nu}_{L,\text{lower}} = 0.27 \times 42.93 = 11.59$

$$h_{\text{lower}} = \frac{11.59 \times 0.030}{0.1} = 3.48\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

Total effective coefficient (both surfaces acting on same plate area):

$$Q = (h_{\text{upper}} + h_{\text{lower}}) \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T$$
$$Q = (6.95 + 3.48) \times 0.1257 \times 65$$
$$Q = 10.43 \times 0.1257 \times 65 = 85.2\,\text{W}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]**

A rectangular horizontal plate of dimensions $1.0\,\text{m} \times 0.5\,\text{m}$ is maintained at $T_s = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is exposed to air at $T_\infty = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$.
**(a)** Calculate the Rayleigh number and identify the flow regime for the upper surface. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the heat transfer rate from the upper surface is measured as $250\,\text{W}$, determine whether the plate surface is clean or fouled, and estimate the equivalent fouling factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Rayleigh Number and Regime:**

$T_f = (100+30)/2 = 65^{\circ}\text{C} = 338\,\text{K}$
$\beta = 1/338 = 2.958 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$
$\Delta T = 100 - 30 = 70\,\text{K}$

Plate area: $A_s = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5\,\text{m}^2$
Perimeter: $P = 2(1.0 + 0.5) = 3.0\,\text{m}$

$$L_c = \frac{A_s}{P} = \frac{0.5}{3.0} = 0.1667\,\text{m}$$

Air properties at $T_f \approx 338\,\text{K}$: $k = 0.0305\,\text{W/m·K}$, $\nu = 21.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\text{Pr} = 0.695$

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{(9.81)(2.958 \times 10^{-3})(70)(0.1667)^3}{(21.0 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$(0.1667)^3 = 4.630 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$
Numerator: $9.81 \times 2.958 \times 10^{-3} \times 70 \times 4.630 \times 10^{-3} = 9.41 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $(21.0 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4.41 \times 10^{-10}$

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{9.41 \times 10^{-3}}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.134 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \text{Pr} = 2.134 \times 10^{7} \times 0.695 = 1.483 \times 10^{7}$$

Since $10^5 \leq \text{Ra} \leq 2 \times 10^7$, **flow is laminar** (just below transition).

$$\text{Nu}_L = 0.54 \times (1.483 \times 10^7)^{0.25} = 0.54 \times 62.07 = 33.52$$

$$h_{\text{clean}} = \frac{33.52 \times 0.0305}{0.1667} = 6.13\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Part (b) — Fouling Analysis:**

Theoretical $Q_{\text{clean}}$:
$$Q_{\text{clean}} = h_{\text{clean}} \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T = 6.13 \times 0.5 \times 70 = 214.6\,\text{W}$$

Since $Q_{\text{measured}} = 250\,\text{W} > Q_{\text{clean}} = 214.6\,\text{W}$, the measured rate exceeds the theoretical clean value. This implies the actual surface coefficient is:

$$h_{\text{actual}} = \frac{250}{0.5 \times 70} = 7.14\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When measured Q exceeds theoretical Q for a clean plate, it usually indicates property values were estimated at slightly different film temperature, or natural draft enhancement. The standard "fouling" interpretation requires $h_{\text{actual}} < h_{\text{clean}}$. If $h_{\text{actual}} > h_{\text{clean}}$, recheck property values; otherwise the surface is essentially clean with $R_f \approx 0$.

Assuming a conservative interpretation (a thin oxide layer has built up such that effective $h$ is $6.13 - \epsilon$, say $h_{\text{actual}} = 5.5\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$):

$$R_f = \frac{1}{h_{\text{actual}}} - \frac{1}{h_{\text{clean}}} = \frac{1}{5.5} - \frac{1}{6.13} = 0.1818 - 0.1631 = 0.0187\,\text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Horizontal Plate Convection**

- ⭐ **Two surfaces, two correlations**: Upper (hot-face-up) uses 0.54 (laminar) / 0.15 (turbulent); Lower uses 0.27 / 0.07. Factor ≈ 2 always.
- ⭐ **Characteristic Length**: $L_c = A_s / P$ — for square plates $L_c = L/4$; for circular disks $L_c = D/4$.
- ⭐ **Property Evaluation Point**: Always at film temperature $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$, never at $T_s$ or $T_\infty$ alone.
- ⭐ **Ideal Gas β**: $\beta = 1/T_f$ in Kelvin — this is the single most common mistake source.
- ⭐ **Rayleigh Regimes**: Laminar if $\text{Ra} \leq 2 \times 10^7$ (use $n = 1/4$); Turbulent if $\text{Ra} > 2 \times 10^7$ (use $n = 1/3$).
- ⭐ **Nusselt Definition**: $\text{Nu}_L = h L_c / k$ — make sure $L_c$ (not $L$) goes into this formula.
- ⭐ **Plate Area Convention**: Use $A_s$ for total plate surface area, not half-plate area, when computing $Q$.
- ⭐ **Symmetry Shortcut**: Hot-top = Cold-bottom, Hot-bottom = Cold-top. Memorise this for instant 3-mark answers.
- ⭐ **Typical Range for Real Plates**: Most engineering applications fall in the laminar regime ($10^5 < \text{Ra} < 10^7$); turbulent requires very large plates or high $\Delta T$.
- ⭐ **KTU 14-Mark Pattern**: Usually combines (a) regime identification + Nu calculation + h, and (b) total heat transfer / comparison between upper & lower surfaces.
- ⭐ **Boundary Condition Reminder**: These correlations assume uniform surface temperature; for uniform heat flux, slightly different constants apply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inclined Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inclined Plate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Inclined Plate in Natural Convection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> An **inclined plate** in natural convection refers to a flat plate whose surface makes an angle **θ** (theta) with the **vertical axis** (or equivalently, an angle of 90°−θ with the horizontal). When such a plate is heated (or cooled) relative to the surrounding quiescent fluid, buoyancy-induced flow develops along the plate. The component of gravity acting along the plate surface is **g·cos θ**, which drives the convective boundary layer.

In KTU's PCAGT503 (Heat and Mass Transfer) under **Module 2 — Natural Convection**, the inclined plate is a canonical geometry where students must apply **modified Grashof/Rayleigh numbers** and understand the transition between vertical-plate-like behavior and horizontal-plate-like behavior.

### Orientation Convention (KTU Board Standard)

| Orientation | Angle θ (from vertical) | Practical Case |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical wall | θ = 0° | Standard vertical plate problem |
| Slightly inclined | 0° < θ < 60° | Treated as modified vertical |
| Steeply inclined | 60° < θ < 90° | Transitions to horizontal behavior |
| Horizontal (facing up) | θ = 90° | Cold plate bottom / Hot plate top |
| Horizontal (facing down) | θ = 90° | Hot plate bottom / Cold plate top |

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Slide Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way (Intuition for first-time learners):**
> Imagine you pour honey down a wall — the honey slides straight down (vertical plate). Now tilt the wall slightly — the honey still slides, but **slower** because only a *part* of gravity pulls it down along the surface. **That part is g·cos θ.** The rest (g·sin θ) just presses the honey *into* the wall and doesn't help it slide.
> 
> Heat on a hot inclined plate behaves exactly the same way: buoyancy acts **vertically downward (or upward)**, but only the **component along the plate** (g·cos θ) drags the fluid up (or down) along the surface and creates the convective current.

### Key Physical Constants

- **g = 9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration, **constant**)
- **β** = Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (**1/K**)
- **ν** = Kinematic viscosity (**m²/s**)
- **α** = Thermal diffusivity (**m²/s**)
- **Pr** = ν/α (Prandtl number, **dimensionless**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why Inclined Plates Matter — The Physics

When a vertical plate is tilted by angle θ:

1. **Buoyancy force** still acts **purely vertical** (gravity does not tilt).
2. Only the **component g·cos θ** acts parallel to the plate — this is what drives the boundary layer flow.
3. The component **g·sin θ** acts **normal to the plate** — it merely changes the effective pressure on the surface, **not** the convective flow magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Modification Rule (Memorize This!)**
> All vertical-plate natural convection correlations (laminar AND turbulent) **can be extended to inclined plates by replacing `g` with `g·cos θ`**, **provided**:
> - The plate is **heated from below** (hot surface facing upward) OR
> - The plate is **heated from above** (cold surface facing downward) so that the flow remains **stable**.

This means replacing **Gr → Gr·cos θ** and **Ra → Ra·cos θ** in every correlation.

## KTU Formula Sheet — Inclined Plate Natural Convection

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Symbols**: L = characteristic length (plate height for inclined), Tₛ = surface temperature, T∞ = ambient temperature, ΔT = Tₛ − T∞, θ = tilt angle from **vertical**.

| # | Formula / Correlation | Range of Validity | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_{\text{eff}} = g \cos \theta$ | All angles, stable flow | Effective gravity along plate |
| 2 | $Gr_L^{\prime} = \dfrac{g \cos\theta \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Inclined plate | **Modified Grashof number** |
| 3 | $Ra_L^{\prime} = Gr_L^{\prime} \cdot Pr$ | Inclined plate | **Modified Rayleigh number** |
| 4 | $\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 \, (Ra_L^{\prime})^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$ | $0 < Pr < \infty$, all $Ra$, **LAM Churchill-Chu** | Universal vertical/inclined correlation |
| 5 | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \dfrac{0.670 \, (Ra_L^{\prime})^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$ | $Ra < 10^9$, laminar | Simpler Churchill-Chu form |
| 6 | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.14 \, (Ra_L^{\prime})^{1/3}$ | $10^4 \leq Ra \leq 10^7$, laminar | **Simplified laminar** (air, Pr ≈ 0.7) |
| 7 | $h = \dfrac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L}$ | All correlations | Heat transfer coefficient |
| 8 | $q^{\prime \prime} = h \, \Delta T$ | All | Heat flux |
| 9 | $q = h \, A \, \Delta T$ | All | Total heat transfer |
| 10 | $Ra_{\text{crit}} \approx 10^9$ | Air, vertical/inclined | **Laminar → Turbulent transition** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Stability Caveat (Board-Favorite Question!)**
> 
> | Condition | Stable? | What to use? |
> |---|---|---|
> | Hot plate, tilted so surface faces **upward** (toward cold sky) | ✅ **Stable** | Use modified Ra = Ra·cos θ |
> | Cold plate, tilted so surface faces **downward** | ✅ **Stable** | Use modified Ra = Ra·cos θ |
> | Hot plate, tilted so surface faces **downward** | ❌ **Unstable** (boundary layer separates, rolls form) | Use horizontal plate **lower surface** correlation |
> | Cold plate, tilted so surface faces **upward** | ❌ **Unstable** | Use horizontal plate **upper surface** correlation |

---

## Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Solar flat-plate collectors** — tilted at angle equal to latitude for optimum sun-tracking.
- **Building façades and slanted roofs** — heat gain/loss through tilted walls.
- **Heat exchangers** with angled fin surfaces.
- **Condenser tubes and evaporator plates** in refrigeration (often inclined for condensate drainage).
- **Electronic equipment** tilted for cooling inside cabinets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Modified Grashof Number for an Inclined Plate

### Starting Point (Vertical Plate Recap)

The vertical plate Grashof number is:

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

This is dimensionless and represents the **ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Driving Force on an Inclined Plate

On a vertical plate, **full gravity g** drives the flow. On a plate tilted by θ from the vertical, only the component of gravity **parallel to the plate** does the work.

The component of gravity acting **along the plate surface** is:

$$g_{\parallel} = g \cos \theta$$

**Reasoning:** Resolve the gravity vector (pointing down) into two components — one parallel and one perpendicular to the inclined plate. Geometry of a triangle: cos(θ) = adjacent/hypotenuse = g_parallel/g.

### Step 2 — Substitute g_parallel into Grashof Number

Replace g in the Grashof number expression with g·cos θ:

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = \frac{(g \cos\theta) \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Compact Form

Factor the original Grashof number out:

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = Gr_L \cdot \cos\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Modified Grashof Number**
> 
> $$\boxed{\,Gr_L^{\prime} = Gr_L \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{g \cos\theta \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Modified Rayleigh Number

By definition $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$, therefore:

$$Ra_L^{\prime} = Gr_L^{\prime} \cdot Pr = Ra_L \cdot \cos\theta$$

---

## Derivation 2 — Mean Nusselt Number Using Simplified Laminar Correlation

For **air (Pr ≈ 0.7)** in the laminar regime, the standard vertical-plate correlation is:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, (Ra_L)^{1/4}$$

For an inclined plate under **stable** orientation, we replace $Ra_L$ with $Ra_L \cdot \cos\theta$:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, (Ra_L \cos\theta)^{1/4}$$

### Worked Step-by-Step Expansion

Using the property $(ab)^{1/4} = a^{1/4} \cdot b^{1/4}$:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, (Ra_L)^{1/4} \cdot (\cos\theta)^{1/4}$$

This makes it explicit that **tilting the plate always reduces Nu** (since $\cos\theta \leq 1$), meaning **less heat is transferred** as the plate becomes more horizontal. This is intuitive — buoyancy loses effectiveness as the plate tilts away from vertical.

---

## Analytical Worked Example (Standard KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
> A vertical plate of height **L = 0.5 m** and width **W = 1 m** is at surface temperature **Tₛ = 80 °C** in air at **T∞ = 20 °C**. The plate is tilted to **θ = 45°** from the vertical. Determine the heat transfer rate.
> 
> Properties of air at film temperature T_f = (80+20)/2 = 50 °C:
> - k = 0.02735 W/m·K
> - ν = 1.798 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
> - Pr = 0.7228
> - β = 1/T_f (ideal gas) = 1/323.15 K⁻¹

### Step 1 — Compute ΔT and β

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 80 - 20 = 60 \, \text{K}$$

$$\beta = \frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{323.15} = 3.0945 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Standard Vertical-Plate Grashof Number

$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.0945 \times 10^{-3})(60)(0.5)^3}{(1.798 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

**Numerator:**

$$(9.81)(3.0945 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.03036$$

$$(0.03036)(60) = 1.8217$$

$$(1.8217)(0.125) = 0.22771$$

**Denominator:**

$$(1.798 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.2328 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Division:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.22771}{3.2328 \times 10^{-10}} = 7.044 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Inclination Correction

$$\cos 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$$

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = Gr_L \cdot \cos\theta = 7.044 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7071 = 4.981 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Modified Rayleigh Number

$$Ra_L^{\prime} = Gr_L^{\prime} \cdot Pr = 4.981 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7228 = 3.600 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Flow Regime

Since $Ra_L^{\prime} = 3.6 \times 10^{8} < 10^9$, the flow is **laminar**. ✅

### Step 6 — Compute Mean Nusselt Number (Churchill-Chu Simplified Form)

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \, (Ra_L^{\prime})^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

**Compute (Ra')^(1/4):**

$$(3.6 \times 10^8)^{0.25} = (3.6)^{0.25} \times (10^8)^{0.25} = 1.3801 \times 10^2 = 138.01$$

**Compute bracket term:**

$$\frac{0.492}{0.7228} = 0.6807$$

$$(0.6807)^{9/16} = (0.6807)^{0.5625}$$

**Compute (0.6807)^0.5625 step-wise:** ln(0.6807) = −0.3848 → × 0.5625 = −0.2165 → e^(−0.2165) = 0.8053

$$1 + 0.8053 = 1.8053$$

**Compute (1.8053)^(4/9):** ln(1.8053) = 0.5909 → × 4/9 = 0.2626 → e^(0.2626) = 1.3003

**Compute numerator:**

$$0.670 \times 138.01 = 92.467$$

**Compute fraction:**

$$\frac{92.467}{1.3003} = 71.11$$

**Final Nu:**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.68 + 71.11 = 71.79$$

### Step 7 — Compute Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$h = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{71.79 \times 0.02735}{0.5} = \frac{1.9635}{0.5} = 3.927 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

### Step 8 — Compute Heat Transfer Rate

Area of plate: A = L × W = 0.5 × 1 = 0.5 m²

$$q = h \, A \, \Delta T = 3.927 \times 0.5 \times 60 = 117.8 \, \text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The total heat transferred from the inclined plate is **q ≈ 117.8 W**.
>
> For comparison, if the plate were truly vertical (θ = 0°), the heat transfer would be higher because cos θ = 1 and Ra' = Ra.

---

## Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Correlation To Use?

| Regime | $Ra_L^{\prime}$ Range | Inclination Stable? | Recommended Correlation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar, all Pr | $10^{-1}$ to $10^7$ | Yes | $\overline{Nu} = 0.555(Ra')^{1/4}$ |
| Laminar, all Pr | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{12}$ | Yes | **Churchill-Chu (full)** |
| Turbulent | $Ra' > 10^9$ | Yes | $\overline{Nu} = 0.13(Ra')^{1/3}$ |
| Hot plate facing down (unstable) | Any | No | Horizontal **lower** surface correlation |
| Cold plate facing up (unstable) | Any | No | Horizontal **upper** surface correlation |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Inclined Plate Geometry & Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inclined Plate Geometry] --> B[Plate tilted by angle theta from vertical]
    B --> C[Length L measured along plate]
    B --> D[Width W into the page]
    A --> E[Buoyancy Force Resolution]
    E --> F["g_parallel = g cos theta (drives flow along plate)"]
    E --> G["g_perp = g sin theta (presses fluid into plate, no flow)"]
    A --> H[Boundary Layer Formation]
    H --> I[Hydrodynamic boundary layer delta]
    H --> J[Thermal boundary layer delta_t]
    A --> K[Flow Stability Check]
    K --> L{Hot surface faces up OR Cold surface faces down}
    L -->|YES| M[Stable flow, use modified Ra = Ra cos theta]
    L -->|NO| N[Unstable, use horizontal plate correlation]
```

## Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of the Inclined Plate Heat Transfer Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Given: L, W, Ts, Tinf, theta, fluid]) --> STEP1[Step 1: Compute film temperature Tf]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Step 2: Look up fluid properties k, nu, Pr, beta]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["Step 3: Compute standard Ra = g beta dT L cubed / nu squared times Pr"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Step 4: Apply inclination correction Ra prime = Ra cos theta]
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Step 5: Check stability of boundary layer]
    STEP5 --> CHECK1{Stable orientation?}
    CHECK1 -->|Yes, stable| CORR1["Apply Churchill-Chu correlation with Ra prime"]
    CHECK1 -->|No, unstable| CORR2[Use horizontal plate upper/lower correlation]
    CORR1 --> STEP6[Step 6: Compute mean Nu]
    CORR2 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> STEP7["Step 7: Compute h = Nu k / L"]
    STEP7 --> STEP8["Step 8: Compute q = h A dT"]
    STEP8 --> RESULT([Heat Transfer Rate q in watts])
```

## Diagram 3 — Comparison: Vertical vs Inclined vs Horizontal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vertical Plate theta = 0 deg] -->|Tilt by theta| I[Inclined Plate 0 less than theta less than 90 deg]
    I -->|Maximum tilt| H[Horizontal Plate theta = 90 deg]
    V --> NOTE1[Buoyancy fully effective]
    I --> NOTE2[Buoyancy reduced by factor cos theta]
    H --> NOTE3[Boundary layer thickness grows differently]
    NOTE1 --> FORM1["Ra effective = Ra for vertical"]
    NOTE2 --> FORM2["Ra effective = Ra cos theta (stable case)"]
    NOTE3 --> FORM3["Use horizontal plate Nu correlation"]
```
