# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define modified Grashof number for natural convection on an inclined plate and explain why the modification is needed.**

**Model Answer:**
The modified Grashof number for an inclined plate is defined as:

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = \frac{g \cos\theta \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2} = Gr_L \cdot \cos\theta$$

where θ is the tilt angle from the vertical. The modification is needed because, when the plate is tilted, only the **component of gravity parallel to the plate surface** (which is g·cos θ) effectively drives the buoyant boundary layer flow. The perpendicular component (g·sin θ) merely presses the fluid against the surface and does not contribute to convective flow along the plate. Hence, replacing g with g·cos θ correctly captures the reduced buoyancy driving force as the plate deviates from vertical. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State the stability condition for applying the modified Rayleigh number Ra·cos θ to an inclined plate.**

**Model Answer:**
The modified Rayleigh number Ra·cos θ is valid **only when the boundary layer flow is stable**, i.e., when there is **no flow separation**. This occurs in two situations:
1. The plate surface is **heated** and tilted so the hot surface **faces upward** (boundary layer rises smoothly along the plate).
2. The plate surface is **cooled** and tilted so the cold surface **faces downward** (boundary layer descends smoothly along the plate).

If a hot surface faces downward (or a cold surface faces upward), the boundary layer becomes **unstable**, leading to plume formation and flow separation. In such cases, horizontal-plate correlations must be used instead of Ra·cos θ. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the modified Grashof number and modified Rayleigh number for natural convection on an inclined plate. Discuss the validity of these expressions with respect to the orientation of the heated/cooled surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smooth vertical plate of height **0.6 m** and width **0.8 m** is maintained at **70 °C** and exposed to atmospheric air at **20 °C**. If the plate is tilted by **30°** from the vertical (hot surface facing upward), estimate:
- (i) The mean heat transfer coefficient using the simplified Churchill-Chu correlation.
- (ii) The total heat transfer rate.

Take air properties at T_f = 45 °C: k = 0.02699 W/m·K, ν = 1.749 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr = 0.7262, β = 1/318.15 K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a vertical plate, the Grashof number is the ratio of buoyancy to viscous force:

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** When the plate is tilted by angle θ from the vertical, gravity vector **g** (acting vertically downward) is resolved into:
- Component along the plate: $g_\parallel = g \cos\theta$
- Component normal to the plate: $g_\perp = g \sin\theta$

Only $g_\parallel$ drives the flow along the plate. Therefore, g is replaced by g·cos θ in the Grashof number.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Modified Grashof number:

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = \frac{g \cos\theta \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2} = Gr_L \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Modified Rayleigh number (since Ra = Gr·Pr):

$$Ra_L^{\prime} = Ra_L \cos\theta$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Validity:
- ✅ Valid when **hot plate faces upward** or **cold plate faces downward** (stable buoyancy-driven flow along the plate).
- ❌ Invalid when **hot plate faces downward** or **cold plate faces upward** — boundary layer separates into plumes; horizontal-plate correlations must be used.
- Angle limit: Most correlations are reliable for θ ≤ 60° from vertical; beyond that, transition to horizontal-plate behavior dominates.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- L = 0.6 m, W = 0.8 m, A = 0.48 m²
- Tₛ = 70 °C, T∞ = 20 °C, ΔT = 50 K
- θ = 30°, cos 30° = 0.8660
- k = 0.02699, ν = 1.749 × 10⁻⁵, Pr = 0.7262
- β = 1/318.15 = 3.143 × 10⁻³ K⁻¹

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute standard Grashof number:

$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.143 \times 10^{-3})(50)(0.6)^3}{(1.749 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator: 9.81 × 3.143×10⁻³ × 50 × 0.216 = 9.81 × 0.03395 = 0.3330

Denominator: (1.749×10⁻⁵)² = 3.059×10⁻¹⁰

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.3330}{3.059 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.0886 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply inclination correction:

$$Gr_L^{\prime} = 1.0886 \times 10^{9} \times 0.8660 = 9.428 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Modified Rayleigh:

$$Ra_L^{\prime} = 9.428 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7262 = 6.847 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Check regime: $Ra' = 6.85 \times 10^8 < 10^9$ → **laminar** ✅

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Apply simplified Churchill-Chu:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 (Ra_L')^{1/4}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{4/9}}$$

- (Ra')^(1/4) = (6.847×10⁸)^0.25 = 162.05
- 0.492/0.7262 = 0.6775
- (0.6775)^(9/16) = (0.6775)^0.5625 ≈ 0.8029
- 1 + 0.8029 = 1.8029
- (1.8029)^(4/9) ≈ 1.2993
- Numerator: 0.670 × 162.05 = 108.57
- Fraction: 108.57 / 1.2993 = 83.56
- **Nu = 0.68 + 83.56 = 84.24**

**Step 6 (0.5 mark):** Mean heat transfer coefficient:

$$h = \frac{\overline{Nu} \cdot k}{L} = \frac{84.24 \times 0.02699}{0.6} = 3.789 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 7 (0.5 mark):** Total heat transfer:

$$q = h \, A \, \Delta T = 3.789 \times 0.48 \times 50 = 90.94 \, \text{W}$$

> **Final Answer:** **h ≈ 3.79 W/m²K** and **q ≈ 90.94 W**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the physical mechanism of natural convection on an inclined plate. Why does the effective driving force become g·cos θ? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hot inclined plate at **100 °C** is placed in air at **25 °C**. The plate has dimensions **0.4 m × 0.4 m**, tilted at **60°** from the vertical (hot surface facing up). Using the correlation $\overline{Nu} = 0.14 (Ra')^{1/3}$, compute:
- (i) Modified Rayleigh number Ra′
- (ii) Mean heat transfer coefficient h
- (iii) Heat transfer rate q

Air properties at T_f = 62.5 °C: k = 0.02808 W/m·K, ν = 1.855 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr = 0.7250, β = 1/335.65 K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Physical Mechanism (4 marks):**
- A hot inclined plate heats the adjacent fluid. The heated fluid becomes less dense and rises due to buoyancy.
- The fluid rises **vertically** under gravity, but because the plate is inclined, only the component of this buoyant motion **along the plate** contributes to the boundary layer flow.
- A thermal boundary layer and hydrodynamic boundary layer develop along the plate, with thickness growing from the leading edge.
- The plate length **L is measured along the surface**, not vertically.

**Why g·cos θ? (3 marks):**
- The gravitational acceleration g acts vertically downward.
- On an inclined plate, the velocity of the buoyant fluid has a component along the plate surface equal to g·cos θ (the projection of g onto the plate's tangent direction).
- The perpendicular component g·sin θ only contributes to static pressure variations across the boundary layer thickness and does not drive flow.
- Hence, the effective driving acceleration for the boundary layer equations is g·cos θ, leading to the modified Grashof/Rayleigh numbers.

---

### Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** L = 0.4 m, A = 0.16 m², Tₛ = 100 °C, T∞ = 25 °C, ΔT = 75 K, θ = 60°, cos 60° = 0.5

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute Gr and Ra′:

$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(1/335.65)(75)(0.4)^3}{(1.855 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator: 9.81 × 2.979×10⁻³ × 75 × 0.064 = 9.81 × 0.01430 = 0.1403

Denominator: (1.855×10⁻⁵)² = 3.441×10⁻¹⁰

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.1403}{3.441 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.077 \times 10^{8}$$

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 4.077 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7250 = 2.956 \times 10^{8}$$

$$Ra_L^{\prime} = Ra_L \cos\theta = 2.956 \times 10^{8} \times 0.5 = 1.478 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute mean Nu using $\overline{Nu} = 0.14(Ra')^{1/3}$:

$$\overline{Nu} = 0.14 \times (1.478 \times 10^{8})^{1/3}$$

Compute cube root: (1.478)^(1/3) ≈ 1.1395; (10⁸)^(1/3) = 10^(2.6667) = 464.16

$$\overline{Nu} = 0.14 \times 1.1395 \times 464.16 = 74.04$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute h:

$$h = \frac{\overline{Nu} \cdot k}{L} = \frac{74.04 \times 0.02808}{0.4} = \frac{2.079}{0.4} = 5.197 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute q:

$$q = h \, A \, \Delta T = 5.197 \times 0.16 \times 75 = 62.36 \, \text{W}$$

> **Final Answer:** Ra′ = 1.478 × 10⁸; **h ≈ 5.20 W/m²K**; **q ≈ 62.36 W**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the cos θ factor** — Using the vertical-plate Grashof/Rayleigh number for an inclined plate loses 2 marks immediately. **Always check if θ is given.**
> 
> 2. **Wrong characteristic length** — On an inclined plate, the characteristic length is the **plate height measured along the surface**, NOT the vertical projection. If the plate's actual length is 0.5 m, use L = 0.5 m, not L·cos θ.
> 
> 3. **Wrong stability check** — Using Ra·cos θ for a **hot surface facing downward** is a critical error. The flow becomes unstable and you must use the horizontal-plate lower-surface correlation. Examiners specifically test this case.
> 
> 4. **Property temperature** — Air properties must be evaluated at the **film temperature** T_f = (Tₛ + T∞)/2, NOT at Tₛ or T∞.
> 
> 5. **Wrong Pr exponent in Churchill-Chu** — The (0.492/Pr) is raised to the power **9/16**, then the (1 + ...) term is raised to **8/27** (full form) or **4/9** (simplified form). Don't confuse these.
> 
> 6. **Sign of ΔT** — Use absolute value $\vert \Delta T \vert$ — heat transfer rate is always positive. **Do NOT use the `|` pipe inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` in LaTeX instead.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Inclined plates in natural convection: only the **component g·cos θ** drives the boundary layer.
- ✅ Modified Rayleigh number: **Ra′ = Ra·cos θ**.
- ✅ Valid only for **stable orientation** (hot up / cold down).
- ✅ Unstable cases → use **horizontal-plate correlations**.
- ✅ Characteristic length L is **along the plate surface**, not vertical projection.
- ✅ Flow regime: **Laminar** if Ra′ < 10⁹; **Turbulent** if Ra′ > 10⁹.
- ✅ Preferred correlation: **Churchill-Chu universal** (valid for all Ra and Pr).
- ✅ Simplified form for air laminar: $\overline{Nu} = 0.14(Ra')^{1/3}$ (alt: 0.555·Ra'^(1/4)).
- ✅ Always evaluate air properties at **film temperature** T_f = (Tₛ + T∞)/2.
- ✅ Tilting reduces heat transfer — Nu drops as θ increases from 0° to 90°.
- ✅ Engineering relevance: **solar collectors, building façades, condenser plates, slanted electronic enclosures**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CYLINDERS AND SPHERES: 1-DIMENSIONAL RADIAL HEAT CONDUCTION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In steady-state, one-dimensional radial heat conduction through curved geometries, the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the heat flow direction is **not constant** (unlike the planar wall case). For a **hollow cylinder** and a **hollow sphere**, the heat flow area varies as a function of the radial coordinate $r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (1-D Radial Conduction in Curved Solids):**
> *Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction in a hollow cylinder (or sphere) is the process in which heat flows radially outward (or inward) through the curved wall, and the temperature at any point within the solid depends only on the radial distance $r$ from the axis (or centre). There is no angular ($\theta$) or axial ($z$) variation, and the rate of heat transfer $Q$ is constant at every radial cross-section.*

**Geometry Conventions Used Throughout This Module:**

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $r_1$ | Inner radius | m |
| $r_2$ | Outer radius | m |
| $L$ | Length of cylinder (axial) | m |
| $T_1$ | Temperature at inner surface | K |
| $T_2$ | Temperature at outer surface | K |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity (assumed constant) | W/(m·K) |
| $Q$ | Steady-state heat transfer rate | W |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Onion-Layer Intuition"**
>
> Imagine peeling an onion. Each layer you peel away is a thin shell whose surface area is **bigger** than the layer just inside it. When heat flows outward through a hollow cylinder or sphere, it must pass through a series of these "concentric shells," each one wider than the last.
>
> Because the same quantity of heat $Q$ must cross every shell (steady state, no heat generation), and the **area grows** as you move outward, the **temperature gradient must shrink** as you move outward. This is why curved surfaces have a **logarithmic** temperature distribution, unlike flat walls which are linear.

**Why does this matter in engineering?**

Steam pipes, boiler tubes, reactor vessels, cryogenic storage tanks, pressure vessels, nuclear fuel rods, electrical cable insulation, and even the human body (radial blood flow) are all governed by cylindrical and spherical heat conduction. KTU examiners love this topic because it is the foundation of insulation design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference State (Assumed Throughout KTU Problems):**
> - Steady state: $\partial T / \partial t = 0$
> - One-dimensional radial flow: $T = T(r)$ only
> - Constant thermal conductivity: $k \neq f(T)$
> - No internal heat generation: $\dot{q}_{gen} = 0$
> - Constant surface temperatures: $T(r_1) = T_1$ and $T(r_2) = T_2$

---

## 1.3 Why the Standard Planar Wall Formula Fails for Curved Surfaces

For a flat wall of thickness $L$:

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

For a curved surface, $A = 2\pi r L$ (cylinder) or $A = 4\pi r^2$ (sphere) — both functions of $r$. You cannot simply plug in an "average" area because the **gradient itself is non-linear**, making the average-area approximation **inaccurate** (it under-predicts heat loss by up to 20–30% for large $r_2/r_1$ ratios).

This is why a **logarithmic mean area** is the correct mathematical tool.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Governing Equation — Laplace's Equation in Radial Coordinates

The general heat equation in cylindrical coordinates, with the assumptions listed above, reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$

In spherical coordinates, it reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are second-order ordinary differential equations (ODEs) in a single variable $r$. Two integrations, with two boundary conditions, yield the full temperature distribution and heat flow rate.

---

## 2.2 Hollow Cylinder — Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. Start with Fourier's Law at radius $r$:
$$Q = -kA(r)\frac{dT}{dr} = -k(2\pi r L)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

2. Rearrange to separate variables:
$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

3. Integrate from $r_1$ to $r$ (and $T_1$ to $T$):
$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T)$$

4. Apply the outer boundary $T(r_2) = T_2$ to get the heat flow:
$$\boxed{Q_{cyl} = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

5. The temperature distribution is logarithmic:
$$T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Thermal Resistance Form (Electrical Analogy):**
$$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} \quad \text{[K/W]}$$

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{cyl}}$$

---

## 2.3 Hollow Sphere — Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. Fourier's Law at radius $r$:
$$Q = -k(4\pi r^2)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

2. Separate variables:
$$Q \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \, dT$$

3. Integrate from $r_1$ to $r$:
$$Q\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}\right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T)$$

4. Apply the outer boundary $T(r_2) = T_2$:
$$\boxed{Q_{sph} = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)} = \frac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{(r_2 - r_1)}}$$

5. Temperature distribution:
$$T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\frac{(1/r_1 - 1/r)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$$

**Thermal Resistance Form:**
$$R_{sph} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Geometry | Heat Flow $Q$ | Thermal Resistance $R_{cond}$ | Temperature Distribution $T(r)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plane Wall** (thickness $L$, area $A$) | $\frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $\dfrac{L}{kA}$ | $T(x) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\dfrac{x}{L}$ |
| 2 | **Hollow Cylinder** (length $L$, radii $r_1, r_2$) | $\dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\dfrac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ |
| 3 | **Hollow Sphere** (radii $r_1, r_2$) | $\dfrac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$ | $\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$ | $T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\dfrac{(1/r_1 - 1/r)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ |
| 4 | **Composite Cylinder** (n layers) | $\dfrac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)/k_i}$ | $\sum \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$ | Piecewise log across each layer |
| 5 | **Composite Sphere** (n layers) | $\dfrac{4\pi (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum (1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})/k_i}$ | $\sum \dfrac{(1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})}{4\pi k_i}$ | Piecewise in $1/r$ across each layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Mean Radius Note:** In composite problems, students often confuse the "equivalent radius" for series resistances. The rule of thumb is to **add the resistances** as if the layers are in series — never average radii.

---

## 2.5 Log Mean Area (LMA) — An Approximation, Not the Standard

> [!NOTE]
> The **Log Mean Area** $A_{lm}$ is an engineering approximation that lets you use the flat-wall formula $Q = kA_{lm}\Delta T/L$ for curved surfaces. It is **never asked as the primary derivation** in KTU but is a common distractor in MCQs.

$$A_{lm} = \frac{A_2 - A_1}{\ln(A_2/A_1)}$$

For a cylinder: $A_{lm} = 2\pi L (r_2 - r_1)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$ (which is exact, since the area scales linearly with $r$).
For a sphere: $A_{lm} = 4\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)/\ln(r_2^2/r_1^2)$ — this is an **approximation** because the sphere formula uses $1/r$ in the integral, not $\ln(r)$.

**Accuracy comparison at $r_2/r_1 = 2$:** Sphere LMA is ~6% lower than the exact value. For KTU board exams, use the **exact** form, not the LMA.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Cylinder Application | Sphere Application |
|---|---|---|
| Power Plants | Steam pipe lagging, boiler tubes | Boiler drum ends, ash hopper domes |
| Cryogenics | LNG transfer lines | Spherical LNG storage tanks |
| Chemical | Reactor cooling jackets | Catalyst pellet heat removal |
| Electrical | Buried power cable insulation | Transformer oil tanks |
| Biomedical | Drug eluting stent analysis | Tissue sphere during cryopreservation |
| HVAC | Chilled water pipes | Pressurised hot water storage tanks |

> [!TIP]
> Whenever a KTU problem mentions a "pipe," "tube," or "reactor" with insulation, your first reflex should be the **hollow cylinder** formula. Whenever it mentions a "tank," "sphere," or "vessel," reach for the **hollow sphere** formula.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Conduction Through a Hollow Cylinder

We consider a long hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, thermal conductivity $k$ (constant), with inner surface temperature $T_1$ and outer surface temperature $T_2$. Steady state, no heat generation, pure radial flow.

**Step 1 — Write Fourier's Law in the radial direction at an arbitrary radius $r$:**

$$Q = -k A(r) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

The cross-sectional area perpendicular to the heat flow at radius $r$ is the lateral area of a cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$A(r) = 2\pi r L$$

Substituting:

$$Q = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to separate variables ($T$ and $r$):**

$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides. Lower limits: $r = r_1$, $T = T_1$. Upper limits: $r = r$, $T = T(r)$:**

$$\int_{r_1}^{r} Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T(r)} dT$$

The left side integrates to $Q \ln(r/r_1)$ and the right side to $-2\pi k L [T(r) - T_1]$:

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi k L [T(r) - T_1] = 2\pi k L [T_1 - T(r)]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q$ using the outer boundary $T(r_2) = T_2$:**

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the temperature distribution $T(r)$ in the cylinder wall:**

From Step 3, solving for $T(r)$:

$$T_1 - T(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)$$

Dividing by the analogous equation for $r_2$:

$$\frac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

$$\boxed{T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2) \frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 6 — Check limiting case (thin wall, $r_2 \to r_1 = r$, thickness $\delta = r_2 - r_1 \ll r$):**

$$\ln(r_2/r_1) = \ln\left(1 + \frac{\delta}{r}\right) \approx \frac{\delta}{r}$$

Substituting:

$$Q \approx \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\delta/r} = \frac{2\pi r L \, k (T_1 - T_2)}{\delta} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{\delta}$$

This reduces to the **planar wall formula** as expected. ✔

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Conduction Through a Hollow Sphere

Consider a hollow sphere of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, thermal conductivity $k$, inner surface temperature $T_1$, outer surface temperature $T_2$. Steady state, no heat generation, pure radial flow.

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law at an arbitrary radius $r$:**

The area perpendicular to radial flow is the surface of a sphere of radius $r$:

$$A(r) = 4\pi r^2$$

$$Q = -k (4\pi r^2) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$Q \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $r_1$ to $r$:**

$$\int_{r_1}^{r} Q \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \int_{T_1}^{T(r)} dT$$

Left side: $\int_{r_1}^{r} \frac{dr}{r^2} = \left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{r_1}^{r} = \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}$

$$Q \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}\right) = -4\pi k [T(r) - T_1] = 4\pi k [T_1 - T(r)]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the outer boundary $T(r_2) = T_2$:**

$$Q \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r_1 r_2$:

$$Q = \frac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}}$$

**Step 5 — Temperature distribution $T(r)$:**

$$T_1 - T(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi k} \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$

Dividing by the $r_2$ form:

$$\frac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{(1/r_1) - (1/r)}{(1/r_1) - (1/r_2)}$$

$$\boxed{T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2) \frac{(1/r_1) - (1/r)}{(1/r_1) - (1/r_2)}}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting case check (thin shell, $\delta = r_2 - r_1 \ll r_1$):**

$$\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{r_1 r_2} = \frac{\delta}{r_1^2}$$ (to first order in $\delta/r_1$)

Substituting:

$$Q \approx \frac{4\pi k r_1^2 (T_1 - T_2)}{\delta} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{\delta}$$

where $A = 4\pi r_1^2$ is the sphere surface area. Again, the planar formula is recovered. ✔

---

## 3.3 Composite (Multi-Layer) Systems — Series Resistance Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental rule (KTU 2024):** For a composite cylinder or sphere with $n$ concentric layers, each layer contributes a thermal resistance **in series**. The total heat flow is:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

This is **identical** to the electrical resistance analogy $I = V/R_{total}$ for resistors in series.

### 3.3.1 Two-Layer Hollow Cylinder Derivation (General Method)

Consider a cylinder with an inner layer (radii $r_1$ to $r_2$, conductivity $k_1$) and an outer layer (radii $r_2$ to $r_3$, conductivity $k_2$). The interface temperature is $T_2$, outer surface is $T_3$.

**Heat flow through layer 1:**
$$Q_1 = \frac{2\pi k_1 L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**Heat flow through layer 2:**
$$Q_2 = \frac{2\pi k_2 L (T_2 - T_3)}{\ln(r_3/r_2)}$$

**Steady state, no generation:** $Q_1 = Q_2 = Q$

**Equating and solving for $Q$:**

$$Q \left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L}\right] = (T_1 - T_3)$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_3)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2}}}$$

The interface temperature $T_2$ (often the unknown the problem asks for) is found by back-substitution:

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU problems frequently ask for the **interface temperature** in composite problems. Always compute $Q$ first, then use it to find $T_2$.

### 3.3.2 Two-Layer Hollow Sphere (Analogous Result)

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_3)}{\dfrac{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}{k_1} + \dfrac{(1/r_2 - 1/r_3)}{k_2}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Critical Radius of Insulation (Most Important KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **counter-intuitive** result that KTU examiners test almost every semester. Adding insulation to a small cylinder or sphere can **increase** the heat loss, not decrease it. The point at which the trend reverses is called the **critical radius of insulation**.

### 3.4.1 Physical Origin (Why Adding Insulation Can Increase $Q$)

For a bare cylinder at temperature $T_s$ exposed to convection at $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$, the total resistance is just the convective one (or combined with conduction if there is also internal resistance). The total resistance is **not monotonic** with outer radius $r_2$ — it first **decreases**, reaches a minimum, then increases.

### 3.4.2 Mathematical Derivation for a Cylinder

Consider a bare cylinder of outer radius $r_1$ (bare) at $T_s$ in a fluid at $T_\infty$, convective coefficient $h$. Now add insulation to outer radius $r_2$ with conductivity $k_{ins}$.

**Total resistance from inner surface to fluid:**

$$R_{tot} = \underbrace{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L}}_{\text{conduction}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)}}_{\text{convection}}$$

**Heat flow:**

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h}}$$

**To find the critical radius, set $dQ/dr_2 = 0$** (or equivalently $dR_{tot}/dr_2 = 0$):

$$\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} L r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi h L r_2^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{ins} r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{r_{cr,\,cyl} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h}}$$

**For a sphere** (by an identical procedure):

$$\boxed{r_{cr,\,sph} = \frac{2 k_{ins}}{h}}$$

### 3.4.3 Engineering Interpretation

| Condition | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $r_2 < r_{cr}$ | Adding insulation **increases** heat loss (resistance decreases) |
| $r_2 = r_{cr}$ | Heat loss is at a **maximum** |
| $r_2 > r_{cr}$ | Adding insulation **decreases** heat loss (resistance increases) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Examiner Note):** Students often write $r_{cr} = k/h$ for both cylinder and sphere. The correct factor is **1** for cylinder and **2** for sphere. Forgetting the factor of 2 will cost 2 marks outright.

### 3.4.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A bare steam pipe of outer radius 25 mm at 200°C is insulated with a material of $k = 0.05$ W/(m·K). The ambient air is at 30°C with $h = 10$ W/(m²·K). Find the critical radius and discuss whether 50 mm thick insulation is sufficient.

**Solution:**

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h} = \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

The bare pipe radius (25 mm) is already much greater than $r_{cr}$ (5 mm). Therefore, **adding any insulation will decrease the heat loss**, as we are on the right side of the maximum.

With 50 mm of insulation, $r_2 = 25 + 50 = 75$ mm $\gg r_{cr}$, so heat loss is reduced. ✔

---

## 3.5 Heat Flow With Convection Boundary Conditions

When surface temperatures are **not** given, but the **fluid** temperatures and convection coefficients are, the problem is solved by writing the total resistance (conduction + convection) and applying:

$$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,inside} - T_{\infty,outside}}{R_{cond} + R_{conv,inside} + R_{conv,outside}}$$

For a pipe with inside convection $h_i$ and outside convection $h_o$, both fluids at $T_{\infty,i}$ and $T_{\infty,o}$:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o})}{\dfrac{1}{h_i r_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

This is the **most general** KTU-style formula for a single-layer pipe.

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Functional Block Diagram — Heat Flow Path in a Composite Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Fluid T_infty_i] --> B[Inner Convective Resistance R_conv_i = 1 by h_i times 2 pi r1 L]
    B --> C[Inner Pipe Wall Conduction R_cond_1 = ln r2 over r1 by 2 pi k1 L]
    C --> D[Insulation Layer Conduction R_cond_2 = ln r3 over r2 by 2 pi kins L]
    D --> E[Outer Surface Temperature T_s_o]
    E --> F[Outer Convective Resistance R_conv_o = 1 by h_o times 2 pi r3 L]
    F --> G[Cold Fluid T_infty_o]
    G --> H[Total Heat Transfer Rate Q equals Delta T over R_total]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a thermal resistance. Heat flows from hot fluid to cold fluid, encountering five resistances in series. The temperature drops across each block are computed using $Q \times R_i$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Critical Radius Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Bare Cylinder or Sphere with h and k_ins] --> P2[Compute r_cr equals k over h for cylinder, or 2k over h for sphere]
    P2 --> P3{Is r2 less than r_cr?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Adding insulation INCREASES Q. Maximum heat loss at r_cr. Avoid this range.]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Adding insulation DECREASES Q. Insulation is effective.]
    P4 --> P6[Recommendation: Use r2 greater than r_cr by large margin]
    P5 --> P7[Recommendation: Standard insulation design applies]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Composite Sphere Heat Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Inner Hot Fluid T_hot] --> S2[Inner Convection R1 equals 1 by 4 pi r1 squared h_i]
    S2 --> S3[Layer 1 Conduction R2 equals 1 over r1 minus 1 over r2 by 4 pi k1]
    S3 --> S4[Interface Temperature T2]
    S4 --> S5[Layer 2 Conduction R3 equals 1 over r2 minus 1 over r3 by 4 pi k2]
    S5 --> S6[Outer Surface T3]
    S6 --> S7[Outer Convection R4 equals 1 by 4 pi r3 squared h_o]
    S7 --> S8[Cold Fluid T_cold]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Q equals T_hot minus T_cold over sum of all R]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note the difference between cylinder and sphere block diagrams:** For a sphere, the resistance expressions have a $(1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})$ term in the numerator, whereas for a cylinder, the conduction term has a $\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)$ in the numerator. This is the **single most important distinction** the KTU examiner tests.

---

## 4.4 Schematic-Style Mermaid — Temperature Profile in a Composite Cylinder

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["T1 (Hot Inner Surface)"] --- R1["R1: ln r2 over r1 by 2 pi k1 L"]
    R1 --- T2["T2 (Interface)"]
    T2 --- R2["R2: ln r3 over r2 by 2 pi k2 L"]
    R2 --- T3["T3 (Cold Outer Surface)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a composite system, the **temperature drop is largest across the layer with the largest thermal resistance**. The plot of $T$ vs. $\ln(r)$ is **piecewise linear**, with the slope in each layer inversely proportional to $k_i$.
