# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State and explain Fourier's law of heat conduction. Why is the heat flow rate constant at every radial cross-section in steady-state conduction through a hollow cylinder?**

**Model Answer:**

Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow by conduction through a uniform medium is proportional to the area perpendicular to the heat flow and to the temperature gradient in the direction of flow:

$$Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where the negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature.

In steady-state conduction through a hollow cylinder with no internal heat generation, the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to any cylindrical shell between $r$ and $r + dr$ requires that the net rate of heat in minus the net rate of heat out equals zero (no energy accumulation). Therefore, $Q(r_1) = Q(r_2) = Q$ at every cross-section.

(3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the thermal resistance of a hollow cylinder and a hollow sphere. Why is the cylinder formula logarithmic while the sphere formula is algebraic?**

**Model Answer:**

For a cylinder: $R_{cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$

For a sphere: $R_{sph} = \dfrac{(1/r_1) - (1/r_2)}{4\pi k} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$

The cylinder has a **logarithmic** expression because its conduction area scales **linearly** with $r$ ($A = 2\pi r L$), and integrating $dr/r$ produces a logarithm.

The sphere has an **algebraic** expression because its area scales with $r^2$ ($A = 4\pi r^2$), and integrating $dr/r^2$ produces a $1/r$ term, not a logarithm.

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A steam pipe of outer diameter 80 mm is covered with 40 mm thick insulation. The thermal conductivity of the insulation is $k = 0.08$ W/(m·K). The inner surface of the pipe is at 200°C and the outer surface of the insulation is at 40°C. Calculate:

(i) The steady-state heat loss per metre length of pipe. (4 marks)

(ii) The temperature at the interface between the pipe and the insulation (i.e., at the pipe outer surface — verify the given boundary). (3 marks)

**(b)** A hollow sphere of inner radius 50 mm and outer radius 100 mm has a thermal conductivity that varies with temperature as $k = k_0(1 + \alpha T)$ where $k_0 = 0.5$ W/(m·K) and $\alpha = 0.002$ K⁻¹. If the inner surface is at 500 K and the outer surface at 300 K, estimate the heat flow rate using the arithmetic mean of boundary temperatures for $k$. Comment on the accuracy. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a)(i):** Heat loss per metre length.

Given: $r_1 = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m (pipe outer radius), $r_2 = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m (insulation outer radius), $T_1 = 200$°C, $T_2 = 40$°C, $k = 0.08$ W/(m·K), $L = 1$ m.

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{2\pi (0.08)(1)(200 - 40)}{\ln(0.08/0.04)}$$

$$\ln(0.08/0.04) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi (0.08)(160)}{0.6931} = \frac{80.4248}{0.6931} = 116.04 \text{ W per metre}$$

**Part (a)(ii):** The inner surface of insulation is at the pipe outer surface, which is given as $T_1 = 200$°C. This is the boundary temperature. There is no "interface" inside the insulation in this single-layer case. The student is expected to recognise that for a single-layer cylinder, the temperature at any radius is given by:

$$T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2) \frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

At $r = r_1$ (pipe-insulation interface): $T = T_1 = 200$°C. At $r = r_2$ (outer insulation surface): $T = T_2 = 40$°C. ✔

**Part (b):** Variable conductivity in a sphere.

With variable $k$, the heat flow equation is:

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_2)}{\int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r^2 k(r)}}$$

With $k = k_0(1 + \alpha T)$, and using the mean of boundary temperatures to estimate $T_{mean} = (500 + 300)/2 = 400$ K:

$$k_{mean} = 0.5 (1 + 0.002 \times 400) = 0.5 (1.8) = 0.9 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

Treating $k$ as constant at 0.9 W/(m·K):

$$Q = \frac{4\pi k_{mean} r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$$

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (0.9)(0.05)(0.10)(500 - 300)}{0.10 - 0.05}$$

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (0.9)(0.05)(0.10)(200)}{0.05}$$

$$Q = 4\pi (0.9)(0.10)(200) = 4\pi (18) = 72\pi \approx 226.2 \text{ W}$$

**Comment on accuracy:** The mean-conductivity approximation is generally accurate to within 2–4% when $\alpha \Delta T$ is small. Here $\alpha (T_1 - T_2) = 0.002 \times 200 = 0.4$, which is a 40% variation, so the approximation is **moderately accurate** but not highly precise. For exactness, the integral $\int dr / (r^2 k_0(1 + \alpha T))$ must be evaluated, requiring the actual $T(r)$ profile.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** A furnace wall is constructed of 200 mm of firebrick ($k = 1.4$ W/(m·K)) lined on the inner side with 100 mm of insulating brick ($k = 0.21$ W/(m·K)). The inside of the furnace is at 1100 K and the outside surface is at 350 K. Treating the wall as a flat composite slab:

(i) Calculate the heat loss per unit area. (3 marks)

(ii) Calculate the interface temperature between the two bricks. (4 marks)

**(b)** For the same furnace, after extended use, the inner surface becomes coated with a thin layer of ash (assumed negligible thermal resistance). A cylindrical thermocouple probe of diameter 3 mm, with $k_{tc} = 18$ W/(m·K), is inserted radially into the wall to measure the interface temperature. If the probe is exposed to the same conditions on both sides as the wall, derive an expression for the **error** in temperature measurement due to the finite conductivity of the probe. What condition minimises this error? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a)(i):** Heat loss per unit area for composite flat wall.

$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2}} = \frac{1100 - 350}{\dfrac{0.2}{1.4} + \dfrac{0.1}{0.21}}$$

$$\frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.2}{1.4} = 0.1429 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$\frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.21} = 0.4762 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$q = \frac{750}{0.1429 + 0.4762} = \frac{750}{0.6191} = 1211.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Part (a)(ii):** Interface temperature $T_2$ between the two bricks.

$$T_2 = T_1 - q \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 1100 - 1211.4 \times 0.1429 = 1100 - 173.1 = 926.9 \text{ K}$$

Alternatively, $T_2 = T_3 + q \cdot (L_2/k_2) = 350 + 1211.4 \times 0.4762 = 350 + 576.9 = 926.9$ K. ✔

**Part (b):** Thermocouple error analysis (this is a conceptual derivation question, not in the strict "cylinders and spheres" main formulas, but related).

**Derivation outline:**

A thermocouple wire of finite conductivity acts as a "fin" conducting heat along its length. The local temperature it senses is determined by the balance between heat conducted along the wire and heat exchanged with the surrounding wall. The exact solution involves solving the fin equation for a wire of radius $r_w$ with perimeter $P$ and cross-section $A$:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{k_{tc} A}(T - T_{wall}(x)) = 0$$

The condition that **minimises error** is that the thermocouple leads be **as thin as possible** (small $A$, small $P$ ratio) and have **low conductivity**, so the lead wires don't "short-circuit" the temperature field. Ideally, $k_{tc} \to 0$.

The error $\Delta T_{error}$ is approximately:

$$\Delta T_{error} \propto \frac{k_{tc} A}{hP \cdot L_{wire}}$$

Minimising $k_{tc}$ and $A$, and increasing $h$ and $L_{wire}$, minimises the error. In practice, lead wires are made of very thin constantan or chromel with $k \approx 17$ W/(m·K), and the leads are run along an isotherm for 20–50 wire diameters before being allowed to exit the wall. This ensures the error is below 0.5%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong resistance formula for sphere:** Writing $R_{sph} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(4\pi k)$ instead of $(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$. The sphere formula has **NO logarithm** — this is the most common error.
>
> 2. **Critical radius factor-of-2 error:** Writing $r_{cr} = k/h$ for both cylinder and sphere. The sphere answer is $2k/h$, not $k/h$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert radii to metres:** All units in the formula must be SI. A common mistake is using $r_1 = 50$ mm directly. The formula gives watts only when $r$ is in metres.
>
> 4. **Averaging temperatures in a $T$-dependent $k$ problem incorrectly:** Always use the arithmetic mean of boundary temperatures for the first-order correction. The rigorous method requires integration.
>
> 5. **Missing the length $L$ in the cylinder formula:** $Q$ has units of watts. Without the factor $L$ (in metres), the answer is in W/m, which is heat loss per unit length, not total $Q$.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the sign convention:** $T_1 > T_2$ for positive heat flow. The formula $Q = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$ gives $Q > 0$ only if $T_1 > T_2$. Always state the assumption explicitly.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to state "1-D, steady state, constant $k$, no generation":** Even if not asked, listing these assumptions at the start of the solution earns **1 easy mark** and shows command of the topic.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet-Point Summary:**

- [x] **Hollow cylinder** heat flow: $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ — a logarithmic expression because area $\propto r$.

- [x] **Hollow sphere** heat flow: $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$ — algebraic, **no logarithm**, because area $\propto r^2$.

- [x] **Cylindrical temperature distribution** is **logarithmic** in $r$ — the $T$ vs. $\ln(r)$ plot is a straight line.

- [x] **Spherical temperature distribution** is **algebraic** in $1/r$ — the $T$ vs. $1/r$ plot is a straight line.

- [x] **Thermal resistance** of a cylinder: $R_{cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$. **Always write the $L$ factor.**

- [x] **Thermal resistance** of a sphere: $R_{sph} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$. **No $L$ factor** (sphere is a closed body).

- [x] **Composite cylinder (n layers):** $Q = \dfrac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)/k_i}$. **Add resistances in series.**

- [x] **Composite sphere (n layers):** $Q = \dfrac{4\pi (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/r_i - 1/r_{i+1})/k_i}$.

- [x] **Critical radius of insulation (cylinder):** $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h}$. Adding insulation below this radius **increases** heat loss.

- [x] **Critical radius of insulation (sphere):** $r_{cr} = \dfrac{2k_{ins}}{h}$. **Factor of 2 is mandatory.**

- [x] **Convection boundary condition on both sides:** Total $R$ = $R_{conv,i} + R_{cond} + R_{conv,o}$. Apply Ohm's-law analogy.

- [x] **Variable $k$ case:** Use arithmetic mean of boundary temperatures, $k_{mean} = k_0(1 + \alpha T_{mean})$, for first-order approximation.

- [x] **Limiting case:** Thin walls ($\delta \ll r$) reduce to the planar formula $Q = kA\Delta T/\delta$.

- [x] **Units check:** All radii in **metres**, all temperatures in **K or °C** (differences only), $k$ in **W/(m·K)**, $L$ in **m**. Output: $Q$ in **watts**.

- [x] **Energy balance identity:** In steady state with no generation, $Q$ is **identical at every radial cross-section** — use this as a check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation: Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by a body in the form of electromagnetic waves as a consequence of its temperature alone, without requiring an intervening medium. It is the only mode of heat transfer that can occur through a perfect vacuum.

In the KTU 2024 framework, thermal radiation is treated as a **surface phenomenon** governed by the electromagnetic wave theory and quantum statistics (Planck's distribution). The relevant wavelength band for engineering heat transfer is the **thermal infrared region**, spanning from approximately **0.1 μm to 100 μm**, with peak emission typically occurring between 0.5 μm and 25 μm depending on the source temperature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing beside a glowing campfire. The warmth reaching your face travels through cold air without heating the air itself. There is no contact (conduction) and no fluid motion (convection) involved. Pure energy, in the form of invisible electromagnetic waves, is being projected by the burning wood and absorbed by your skin. This is thermal radiation. The same principle explains how the Sun (surface temperature ~5778 K) transfers heat to Earth across 150 million kilometers of vacuum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Unlike conduction and convection, radiation does **not** require a medium. The energy transfer speed equals the speed of light, $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$ in vacuum.

### 1.3 Fundamental Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | SI Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Emissive Power | $E$ | Total radiation energy leaving a surface per unit area per unit time, integrated over all directions and wavelengths | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Irradiation | $G$ | Total radiation energy incident on a surface per unit area per unit time, integrated over all directions and wavelengths | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Radiosity | $J$ | Total radiation energy leaving a surface per unit area per unit time (includes reflected + emitted) | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Intensity (Radiance) | $I$ | Radiation energy emitted per unit projected area per unit solid angle per unit time, in a given direction | $\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{sr)}$ |
| Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the surface | Dimensionless |
| Reflectivity | $\rho$ | Fraction of incident radiation reflected by the surface | Dimensionless |
| Transmissivity | $\tau$ | Fraction of incident radiation transmitted through the body | Dimensionless |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | Ratio of emissive power of a real surface to that of a black body at the same temperature | Dimensionless |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | Distance between successive crests of the EM wave | $\mu\text{m}$ or $\text{nm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Each property can be **monochromatic** (function of wavelength $\lambda$, denoted with subscript $\lambda$) or **total** (integrated over all wavelengths). They can also be **directional** (function of direction $\theta, \phi$) or **hemispherical** (integrated over the entire hemisphere above the surface).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Energy Balance at a Surface

When radiation strikes a real surface, the incident energy is split into three competing fates: **absorption**, **reflection**, and **transmission**. The energy must be conserved, which yields the foundational identity of radiation heat transfer.

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to radiation incident on a semi-transparent or opaque element:

$$E_{\text{incident}} = E_{\text{absorbed}} + E_{\text{reflected}} + E_{\text{transmitted}}$$

Dividing throughout by $E_{\text{incident}}$ produces the dimensionless property balance:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

This is the **radiative energy conservation identity**. It is exact, valid for both monochromatic and total hemispherical quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an opaque body:** $\tau = 0$, hence the simplified identity reduces to $\alpha + \rho = 1$. Most engineering surfaces in HMT problems (metals, painted walls, oxidized steel) are treated as opaque.

### 2.2 The Concept of a Black Body

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Black Body** is an idealized surface that absorbs **all** incident radiation at every wavelength, every direction, and every temperature. Therefore, for a black body, $\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$. It is the **perfect absorber** and simultaneously the **best possible emitter** at any given temperature.

A practical realization is a small hole leading into a large, internally blackened isothermal cavity. Radiation entering the hole undergoes multiple reflections and is essentially fully absorbed before it can re-emerge.

### 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law (The Bridge between Emission and Absorption)

Consider two parallel large plates facing each other, both at temperature $T$ in a vacuum enclosure. After radiative equilibrium, no net energy exchange occurs between them, meaning each plate must emit exactly as much energy as it absorbs from the other.

This thermodynamic equilibrium argument, formalized by **Gustav Kirchhoff** in 1859, produces the cornerstone identity of radiation analysis:

$$\frac{E_1(T)}{\alpha_1(T)} = \frac{E_2(T)}{\alpha_2(T)} = \frac{E_3(T)}{\alpha_3(T)} = \cdots = E_b(T)$$

where $E_b(T)$ is the emissive power of a black body at temperature $T$. In words: **the ratio of emissive power to absorptivity is the same for all surfaces at the same temperature, and equals the emissive power of a black body.**

The natural corollary is the definition of emissivity:

$$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{E(T)}{E_b(T)} = \alpha(T)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **At thermodynamic equilibrium, emissivity equals absorptivity for every real surface.** This is why good emitters are also good absorbers (e.g., a black painted surface), and poor emitters are also poor absorbers (e.g., a polished silver mirror).

### 2.4 Planck's Distribution, Wien's Displacement & Stefan-Boltzmann

These three laws together describe the spectral and total emission of a black body.

- **Planck's Law** (spectral distribution of black body emissive power):

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1 \right]}$$

where $C_1 = 2 \pi h c^2 = 3.742 \times 10^8 \; \text{W} \cdot \mu\text{m}^4/\text{m}^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k = 1.439 \times 10^4 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$.

- **Wien's Displacement Law** (peak emission wavelength):

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** (total emissive power of a black body):

$$E_b(T) = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

### 2.5 Lambert's Cosine Law (Directional Distribution)

For a **diffuse surface** (one whose intensity is independent of direction), the emitted flux in any direction $\theta$ measured from the surface normal follows:

$$I_{\theta} = I_n \cos\theta$$

The intensity is maximum in the normal direction ($\theta = 0$) and falls to zero at grazing angles ($\theta = 90^\circ$). The result is that the total emissive power of a diffuse emitter becomes:

$$E = \pi I_n$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Relation / Law | Mathematical Form | Key Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Energy balance | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | All surfaces |
| 2 | Opaque body | $\alpha + \rho = 1$ | $\tau = 0$ |
| 3 | Black body | $\alpha = 1, \; \rho = 0, \; \tau = 0$ | Ideal absorber/emitter |
| 4 | Kirchhoff's Law (total) | $E(T) / \alpha(T) = E_b(T)$ | At thermal equilibrium |
| 5 | Emissivity definition | $\varepsilon(T) = E(T) / E_b(T) = \alpha(T)$ | All real surfaces |
| 6 | Planck's Law | $E_{b\lambda} = C_1 / [\lambda^5 (\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1)]$ | Black body only |
| 7 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ | Black body peak |
| 8 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Black body total |
| 9 | Real surface | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Diffuse grey body |
| 10 | Lambert's Cosine | $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ | Diffuse emitter |
| 11 | Diffuse flux | $E = \pi I_n$ | Hemispherical integration |
| 12 | Radiosity | $J = E + \rho G$ | Opaque diffuse surface |
| 13 | Spectral limits | $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$ | Monochromatic form |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These basic radiation laws underpin the design of every thermal system that involves temperature-driven energy emission: furnace design, re-entry vehicle thermal protection, solar thermal collectors, infrared thermography, nuclear reactor shielding, LED and laser thermal management, satellite thermal control (painted as black/white patches), and fire detection sensors. The Kirchhoff identity is the conceptual foundation that allows engineers to measure absorption with an emission experiment (an *emissometer*), and to use the much simpler Stefan-Boltzmann relation to compute real-surface emission via a single $\varepsilon$ value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Conservation Identity $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$

Let a parallel beam of monochromatic radiation of intensity $G_\lambda$ strike a thin slab of material of thickness $dx$ at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Energy Influx.**
The total incident energy per unit area per unit time at wavelength $\lambda$ is $G_\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Absorbed Fraction.**
A portion of this energy is absorbed within the elemental layer. By the definition of monochromatic absorptivity, the absorbed energy is:

$$G_{\lambda, \text{abs}} = \alpha_\lambda \, G_\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Reflected Fraction.**
The portion of the incident energy that bounces back from the front face of the layer is:

$$G_{\lambda, \text{ref}} = \rho_\lambda \, G_\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Transmitted Fraction.**
The portion that emerges from the back face after passing through the entire thickness is:

$$G_{\lambda, \text{trans}} = \tau_\lambda \, G_\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Energy Balance.**
The total incident energy must equal the sum of all the fractions because energy is neither created nor destroyed at the surface:

$$G_\lambda = G_{\lambda, \text{abs}} + G_{\lambda, \text{ref}} + G_{\lambda, \text{trans}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitution.**
Insert the three component expressions:

$$G_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda G_\lambda + \rho_\lambda G_\lambda + \tau_\lambda G_\lambda$$

**Step 7 — Cancellation.**
Divide through by the common incident flux $G_\lambda$ (which is finite and non-zero for any physical beam):

$$1 = \alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda$$

**Step 8 — Total Hemispherical Form.**
Integrating over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$ and over the entire hemispherical solid angle above the surface preserves the relation:

$$\alpha(T) + \rho(T) + \tau(T) = 1$$

For an **opaque surface**, by definition $\tau = 0$, which yields the engineering short form:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> A direct consequence: a good absorber ($\alpha \to 1$) is a poor reflector ($\rho \to 0$), and vice versa. This is why polished metals (low $\alpha$, high $\rho$) make excellent radiation shields.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Kirchhoff's Law (Total Hemispherical Form)

Consider a closed evacuated enclosure of volume $V$ with internal surfaces labeled 1, 2, 3, $\ldots, n$, all maintained at the common temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition.**
Because every surface is at the same temperature, no net heat transfer can occur in the long-time limit. Therefore, the total radiation emitted by surface $i$ must equal the total radiation it absorbs from all other surfaces in the enclosure:

$$E_i(T) = \alpha_i(T) \, G_i$$

where $G_i$ is the irradiation on surface $i$ from all other surfaces.

**Step 2 — Universal Irradiation Field.**
Because the geometry and orientation are arbitrary, $G_i$ depends on position. However, in thermal equilibrium the irradiation field can be related to the emission field of a hypothetical black body filling the enclosure.

**Step 3 — Introducing the Black Body Cavity.**
A black body cavity at temperature $T$ produces, at every internal point, an irradiation equal to $E_b(T)$ (the black body emissive power). If a small test surface of absorptivity $\alpha_i$ is placed inside, it absorbs $\alpha_i E_b(T)$.

**Step 4 — Thermodynamic Reciprocity Argument.**
Reversibility of radiative equilibrium requires that surface $i$ must re-emit exactly the energy it absorbs (otherwise a temperature difference would spontaneously develop, violating the Second Law). Hence:

$$E_i(T) = \alpha_i(T) \, E_b(T)$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging.**
Divide both sides by $\alpha_i(T)$:

$$\frac{E_i(T)}{\alpha_i(T)} = E_b(T)$$

**Step 6 — Universality.**
Since surface $i$ is arbitrary, this identity holds for **every** surface in the enclosure:

$$\frac{E_1(T)}{\alpha_1(T)} = \frac{E_2(T)}{\alpha_2(T)} = \cdots = E_b(T)$$

**Step 7 — Definition of Emissivity.**
Rearranging the equality $E_i(T) = \alpha_i(T) E_b(T)$ by dividing both sides by $E_b(T)$:

$$\varepsilon_i(T) = \frac{E_i(T)}{E_b(T)} = \alpha_i(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At thermal equilibrium, the **total hemispherical emissivity** of a surface equals its **total hemispherical absorptivity**. This makes $\varepsilon$ a directly measurable quantity via a single absorptivity experiment.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of Surface Types

| Surface Type | $\alpha$ | $\rho$ | $\tau$ | $\varepsilon$ | Real Example |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Black Body | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ | Cavity hole, lampblack |
| Grey Body | $\alpha_g$ | $1 - \alpha_g$ | $0$ | $\varepsilon_g$ | Painted metal, oxidized steel |
| Specular Reflector | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 1$ | $0$ | $\approx 0$ | Polished silver, polished gold |
| Transparent Body | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $1$ | $\approx 0$ | Clean glass, dry air, vacuum |
| Selective Surface | $\lambda$-dependent | $\lambda$-dependent | $0$ | $\lambda$-dependent | Solar absorber coatings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grey Body Assumption:** In most undergraduate KTU problems, surfaces are treated as grey ($\varepsilon$ constant with $\lambda$) and diffuse (Lambert's cosine law holds), which reduces the Stefan-Boltzmann relation to the simple form $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Radiation Interaction at a Generic Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[SOURCE OF RADIATION] -->|G total incident| surfNode1
    surfNode1[REAL SURFACE ELEMENT]
    surfNode1 -->|alpha fraction absorbed| absorbed[ABSORBED ENERGY G_alpha]
    surfNode1 -->|rho fraction reflected| reflected[REFLECTED ENERGY G_rho]
    surfNode1 -->|tau fraction transmitted| transmitted[TRANSMITTED ENERGY G_tau]
    surfNode1 -->|emitted E at temperature T| emitted[EMITTED ENERGY E_epsilon sigma T4]
    absorbed --> bal[RADIATION ENERGY BALANCE]
    reflected --> bal
    transmitted --> bal
    emitted --> bal
    bal --> out[NET HEAT TRANSFER Q_dot]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Logical Structure of the Three Foundational Radiation Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[THERMAL RADIATION THEORY] --> B[PLANCKS DISTRIBUTION LAW]
    A --> C[WIENS DISPLACEMENT LAW]
    A --> D[STEFAN BOLTZMANN LAW]
    B -->|integrate over all wavelengths and solid angles| D
    B -->|locate spectral peak via derivative zero| C
    C --> E[SOLAR COLLECTOR DESIGN]
    C --> F[INFRARED THERMOGRAPHY]
    D --> G[FURNACE HEAT LOAD CALCULATION]
    D --> H[RE ENTRY VEHICLE THERMAL DESIGN]
    D --> I[RADIATION SHIELD EFFECTIVENESS]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Classification of Radiative Properties by Spectral and Directional Dependence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[RADIATIVE PROPERTIES] --> S[SPECTRAL DEPENDENCE]
    P --> DI[DIRECTIONAL DEPENDENCE]
    S --> S1[MONOCHROMATIC PROPERTY alpha_lambda, rho_lambda, epsilon_lambda]
    S --> S2[TOTAL PROPERTY alpha rho epsilon integrated over all wavelengths]
    DI --> DI1[DIRECTIONAL PROPERTY at specific theta and phi]
    DI --> DI2[HEMISPHERICAL PROPERTY integrated over 2 pi steradian hemisphere]
    S1 --> COMB1[SPECTRAL DIRECTIONAL]
    S1 --> COMB2[SPECTRAL HEMISPHERICAL]
    S2 --> COMB3[TOTAL DIRECTIONAL]
    S2 --> COMB4[TOTAL HEMISPHERICAL]
    DI1 --> COMB1
    DI2 --> COMB2
    DI1 --> COMB3
    DI2 --> COMB4
```

### 4.4 Electromagnetic Spectrum Block (Thermal Infrared Region)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    em[COMPLETE ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM] --> g[Gamma Rays less than 0.01 nm]
    em --> x[X Rays 0.01 to 10 nm]
    em --> uv[Ultraviolet 10 to 400 nm]
    em --> vis[Visible Light 400 to 700 nm]
    em --> ir[Thermal Infrared 0.7 to 1000 micrometer]
    em --> mw[Microwaves 1 mm to 1 m]
    em --> r[Radio Waves greater than 1 m]
    ir --> ir1[Near IR 0.7 to 3 micrometer]
    ir --> ir2[Mid IR 3 to 8 micrometer]
    ir --> ir3[Far IR 8 to 1000 micrometer]
    ir3 --> hmt[KTU HMT WAVELENGTH BAND 0.1 to 100 micrometer]
    ir1 --> hmt
    ir2 --> hmt
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Heat and Mass Transfer syllabus emphasizes the **thermal infrared band** because most engineering temperatures of interest (300 K to 3000 K) emit their peak radiative energy in the range of 1 μm to 10 μm, well within this band.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the following terms with units: (i) Emissive power, (ii) Irradiation, (iii) Radiosity.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Emissive Power (E):** The total rate of radiation energy emitted per unit area of a surface, integrated over all wavelengths and over the entire hemispherical solid angle above the surface. **SI unit:** $\text{W/m}^2$.

(ii) **Irradiation (G):** The total rate of radiation energy incident on a surface per unit area, integrated over all wavelengths and all directions of the incoming hemisphere. **SI unit:** $\text{W/m}^2$.

(iii) **Radiosity (J):** The total rate of radiation energy leaving a surface per unit area. It includes **both** the emitted component and the reflected component of the incident radiation. For an opaque, diffuse, grey surface, $J = E + \rho G = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + (1 - \varepsilon) G$. **SI unit:** $\text{W/m}^2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and explain Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation. Mention its significance in engineering.*

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of the total emissive power of a surface to its total absorptivity is the same for all surfaces at the same temperature, and equals the emissive power of a black body at that temperature:

$$\frac{E(T)}{\alpha(T)} = E_b(T)$$

**Explanation:** This law establishes the equivalence between a surface's ability to emit radiation and its ability to absorb radiation. It follows directly from the Second Law of Thermodynamics applied to a closed isothermal cavity.

**Significance:** It allows direct measurement of emissivity by measuring absorptivity. It also permits the use of the simple Stefan-Boltzmann relation $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ for any real surface, using the emissivity value. Industries rely on this to characterize materials for furnace lining, solar absorbers, and thermal shields.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a) Define absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity. Derive the relationship between them for a general surface. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of total incident radiation that is absorbed by a body. $\alpha = G_{\text{abs}} / G$
- **Reflectivity ($\rho$):** The fraction of total incident radiation that is reflected from the front face. $\rho = G_{\text{ref}} / G$
- **Transmissivity ($\tau$):** The fraction of total incident radiation that is transmitted through the body. $\tau = G_{\text{trans}} / G$

**Derivation:**

Let $G$ be the total incident radiation on a body. Energy conservation requires:

$$G = G_{\text{abs}} + G_{\text{ref}} + G_{\text{trans}}$$

Substituting the definitions of the three properties:

$$G = \alpha G + \rho G + \tau G$$

Dividing by $G$:

$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

This is the **radiation energy balance identity**. For an **opaque surface**, $\tau = 0$, giving $\alpha + \rho = 1$.

---

**(b) What is a black body? Explain Planck's distribution law and derive Wien's displacement law from it. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Black Body Definition:** A black body is an idealized surface that absorbs all incident radiation at every wavelength, direction, and temperature, i.e., $\alpha = 1, \rho = 0, \tau = 0$. It is the best possible emitter at any temperature. A practical realization is the small aperture of a large, blackened isothermal cavity.

**Planck's Distribution Law:**

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1 \right]}$$

where $C_1 = 3.742 \times 10^8 \; \text{W} \cdot \mu\text{m}^4/\text{m}^2$ and $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^4 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$.

**Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law:**

The wavelength of peak emission $\lambda_{\max}$ is found by setting the partial derivative of $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $\lambda$ to zero:

$$\frac{\partial E_{b\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$$

Let $x = C_2 / (\lambda_{\max} T)$. The differentiation gives the transcendental equation:

$$5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$$

The numerical solution is $x = 4.9651$. Therefore:

$$\frac{C_2}{\lambda_{\max} T} = 4.9651$$

$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^4}{4.9651} = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$

This is **Wien's Displacement Law**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) What is meant by intensity of radiation? State and explain Lambert's cosine law. Derive the relation $E = \pi I_n$ for a diffuse emitter. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition of Intensity (Radiance):** The intensity of radiation $I$ in a given direction is defined as the rate of radiation energy emitted per unit area **projected normal to the direction** per unit solid angle.

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dA_p \, d\omega \, dt} = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega \, dt}$$

where $dA_p = dA \cos\theta$ is the projected area, $\theta$ is the angle from the surface normal, and $d\omega$ is the differential solid angle.

**Lambert's Cosine Law:** A **diffuse surface** is one for which the intensity is **independent of direction**. The radiant flux $dE$ emitted in a direction $\theta$ from the normal is therefore proportional to $\cos\theta$:

$$I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$$

**Derivation of $E = \pi I_n$:**

Consider an elemental area $dA$ emitting diffusely. The energy emitted into a solid angle $d\omega$ in direction $\theta$ per unit time is:

$$dQ = I_n \cos\theta \, dA \, d\omega$$

For a hemisphere above the surface, the solid angle in spherical coordinates is $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$. Therefore:

$$E = \frac{dQ_{\text{total}}}{dA} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} I_n \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Evaluating the $\phi$ integral:

$$E = 2\pi I_n \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

The $\theta$ integral gives $1/2$:

$$E = 2\pi I_n \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi I_n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** This identity is heavily used in deriving view factors and radiosity integrals in subsequent modules. Memorize the derivation, not just the result.

---

**(b) State and prove Kirchhoff's law. Explain the physical significance of emissivity. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of the emissive power to the absorptivity is the same for all surfaces at the same temperature and equals the black body emissive power:

$$\frac{E_i(T)}{\alpha_i(T)} = E_b(T)$$

**Proof:**

Consider a closed evacuated enclosure at uniform temperature $T$ containing two large parallel plates of arbitrary materials. In thermal equilibrium, no net heat transfer occurs between the plates, so each plate emits exactly as much radiation as it absorbs:

$$E_1 = \alpha_1 G \quad \text{and} \quad E_2 = \alpha_2 G$$

where $G$ is the irradiation on each plate. Dividing the first equation by $\alpha_1$ and the second by $\alpha_2$:

$$\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = G = \frac{E_2}{\alpha_2}$$

Since the system is in equilibrium with a black body cavity at the same temperature, the irradiation $G$ equals the black body emissive power $E_b(T)$. Thus:

$$\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = \frac{E_2}{\alpha_2} = E_b(T)$$

Generalizing to any number of surfaces:

$$\frac{E_i(T)}{\alpha_i(T)} = E_b(T) \quad \text{for all } i$$

**Emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E(T)}{E_b(T)} = \alpha(T)$$

Emissivity is a dimensionless measure of how efficiently a real surface emits radiation compared to a black body. $\varepsilon = 1$ for a black body, $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ for a grey body, and $\varepsilon \approx 0$ for polished metals. Its physical significance lies in allowing engineers to compute real surface emission simply as $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$:** They are equal only at thermal equilibrium. For a non-equilibrium scenario, $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ are evaluated at different temperatures (e.g., solar absorptivity at 5778 K and terrestrial emissivity at 300 K for selective surfaces).
> 2. **Missing units in Part A:** The KTU evaluator deducts marks if units are not written beside quantities. Always include $\text{W/m}^2$ for flux quantities and the appropriate steradian-based unit for intensity.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the black body condition in Stefan-Boltzmann:** The relation $E_b = \sigma T^4$ applies **only** to a black body. For a real surface, it must be $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping integration steps in Part B derivations:** A 7-mark derivation question expects at least 4–6 clearly written steps. Writing only the final formula with no intermediate algebra attracts only 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing radiosity and emissive power:** Radiosity includes the reflected component. Many students write $J = E$ which is wrong for non-opaque or for surfaces with non-zero reflectivity.
> 6. **Wien's constant units:** Always quote $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$. Writing the constant in $\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ without the $\mu\text{m}$ conversion is a recurring error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Thermal radiation is **electromagnetic energy emission due to temperature**; it does not require a medium.
- The **energy balance identity** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ is the starting point of every radiation problem.
- **Black body** absorbs all ($\alpha = 1$) and is the best emitter at any temperature.
- **Kirchhoff's Law** equates emission and absorption: $E / \alpha = E_b$, leading to $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at thermal equilibrium.
- **Planck's Law** is the fundamental spectral distribution; **Wien's Law** and **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** are its direct consequences.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$ — hotter bodies emit at shorter wavelengths.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$.
- **Lambert's Cosine Law** for diffuse surfaces: $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$, integrating to $E = \pi I_n$.
- **Grey body assumption** (constant $\varepsilon$ with wavelength) is the standard KTU simplification for most problems.
- **Radiosity** $J$ includes both emitted and reflected components: $J = E + \rho G$ for opaque surfaces.
- The **thermal infrared** wavelength band of interest in HMT is **0.1 to 100 μm**.
- The four critical physical constants to memorize: $C_1$, $C_2$, $\sigma$, and the Wien product $2897.8 \; \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{K}$.
- Always specify whether a relation applies to a **black body** or a **real (grey) body** — this is the most heavily examined distinction in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorptivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Absorptivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**Absorptivity (α)** is defined as the ratio of the radiant energy absorbed by a surface to the total radiant energy incident upon that surface, for a given wavelength interval and at a specified temperature. It is a dimensionless radiative property that quantifies how efficiently a body absorbs incident electromagnetic radiation (thermal radiation).

Mathematically, for radiation over a finite wavelength band:

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Radiation energy absorbed by the surface}}{\text{Total radiation energy incident on the surface}}$$

The value of absorptivity always lies in the range **0 ≤ α ≤ 1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Absorptivity is a **directional and spectral property**. A surface can have different absorptivity values when measured at different wavelengths and different incident directions. This is a frequent **2-mark/3-mark question** in KTU examinations.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing outside during a **scorching summer afternoon** wearing two different T-shirts — one **black** and one **white**.

- The **black T-shirt** absorbs almost all the sunlight (radiation) falling on it → it becomes very hot. Its **absorptivity is high (α ≈ 0.95–0.98)**.
- The **white T-shirt** reflects most of the sunlight and absorbs very little → it remains cool. Its **absorptivity is low (α ≈ 0.05–0.10)**.

In simple words, **absorptivity is a measure of how "hungry" a surface is for incident radiation**. A surface with high absorptivity "eats" most of the radiation that strikes it, while a surface with low absorptivity "rejects" (reflects or transmits) most of it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Absorptivity depends on:
> 1. The **nature of the surface** (material, color, roughness, oxidation).
> 2. The **wavelength (λ)** of the incident radiation.
> 3. The **temperature (T)** of the surface.
> 4. The **angle of incidence (θ)** of the incoming radiation.

---

### Types of Absorptivity (KTU Classification)

Based on the wavelength range considered, absorptivity is classified into:

| Type | Symbol | Wavelength Range | Definition |
|------|--------|------------------|------------|
| **Monochromatic / Spectral Absorptivity** | αλ | At a single wavelength λ | Fraction of radiation absorbed at one specific wavelength |
| **Spectral (Band) Absorptivity** | αλ₁₋λ₂ | Over a wavelength band λ₁ to λ₂ | Fraction absorbed over a finite band of wavelengths |
| **Total Absorptivity** | α | Over the entire spectrum (0 to ∞) | Fraction absorbed over all wavelengths |

Similarly, based on direction:

- **Hemispherical Absorptivity** — averaged over all incident directions over the entire hemisphere.
- **Directional Absorptivity** — at a specific angle of incidence θ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most commonly used definition in KTU problems is the **total hemispherical absorptivity**, denoted simply as **α**.

---

### Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Speed of light:** c = **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Wien's displacement constant:** b = **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K**
- **Unit of absorptivity:** **Dimensionless** (pure ratio, 0 to 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Breakdown of the Concept

**Step 1: Understanding Incident Radiation on a Surface**

When thermal radiation strikes a real (non-ideal) surface, the incident energy is partitioned into three fundamental outcomes:

1. **Absorbed fraction (α)** — energy that is absorbed and converted into internal energy (heat) of the body.
2. **Reflected fraction (ρ)** — energy that is bounced back from the surface.
3. **Transmitted fraction (τ)** — energy that passes through the body (only for semi-transparent materials like glass, thin plastic films).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Principle (Fundamental Equation of Radiative Properties):**
> For any surface at thermodynamic equilibrium, the sum of these three fractions must equal unity.

**Step 2: Why is Absorptivity Important?**

The "Why" behind absorptivity lies in **Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation** (explained below), which connects a surface's ability to **emit** radiation to its ability to **absorb** radiation. This is foundational for solving all radiation heat transfer problems, including:

- Design of **solar collectors** (high-α surfaces like black paint).
- Design of **thermal insulation** in spacecraft (low-α coatings).
- **Greenhouse effect** analysis (glass has high τ for shortwave, low τ for longwave).

**Step 3: How is Absorptivity Determined Experimentally?**

The "How" of absorptivity measurement involves:
- Directing a calibrated radiation source (often a solar simulator) onto the test surface.
- Measuring reflected and transmitted components using integrating spheres or calorimeters.
- Computing α = 1 − ρ − τ (for opaque surfaces, τ = 0, so α = 1 − ρ).

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | **α = G_abs / G_inc** | Definition of total absorptivity | Dimensionless; 0 ≤ α ≤ 1 |
| 2 | **αλ = G_λ,abs / G_λ,inc** | Monochromatic (spectral) absorptivity at wavelength λ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **α = ∫₀^∞ α_λ · G_λ,inc · dλ / ∫₀^∞ G_λ,inc · dλ** | Total absorptivity as weighted average of spectral values | Integration over full spectrum |
| 4 | **α + ρ + τ = 1** | Conservation of incident radiation (general surface) | Valid for each wavelength and total |
| 5 | **α + ρ = 1** (opaque surface) | For opaque (τ = 0) surfaces | Most engineering surfaces are opaque |
| 6 | **α = ε** (at thermal equilibrium) | **Kirchhoff's Law** — absorptivity equals emissivity for the same wavelength and direction | A body that absorbs well also emits well |
| 7 | **G_abs = α · G_inc** | Absorbed radiation flux | W/m² |
| 8 | **q_abs = α · σ · T_surr⁴** | Absorbed power from surroundings (for a small body in large enclosure) | W/m²; uses T of source |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** Never use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside the markdown table for absolute value — LaTeX formatting can break the table. Use `\vert` instead (e.g., `$\alpha \vert_{T=300K}$`).

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Engineering:** Selective absorber surfaces in solar water heaters have **α ≈ 0.95** for solar wavelengths (0.3–2.5 μm) but **ε ≈ 0.05** for infrared wavelengths, maximizing energy capture while minimizing thermal re-radiation loss.
- **Building Materials:** Roof paints are chosen with specific α values to control indoor thermal comfort — **cool roofs** use high-reflectance (low α) coatings to reduce urban heat island effect.
- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft thermal control surfaces (OSRs — Optical Solar Reflectors) have **α ≈ 0.05 and ε ≈ 0.8** to reject solar heat while efficiently emitting internal heat.
- **Industrial Furnaces:** Inner walls of furnaces have high α (≈ 0.9) to maximize heat absorption from flames.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: From the Definition to the Energy Balance

**Starting Point:** Consider a small elemental area *dA* on a surface. Let *G_inc* be the total incident radiation flux (W/m²) striking this area, arriving from all directions over the entire hemisphere above the surface.

Let the radiation flux absorbed by the surface be *G_abs*. By the formal definition:

$$
\alpha = \frac{G_{abs}}{G_{inc}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
G_{abs} = \alpha \cdot G_{inc} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
$$

**Energy Conservation Step:** The incident radiation must be either absorbed, reflected, or transmitted. Therefore:

$$
G_{inc} = G_{abs} + G_{ref} + G_{trans}
$$

Dividing throughout by *G_inc*:

$$
1 = \frac{G_{abs}}{G_{inc}} + \frac{G_{ref}}{G_{inc}} + \frac{G_{trans}}{G_{inc}}
$$

Substituting the definitions of absorptivity, reflectivity (ρ), and transmissivity (τ):

$$
1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}
$$

**Special Case — Opaque Surface:** Most engineering surfaces (metals, painted surfaces, concrete) are opaque, meaning **τ = 0**. The equation reduces to:

$$
\alpha + \rho = 1 \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}
$$

This means for opaque surfaces, knowing the reflectivity automatically gives the absorptivity.

---

### Derivation 2: Deriving the Spectral (Monochromatic) Form

**Setup:** Consider radiation incident at a single wavelength λ with a narrow bandwidth dλ. The incident monochromatic intensity is *G_λ,inc* and the absorbed monochromatic intensity is *G_λ,abs*.

By definition of monochromatic absorptivity:

$$
\alpha_{\lambda} = \frac{G_{\lambda,abs}}{G_{\lambda,inc}} \quad \text{...(Equation 4)}
$$

**Total Absorptivity as a Weighted Average:** To obtain the total (bolometric) absorptivity, we must integrate the absorbed radiation over the entire spectrum and divide by the total incident radiation:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,abs} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \cdot G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda} \quad \text{...(Equation 5)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Equation 5 shows that **total absorptivity is the spectral absorptivity α_λ weighted by the incident spectral distribution G_λ,inc**. This is a critically important result because:

- If *G_λ,inc* is from the **sun (≈ 5800 K blackbody)**, the relevant range is 0.2–3 μm.
- If *G_λ,inc* is from a **furnace (≈ 1500 K)**, the relevant range shifts to 1–10 μm.
- The *same surface* can have **different total absorptivities** depending on the source spectrum — this is the basis of **selective surfaces** in solar engineering.

---

### Derivation 3: Kirchhoff's Law — The Identity α = ε

**Setup:** Consider two infinite parallel surfaces facing each other in a vacuum enclosure at thermodynamic equilibrium. Let surface 1 be a **real surface** with absorptivity α₁ and emissivity ε₁, and surface 2 be an **ideal blackbody** with α₂ = ε₂ = 1.

At thermal equilibrium, both surfaces are at the same temperature T. The radiant energy leaving surface 2 that strikes surface 1 is:

$$
G_{1 \leftarrow 2} = \sigma T^{4} \quad \text{(blackbody emission)}
$$

The energy absorbed by surface 1 from this flux:

$$
G_{1,abs} = \alpha_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4} \quad \text{...(Equation 6)}
$$

By Kirchhoff's hypothesis, at thermal equilibrium the radiation balance requires that the energy absorbed by surface 1 from surface 2's emission equals the energy emitted by surface 1 toward surface 2:

$$
\varepsilon_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4} = \alpha_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4} \quad \text{...(Equation 7)}
$$

Dividing both sides by σT⁴:

$$
\boxed{\alpha_{1} = \varepsilon_{1}} \quad \text{...(Equation 8)}
$$

**General Form (Spectral and Directional):**

$$
\alpha_{\lambda,\theta}(T) = \varepsilon_{\lambda,\theta}(T) \quad \text{...(Equation 9)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** A good absorber is a good emitter, and a poor absorber is a poor emitter. This is one of the most fundamental results in thermal radiation and is **frequently asked in KTU exams** (often worth 5–7 marks in derivations).

---

### Analytical Matrix — Absorptivity vs. Surface Type

| Surface Type | Typical α (Solar) | Typical α (Thermal IR) | Physical Reason |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Polished Silver** | 0.02 – 0.05 | 0.02 – 0.05 | High electrical conductivity → reflects all wavelengths |
| **Polished Aluminum** | 0.05 – 0.10 | 0.05 – 0.10 | Similar to silver, slightly higher |
| **White Paint** | 0.15 – 0.30 | 0.90 – 0.95 | Pigment reflects visible, absorbs IR |
| **Black Paint (Matte)** | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.95 – 0.98 | Carbon pigment absorbs across all λ |
| **Brick / Concrete** | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.85 – 0.93 | Mineral content absorbs strongly in IR |
| **Glass (Clear, 3 mm)** | ~ 0.04 (transmitted) | ~ 0.85 | Transmits visible, absorbs longwave IR |

> [!NOTE]
> Note how some surfaces (like white paint) are **spectrally selective** — they are *poor* absorbers of solar radiation but *excellent* absorbers of thermal IR. This selectivity is exploited in many engineering applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Partitioning of Incident Radiation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incident Radiation G_in"]:::incoming
    B["ABSORBED FRACTION alpha G_in"]:::absorbed
    C["REFLECTED FRACTION rho G_in"]:::reflected
    D["TRANSMITTED FRACTION tau G_in"]:::transmitted

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D

    E["Conservation: alpha + rho + tau = 1"]:::conservation
    B -.- E
    C -.- E
    D -.- E

    F["OPAQUE SURFACE tau = 0"]:::opaque
    G["alpha + rho = 1"]:::opaqueResult
    F --> G

    classDef incoming fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef absorbed fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef reflected fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef transmitted fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef conservation fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opaque fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opaqueResult fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

---

### Diagram 2: Functional Architecture of Absorptivity in Radiation Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    WAVE["Wavelength Spectrum lambda 0 to infinity"]:::wave
    SURF["Real Surface"]:::surface

    SRC -->|"G_lambda_inc"| WAVE
    WAVE -->|"incident flux"| SURF

    SURF -->|"Absorbed alpha lambda"| ABS["G_lambda_abs Converted to Internal Energy"]:::abs
    SURF -->|"Reflected rho lambda"| REF["G_lambda_ref Bounced back"]:::ref
    SURF -->|"Transmitted tau lambda"| TR["G_lambda_trans Passed through"]:::trans

    ABS --> THERMAL["Thermal Energy Increase of Body"]:::thermal
    THERMAL --> EMIT["Emission alpha lambda equals epsilon lambda Kirchhoff Law"]:::emit

    classDef source fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef wave fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```
