
---

### Diagram 3: Absorptivity Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Absorptivity alpha"]:::root

    A["Based on Wavelength"]:::cat1
    A1["Monochromatic alpha lambda"]:::leaf1
    A2["Spectral Band alpha lambda1 to lambda2"]:::leaf1
    A3["Total alpha integrated over 0 to infinity"]:::leaf1

    B["Based on Direction"]:::cat2
    B1["Directional alpha theta"]:::leaf2
    B2["Hemispherical alpha averaged over hemisphere"]:::leaf2

    C["Based on Surface Type"]:::cat3
    C1["Opaque alpha = 1 minus rho"]:::leaf3
    C2["Transparent alpha = 1 minus rho minus tau"]:::leaf3

    ROOT --> A
    ROOT --> B
    ROOT --> C

    A --> A1
    A --> A2
    A --> A3

    B --> B1
    B --> B2

    C --> C1
    C --> C2

    classDef root fill:#000080,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef cat1 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef cat2 fill:#5F9EA0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef cat3 fill:#778899,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef leaf1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef leaf2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leaf3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define absorptivity. State the range of values it can take and give one practical example of a high-absorbivity surface.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Absorptivity (α) is the ratio of the radiant energy absorbed by a surface to the total radiant energy incident on it, over a specified wavelength interval and direction.

**Mathematical form (1 mark):**

$$
\alpha = \frac{G_{abs}}{G_{inc}}
$$

**Range and example (1 mark):** The value of α lies between **0 and 1** (0 ≤ α ≤ 1). A practical example of a high-absorbivity surface is **matte black paint**, which has α ≈ 0.95–0.98 across the solar spectrum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State and explain Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation. What is its significance in heat transfer?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** Kirchhoff's law states that at thermal equilibrium, the **monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity** at the same wavelength, temperature, and direction:

$$
\varepsilon_{\lambda}(T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(T)
$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** A body that is a good absorber of radiation at a given wavelength is also an efficient emitter at that wavelength. Conversely, a poor absorber is a poor emitter. The total hemispherical form is:

$$
\varepsilon(T) = \alpha(T)
$$

when the surface is in equilibrium with its surroundings.

**Significance (1 mark):** This law allows us to determine a surface's **emissivity (a difficult property to measure directly)** by simply measuring its absorptivity, which is experimentally easier. It is foundational for radiation network analysis and for designing surfaces with specific thermal-radiative behavior (e.g., solar absorbers, space radiators).

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

**QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — Option 1**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

(a) Derive the relationship **α + ρ + τ = 1** for a general surface exposed to thermal radiation. What simplification applies for an opaque surface? **[(7 marks)]**

(b) Explain the **spectral (monochromatic) and total absorptivity** concepts. Show that the total absorptivity is a weighted average of the spectral values, weighted by the incident spectral distribution. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a surface element of area *dA* receiving incident radiation flux *G_inc* (W/m²) from the entire hemisphere.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The incident radiation is partitioned into three components:
- Absorbed: G_abs = α·G_inc
- Reflected: G_ref = ρ·G_inc
- Transmitted: G_trans = τ·G_inc

**Step 3 (2 marks):** By the principle of conservation of energy, the sum of all three components must equal the incident flux:

$$
G_{inc} = G_{abs} + G_{ref} + G_{trans}
$$

$$
G_{inc} = \alpha \cdot G_{inc} + \rho \cdot G_{inc} + \tau \cdot G_{inc}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Dividing throughout by G_inc:

$$
1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For an **opaque surface**, the transmissivity τ = 0 (no radiation passes through), so the equation simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\alpha + \rho = 1}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Spectral vs Total Absorptivity [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The **spectral (monochromatic) absorptivity** α_λ is defined as the fraction of incident radiation at a specific wavelength λ that is absorbed by the surface:

$$
\alpha_{\lambda} = \frac{G_{\lambda,abs}}{G_{\lambda,inc}}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The **total absorptivity** α considers all wavelengths from 0 to ∞:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,abs} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substituting G_λ,abs = α_λ · G_λ,inc:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \cdot G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,inc} \, d\lambda}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** This integral expression is precisely the **weighted average** of α_λ, with the incident spectral flux G_λ,inc as the weighting function. **The total absorptivity therefore depends on both the surface property (α_λ) AND the source spectrum (G_λ,inc).** The same surface can have very different total absorptivities when exposed to sunlight versus a furnace.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** This concept is the basis of **selective surfaces** in solar collectors, where the surface is engineered to have high α_λ in the solar wavelength range (0.3–2.5 μm) and low α_λ (and thus low ε_λ) in the thermal IR range (2.5–50 μm).

---

**QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

(a) State and prove **Kirchhoff's law** of thermal radiation. **[(7 marks)]**

(b) A small metal plate with absorptivity α = 0.7 and emissivity ε = 0.7 is placed in a large furnace whose walls are at 1500 K. The plate reaches a steady temperature of 900 K. Determine the **net radiation heat flux** absorbed by the plate per unit area. Use σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴). **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Kirchhoff's Law Proof [7 marks]**

**Statement (1 mark):** Kirchhoff's law states that for a body in thermal equilibrium, the spectral emissivity equals the spectral absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature: ε_λ(T) = α_λ(T).

**Setup (1 mark):** Consider two infinite parallel plates in a vacuum enclosure in thermal equilibrium at temperature T. Plate 1 is a real surface (ε₁, α₁) and Plate 2 is a perfect blackbody (ε₂ = α₂ = 1).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Radiation emitted by the blackbody Plate 2 toward Plate 1:

$$
G_{1 \leftarrow 2} = \sigma T^{4}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The fraction of this radiation absorbed by Plate 1 is:

$$
G_{1,abs} = \alpha_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** At thermal equilibrium, the energy absorbed by Plate 1 from Plate 2's emission must equal the energy emitted by Plate 1 toward Plate 2 (net zero energy transfer):

$$
\varepsilon_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4} = \alpha_{1} \cdot \sigma T^{4}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Dividing throughout by σT⁴:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{1} = \alpha_{1}}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This proves Kirchhoff's identity. The result extends to the total (bolometric) hemispherical form: **ε(T) = α(T)** when the surrounding radiation spectrum matches the surface's own emission spectrum (gray-surface approximation or enclosure equilibrium).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Absorptivity of plate: α = 0.7
- Emissivity of plate: ε = 0.7
- Furnace wall temperature: T_surr = 1500 K
- Plate steady temperature: T_p = 900 K
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)

**To Find:** Net radiation heat flux absorbed by the plate, q_net (W/m²).

**Assumption (1 mark):** The plate is "small" relative to the furnace, so the furnace walls completely surround the plate and form a uniform radiation environment (view factor = 1). The plate's own emission does not reflect back onto itself.

**Step 1 — Radiation incident on the plate from furnace walls (1 mark):**

$$
G_{inc} = \sigma \cdot T_{surr}^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1500)^{4}
$$

$$
G_{inc} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
G_{inc} = 2.87 \times 10^{5} \, \text{W/m}^{2} = 287,043 \, \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Radiation absorbed by the plate (1 mark):**

$$
G_{abs} = \alpha \cdot G_{inc} = 0.7 \times 2.87 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
G_{abs} = 2.009 \times 10^{5} \, \text{W/m}^{2} \approx 200,930 \, \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Radiation emitted by the plate itself (1 mark):**

$$
G_{emit} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_{p}^{4} = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (900)^{4}
$$

$$
G_{emit} = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.561 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
G_{emit} = 0.7 \times 37,200.87 = 26,041 \, \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Net radiation heat flux absorbed (2 marks):**

$$
q_{net,abs} = G_{abs} - G_{emit}
$$

$$
q_{net,abs} = 200,930 - 26,041
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{net,abs} \approx 1.749 \times 10^{5} \, \text{W/m}^{2} = 174,889 \, \text{W/m}^{2}}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The net absorbed flux is positive and very large, indicating the plate continuously absorbs radiation from the furnace. In a true steady state, this absorbed energy must be balanced by other heat loss modes (conduction, convection) — but in vacuum, the plate would continue to heat up until its temperature reached 1500 K, at which point G_abs = G_emit and q_net = 0.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Absorptivity Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing α with ε** — At thermal equilibrium they are equal, but for a *cold body in a hot environment* (or vice versa), the surface may absorb at one temperature's spectrum and emit at another. Always check whether Kirchhoff's law strictly applies.
> 2. **Forgetting the τ = 0 condition** — Many students write α + ρ + τ = 1 for opaque surfaces, which is unnecessarily complex. Simplify to α + ρ = 1.
> 3. **Wrong unit for absorptivity** — It is **dimensionless**; do not write W/m² or %.
> 4. **Sign convention errors in net flux** — q_net = G_abs − G_emit. Positive means net absorption.
> 5. **Using α instead of α_λ** — Total absorptivity α is an integrated quantity. If the problem gives spectral data, integrate carefully.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Absorptivity (α)** = ratio of absorbed to incident radiation; **dimensionless**; range **0 ≤ α ≤ 1**.
- **Energy balance:** α + ρ + τ = 1 (general); α + ρ = 1 (opaque).
- **Spectral vs Total:** α_λ is wavelength-specific; α is the spectral average weighted by G_λ,inc.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** ε_λ(T) = α_λ(T) at thermal equilibrium — a good absorber is a good emitter.
- **Selective surfaces** exploit wavelength-dependent α_λ (e.g., α ≈ 0.95 for solar, ε ≈ 0.05 for IR).
- **Opaque surfaces** (most engineering materials) have τ = 0, simplifying analysis to α = 1 − ρ.
- **Practical extremes:** Polished metals (α ≈ 0.02–0.10), matte black paint (α ≈ 0.95–0.98).
- **In KTU problems,** when a small body is in a large enclosure, the incident flux is σT_surr⁴, and the body's emission is ε·σT_body⁴.
- **Total hemispherical absorptivity** is the most commonly used form in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflectivity and Transmittivity of Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reflectivity and Transmittivity of Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

When thermal radiation strikes the surface of a material, the incident radiation energy interacts with the surface in three possible ways. The fraction of incident radiation that is reflected back is called **Reflectivity (ρ)**, the fraction that passes through the body is called **Transmittivity (τ)**, and the fraction absorbed within the body is called **Absorptivity (α)**.

$$ \alpha + \rho + \tau = 1 $$

This fundamental equation is known as the **Radiation Energy Balance Equation** and applies at any specified wavelength and direction for a given semi-transparent material exposed to incident radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A surface is called an **opaque surface** if τ = 0, in which case the energy balance reduces to α + ρ = 1. A surface is called a **transparent surface (diatermous)** if α = 0 and ρ = 0, leaving τ = 1. A **black surface** has α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0. A **perfect reflector (white body)** has ρ = 1.

### 1.2 Concept Definitions

**Reflectivity (ρ):** The fraction of incident radiation that is reflected by a surface, expressed mathematically as:

$$ \rho = \frac{\text{Radiation energy reflected}}{\text{Total incident radiation energy}} $$

**Transmittivity (τ):** The fraction of incident radiation that passes completely through a body, expressed as:

$$ \tau = \frac{\text{Radiation energy transmitted}}{\text{Total incident radiation energy}} $$

**Absorptivity (α):** The fraction of incident radiation that is absorbed by the body:

$$ \alpha = \frac{\text{Radiation energy absorbed}}{\text{Total incident radiation energy}} $$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Sunlight and the Window:**
> Imagine you are standing inside a room and sunlight is falling on the glass window. Three things can happen to the sunlight: (1) some part of it **bounces back** out into the atmosphere — that is **reflection** (ρ), (2) some part of it gets **trapped and converted to heat** in the glass — that is **absorption** (α), and (3) some part of it **passes through** the glass and lights up your room — that is **transmission** (τ). The sum of all three fractions must equal the entire incoming sunlight (which we normalize to 1). A **mirror** has very high ρ (almost all light bounces back). A **clear glass** has very high τ (almost all light passes through). A **black painted wall** has very high α (almost all light is absorbed and converted to heat).

### 1.4 Key Physical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three properties α, ρ, and τ depend on:
> 1. The **wavelength (λ)** of the incident radiation — leads to spectral properties αλ, ρλ, τλ.
> 2. The **direction (θ, φ)** of the incident radiation — leads to directional properties αθ, ρθ, τθ.
> 3. The **temperature** and **nature** of the surface (smooth, polished, rough, oxidized, painted).
>
> Therefore, the complete characterization requires hemispherical-spectral integration, which is the foundation of advanced radiation analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Surface Behavior

| Surface Type | Condition | Behavior |
|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **Black Body** | α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0 | Absorbs all incident radiation (ideal absorber) |
| **White Body (Perfect Reflector)** | α = 0, ρ = 1, τ = 0 | Reflects all incident radiation |
| **Transparent Body (Diatermous)** | α = 0, ρ = 0, τ = 1 | Transmits all incident radiation |
| **Opaque Body** | τ = 0, so α + ρ = 1 | No transmission through the body |
| **Diffuse (Gray) Body** | α, ρ, τ independent of λ | Properties constant over all wavelengths |
| **Specular Reflector** | Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection | Mirror-like smooth surface |
| **Diffuse Reflector** | Reflected intensity ∝ cos θ | Radiation reflected uniformly in all directions |

### 2.2 Spectral, Directional, and Hemispherical Properties

The properties α, ρ, and τ can be defined at multiple levels of specificity:

**Step 1 — Monochromatic (Spectral) Directional Property:**
At a specific wavelength λ and direction (θ, φ), we have:

$$ \alpha_{\lambda}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{I_{\lambda, \text{abs}}(\theta, \phi)}{I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi)} $$

**Step 2 — Hemispherical Spectral Property:**
Integrating over all incoming directions (hemisphere) at fixed wavelength:

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} = \frac{\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \alpha_{\lambda}(\theta, \phi) \, I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}{\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi} $$

**Step 3 — Hemispherical Total Property:**
Integrating over all wavelengths and all directions:

$$ \alpha = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda}{G} $$

The same integration logic applies for reflectivity (ρ) and transmittivity (τ).

### 2.3 Specular vs Diffuse Reflection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specular Reflection** occurs when radiation strikes a smooth, polished surface and is reflected at an angle equal to the angle of incidence (law of reflection). The reflected beam maintains its angular relationship with the surface normal.
>
> **Diffuse Reflection** occurs when radiation strikes a rough, matte surface and is scattered uniformly in all directions above the surface. A perfectly diffuse reflector obeys Lambert's cosine law — the reflected intensity is proportional to cos θ, where θ is measured from the surface normal.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $ \alpha + \rho + \tau = 1 $ | Energy balance for semi-transparent surface | Foundational radiation equation |
| 2 | $ \alpha + \rho = 1 $ | Energy balance for opaque surface (τ = 0) | Most engineering surfaces |
| 3 | $ \rho_{\lambda} = 1 - \alpha_{\lambda} $ | Spectral reflectivity of opaque body | Blackbody surface (τ = 0) |
| 4 | $ \alpha = \varepsilon $ | Kirchhoff's Law (at thermal equilibrium) | Relating absorptivity and emissivity |
| 5 | $ \tau_{\lambda} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L} $ | Bouguer's Law (Beer-Lambert) for absorbing media | Transmittivity of glass, water, gases |
| 6 | $ K_{\lambda} = a_{\lambda} \rho_{m} $ | Absorption coefficient relation | Where $a_{\lambda}$ is mass absorption coefficient, $\rho_m$ is mass density |
| 7 | $ \rho = 1 - \alpha - \tau $ | Reflectivity from balance equation | Solving numerical problems |
| 8 | $ \alpha_{\text{gray}} = \varepsilon_{\text{gray}} $ | Gray body Kirchhoff relation | For wavelength-independent bodies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants/Metrics (in Bold):**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Wien's Displacement Constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$
> - These constants are essential for numerical problems involving radiation heat transfer combined with reflectivity/transmittivity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Solar Collectors:** Selective coatings maximize α in the solar spectrum while minimizing ε in the infrared region.
> 2. **Building Energy Design:** Low-emissivity (Low-E) window coatings have very low τ for infrared, reducing heat loss.
> 3. **Greenhouse Design:** Plastic films are chosen with high τ in visible range (0.4–0.7 μm) for plant photosynthesis.
> 4. **Furnace Linings:** Refractory bricks have high α to absorb radiant heat, then re-radiate isotropically.
> 5. **Satellite Thermal Control:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) uses alternating layers with low α and low ε to minimize radiative heat transfer.

### 2.6 Factors Affecting Reflectivity and Transmittivity

1. **Surface Roughness:** Smooth → specular reflection; Rough → diffuse reflection.
2. **Wavelength:** Metals reflect longer wavelengths better; dielectrics reflect shorter wavelengths.
3. **Angle of Incidence:** Reflectivity increases as angle approaches 90° (grazing incidence) per Fresnel equations.
4. **Temperature:** Reflectivity of metals typically decreases slightly with increasing temperature.
5. **Material Composition:** Polished silver has ρ ≈ 0.97; polished aluminum has ρ ≈ 0.85; black paint has ρ ≈ 0.05.
6. **Surface Oxidation and Contamination:** Tarnished or oxidized surfaces drastically alter ρ and τ values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radiation Energy Balance (α + ρ + τ = 1)

**Step 1:** Consider a body of arbitrary shape exposed to thermal radiation. Let $G$ be the total incident radiation (irradiation) striking the surface per unit area per unit time.

**Step 2:** By the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, the total incident radiation must be distributed among three possible fates: absorbed, reflected, or transmitted.

$$ G = G_{\text{abs}} + G_{\text{ref}} + G_{\text{trans}} $$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides of the equation by the total incident radiation $G$:

$$ \frac{G}{G} = \frac{G_{\text{abs}}}{G} + \frac{G_{\text{ref}}}{G} + \frac{G_{\text{trans}}}{G} $$

$$ 1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau $$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$ \boxed{\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1} $$

This is the foundational energy balance equation for thermal radiation interaction with a semi-transparent medium.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Bouguer's Law (τ = e^{-KL})

**Step 1:** Consider radiation of intensity $I_\lambda$ incident on an absorbing medium of thickness $L$. As the radiation traverses an infinitesimal layer $dx$, the decrease in intensity is proportional to the local intensity and the thickness traversed.

$$ dI_{\lambda} = -K_{\lambda} \, I_{\lambda} \, dx $$

where $K_{\lambda}$ is the **monochromatic absorption coefficient** of the medium (units: m⁻¹).

**Step 2:** Rearrange the equation to separate variables:

$$ \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -K_{\lambda} \, dx $$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from the incident intensity $I_{\lambda, 0}$ at $x = 0$ to the transmitted intensity $I_{\lambda, L}$ at $x = L$:

$$ \int_{I_{\lambda, 0}}^{I_{\lambda, L}} \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -K_{\lambda} \int_{0}^{L} dx $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{\lambda, L}}{I_{\lambda, 0}}\right) = -K_{\lambda} \, L $$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$ \frac{I_{\lambda, L}}{I_{\lambda, 0}} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L} $$

**Step 5:** By definition, transmittivity is the ratio of transmitted to incident intensity:

$$ \boxed{\tau_{\lambda} = \frac{I_{\lambda, L}}{I_{\lambda, 0}} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L}} $$

This is **Bouguer's Law** (also known as Beer's Law or Beer-Lambert Law).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Relation Between Kλ and Material Properties

**Step 1:** The absorption coefficient $K_{\lambda}$ of an absorbing medium is related to its mass absorption coefficient $a_{\lambda}$ (units: m²/kg) and mass density $\rho_m$ (units: kg/m³):

$$ K_{\lambda} = a_{\lambda} \, \rho_m $$

**Step 2:** For a gas mixture, the total absorption coefficient is the sum of partial absorption coefficients:

$$ K_{\lambda, \text{total}} = \sum_{i} a_{\lambda, i} \, \rho_{m, i} $$

where the summation is over all absorbing species $i$ in the gas mixture.

**Step 3:** Combining with Bouguer's Law:

$$ \tau_{\lambda} = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i} a_{\lambda, i} \, \rho_{m, i} \, L\right) $$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A glass plate of thickness $L = 5 \, \text{mm}$ has a spectral absorption coefficient $K_{\lambda} = 200 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ at wavelength $\lambda = 1.5 \, \mu\text{m}$. If the reflectivity of the glass at this wavelength is $\rho_{\lambda} = 0.04$ (single surface), determine the spectral absorptivity $\alpha_{\lambda}$ and spectral transmittivity $\tau_{\lambda}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute transmittivity using Bouguer's Law:**

$$ \tau_{\lambda} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L} = e^{-(200)(0.005)} = e^{-1.0} $$

$$ \tau_{\lambda} = 0.3679 $$

**Step 2 — Apply energy balance:**

For a single-surface case where only one reflection occurs:

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} + \rho_{\lambda} + \tau_{\lambda} = 1 $$

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \rho_{\lambda} - \tau_{\lambda} = 1 - 0.04 - 0.3679 $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha_{\lambda} = 0.5921} $$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Hemispherical Total Reflectivity

**Problem:** A surface has the following spectral reflectivity data at solar irradiation conditions:

| Wavelength Band (μm) | $G_{\lambda} \cdot \Delta\lambda \, (\text{W/m}^2)$ | $\rho_{\lambda}$ |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| 0.2 – 0.6 | 300 | 0.30 |
| 0.6 – 1.0 | 400 | 0.40 |
| 1.0 – 2.0 | 200 | 0.55 |

Determine the total (hemispherical) reflectivity of the surface.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total incident irradiation:**

$$ G = \sum G_{\lambda} \Delta\lambda = 300 + 400 + 200 = 900 \, \text{W/m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted reflected radiation:**

$$ G_{\text{ref}} = \sum \rho_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \Delta\lambda = (0.30)(300) + (0.40)(400) + (0.55)(200) $$

$$ G_{\text{ref}} = 90 + 160 + 110 = 360 \, \text{W/m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Compute total reflectivity:**

$$ \rho = \frac{G_{\text{ref}}}{G} = \frac{360}{900} $$

$$ \boxed{\rho = 0.40} $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Radiation Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Radiation G] --> B{Medium Type}
    B -->|Opaque Body| C[Absorption alpha G]
    B -->|Semi-transparent Body| D[Transmittivity Pathway]
    C --> E[Reflection rho G]
    D --> F[Pass-through Radiation tau G]
    B -->|Thin Film| G[Multi-reflection Cascade]
    G --> H[Net Transmittivity]
    H --> I[Effective alpha + rho + tau = 1]
    C --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J{Thermal Equilibrium?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Kirchhoff Law alpha = epsilon]
    J -->|No| L[Non-equilibrium Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Surfaces by Radiation Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All Radiation-Striking Surfaces] --> B{Semi-transparent?}
    B -->|Yes, tau greater than 0| C[alpha + rho + tau = 1]
    B -->|No, tau = 0| D[Opaque Body]
    D --> E[alpha + rho = 1]
    C --> F{Reflective Behavior}
    E --> F
    F -->|Smooth, Mirror-like| G[Specular Reflector]
    F -->|Rough, Matte| H[Diffuse Reflector]
    C --> I{Wavelength Dependence?}
    I -->|Independent| J[Gray Body]
    I -->|Dependent| K[Non-Gray Body]
    G --> L[KTU: Fresnel Equation Applicable]
    H --> M[KTU: Lambert Cosine Law Applicable]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Radiation Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source at T1] -->|Emits E1| B[Radiative Medium]
    B -->|Part Absorbed| C[Surface at T2]
    B -->|Part Transmitted| D[Far Surface]
    C -->|Absorptivity alpha| E[Internal Energy Gain]
    C -->|Reflectivity rho| F[Back to Medium]
    C -->|Emissivity epsilon| G[Emission E2]
    G -->|Kirchhoff alpha = epsilon| B
    E --> H[Heat Sink or Work Output]
    F -->|Multiple Reflections| B
    D -->|Final Transmitted| I[Beyond System Boundary]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Problem] --> B{Wavelength Specified?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Spectral Properties]
    B -->|No| D[Use Total Properties]
    C --> E{Material Type Known?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Opaque Body| F[Apply alpha + rho = 1]
    E -->|Semi-transparent| G[Apply alpha + rho + tau = 1]
    E -->|Absorbing Medium| H[Apply Bouguer Law tau = exp-KL]
    F --> I{Surface Smooth?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Apply Fresnel Equations for Reflectivity]
    I -->|No| K[Apply Diffuse Approximation]
    J --> L[Compute Numerical Answer]
    K --> L
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Set B]**

Define the terms **absorptivity (α)**, **reflectivity (ρ)**, and **transmittivity (τ)**. Write the energy balance equation relating them for a semi-transparent body and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Absorptivity (α)* is the fraction of incident radiation energy absorbed by a body. *Reflectivity (ρ)* is the fraction of incident radiation energy reflected from the body surface. *Transmittivity (τ)* is the fraction of incident radiation energy that passes completely through the body. The energy balance for a semi-transparent body is:

$$ \alpha + \rho + \tau = 1 $$

This equation signifies the **Law of Conservation of Energy** applied to radiation — every photon of incident radiation must be either absorbed, reflected, or transmitted; nothing is lost in the process. For an opaque body, τ = 0, giving α + ρ = 1.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

Differentiate between **specular reflection** and **diffuse reflection**. Give one engineering example for each type of surface.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Specular Reflection | Diffuse Reflection |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Surface type | Smooth, polished, mirror-like | Rough, matte |
| Angle of reflection | Equals angle of incidence (law of reflection) | Scattered in all directions |
| Governing law | Law of reflection ($\theta_i = \theta_r$) | Lambert's cosine law ($I \propto \cos\theta$) |
| Example | Polished silver mirror, polished aluminum | White plaster wall, frosted glass, snow |

**Example:** A polished stainless steel cooker lid exhibits specular reflection of the flame's image. A white-painted wall reflects diffuse radiation uniformly in all directions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts: Part (a) 7 marks and Part (b) 7 marks. Solve any ONE full question from the choice provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Regular Exam, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $\tau_{\lambda} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L}$ for a homogeneous absorbing medium. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The medium is homogeneous (constant absorption coefficient $K_{\lambda}$ along the path).
2. The radiation is monochromatic at wavelength $\lambda$.
3. No scattering occurs — only true absorption.
4. No emission from the medium (cold absorbing slab).
5. Bouguer's experimental law applies for small path lengths.

**Derivation:**

Consider a beam of monochromatic intensity $I_{\lambda}$ entering a homogeneous absorbing medium of thickness $L$ at $x = 0$. In traversing an infinitesimal element $dx$, the intensity decreases in proportion to the local intensity and the thickness:

$$ dI_{\lambda} = -K_{\lambda} \, I_{\lambda} \, dx \quad \text{...(i)} $$

Separating variables:

$$ \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -K_{\lambda} \, dx \quad \text{...(ii)} $$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $I_{\lambda} = I_{\lambda, 0}$) to $x = L$ (where $I_{\lambda} = I_{\lambda, L}$):

$$ \int_{I_{\lambda,0}}^{I_{\lambda,L}} \frac{dI_{\lambda}}{I_{\lambda}} = -K_{\lambda} \int_{0}^{L} dx $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{\lambda,L}}{I_{\lambda,0}}\right) = -K_{\lambda} \, L $$

$$ \frac{I_{\lambda,L}}{I_{\lambda,0}} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L} $$

By definition, the spectral transmittivity is:

$$ \boxed{\tau_{\lambda} = \frac{I_{\lambda,L}}{I_{\lambda,0}} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L}} $$

**(b)** A glass sheet 6 mm thick has spectral absorption coefficient $K_{\lambda} = 150 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$. The reflectivity of each surface is 0.05. Determine the spectral transmittivity and absorptivity of the glass sheet (considering both surfaces reflect). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Internal transmittivity (between reflections):**

$$ \tau_{\text{internal}} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L} = e^{-(150)(0.006)} = e^{-0.9} $$

$$ \tau_{\text{internal}} = 0.4066 $$

**Step 2 — Total transmittivity considering two-surface reflections:**

For a glass sheet with reflectivity $\rho$ on each face, considering multiple internal reflections, the total transmittivity is:

$$ \tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{(1-\rho)^2 \, \tau_{\text{internal}}}{1 - \rho^2 \, \tau_{\text{internal}}^2} $$

Substituting values:

$$ \tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{(1 - 0.05)^2 \times 0.4066}{1 - (0.05)^2 \times (0.4066)^2} $$

$$ \tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{(0.9025)(0.4066)}{1 - (0.0025)(0.1653)} $$

$$ \tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{0.3669}{1 - 0.000413} = \frac{0.3669}{0.99959} $$

$$ \tau_{\text{total}} \approx 0.3671 $$

**Step 3 — Spectral absorptivity (from energy balance):**

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \rho_{\text{eff}} - \tau_{\text{total}} $$

For the two-reflecting-surface case:

$$ \rho_{\text{eff}} = 1 - \tau_{\text{total}} - \alpha_{\lambda} $$

Using energy balance:

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\text{total}} - (1 - \tau_{\text{total}})(1 - e^{-2K_{\lambda} L})/(1 + e^{-2K_{\lambda} L})... $$

**Simpler approach — energy balance for full system:**

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} = 1 - \tau_{\text{total}} - \rho_{\text{front + back, eff}} $$

For approximate single-surface treatment:

$$ \alpha_{\lambda} \approx 1 - \rho_{\lambda} - \tau_{\text{total}} = 1 - 0.05 - 0.3671 $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha_{\lambda} \approx 0.5829, \quad \tau_{\lambda} \approx 0.3671} $$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Regular Exam, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **spectral reflectivity**, **directional reflectivity**, and **hemispherical reflectivity** with neat mathematical expressions. Why are these distinctions important in engineering radiation analysis? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Spectral (Monochromatic) Reflectivity:**
The reflectivity at a single wavelength λ for radiation incident from a particular direction (θ, φ):

$$ \rho_{\lambda}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{I_{\lambda, \text{ref}}(\theta, \phi)}{I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi)} $$

**Directional Reflectivity:**
The reflectivity for radiation of all wavelengths incident from a specific direction (θ, φ), integrated over all wavelengths:

$$ \rho_{\theta}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \rho_{\lambda}(\theta, \phi) \, I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \, d\lambda} $$

**Hemispherical Reflectivity:**
The reflectivity averaged over all wavelengths and all incident directions in the hemisphere:

$$ \rho = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \rho_{\lambda}(\theta, \phi) \, I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_{\lambda, \text{inc}}(\theta, \phi) \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, d\lambda} $$

**Engineering Importance:**
1. **Selective Surfaces:** Solar absorbers require high ρ in infrared and low ρ in visible → need spectral analysis.
2. **Anisotropic Materials:** Crystalline or fibrous materials reflect differently along different directions → need directional analysis.
3. **Real-world design:** Engineering calculations require hemispherical averages to capture real solar/thermal loads.
4. **Validation:** Theory (e.g., Fresnel equations) gives directional values; measurements give hemispherical values → consistency requires careful conversion.

**(b)** A polished copper surface has the following spectral reflectivity at room temperature:

| $\lambda$ (μm) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{\lambda}$ | 0.45 | 0.65 | 0.82 | 0.93 |

The incident spectral irradiation $G_{\lambda}$ is approximately constant at $G_{\lambda} = 500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·μm}$ over the range 0 to 5 μm. Compute the hemispherical total reflectivity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the weighted average formula for hemispherical total reflectivity** (assuming uniform spectral irradiation $G_{\lambda}$):

$$ \rho = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \rho_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda} $$

**Step 2 — Use trapezoidal integration over the 4 intervals (each of width Δλ = 1 μm):**

Numerator:

$$ \int \rho_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda \approx \sum \rho_{\lambda} \cdot G_{\lambda} \cdot \Delta\lambda $$

$$ \approx (0.45 + 0.65)(0.5)(1) + (0.65 + 0.82)(0.5)(1) + (0.82 + 0.93)(0.5)(1) + (0.93)(0.5)(1) $$

Using trapezoidal rule more carefully:

$$ \approx (1/2)(0.45)(500)(1) + (1/2)(0.45 + 0.65)(500)(1) + (1/2)(0.65 + 0.82)(500)(1) + (1/2)(0.82 + 0.93)(500)(1) + (1/2)(0.93)(500)(1) $$

**Step 3 — Simplified summation using midpoint approach** (treating each value as representative of 1 μm band):

$$ G = (0.45 + 0.65 + 0.82 + 0.93) \times 500 \times 1 = 2.85 \times 500 = 1425 \, \text{W/m}^2 $$

$$ G_{\text{ref}} = (0.45 \times 500) + (0.65 \times 500) + (0.82 \times 500) + (0.93 \times 500) $$

$$ G_{\text{ref}} = 225 + 325 + 410 + 465 = 1425 \, \text{W/m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Compute hemispherical total reflectivity:**

$$ \rho = \frac{G_{\text{ref}}}{G} = \frac{1425}{1425} $$

$$ \boxed{\rho = 0.7125} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** If $G_\lambda$ is given in W/m²·μm, the integral must be over wavelength in μm, not meters. (3-mark penalty)
> 2. **Confusing spectral and total properties:** Students often use $\rho$ (total) where the problem gives $\rho_\lambda$ (spectral), losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring angle of incidence:** For specular surfaces, reflectivity depends strongly on angle. Use Fresnel equations or empirical charts.
> 4. **Forgetting Kirchhoff's Law application:** In equilibrium, $\alpha = \varepsilon$; this is often needed to combine absorption and emission data.
> 5. **Sign errors in Bouguer's Law derivation:** The negative sign in $dI = -KI\,dx$ is critical — losing this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Not handling two-surface reflections in thin films:** A glass sheet has TWO reflecting surfaces — neglecting this underestimates total absorptivity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Reflectivity and Transmittivity of Radiation:**
>
> - [ ] **Energy Balance:** Always remember $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ for semi-transparent; $\alpha + \rho = 1$ for opaque.
> - [ ] **Three Modes:** Absorbed (α), Reflected (ρ), Transmitted (τ) — they must sum to 1.
> - [ ] **Black Body:** α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0, ε = 1.
> - [ ] **White Body:** ρ = 1, α = 0, τ = 0.
> - [ ] **Transparent Body:** τ = 1, α = 0, ρ = 0.
> - [ ] **Kirchhoff's Law:** α = ε at thermal equilibrium — this is the critical bridge between absorption and emission.
> - [ ] **Bouguer's Law:** $\tau_{\lambda} = e^{-K_{\lambda} L}$ — controls how radiation decays through absorbing media.
> - [ ] **Three Levels of Property:** Spectral (per wavelength), Directional (per angle), Hemispherical (averaged).
> - [ ] **Specular vs Diffuse:** Specular obeys law of reflection; Diffuse obeys Lambert's cosine law.
> - [ ] **Engineering Uses:** Solar collectors (selective α), greenhouse films (high τ), Low-E windows (low ε).
> - [ ] **Units Check:** $K_\lambda$ in m⁻¹, $L$ in m, $\tau$ is dimensionless.
> - [ ] **Real Surface Reflectivity Examples:** Polished silver ≈ 0.97; Aluminum ≈ 0.85; White paint ≈ 0.80; Black paint ≈ 0.05.
> - [ ] **For two-reflecting-surface sheet:** Use $\tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{(1-\rho)^2 \tau_i}{1 - \rho^2 \tau_i^2}$ with multi-reflection correction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws of Radiation - Stefan-Boltzmann Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laws of Radiation - Stefan-Boltzmann Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Laws of Radiation: Stefan-Boltzmann Law

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** is a fundamental law of thermal radiation which states that the **total hemispherical emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute thermodynamic temperature**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$E_b = \sigma \, T^4$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = total hemispherical emissive power of the blackbody, in $\text{W/m}^2$
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $= \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the blackbody surface, in Kelvin (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fourth-power dependence on temperature is the single most important feature of this law. Doubling the absolute temperature of a radiating body increases its emitted energy by a factor of **$2^4 = 16$ times**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Law:** Josef Stefan proposed the $T^4$ relation experimentally in **1879** based on observations. Ludwig Boltzmann provided the theoretical thermodynamic derivation in **1884** using classical electromagnetism and thermodynamic reasoning. It was later rigorously confirmed from quantum mechanics via Planck's law.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why $T^4$ Matters"

Imagine you are sitting around a **red-hot campfire** on a cold winter night. The wood is glowing cherry-red, perhaps at around $800 \ \text{K}$. If you could magically pump in more oxygen and push the temperature of the same fire to $1600 \ \text{K}$ (twice the temperature), the radiant heat you receive from the fire would not double, triple, or even quadruple — it would become **sixteen times** more intense.

This is the essence of Stefan-Boltzmann's fourth-power law: **radiation is an extremely temperature-sensitive mode of heat transfer**, which is precisely why high-temperature furnaces, solar concentrators, and incandescent filaments rely on radiation as their dominant heat transfer mode.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{5.670374419 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$ |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | $\text{J} \cdot \text{s}$ |
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | $\text{J/K}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!TIP]
> For engineering problems in KTU examinations, the rounded value **$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$** is universally accepted unless otherwise specified in the question paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building the Concept Layer by Layer

To truly understand Stefan-Boltzmann Law, the student must walk through the following hierarchy of concepts:

- **Step 1 — Radiative Energy at a Point:** Every body at a temperature above absolute zero emits electromagnetic waves due to the thermal agitation of its molecules.
- **Step 2 — Spectral vs. Total Quantities:** Radiation is emitted over a *spectrum* of wavelengths. The quantity emitted per unit wavelength is called the *spectral emissive power* ($E_{b\lambda}$), while the *integrated total* over all wavelengths is the *total emissive power* ($E_b$).
- **Step 3 — Blackbody Idealization:** A *blackbody* is a perfect absorber and a perfect emitter. It absorbs **all** incident radiation (absorptivity $\alpha = 1$) and emits the maximum possible energy at every wavelength for a given temperature.
- **Step 4 — The Stefan-Boltzmann Identity:** Integrating the spectral emissive power over the entire wavelength range (from $0$ to $\infty$) yields the total emissive power, which collapses remarkably into the elegant $T^4$ form.
- **Step 5 — Extension to Real Bodies:** Real (non-black) bodies are characterized by an *emissivity* $\varepsilon$, defined as the ratio of emissive power of the real body to that of a blackbody at the same temperature.

## 2.2 Emissivity — The "Imperfection Factor"

For a **real (gray or non-gray) surface**, the Stefan-Boltzmann law is generalized as:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$

Where $\varepsilon$ is the *emissivity* of the surface, with $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kirchhoff's Law (Companion to Stefan-Boltzmann):** For a surface in thermal equilibrium, the emissivity equals the absorptivity at each wavelength: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. This is why good emitters are also good absorbers (e.g., lampblack: $\varepsilon \approx 0.97$), while polished metals are poor emitters and poor absorbers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** compilation for KTU ESE and internal examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Blackbody) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Real Surface Emissive Power | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | Emissivity Definition | $\varepsilon = E \, / \, E_b$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Heat Radiated by Surface Area $A$ | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W |
| 5 | Net Radiation Heat Transfer (Two Surfaces) | $Q_{net} = \sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W (blackbody, view factor = 1) |
| 6 | Net Radiation with Emissivities (parallel plates) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant in terms of $h,k,c$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$ | Universal constant |
| 8 | Planck Distribution (foundation) | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \right]}$ | $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The Stefan-Boltzmann law is the cornerstone of designing **furnaces, boilers, solar thermal collectors, nuclear reactor cooling, satellite thermal control, infrared thermography, pyrometers**, and **fire detection systems**. KTU examiners frequently pose numerical problems involving radiation exchange between two surfaces using this formula set.

## 2.4 Physical Reasoning Behind $T^4$

The fourth-power law is **not arbitrary** — it is the macroscopic signature of an underlying quantum-statistical truth. Boltzmann's classical derivation combined:
1. Maxwell's electromagnetic radiation pressure inside an enclosure, $P = u/3$.
2. The thermodynamic relation between internal energy density $u$ and temperature.
3. Integration over all wavelengths of standing waves inside a cavity.

The result is that radiation energy density scales as $T^4$, and since emissive power is proportional to energy density times the speed of light, we get $E_b = \sigma T^4$. This same relation is what makes the **Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB)** at $2.725 \ \text{K}$ radiate the unmistakable $T^4$ signature of the Big Bang's afterglow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law is rigorously derived by integrating the **Planck spectral distribution** of a blackbody over the entire wavelength spectrum from zero to infinity.

### Step 1 — Start with Planck's Spectral Emissive Power

Planck's law gives the spectral emissive power (energy per unit area per unit time per unit wavelength) of a blackbody at temperature $T$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \, \left[ e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1 \right]}$$

The constants in this relation are combined as:
- $C_1 = 2 \pi h c^2$ = first radiation constant
- $C_2 = \dfrac{h c}{k}$ = second radiation constant

Therefore:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \, \left[ e^{C_2 / \lambda T} - 1 \right]}$$

### Step 2 — Define Total Hemispherical Emissive Power

The **total emissive power** $E_b$ is the integral of the spectral emissive power over all wavelengths from zero to infinity:

$$E_b(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

Substituting Planck's expression:

$$E_b(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2 / \lambda T} - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Substitution

Let us introduce a dimensionless substitution to convert the integral into a recognizable standard form:

$$x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

This implies:

$$\lambda = \frac{C_2}{x T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\lambda = -\frac{C_2}{x^2 T} \, dx$$

When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$.

Therefore, the limits of integration reverse, absorbing the negative sign:

$$E_b(T) = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{x T}\right)^5 \left[ e^{x} - 1 \right]} \cdot \left( -\frac{C_2}{x^2 T} \right) dx$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Algebraic Expression

After flipping limits and simplifying:

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{C_2}{T} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^5 \cdot x^{-2}}{x^5 \left[ e^{x} - 1 \right]} \, dx$$

Combining the powers of $x$ (note: $C_1 T^5 / C_2^5 \cdot C_2 / T = C_1 T^4 / C_2^4$):

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 \, T^4}{C_2^4} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Standard Integral

The integral $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$ is a celebrated definite integral that appears throughout statistical mechanics and quantum theory.

Expanding $\dfrac{1}{e^x - 1}$ as a geometric series for $x > 0$:

$$\frac{1}{e^x - 1} = \frac{e^{-x}}{1 - e^{-x}} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-n x}$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} x^3 \, e^{-n x} \, dx$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate Using the Gamma Function

Using the standard result $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{m} e^{-n x} dx = \dfrac{\Gamma(m+1)}{n^{m+1}}$ with $m = 3$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} x^3 e^{-n x} dx = \frac{3!}{n^4} = \frac{6}{n^4}$$

Therefore the sum becomes:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{6}{n^4} = 6 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^4} = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The series $\displaystyle \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^4} = \dfrac{\pi^4}{90}$ is known as the **Basel Problem** (a famous result of Euler, 1734).

### Step 7 — Assemble the Final Result

Substituting back:

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

Now substitute $C_1 = 2 \pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = \dfrac{h c}{k}$:

$$\frac{C_1}{C_2^4} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\left( \dfrac{h c}{k} \right)^4} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2 \, k^4}{h^4 c^4} = \frac{2 \pi k^4}{h^3 c^2}$$

Thus:

$$E_b(T) = \frac{2 \pi k^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} \cdot T^4 = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \, T^4$$

Defining the Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma$:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4}$$

And the celebrated **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$

This is a **complete, rigorous derivation** from first principles — exactly the form KTU board examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Quick Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Governing Equation | When Applicable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single blackbody surface emitting into vacuum | $Q = \sigma A T^4$ | Small surface in large enclosure |
| Gray surface in large enclosure | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Real material surface |
| Two infinite parallel plates (blackbody) | $q = \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Perfect absorbers/emitters |
| Two infinite parallel plates (real surfaces) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | Practical engineering cases |
| Concentric cylinders (outer encloses inner) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$ | Cylindrical furnace geometry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Logical Hierarchy of the Stefan-Boltzmann Law

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy: starting from the microscopic quantum origin (Planck's law), through integration to the macroscopic Stefan-Boltzmann identity, and finally to engineering applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Quantum Origin Planck Distribution"] --> nodeB["Spectral Emissive Power E_b_lambda"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Integration over all wavelengths 0 to infinity"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Standard Integral pi to power 4 over 15"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Stefan-Boltzmann Constant sigma"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Blackbody Law E_b equals sigma T to power 4"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Real Surface E equals epsilon sigma T to power 4"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Engineering Applications Furnaces Solar Pyrometers"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Net Exchange Between Two Surfaces"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Parallel Plates"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Concentric Cylinders"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["View Factor F12 Formulations"]

    style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radiation Heat Exchange System

For complex physical geometries (which Mermaid cannot directly render as engineering drawings), the following block diagram captures the system-level architecture of a radiation heat-exchange problem typical of KTU questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1["HOT SURFACE T1 epsilon 1"] --> emit1["EMITS q1 equals epsilon 1 sigma T1 to power 4"]
    src2["COLD SURFACE T2 epsilon 2"] --> emit2["EMITS q2 equals epsilon 2 sigma T2 to power 4"]
    emit1 --> mid["INTERVENING MEDIUM usually vacuum or air"]
    mid --> absorb1["Absorbed by Cold Surface alpha 2 equals epsilon 2"]
    emit2 --> mid
    mid --> absorb2["Absorbed by Hot Surface alpha 1 equals epsilon 1"]
    absorb1 --> net["NET HEAT TRANSFER q_net equals sigma T1 to power 4 minus T2 to power 4 divided by radiation resistance"]
    absorb2 --> net
    net --> out["RESULT Q equals q_net times Area A in Watts"]

    style src1 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style src2 fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style net fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000
    style out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the T to the Power 4 Behavior

A qualitative sketch of how emissive power explodes with temperature is captured below as a numerical snapshot table (which mirrors what a Mermaid plot would show):

| Temperature T (K) | $T^4$ | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ ($\text{W/m}^2$) | Physical Context |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 300 | $8.1 \times 10^9$ | 459 | Human body / room temp |
| 500 | $6.25 \times 10^{10}$ | 3544 | Oven / toaster |
| 1000 | $1.0 \times 10^{12}$ | 56700 | Furnace / molten metal |
| 2000 | $1.6 \times 10^{13}$ | 907200 | Tungsten filament / lava |
| 5800 | $1.13 \times 10^{15}$ | $6.4 \times 10^7$ | Surface of the Sun |

This table vividly demonstrates why the Sun, despite being physically far away, dominates Earth's energy budget — its temperature is roughly $20 \times$ that of room temperature, so its emissive power per unit area is $20^4 = 160000 \times$ larger.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Mention its significance in heat transfer.**

**Model Answer:**
The Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total hemispherical emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. Mathematically, $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.
**Significance:** It is the foundational equation of all radiation heat transfer calculations. It quantifies how rapidly a body's emitted radiative energy grows with temperature, making it indispensable for designing furnaces, solar collectors, boilers, and thermal insulation systems.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define emissivity. How is it related to absorptivity for a body in thermal equilibrium?**

**Model Answer:**
Emissivity $\varepsilon$ of a surface is defined as the ratio of its total hemispherical emissive power to that of a blackbody at the same temperature: $\varepsilon = E / E_b$, where $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.
For a body in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings, **Kirchhoff's Law** states that the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity at every wavelength and temperature: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. This means a good emitter is also a good absorber.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann Law by integrating Planck's spectral distribution over the entire wavelength spectrum. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
We start with Planck's spectral emissive power:
$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1)}$$

Total emissive power:
$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)} \, d\lambda$$

Substitute $x = C_2 / (\lambda T)$, so $\lambda = C_2 / (xT)$ and $d\lambda = -C_2/(x^2 T) \, dx$. The limits flip from $(\infty, 0)$ to $(0, \infty)$, removing the negative sign:

$$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{C_2}{T} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$$

Expanding $\dfrac{1}{e^x - 1} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-nx}$ and integrating term by term using $\int_{0}^{\infty} x^3 e^{-nx} dx = 6/n^4$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = 6 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^4} = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

Substituting back $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k$:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{(hc/k)^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} \cdot T^4 = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. ∎

**(b) Two large parallel plates separated by a large distance have temperatures $T_1 = 1000 \ \text{K}$ and $T_2 = 400 \ \text{K}$, and emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.4$ respectively. Calculate the net heat transfer rate per unit area between the plates. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Given:
- $T_1 = 1000 \ \text{K}$, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$
- $T_2 = 400 \ \text{K}$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.4$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

For two infinite parallel plates, net heat flux is:

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{0.4} - 1 = 1.667 + 2.5 - 1 = 3.167$$

Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 = (400)^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} \ \text{K}^4$$

Compute numerator:

$$\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10})$$
$$= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11} = 5.525 \times 10^{4} \ \text{W/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$q = \frac{5.525 \times 10^{4}}{3.167} = 1.745 \times 10^{4} \ \text{W/m}^2 \approx 17.45 \ \text{kW/m}^2$$

**Answer:** $q \approx 17.45 \ \text{kW/m}^2$ flowing from plate 1 to plate 2. ∎

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) State and explain the laws of blackbody radiation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The complete theoretical description of blackbody radiation rests upon four foundational laws:

**1. Planck's Law (1901):** The spectral distribution of emissive power of a blackbody is:
$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)}$$
This law unified the Rayleigh-Jeans and Wien approximations into a single expression valid across all wavelengths.

**2. Wien's Displacement Law (1893):** The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is maximum is inversely proportional to absolute temperature:
$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{K}$$
This explains why hot bodies shift from red (low T) to white-blue (very high T).

**3. Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1879/1884):** The total emissive power is proportional to the fourth power of absolute temperature: $E_b = \sigma T^4$.

**4. Kirchhoff's Law (1860):** For a body in thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$.

These four laws collectively describe the emission, spectral distribution, peak location, and equilibrium behavior of blackbody radiation.

**(b) A furnace has a cylindrical inner surface of diameter $0.6 \ \text{m}$ and length $1 \ \text{m}$, maintained at a temperature of $1200 \ \text{K}$ with emissivity $0.85$. It is enclosed by a much larger surrounding surface at $300 \ \text{K}$. Calculate the net rate of heat loss from the furnace. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Given:
- Diameter $D = 0.6 \ \text{m}$, length $L = 1 \ \text{m}$
- $T_1 = 1200 \ \text{K}$, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.85$
- $T_2 = 300 \ \text{K}$, surroundings are large ($\varepsilon_2 \approx 1$, $A_2 \gg A_1$)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

Inner cylindrical surface area:
$$A_1 = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.6 \times 1 = 1.885 \ \text{m}^2$$

Since the surroundings are very large ($A_2 \gg A_1$), the formula for a small surface inside a large enclosure simplifies to:

$$Q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma A_1 (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:
$$T_1^4 = (1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9} \ \text{K}^4$$

Difference:
$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 0.0081 \times 10^{12} \approx 2.0655 \times 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$

Final calculation:

$$Q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.885 \times 2.0655 \times 10^{12}$$
$$Q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 1.885 \times 2.0655 \times 10^{4}$$
$$Q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 1.885 \times 20655$$
$$Q \approx 0.85 \times 220727 \approx 187618 \ \text{W}$$

**Answer:** $Q \approx 187.6 \ \text{kW}$. ∎

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Temperature Units Error:** Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin. This single mistake can wipe out 100% of the marks in a numerical. *Always work in Kelvin.*
> 2. **Missing Emissivity Factor:** Using $E_b = \sigma T^4$ for a real surface instead of $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. KTU examiners specifically test whether students distinguish blackbody vs. real body.
> 3. **Wrong Resistance Formula:** For parallel plates with emissivities, students frequently write $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2$ in the numerator instead of the proper radiation resistance $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$.
> 4. **Neglecting the Surroundings' Contribution:** Forgetting to subtract $T_2^4$ (i.e., only using $T_1^4$). The net radiation always involves a difference of $T^4$ terms.
> 5. **Constant Value:** Using $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ incorrectly (e.g., $5.67 \times 10^{-6}$). Memorize it as $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
> 6. **In derivation questions:** Skipping the substitution step or the Basel Problem evaluation. KTU examiners *demand* every intermediate step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Preparation:**

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** is $E_b = \sigma T^4$ for **blackbodies**; $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ for **real bodies**.
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: **$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$**.
- Always use **absolute temperature in Kelvin**.
- The **fourth power** is the most distinctive feature — temperature sensitivity is extreme.
- **Emissivity $\varepsilon$** = ratio of real emissive power to blackbody emissive power, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for bodies in thermal equilibrium.
- **Planck's Law** is the foundation; integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths gives Stefan-Boltzmann.
- The standard integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$ is essential for derivations.
- For **two infinite parallel plates** (with emissivities): $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$.
- For a **small object in a large enclosure**: $Q = \varepsilon_1 \sigma A_1 (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.
- Engineering applications: furnaces, solar collectors, pyrometers, IR cameras, satellite thermal control.
- The law is named after **Josef Stefan** (experimental, 1879) and **Ludwig Boltzmann** (theoretical, 1884).
- A common derivation trick: use the substitution $x = C_2 / (\lambda T)$ and the **Basel Problem** result $\sum \frac{1}{n^4} = \frac{\pi^4}{90}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kirchhoff's Law - Black Body Radiation - Grey Body Radiation - Shape Factor Algebra - Solid Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kirchhoff's Law - Black Body Radiation - Grey Body Radiation - Shape Factor Algebra - Solid Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation states that *at thermal equilibrium, the ratio of the total emissive power of a body to its total absorptivity is constant and is equal to the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature*. Mathematically, for a body at temperature $T$:

$$\frac{E(T)}{\alpha(T)} = E_b(T)$$

For spectral (monochromatic) form, the law holds for each wavelength and direction:

$$\frac{E_\lambda(T)}{\alpha_\lambda(T)} = E_{b,\lambda}(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Consequence:** A good emitter is also a good absorber. A surface that strongly emits radiation at a given wavelength must also strongly absorb radiation at the same wavelength when in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a crowded marketplace. A shop that loudly advertises its products (good *emitter*) must also be willing to receive customer inquiries and payments willingly (good *absorber*). If it only shouts but refuses to listen, no business happens and it eventually falls silent. Similarly, a thermal surface that strongly radiates energy *must* also be highly capable of absorbing the same kind of energy, otherwise thermal equilibrium would be impossible.

---

### 1.2 Black Body Radiation

**Formal Definition:**
A **black body** is an idealized physical body that absorbs *all* incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. By Kirchhoff's law, it is also the *perfect emitter* at every wavelength.

- Spectral absorptivity: $\alpha_\lambda = 1$ for all $\lambda$
- Total absorptivity: $\alpha = 1$
- Spectral emissivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ for all $\lambda$
- Total emissivity: $\varepsilon = 1$

A black body is therefore a **diffuse, perfect emitter** obeying **Lambert's Cosine Law**, meaning the intensity of emitted radiation is uniform in all directions of the hemisphere.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A black body is like a perfect "sponge" of light. Just as a perfect black sponge soaks up every drop of water that touches it (and is therefore the *best possible* source of dampness if you squeeze it), a black body swallows every photon of every wavelength hitting it, and consequently radiates more energy than any real surface at the same temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Black Body Radiation Laws (KTU Must-Know):**
> 1. **Planck's Law** – Spectral distribution of black-body radiation
> 2. **Wien's Displacement Law** – Peak wavelength relation
> 3. **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** – Total emitted power
> 4. **Lambert's Cosine Law** – Directional distribution

---

### 1.3 Grey Body Radiation

**Formal Definition:**
A **grey body** is a surface whose **monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is constant (independent of wavelength)** over the spectral range of interest. Its total emissivity $\varepsilon$ is also constant and lies between 0 and 1:

$$0 < \varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} < 1$$

The total emissive power of a grey body is:

$$E = \varepsilon \, E_b = \varepsilon \, \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 \cdot K^4}$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A grey body is like a singer with a "flat" voice — neither perfectly loud (black body) nor completely silent (white/reflector). It performs the same way at every pitch (wavelength). Most engineering surfaces (oxidized metals, painted surfaces) behave as grey bodies over the wavelength range of typical thermal radiation (0.1–100 µm).

---

### 1.4 Shape Factor (View Factor) Algebra

**Formal Definition:**
The **shape factor** (or **view factor** or **configuration factor**), denoted $F_{i \to j}$ or simply $F_{ij}$, is the fraction of the total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly, without being reflected or re-emitted by any intermediate surface.

$$F_{ij} = \frac{\text{Radiation leaving surface } i \text{ that directly reaches surface } j}{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine standing at one end of a long hall holding a glowing lantern. The *shape factor* tells you "what fraction of your lantern's light actually lands on the opposite wall," accounting for the geometry of the room, the distance, and the angles involved. It is purely a *geometric* property — independent of surface temperatures or materials.

---

### 1.5 Solid Angle

**Formal Definition:**
The **solid angle** $\omega$ (omega) is the two-dimensional angular measure subtended by a surface as seen from a point. It is the 3-D analogue of the planar angle. The SI unit is the **steradian (sr)**.

For a finite area $A$ observed from a point at distance $r$:

$$\omega = \frac{A}{r^2} \quad \text{[steradians]}$$

For an infinitesimal area $dA$ inclined at angle $\theta$ to the line of sight:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A planar angle (in radians) measures "how wide" a line is from a point (length ÷ radius). A solid angle does the *same thing in 3-D* — it measures "how wide" a patch of area is from a point (projected area ÷ squared distance). A full hemisphere covers $2\pi$ sr; a full sphere covers $4\pi$ sr.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Solid Angles Matter in Radiation:** Every radiation heat transfer equation ultimately comes from integrating the radiant intensity over the solid angle subtended by the receiving surface. Shape factors are dimensionless "normalized solid angles."

---

### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s}$ |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$ |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K}$ |
| Solid angle of hemisphere | $\omega_{hemi}$ | $2\pi$ sr |
| Solid angle of full sphere | $\omega_{sphere}$ | $4\pi$ sr |
| Wien's displacement constant | $b$ | $\mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Kirchhoff's Law

**The Physical Setup (Why):**
Consider a small body $S$ enclosed inside a large isothermal enclosure at temperature $T$. The cavity walls act as a black body (since the cavity absorbs all radiation incident on it). The body $S$ and the cavity are in **thermal equilibrium** — meaning no net heat transfer occurs.

**The Energy Balance (How):**
1. The body $S$ emits radiation at a rate $E \cdot A$ (emissive power × area).
2. The body $S$ absorbs a fraction $\alpha$ of the radiation incident on it from the cavity walls.
3. The radiation incident on $S$ equals the black-body emissive power of the cavity walls, $E_b$, multiplied by $A$ (since the cavity is at uniform temperature $T$).

For thermal equilibrium (no net heat gain/loss):

$$E \cdot A = \alpha \cdot E_b \cdot A$$

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$E = \alpha \, E_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{E}{\alpha} = E_b$$

This is **Kirchhoff's Law**. Since $E_b$ depends only on the temperature of the cavity (a black-body property), the ratio $E/\alpha$ must be the same for *all* bodies at the same temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> Defining **emissivity** as $\varepsilon = E / E_b$, we immediately obtain $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for any body in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings.

---

### 2.2 Black Body Laws — Detailed Breakdown

**Planck's Law (Spectral Distribution):**
The spectral emissive power of a black body at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$:

$$E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1 \right]}$$

where $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = \mathbf{1.191 \times 10^{-16} \, W \cdot m^2}$ and $C_2 = h c / k_B = \mathbf{1.439 \times 10^{-2} \, m \cdot K}$.

**Wien's Displacement Law:**
The wavelength at which the black-body spectrum peaks:

$$\lambda_{max} \, T = b = \mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K}$$

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Emissive Power):**
By integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b,\lambda} \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$

**Lambert's Cosine Law:**
The intensity (radiance) of a black body is independent of direction. The emissive power leaving a differential area in a given direction:

$$dE_b = I_b \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

where $I_b$ is the **black-body intensity** (W/m²·sr), and $\theta$ is the angle from the surface normal.

For integration over the entire hemisphere:

$$E_b = \int_{\text{hemi}} I_b \cos\theta \, d\omega = I_b \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \pi I_b$$

Therefore:

$$I_b = \frac{E_b}{\pi} = \frac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$$

---

### 2.3 Grey Body Analysis

For a grey body, since $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant}$, the spectral distribution has the *same shape* as a black body's spectrum — just scaled by $\varepsilon$:

$$E_\lambda = \varepsilon \, E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

Total emissive power:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma T^4$$

The total absorptivity also equals $\varepsilon$ (by Kirchhoff). This is why engineers use a single $\varepsilon$ value in calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral vs. Total Emissivity:** A real surface can have $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varying with $\lambda$. The total emissivity is the *spectral average* weighted by the black-body spectrum at that temperature. This is why tabulated $\varepsilon$ values are valid *only* at the temperature for which they were measured.

---

### 2.4 Shape Factor (View Factor) Algebra — Rules

**Definition Restated:**

$$F_{ij} = \frac{1}{A_i} \int_{A_i} \int_{A_j} \frac{\cos\theta_i \cos\theta_j}{\pi r^2} \, dA_j \, dA_i$$

This integral evaluates the *geometric* view from $dA_i$ to $dA_j$.

**The Four Cardinal Rules of Shape Factor Algebra:**

1. **Reciprocity Rule (Clausius):**
$$A_i \, F_{ij} = A_j \, F_{ji}$$

2. **Summation Rule (Closure):**
For a closed enclosure surface $i$ enclosing $N$ surfaces:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$$

3. **Superposition Rule:**
For a composite surface $M = (i + k)$:
$$A_M \, F_{M,j} = A_i \, F_{ij} + A_k \, F_{kj}$$

4. **Symmetry Rule:**
If geometry is symmetric about a plane, surfaces have equal view factors.

---

### 2.5 Solid Angle — Derivation Logic

**Differential Solid Angle in Spherical Coordinates:**

$$d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**For a Differential Area $dA_j$ at Distance $r$ Tilted at $\theta$:**
Project $dA_j$ onto a sphere of radius $r$; the projected area is $dA_j \cos\theta_j$. Thus:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA_j \cos\theta_j}{r^2}$$

**Linkage to Shape Factors:**
The shape factor can be expressed as an integral over solid angle. The fraction of energy leaving $dA_i$ that reaches $dA_j$ in a direction $\theta_i$ within a *cone of solid angle* $d\omega$ subtended at $dA_i$ by $dA_j$:

$$dF_{dA_i \to dA_j} = \frac{\cos\theta_i \, d\omega}{\pi}$$

where the denominator $\pi$ represents the total projected solid angle of the hemisphere (because $\int_{\text{hemi}} \cos\theta \, d\omega = \pi$).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all formulas below, the symbol `\vert` is used wherever absolute value might appear, to maintain LaTeX–Markdown compatibility.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kirchhoff's Law (spectral) | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Kirchhoff's Law (total, grey) | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Holds strictly only for grey/diffuse surfaces |
| 3 | Stefan–Boltzmann (black) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ |
| 4 | Stefan–Boltzmann (grey) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² |
| 5 | Planck's Distribution | $E_{b,\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \right]}$ | W/m²·µm or W/m³ |
| 6 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max} \, T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K |
| 7 | Lambert's Cosine Intensity | $I_b = E_b / \pi$ | W/m²·sr |
| 8 | Black-body intensity | $I_b = \sigma T^4 / \pi$ | W/m²·sr |
| 9 | Differential solid angle | $d\omega = dA \cos\theta / r^2$ | sr |
| 10 | Solid angle (full sphere) | $\omega = 4\pi$ | sr |
| 11 | Solid angle (hemisphere) | $\omega = 2\pi$ | sr |
| 12 | View factor (definition) | $F_{ij} = Q_{i \to j} / Q_{i, \text{emitted}}$ | Dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| 13 | Reciprocity | $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$ | $0 \le F \le 1$ |
| 14 | Summation (enclosure) | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$ | Closed cavity only |
| 15 | Superposition | $A_M F_{M,j} = A_i F_{ij} + A_k F_{kj}$ | $M = i \cup k$ |
| 16 | Two parallel infinite plates | $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$ | Both sides see each other fully |
| 17 | Concentric cylinders/spheres (inner 1, outer 2) | $F_{12} = 1$, $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$ | Inner fully "sees" outer |
| 18 | Identical parallel rectangles (coeff) | $F_{12}$ from Hottel's chart or string method | Geometric lookup |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Kirchhoff's Law | Selective solar absorbers (high $\alpha$ for sun, low $\varepsilon$ for IR) — used in solar water heaters and spacecraft thermal control |
| Black Body | Furnace design, calibration of radiation pyrometers, calibration sources for IR cameras |
| Grey Body Approximation | Furnace linings, building heat loss calculations, turbine blade cooling |
| Shape Factor | Solar collector geometry, furnace interior design, cryogenic insulation layout, spacecraft radiator placement |
| Solid Angle | Radiation dosimetry, infrared sensor design, antenna gain analysis |
