U_s \cdot A_s = -\frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right)}{\Psi}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $q$ (since $\Psi q = 1$):

$$
U_s \cdot A_s = \frac{q \cdot \left[ \ln(\Delta T_1) - \ln(\Delta T_2) \right]}{1}
$$

$$
q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

### Step 9 — Identify the LMTD Term

$$
\boxed{\ \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}\ }
$$

This logarithmic mean is **always between the arithmetic mean and the smaller of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$**.

### Step 10 — Arithmetic Mean as the Limiting Case

When $\Delta T_1 \to \Delta T_2$, applying L'Hôpital's rule:

$$
\lim_{\Delta T_1 \to \Delta T_2} \Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2
$$

The LMTD correctly collapses to the constant temperature difference.

## 3.2 Special Case — Counter Flow vs Parallel Flow Comparison

| Property | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum possible $T_{c,o}$ | Approaches $T_{h,o}$ but cannot exceed | Can **exceed** $T_{h,o}$ |
| LMTD value | Smaller | **Larger** (for same inlet/outlet temps) |
| Heat transfer area required | Larger | **Smaller** (more efficient) |
| Used in practice | Rare | **Standard for design** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Proven fact from calculus:** For the *same* four terminal temperatures, the **counter-flow LMTD is always greater than or equal to the parallel-flow LMTD**. This is why counter-flow is preferred — it requires less surface area for the same duty.

## 3.3 Correction Factor F for Multi-Pass Exchangers

For multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchangers (1-2, 2-4, etc.), the LMTD of a counter-flow arrangement $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is computed, then corrected by an empirical factor $F$:

$$
\Delta T_{m} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}
$$

$$
q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}
$$

The factor $F$ depends on two dimensionless groups:

$$
P = \frac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}} \quad ; \quad R = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}
$$

$F$ is read from **standard charts** (provided in KTU data handbook for shell-and-tube 1-2 and 2-4 arrangements).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LMTD Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Heat Exchanger Problem] --> B{Identify Flow Configuration}
    B --> C1[Parallel Flow]
    B --> C2[Counter Flow]
    B --> C3[Multi-pass 1-2 / 2-4]

    C1 --> D1[Compute dT1 and dT2 at both ends]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1

    D1 --> E[Apply LMTD Formula]
    E --> F[Compute DeltaTlm Value]

    F --> G{Problem Type?}
    G --> G1[Rating: Given A, find q]
    G --> G2[Sizing: Given q, find A]

    C3 --> H[Compute P and R Parameters]
    H --> I[Read F from Chart or Table]
    I --> J[Apply F Correction Factor]
    J --> K[Corrected DeltaTm equals F times LMTDcf]
    K --> L[Use in Final Heat Transfer Equation]

    F --> G1
    F --> G2
    K --> G1
    K --> G2

    G1 --> M[Output: Heat Transfer Rate q]
    G2 --> N[Output: Required Surface Area As]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Temperature Endpoint Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOTFLUID[Hot Fluid Stream]
        H1[Hot Inlet Thi] --> H2[Hot Outlet Tho]
    end
    subgraph COLDFLUID[Cold Fluid Stream]
        K1[Cold Inlet Tci] --> K2[Cold Outlet Tco]
    end

    H1 -->|delta1 Parallel| P1[dT1 equals Thi minus Tci]
    K1 --> P1
    H2 -->|delta2 Parallel| P2[dT2 equals Tho minus Tco]
    K2 --> P2

    H1 -->|delta1 Counter| Q1[dT1 equals Thi minus Tco]
    K2 --> Q1
    H2 -->|delta2 Counter| Q2[dT2 equals Tho minus Tci]
    K1 --> Q2
```

## 4.3 Functional Mapping — LMTD Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Stage 1: Inputs]
        I1[Thi, Tho, Tci, Tco in C or K]
        I2[Overall U in W per m2 K]
        I3[Surface Area As in m2]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Stage 2: LMTD Computation]
        S1[Identify End Temperature Differences]
        S2[Compute dT1 and dT2]
        S3[Apply Natural Logarithm]
        S4[Output DeltaTlm in K]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Stage 3: Final Result]
        O1[Rating Mode: q equals U times As times DeltaTlm]
        O2[Sizing Mode: As equals q divided by U times DeltaTlm]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> O1
    S4 --> O2
    I2 --> O1
    I3 --> O1
    I2 --> O2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The flowcharts above represent the **logical decision tree** a KTU student must follow. For drawing physical temperature profiles in the answer script, students are expected to sketch by hand: a vertical vs horizontal axis showing $T$ vs $A$, with the hot fluid curve descending and the cold fluid curve ascending — these sketches frequently fetch 1–2 extra marks in descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Under what conditions is the LMTD method preferred over the Effectiveness-NTU method?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is defined as the temperature difference that, if held constant along the entire heat exchanger surface, would transfer the same amount of heat as the actual variable temperature difference.

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

The LMTD method is preferred over the $\varepsilon$-NTU method when:

1. **Both inlet and outlet temperatures of the hot and cold fluids are known** (rating problem).
2. The fluid flow rates are specified.
3. The heat exchanger is to be **sized** (area to be determined) or its duty **rated**.
4. The heat exchanger type (parallel, counter, or multi-pass) is fixed in advance.

In contrast, $\varepsilon$-NTU is preferred when only inlet temperatures are known and an outlet temperature is to be predicted, particularly for direct comparison of different heat exchanger configurations. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **State the assumptions made in the derivation of the LMTD. Why is counter-flow preferred over parallel-flow?**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions in LMTD Derivation:**

1. The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is **constant** along the entire length of the heat exchanger (does not vary with position or temperature).
2. The specific heats of both fluids ($C_{p,h}$ and $C_{p,c}$) are **constant** over the temperature range encountered.
3. **No phase change** occurs in either the hot or cold fluid within the exchanger.
4. **No heat loss** to the surroundings; the exchanger is perfectly insulated.
5. The flow is steady-state and either strictly parallel or strictly counter-current.
6. Longitudinal conduction along the fluids and the wall is negligible.

**Counter-flow is preferred over parallel-flow because:**

- For the **same inlet and outlet temperatures**, counter-flow produces a **higher LMTD**, which yields greater heat transfer for the same surface area (or requires less area for the same duty).
- In counter-flow, the **cold fluid outlet temperature can exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature**, a feature impossible in parallel-flow. This makes it ideal for **heating** applications (e.g., feedwater heaters) and **cooling** applications (e.g., condensers). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students must answer **one full question from Module 4** (with internal choice). Two fully solved alternatives are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to condense steam at atmospheric pressure. Saturated steam enters at **100 °C** with an enthalpy of vaporization of **2257 kJ/kg**, and the mass flow rate is **0.5 kg/s**. Cooling water enters at **20 °C** and leaves at **50 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **2000 W/m²·K**. Determine:
   (i) The heat transfer rate.
   (ii) The LMTD.
   (iii) The required heat transfer surface area.
                                                                                     **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What are the limitations of the LMTD method? Explain the necessity of the correction factor $F$ for multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchangers.                                                                                                              **[7 Marks]**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a)** `[7 Marks]`

**Given Data:**
- Steam (hot fluid): saturated at $T_{h,i} = T_{h,o} = 100\ °\text{C}$ (condensing, isothermal)
- Enthalpy of vaporization: $h_{fg} = 2257\ \text{kJ/kg} = 2.257 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$
- Steam mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_h = 0.5\ \text{kg/s}$
- Cooling water: $T_{c,i} = 20\ °\text{C}$, $T_{c,o} = 50\ °\text{C}$
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U_s = 2000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Configuration: **Counter-flow**

**Step 1 — Compute Heat Transfer Rate $q$:**

Since the steam condenses (phase change), the heat released is from condensation only:

$$
q = \dot{m}_h \cdot h_{fg} = 0.5 \times 2.257 \times 10^6
$$

$$
q = 1.1285 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 1128.5\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the End Temperature Differences:**

For **counter-flow** configuration:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 100 - 50 = 50\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 100 - 20 = 80\ °\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} = \frac{50 - 80}{\ln(50/80)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{-30}{\ln(0.625)} = \frac{-30}{-0.4700}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = 63.83\ °\text{C} \approx 63.83\ \text{K}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Required Surface Area $A_s$:**

$$
A_s = \frac{q}{U_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{1.1285 \times 10^6}{2000 \times 63.83}
$$

$$
A_s = \frac{1.1285 \times 10^6}{127660}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_s = 8.84\ \text{m}^2}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- $q = 1128.5\ \text{kW}$
- $\Delta T_{lm} = 63.83\ \text{K}$
- $A_s = 8.84\ \text{m}^2$

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b)** `[7 Marks]`

**Limitations of the LMTD Method:**

1. **Requires known terminal temperatures:** The LMTD method needs all four terminal temperatures ($T_{h,i}$, $T_{h,o}$, $T_{c,i}$, $T_{c,o}$) as input. It cannot be used directly when an outlet temperature is unknown (the $\varepsilon$-NTU method is then used).

2. **Constant $U$ assumption:** The derivation assumes $U$ is constant along the exchanger. In real systems, $U$ varies significantly with position due to changes in fluid properties and flow regimes (especially during phase change), reducing LMTD accuracy.

3. **Constant $C_p$ assumption:** Specific heats are assumed constant, which is only valid for narrow temperature ranges. For large temperature spans, the variation of $C_p$ introduces error.

4. **No phase change assumption:** The standard LMTD derivation assumes sensible heat transfer only. For condensing or boiling fluids, the standard energy balance needs modification.

5. **Inefficiency for cross-flow and multi-pass exchangers:** For complex geometries (1-2, 2-4, cross-flow), the simple LMTD is not directly applicable and requires the empirical correction factor $F$.

**Necessity of Correction Factor $F$:**

For multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchangers (1-shell pass, 2-tube pass; 2-shell pass, 4-tube pass), the flow is neither pure parallel nor pure counter-flow. The temperature profile is more complex, and the simple LMTD of a counter-flow arrangement overestimates the true mean temperature difference.

Therefore, a **correction factor $F$** is introduced:

$$
\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}
$$

The factor $F$ is empirically determined and is a function of two dimensionless parameters:

- $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}}$ — thermal effectiveness of the hot fluid
- $R = \dfrac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}$ — heat capacity ratio

$F$ values are read from **standard charts (LMTD correction factor plots)** provided in textbooks or the KTU data handbook. The factor $F$ always lies between **0 and 1**, with $F = 1$ representing the pure counter-flow case.

The **corrected** heat transfer equation is:

$$
q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}
$$

This correction is mandatory in real-world TEMA-standard shell-and-tube designs. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **QUESTION B** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** In a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with **1 shell pass and 2 tube passes**, oil is cooled from **150 °C** to **100 °C** by water entering at **20 °C** and leaving at **50 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **1500 W/m²·K**. The correction factor $F$ for this configuration, based on the given terminal temperatures, is **0.88**.
   (i) Calculate the LMTD based on a counter-flow assumption.
   (ii) Compute the actual $\Delta T_m$ using the correction factor.
   (iii) Determine the rate of heat transfer if the heat exchanger surface area is **5 m²**.
                                                                                     **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Two fluid streams exchange heat in a parallel-flow heat exchanger. The hot fluid enters at **200 °C** and leaves at **120 °C**, while the cold fluid enters at **40 °C** and leaves at **110 °C**. Prove analytically that the **counter-flow LMTD would be greater** than the parallel-flow LMTD for the same four terminal temperatures.                                                                                                       **[7 Marks]**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a)** `[7 Marks]`

**Given Data:**
- Configuration: 1 shell pass, 2 tube passes (1-2)
- Oil (hot): $T_{h,i} = 150\ °\text{C}$, $T_{h,o} = 100\ °\text{C}$
- Water (cold): $T_{c,i} = 20\ °\text{C}$, $T_{c,o} = 50\ °\text{C}$
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U_s = 1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Correction factor: $F = 0.88$
- Surface area: $A_s = 5\ \text{m}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute End Temperature Differences for Counter-flow Reference:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 50 = 100\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 100 - 20 = 80\ °\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Counter-flow LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{100 - 80}{\ln(100/80)} = \frac{20}{\ln(1.25)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{20}{0.22314}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lm,CF} = 89.62\ °\text{C}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Correction Factor:**

$$
\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 0.88 \times 89.62
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_m = 78.87\ °\text{C}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$
q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_m = 1500 \times 5 \times 78.87
$$

$$
q = 591525\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{q = 591.5\ \text{kW}}
$$

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b)** `[7 Marks]`

**Given Terminal Temperatures:**
- Hot fluid: $T_{h,i} = 200\ °\text{C}$, $T_{h,o} = 120\ °\text{C}$
- Cold fluid: $T_{c,i} = 40\ °\text{C}$, $T_{c,o} = 110\ °\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Compute Parallel-flow LMTD:**

In parallel-flow, both fluids enter at the same end:

$$
\Delta T_1^{PF} = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} = 200 - 40 = 160\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2^{PF} = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} = 120 - 110 = 10\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm}^{PF} = \frac{160 - 10}{\ln(160/10)} = \frac{150}{\ln(16)} = \frac{150}{2.7726}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm}^{PF} = 54.10\ °\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Counter-flow LMTD (Same Terminal Temperatures):**

In counter-flow, fluids enter at opposite ends:

$$
\Delta T_1^{CF} = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 200 - 110 = 90\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2^{CF} = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 120 - 40 = 80\ °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm}^{CF} = \frac{90 - 80}{\ln(90/80)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.125)} = \frac{10}{0.11778}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm}^{CF} = 84.90\ °\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$
\Delta T_{lm}^{CF} = 84.90\ °\text{C} \quad > \quad \Delta T_{lm}^{PF} = 54.10\ °\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Analytical Reasoning (Why?):**

The logarithmic mean function is **monotonically increasing and concave** with respect to the smaller endpoint. The counter-flow arrangement "balances" the two endpoint temperature differences (90 and 80), keeping the LMTD close to both values. The parallel-flow arrangement has widely unequal endpoints (160 and 10), which makes the LMTD drop significantly closer to the smaller value.

Mathematically, the ratio of endpoints in parallel-flow ($\dfrac{160}{10} = 16$) is much larger than in counter-flow ($\dfrac{90}{80} = 1.125$). A larger ratio means a larger logarithm in the denominator, and since the numerator is fixed, the LMTD is smaller. Therefore, **counter-flow LMTD is always greater** for the same terminal temperatures. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in LMTD Problems:**
>
> 1. **Endpoint Misidentification:** Students frequently swap $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$, or take both from the same physical end in counter-flow. **Always assign $\Delta T_1$ to the side where hot fluid INLET meets cold fluid INLET in parallel-flow, and where hot fluid INLET meets cold fluid OUTLET in counter-flow.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Correction Factor:** In multi-pass shell-and-tube problems, neglecting $F$ leads to an **overestimation of $q$ by 10–30%**. This is the single most common mistake; the KTU examiner deducts **2–3 marks** outright.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Using $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kg but mass flow in kg/s without conversion. Always use **SI-consistent units** (W, J/kg, K, m²).
>
> 4. **Sign of $\Delta T$:** Always keep $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ (the larger divided by the smaller). Otherwise, the logarithm becomes negative, and the LMTD becomes negative (unphysical). **Convention:** $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ by definition.
>
> 5. **Phase Change Confusion:** During condensation/evaporation, the fluid temperature is **isothermal** — students incorrectly assume a temperature drop and overcomplicate the calculation. Use $q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ directly.
>
> 6. **$\Delta T_{lm}$ vs $\Delta T_m$:** The corrected value $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is what goes into the final $q$ equation — not the raw counter-flow LMTD alone.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **LMTD Method — Final Revision Bullet Sheet:**
>
> - **Core Equation:** $q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$
> - **LMTD Formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — ensure $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$.
> - **Limiting Case:** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, then $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1$ (constant $\Delta T$).
> - **Parallel-flow endpoints:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ ; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$.
> - **Counter-flow endpoints:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ ; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$.
> - **Counter-flow > Parallel-flow:** Always true for the same four terminal temperatures.
> - **Correction Factor $F$:** Required for 1-2, 2-4, and cross-flow configurations; depends on $P$ and $R$.
> - **Multi-pass equation:** $q = U_s \cdot A_s \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$.
> - **Assumptions to memorize:** $U = $ const, $C_p = $ const, no phase change, no heat loss, steady-state, no axial conduction.
> - **LMTD used when:** Terminal temperatures are KNOWN.
> - **$\varepsilon$-NTU used when:** Terminal temperatures NOT fully known (only inlets + one outlet).
> - **Best configuration:** Counter-flow gives maximum LMTD → minimum area for same duty.
> - **Phase change heat rate:** $q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ (no temperature drop, isothermal fluid).
> - **Sanity check:** LMTD always lies between the smaller $\Delta T$ and the arithmetic mean.
> - **Avoid:** Using $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ directly in multi-pass without $F$ — guaranteed mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the reciprocal of the **total thermal resistance per unit reference area** offered by a composite system in which heat is transferred by a combination of **convection, conduction, and (optionally) radiation** between two fluid streams separated by a solid wall.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
U = \frac{1}{\displaystyle \sum_{j} R_{j,\text{ref}}} = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total,ref}}}
$$

where $R_{\text{ref}}$ is the overall thermal resistance evaluated on a **chosen reference area** (typically the inner area $A_i$ or the outer area $A_o$).

The heat transfer rate through the composite wall becomes:

$$
Q = U \cdot A_{\text{ref}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{overall}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the derivation of **U** for:
> 1. Plane composite walls (with and without fins)
> 2. Cylindrical walls (bare and insulated pipes)
> 3. The concept of **Critical Radius of Insulation**
> 4. Influence of **Fouling Factors** in real heat exchangers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-lane toll booth system on a highway:
- Cars (heat) must pass through **Booth 1** (inner convection $h_i$)
- Then travel through a **Tunnel** (conduction through the wall, $k/\Delta x$)
- Finally exit through **Booth 2** (outer convection $h_o$)

The **slowest booth or the longest tunnel** governs the total flow rate. The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** is like a single equivalent "toll booth speed" that represents the entire system's combined slowness — it tells you the *effective* heat flux for a given temperature difference.

If one stage (e.g., a thin copper wall) is super-fast, the bottleneck usually lies in the **fluid boundary layers** or the **insulation layer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> $U$ is a single number that summarizes how easily heat crosses a wall-and-fluid system. A **high $U$** means heat flows easily (good conductor, thin wall, aggressive convection). A **low $U$** means heat struggles to pass (insulation, fouling, weak convection).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | **W/(m²·K)** |
| Reference Area | $A_i, A_o, A_m$ | **m²** |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | **W/(m·K)** |
| Convective Coefficient | $h$ | **W/(m²·K)** |
| Wall Thickness | $\Delta x$ | **m** |
| Thermal Resistance | $R$ | **K/W** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Reasoning Behind $U$

In any realistic heat transfer problem (a boiler tube, a refrigerator wall, a double-glazed window), heat does not travel by a single mechanism. It encounters a **series of resistances**:

1. **Convective resistance** of the hot fluid (inner side, $1/h_iA_i$)
2. **Conductive resistance** of the solid wall ($L/kA_m$ for plane wall or $\ln(r_o/r_i)/2\pi k L$ for cylinder)
3. **Convective resistance** of the cold fluid (outer side, $1/h_oA_o$)
4. **Fouling resistance** on either side (in real heat exchangers, $R_{f,i}/A_i$ and $R_{f,o}/A_o$)

These resistances act **in series** because the same heat flux $Q$ flows through every layer at steady state.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Build-up

**Step 1 — Energy balance at steady state:**
At steady state, the heat rate through each layer is identical:

$$
Q = \frac{T_{\infty,i} - T_1}{\frac{1}{h_i A_i}} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\frac{L}{k A_m}} = \frac{T_2 - T_{\infty,o}}{\frac{1}{h_o A_o}}
$$

**Step 2 — Summing temperature drops:**
Adding the individual temperature differences:

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = Q \left[ \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{L}{k A_m} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Defining U on a chosen reference area:**
Comparing with $Q = U A_{\text{ref}} \Delta T_{\text{overall}}$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{L}{k A_m} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}}
$$

**Step 4 — Area relationships:**
For a plane wall: $A_i = A_o = A_m = A$, so the formula simplifies to:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

For a cylindrical wall: the areas differ. Using $A_i = 2\pi r_i L$, $A_o = 2\pi r_o L$, and $A_m = 2\pi r_m L$ (logarithmic mean), the same derivation yields:

$$
\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}
$$

**Step 5 — Converting U between reference areas:**
Since $Q = U_i A_i \Delta T = U_o A_o \Delta T$, we have:

$$
\boxed{U_i A_i = U_o A_o = U_m A_m}
$$

This means whichever area you choose, the product $UA$ stays constant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{L}{k A_m} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | General overall resistance (plane / cylindrical) |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Plane wall (equal areas) |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Cylindrical wall (pipe) |
| 4 | $U_i A_i = U_o A_o = U_m A_m$ | Equivalence of U across reference areas |
| 5 | $r_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}$ | Critical radius of insulation (cylinder) |
| 6 | $r_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}$ (no critical for plane wall) | For plane wall, $Q$ always increases with insulation thickness |
| 7 | $\frac{1}{U_{\text{dirty}}} = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Fouling factors in service |
| 8 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ (overall F) | Used inside heat exchanger design (Module 5 prep) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** For a cylinder, increasing insulation thickness **first increases** $Q$ up to $r_{\text{cr}}$, then decreases it. For a plane wall, **Q always decreases** monotonically — there is no critical radius.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plant boilers**: Engineers use $U_o$ to size the boiler tube bundle given steam-side and gas-side $h$ values.
- **Cryogenic LNG storage**: Multi-layer insulation systems rely on minimizing $U$ by stacking low-$k$ layers and suppressing $h$ in vacuum gaps.
- **Building envelopes (walls, windows)**: Architects use $U$-factor (with imperial units BTU/hr·ft²·°F) to comply with energy codes.
- **Heat exchangers**: The cleanliness factor $1/U_{\text{dirty}} = 1/U_{\text{clean}} + R_f$ dictates shutdown frequency for maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Composite Plane Wall

**Problem Statement:** A plane composite wall is composed of three layers with thermal conductivities $k_A$, $k_B$, $k_C$ and thicknesses $L_A$, $L_B$, $L_C$ in series. Hot fluid at $T_{\infty,i}$ cools to $T_{\infty,o}$ via convection coefficients $h_i$ and $h_o$. Derive the expression for $U$ based on the wall area $A$.

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state, 1-D heat flow
- Constant $k$ for each layer
- Perfect thermal contact between layers (no contact resistance)
- Uniform $h_i$ and $h_o$ on respective sides

**Derivation:**

At steady state, heat rate through each resistance is the same:

$$
Q = h_i A (T_{\infty,i} - T_1)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{k_A}{L_A} A (T_1 - T_2)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{k_B}{L_B} A (T_2 - T_3)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{k_C}{L_C} A (T_3 - T_4)
$$

$$
Q = h_o A (T_4 - T_{\infty,o})
$$

Now rewrite each as a temperature drop:

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_1 = \frac{Q}{h_i A}
$$

$$
T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_A}{k_A A}
$$

$$
T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q L_B}{k_B A}
$$

$$
T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q L_C}{k_C A}
$$

$$
T_4 - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{Q}{h_o A}
$$

Summing all five temperature drops:

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{Q}{A} \left[ \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_A}{k_A} + \frac{L_B}{k_B} + \frac{L_C}{k_C} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]
$$

Isolating $Q$:

$$
Q = \frac{A (T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o})}{\displaystyle \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_A}{k_A} + \frac{L_B}{k_B} + \frac{L_C}{k_C} + \frac{1}{h_o}}
$$

Comparing with the definition $Q = U A \Delta T_{\text{overall}}$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_A}{k_A} + \frac{L_B}{k_B} + \frac{L_C}{k_C} + \frac{1}{h_o}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Overall Coefficient for a Cylindrical Wall (with Areas)

**Setup:** A pipe of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, length $L$, conductivity $k$. Inner fluid $h_i$, outer fluid $h_o$.

**Heat rate through each resistance:**

$$
Q = h_i (2\pi r_i L)(T_{\infty,i} - T_s,i)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_s,i - T_s,o)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

$$
Q = h_o (2\pi r_o L)(T_s,o - T_{\infty,o})
$$

Temperature drops:

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_s,i = \frac{Q}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)}
$$

$$
T_s,i - T_s,o = \frac{Q \ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}
$$

$$
T_s,o - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{Q}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}
$$

Summing:

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = Q \left[ \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o} \right]
$$

with $A_i = 2\pi r_i L$ and $A_o = 2\pi r_o L$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{\displaystyle \frac{1}{U_i A_i}} = U_i A_i \, \Delta T = U_o A_o \, \Delta T}
$$

where:

$$
\frac{1}{U_i} = 1 + \frac{h_i}{k} \ln(r_o / r_i) + \frac{h_i r_i}{h_o r_o}
$$

is obtained by multiplying through by $h_i$ and using $A_i/A_o = r_i/r_o$.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Critical Radius of Insulation

**Setup:** Bare pipe of radius $r_1$ with insulation of outer radius $r_2$, conductivity $k_{\text{ins}}$, ambient convection $h_o$.

**Heat loss from the outer surface:**

$$
Q = \frac{T_s - T_{\infty}}{(r_2 - r_1)/(k_{\text{ins}} \cdot 4\pi r_1 r_2)} \quad \text{(sphere, conceptual)}
$$

For a **cylinder**, with outer surface area $A_o = 2\pi r_2 L$:

$$
Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_{s,1} - T_{\infty,o})}{\displaystyle \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{k_{\text{ins}}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}}
$$

Define the denominator as $\Phi(r_2)$:

$$
\Phi(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}{k_{\text{ins}}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}
$$

For maximum $Q$, minimize $\Phi(r_2)$:

$$
\frac{d\Phi}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k_{\text{ins}} r_2} - \frac{1}{h_o r_2^{2}} = 0
$$

Multiply through by $r_2^{2}$:

$$
\frac{r_2}{k_{\text{ins}}} = \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If the **outer radius of insulation is less than $r_{\text{cr}}$**, adding more insulation **increases** heat loss (counterintuitive!). This happens because the increase in surface area dominates over the added conductive resistance. Once $r_2 > r_{\text{cr}}$, the conductive term wins and $Q$ decreases.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A steam pipe with $r_1 = 5$ cm carries saturated steam at $T_s = 200$ °C. It is insulated with a material of $k_{\text{ins}} = 0.05$ W/(m·K). The ambient air is at $T_\infty = 25$ °C with $h_o = 10$ W/(m²·K). Inner steam-side coefficient $h_i = 100$ W/(m²·K) is very high. Calculate:
1. The critical radius of insulation.
2. The overall $U_o$ when insulation outer radius $r_2 = 6$ cm.
3. The heat loss per metre length.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** $r_{\text{cr}} = k_{\text{ins}} / h_o = 0.05 / 10 = 0.005$ m = **0.5 cm**

**Part (b):** $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.06$ m, $r_2/r_1 = 1.2$.

Since $h_i \to \infty$, $1/h_i \approx 0$. So:

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o / r_i)}{k_{\text{ins}}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{0.06 \cdot \ln(1.2)}{0.05}
$$

$\ln(1.2) \approx 0.1823$. So:

$$
\frac{0.06 \times 0.1823}{0.05} = \frac{0.01094}{0.05} = 0.2188 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} = 0.1 + 0.2188 = 0.3188
$$

$$
U_o = 1 / 0.3188 \approx 3.137 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

**Part (c):** Heat loss per metre ($L = 1$ m), $A_o = 2\pi r_2 L = 2\pi (0.06)(1) = 0.37699$ m²:

$$
Q/L = U_o \cdot A_o \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 3.137 \times 0.37699 \times (200 - 25)
$$

$$
Q/L = 3.137 \times 0.37699 \times 175 \approx 207.0 \text{ W/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Since $r_2 = 6$ cm $> r_{\text{cr}} = 0.5$ cm, we are on the **safe side** of the curve — adding more insulation will now reduce heat loss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thermal Resistance Network

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual flow of heat through a generic composite wall (plane geometry) and the corresponding resistance network. This serves as a substitute for the standard textbook figure of "thermal resistance circuit."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HOT FLUID T infinity i"] --> B["Inner Convection Resistance R conv i = 1 divided by h i A"]
    B --> C["Wall Conduction Resistance R cond = L divided by k A"]
    C --> D["Fouling Layer Inner R f i divided by A"]
    D --> E["Fouling Layer Outer R f o divided by A"]
    E --> F["Outer Convection Resistance R conv o = 1 divided by h o A"]
    F --> G["COLD FLUID T infinity o"]

    H["Overall Resistance R total"] -.summation.- B
    H -.summation.- C
    H -.summation.- D
    H -.summation.- E
    H -.summation.- F

    I["Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U = 1 divided by R total A"] -.derived from.- H

    J["Heat Rate Q = U A delta T overall"] -.equals.- I

    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style G fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#0000cc
    style H fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Critical Radius Behaviour

The following diagram captures the **counter-intuitive behaviour** of heat loss vs. insulation thickness for a cylindrical pipe.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Insulation thickness r2 = r1 JUST BARExxsmall"] --> B["Q INCREASES with r2"]
    B --> C["r2 = r CRITICAL equals k over h oxxsmall Q MAXIMUM"]
    C --> D["r2 GREATER than r CRITICAL"]
    D --> E["Q DECREASES with r2"]
    E --> F["r2 INFINITYxxsmall Q approaches 2 pi k L T s minus T infinity"]

    style A fill:#fff0f0
    style B fill:#ffcc00
    style C fill:#ff3300,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ccff66
    style E fill:#66ccff
    style F fill:#3399ff,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Reference Area Equivalence

This diagram clarifies the relationship between the three commonly used reference areas for $U$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Heat Rate Q is INVARIANT"] --> B["U i times A i equals U o times A o equals U m times A m"]
    B --> C["A i = 2 pi r i L Inner Surface"]
    B --> D["A m = 2 pi r m L Log Mean Radius"]
    B --> E["A o = 2 pi r o L Outer Surface"]
    C --> F["U i = 1 divided by h i + r i over k ln r o over r i + r i over r o times 1 over h o"]
    D --> G["U m = 1 divided by r m over r i h i + ln r o over r i over k + r m over r o times 1 over h o"]
    E --> H["U o = 1 divided by r o over r i h i + r o over k ln r o over r i + 1 over h o"]

    style A fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
    style B fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** When a problem states "steam condenses on the inner surface," use $U_i$ (inner reference). When it states "ambient air outside," use $U_o$ (outer reference). When unsure, **always** write $Q = U_i A_i \Delta T = U_o A_o \Delta T$ and let the problem tell you which is most convenient.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient. State the units and physical significance of $U$.**

**Model Answer:**
The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$ is defined as the heat transfer rate per unit area per unit overall temperature difference across a composite system combining convection, conduction, and (possibly) radiation.

$$
Q = U A \Delta T_{\text{overall}}
$$

**Units:** W/(m²·K) in SI, or BTU/(hr·ft²·°F) in imperial.

**Physical Significance:** $U$ indicates the **ease of heat flow** through a composite wall. A high $U$ corresponds to a thin, highly conductive wall with strong convection; a low $U$ indicates a thick, insulating wall or weak convection.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by critical radius of insulation? For what kind of geometry does it exist?**

**Model Answer:**
The critical radius of insulation $r_{\text{cr}}$ is the outer radius of insulation on a cylindrical (or spherical) surface at which the **heat transfer rate is maximum**. Adding insulation beyond $r_{\text{cr}}$ reduces heat loss; adding insulation below $r_{\text{cr}}$ surprisingly **increases** heat loss due to the dominant increase in surface area.

$$
r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}
$$

It exists for **curved surfaces** (cylinder, sphere) but **not** for plane walls, where heat loss always decreases monotonically with insulation thickness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Derive an expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient based on the inner surface area for a cylindrical pipe wall. Include the case of a clean pipe (no fouling) carrying steam being cooled by ambient air.**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Derive $U_i$ for a clean cylindrical wall

**Model Solution:**

Let:
- Inner radius = $r_1$, outer radius = $r_2$, length = $L$
- Pipe conductivity = $k$, inner convection = $h_i$, outer convection = $h_o$
- $T_{\infty,i}$ = steam temperature, $T_{\infty,o}$ = ambient temperature

**Step 1 — Heat rate through each resistance:**

$$
Q = h_i (2\pi r_1 L)(T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,1})
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_{s,1} - T_{s,2})}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

$$
Q = h_o (2\pi r_2 L)(T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,o})
$$

**Step 2 — Express each as a temperature drop:**

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,1} = \frac{Q}{h_i A_i}, \quad A_i = 2\pi r_1 L
$$

$$
T_{s,1} - T_{s,2} = \frac{Q \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}
$$

$$
T_{s,2} - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{Q}{h_o A_o}, \quad A_o = 2\pi r_2 L
$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = Q \left[ \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Define $U_i$:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $A_i = 2\pi r_1 L$:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_i} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_1 \ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{r_1}{h_o r_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{U_i} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_1 \ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{r_1}{r_2 \, h_o}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Compute $U_i$ and heat loss per metre

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.04$ m, $r_2 = 0.05$ m, $k = 50$ W/(m·K) (steel), $h_i = 5000$ W/(m²·K) (steam), $h_o = 15$ W/(m²·K) (air), $T_{\infty,i} = 180$ °C, $T_{\infty,o} = 30$ °C.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_i} = \frac{1}{5000} + \frac{0.04 \times \ln(0.05/0.04)}{50} + \frac{0.04}{0.05 \times 15}
$$

$$
\ln(0.05/0.04) = \ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term:**

- $\frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002$
- $\frac{0.04 \times 0.2231}{50} = \frac{0.008926}{50} = 0.0001785$
- $\frac{0.04}{0.05 \times 15} = \frac{0.04}{0.75} = 0.05333$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_i} = 0.0002 + 0.0001785 + 0.05333 \approx 0.05371
$$

$$
U_i \approx 18.62 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per metre:**

$A_i = 2\pi (0.04)(1) = 0.2513$ m²

$$
Q/L = U_i A_i \Delta T = 18.62 \times 0.2513 \times (180 - 30) \approx 702.1 \text{ W/m}
$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A thick-walled steel pipe carries hot oil. Discuss the effect of adding insulation on the outside surface. Derive the critical radius of insulation and explain its physical significance with a suitable sketch description.**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Derive the critical radius of insulation

**Model Solution:**

Consider a cylindrical pipe with bare outer radius $r_1$ insulated to outer radius $r_2$ ($r_2 > r_1$). Heat from the surface of the pipe at $T_{s,1}$ passes through insulation (conductivity $k_{\text{ins}}$) and convects to ambient ($T_\infty, h_o$).

**Step 1 — Total resistance between $T_{s,1}$ and $T_\infty$:**

$$
R_{\text{total}} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{\text{ins}} L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_2 L)}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_{s,1} - T_\infty)}{\displaystyle \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{\text{ins}}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximize $Q$ by minimizing the denominator $D(r_2)$:**

$$
D(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{\text{ins}}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}
$$

$$
\frac{dD}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k_{\text{ins}} r_2} - \frac{1}{h_o r_2^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$
\frac{r_2}{k_{\text{ins}}} = \frac{1}{h_o} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Discuss the physical significance

**Model Solution:**

For a cylinder, when insulation is **thin** ($r_2 < r_{\text{cr}}$), increasing $r_2$ **increases** $Q$. This is because:
- The added conductive resistance grows only **logarithmically** ($\ln r_2 / r_1$)
- The convective resistance decreases as $1/(h_o r_2)$ — meaning **more surface area** dominates
- Net effect: heat loss increases

When insulation is **thick** ($r_2 > r_{\text{cr}}$), increasing $r_2$ **decreases** $Q$:
- The conductive term dominates
- Surface area growth no longer compensates

**Engineering Implications:**
1. Electrical wires carrying current: insulation radius often less than $r_{\text{cr}}$, so the wire actually dissipates heat better with thin insulation.
2. For thermal insulation of pipes (e.g., steam lines), insulation outer radius must always exceed $r_{\text{cr}}$ to be effective.
3. Choose insulation material such that **operating $r_2 \gg r_{\text{cr}}$** for safe design margins.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in $D(r_2)$ derivative:** Students frequently forget the negative sign on $\frac{1}{h_o r_2^2}$ — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing reference areas:** Many students incorrectly equate $U_i = U_o$. Always state and use $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$.
> 3. **Ignoring $h_i$:** In steam condensation problems, $h_i$ is often huge (5000+), so $1/h_i$ is negligible. Do not blindly include it if it does not affect the result.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** If $r$ is in cm but $k$ is in W/(m·K), convert $r$ to metres first. This single mistake loses a full 14-mark question.
> 5. **Critical radius formula:** Many students write $r_{\text{cr}} = h_o / k$ (inverted). Mark it wrong instantly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Overall $U$** is the **reciprocal of total thermal resistance** on a chosen reference area.
- For a **plane wall**, all areas are equal: $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$.
- For a **cylindrical wall**, use areas explicitly: $\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$.
- **Invariant relation:** $U_i A_i = U_o A_o = U_m A_m$ — choose whichever is convenient.
- **Critical radius** $r_{\text{cr}} = k_{\text{ins}} / h_o$ exists **only for cylinders and spheres** (not plane walls).
- For $r_2 < r_{\text{cr}}$: heat loss **increases** with insulation (counterintuitive).
- For $r_2 > r_{\text{cr}}$: heat loss **decreases** with insulation (normal expectation).
- **Fouling factor** $R_f$ adds series resistance: $\frac{1}{U_{\text{dirty}}} = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$.
- **Units consistency** is essential — keep SI throughout (m, W, K).
- The **largest resistance dominates** the overall $U$. Quick estimate: $U \approx \min(h_i, k/L, h_o)$.
- KTU 2024 frequently tests the **critical radius derivation** as a 14-mark full question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fouling Factors - Heat Exchanger Performance - Transfer Units - Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fouling Factors - Heat Exchanger Performance - Transfer Units - Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fouling Factors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fouling Factor (R_f):** An additional thermal resistance introduced into the overall heat transfer equation to account for the deposition of unwanted material (scale, rust, sludge, biological growth, crystallization, or chemical reaction products) on the inner and/or outer surfaces of heat exchanger tubes over a period of operation.

The **dirty/operating overall heat transfer coefficient (U_d)** is related to the **clean overall heat transfer coefficient (U_c)** through the fouling factor as:

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

Where:
- $R_{f,i}$ = **Inner (Tube-side) Fouling Factor** in $\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
- $R_{f,o}$ = **Outer (Shell-side) Fouling Factor** in $\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$

> [!NOTE]
> The fouling factor is essentially a **"safety penalty"** that engineers deliberately add during the design of a heat exchanger so that the unit continues to perform satisfactorily even after months of operation when its surfaces become dirty.

## 1.2 Heat Exchanger Performance — Formal Definition

Heat exchanger performance refers to the ability of the equipment to transfer a specified amount of heat between two fluid streams under given operating conditions. It is quantified using two principal methods:

1. **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) Method** — Used when outlet temperatures (or at least one of them) are known or can be assumed.
2. **Effectiveness–Number of Transfer Units (ε–NTU) Method** — Used when outlet temperatures are unknown, but inlet temperatures and flow rates are known.

## 1.3 Transfer Units (NTU) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Number of Transfer Units (NTU):** A dimensionless number that represents the *thermal size* or *heat transfer capacity* of a heat exchanger. It is the ratio of the *true temperature change experienced by a fluid* to the *mean temperature difference driving force*.

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}$$

Where:
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient, $\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$
- $A$ = Heat transfer area, $\text{m}^2$
- $C_{\min}$ = Smaller of the two fluid heat capacity rates, $\text{W/K}$

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Fouling (The "Dirty Pipe" Analogy):**
> Imagine drinking water through a fresh, clean straw versus one that has been used for a week. The old straw feels harder to sip from because a thin layer of residue has built up inside. That residue restricts the flow and requires you to "push harder." Similarly, in a heat exchanger, **fouling deposits act like an extra insulating layer**, blocking the easy flow of heat. Engineers anticipate this by designing the unit with a slightly bigger surface area — this is what the fouling factor handles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — LMTD (The "Average Hill" Analogy):**
> If you drive up and down a mountain between two points, the *effective* average elevation difference is NOT simply the average of the top and bottom — it is the **logarithmic mean**. Temperature differences at the two ends of a heat exchanger behave the same way, and LMTD is the correct way to average them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Transfer Units (The "Stepping Stones" Analogy):**
> Imagine a fluid cooling down by passing through a series of identical "temperature-drop stations." The total number of stations it passes through is the **NTU**. Each station drops the temperature a little, and the total cooling is governed by how many stations (NTU) the fluid traverses and how efficient each station is (effectiveness).

---

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Practice

| Concept | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Fouling Factor | Sizing of condensers, boilers, coolers in power plants and refineries |
| LMTD | Direct design calculation when outlet temps are known |
| ε–NTU | Performance evaluation, rating of existing heat exchangers |
| Transfer Units | Heat exchanger *selection*, *comparison*, and *scaling* studies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Fouling Factor Values (TEMA, by convention):**
> - Distilled water: $R_f = 0.00009 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
> - Sea water (below 50°C): $R_f = 0.00009 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
> - Cooling tower water: $R_f = 0.0002 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
> - Crude oil: $R_f = 0.0002$ to $0.001 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
> - Heavy fuel oil: $R_f = 0.0009 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
> - Steam (clean): $R_f = 0.00009 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fouling Factor — Theoretical Background

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Fouling

Fouling occurs in **five stages**:
1. **Initiation** — Delay period before deposit formation begins.
2. **Transport** — Suspended particles, dissolved salts, or reactants are transported to the surface.
3. **Attachment** — Particles stick to the wall by adhesion, chemical bonding, or crystallization.
4. **Removal** — Shear forces from the flowing fluid partially erode the deposit.
5. **Aging/Consolidation** — Remaining deposit hardens, becoming more insulating over time.

### 2.1.2 Inclusion in Design Equation

The basic rate equation for a heat exchanger is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

For a *clean* exchanger:

$$\frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

For a *dirty/operating* exchanger:

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{x}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wall thermal resistance $\frac{x}{k_w}$ is retained; the fouling resistances $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ are simply **added** to the total resistance chain.

### 2.1.3 Effect of Fouling on Performance

- $U_d < U_c$ (always true)
- $Q_{\text{actual}} < Q_{\text{design}}$
- Outlet temperatures deviate from design intent
- To compensate, **extra area** (typically 10–25%) is added during design — this is called the **overdesign** or **fouling allowance**.

---

## 2.2 LMTD Method — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 LMTD Formula for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1$ = Temperature difference at one end (inlet of hot — inlet of cold, or outlet — outlet)
- $\Delta T_2$ = Temperature difference at the other end

### 2.2.2 Correction Factor for Multi-Pass and Cross-Flow Exchangers

Real exchangers (shell-and-tube, cross-flow) are not pure counter-flow. A **Correction Factor $F$** is applied:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

Where $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the LMTD calculated as if the exchanger were counter-flow.

> [!NOTE]
> $F$ depends on two parameters: $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}}$ and $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$, and is read from standard **F-charts** for the given flow arrangement (1-2, 2-4, cross-flow, etc.).

---

## 2.3 Effectiveness–NTU Method

### 2.3.1 Definitions

- **Heat capacity rate:** $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ [W/K]
- **Minimum heat capacity rate:** $C_{\min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$
- **Maximum heat capacity rate:** $C_{\max} = \max(C_h, C_c)$
- **Heat capacity ratio:** $C^* = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ (always $\leq 1$)

### 2.3.2 Heat Exchanger Effectiveness

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max,possible}} = \frac{C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

$Q_{\max,possible}$ is the heat transfer that *would* occur in a counter-flow heat exchanger of infinite area.

### 2.3.3 NTU-Effectiveness Equations (Key Results)

| Exchanger Type | Effectiveness Equation |
|---|---|
| Counter-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}\right]}{1 - C^* \exp\left[-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}\right]}$ |
| Parallel-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-(1+C^*)\text{NTU}\right]}{1 + C^*}$ |
| Shell & Tube (1 shell, 2 tubes) | $\varepsilon = 2 \left\{ (1+C^*) + \sqrt{1+C^{*2}} \cdot \dfrac{1+\exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C^{*2}})}{1-\exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C^{*2}})} \right\}^{-1}$ |
| Cross-flow (both unmixed) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{ \dfrac{\text{NTU}^{0.22}}{C^*}\left[\exp(-C^* \cdot \text{NTU}^{0.78}) - 1 \right] \right\}$ |
| All fluids mixed | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{C^*} \left(1 - \exp\left\{-C^*[1-\exp(-\text{NTU})]\right\} \right)$ |

### 2.3.4 Special Case: $C^* = 1$ (Balanced Flow)

For a counter-flow exchanger when $C^* = 1$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol/Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Fouling + clean coefficient |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Basic rate equation |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}$ | LMTD for counter-flow |
| 4 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | Multi-pass with correction |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}}$ | F-chart P-parameter |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$ | F-chart R-parameter |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}}$ | Effectiveness |
| 8 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{UA}{C_{\min}}$ | Number of transfer units |
| 9 | $C^* = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ | Heat capacity ratio |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{\text{counter}} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}}}{1 - C^* e^{-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}}}$ | ε–NTU for counter-flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** ε–NTU charts are the *industry standard* for rating existing heat exchangers, designing compact heat exchangers in aerospace, and selecting heat exchangers from manufacturers' catalogues where only NTU and $C^*$ are tabulated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD for Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger:
- Hot fluid enters at $T_{h,i}$ and exits at $T_{h,o}$
- Cold fluid enters at $T_{c,i}$ and exits at $T_{c,o}$
- $T_{h,i} > T_{c,o}$ (counter-flow condition)

At an elemental area $dA$:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

For the hot fluid (cooling):

$$dQ = -C_h \cdot dT_h$$

For the cold fluid (heating):

$$dQ = C_c \cdot dT_c$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{dA} &= \frac{dT_h}{dA} - \frac{dT_c}{dA} \\
&= -\frac{dQ}{C_h \cdot dA} - \frac{dQ}{C_c \cdot dA} \\
&= -U(T_h - T_c)\left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Then:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dA} = -U \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$$

Integrating from $\Delta T_1$ (area 0) to $\Delta T_2$ (area $A$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) \int_0^A dA \\
\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) &= -UA\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Energy balance over the whole exchanger: $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$

Therefore:

$$UA = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}}$$

Now:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\frac{1}{UA}\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Comparing with $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This logarithmic mean is what makes the LMTD the *correct* average temperature difference for a counter-flow heat exchanger with constant $U$ and constant $c_p$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of ε–NTU Relation for Counter-Flow Exchanger

### 3.2.1 Setup

Define:
- $C_h$ and $C_c$ = heat capacity rates of hot and cold fluids
- $C^* = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$
- Assume $C_h = C_{\min}$ (the smaller one), so $C_c = C_{\max} = C_{\min}/C^*$

### 3.2.2 Derivation

Energy balance: $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$

Total heat transfer:

$$Q = UA \cdot \frac{(T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) - (T_{h,o} - T_{c,i})}{\ln\left[\frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}}\right]}$$

Let $\theta_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\theta_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$.

Effectiveness:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

Since $C_h = C_{\min}$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$$

Solving for outlet temperatures using $Q = UA \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ and substituting:

After algebraic manipulation (see standard derivation in Incropera):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\left[-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}\right]}{1 - C^* \exp\left[-(1-C^*)\text{NTU}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $C^* = 0$ (one fluid with infinite capacity, e.g., condensing steam), the equation reduces to $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$, which is the well-known formula for a constant-temperature heat source/sink.

### 3.2.3 Limiting Case: $C^* = 1$

Using L'Hôpital's rule on the above expression:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Fouling + LMTD)

**Problem:** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger has $U_c = 1200 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$. Recommended fouling factors are $R_{f,i} = 0.0002$ and $R_{f,o} = 0.0003 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$.

(a) Calculate the dirty overall heat transfer coefficient $U_d$.

(b) If the clean area is $A_c = 10 \text{ m}^2$, what additional area is needed to maintain the same heat duty at the same LMTD?

### Solution

**Part (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_c} &= \frac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_{f,\text{total}} &= R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} = 0.0002 + 0.0003 = 0.0005 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
\frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,\text{total}} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.3333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
U_d &= \frac{1}{1.3333 \times 10^{-3}} = 750 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):**

Since $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ must remain constant:

$$U_c \cdot A_c = U_d \cdot A_d$$

$$A_d = \frac{U_c \cdot A_c}{U_d} = \frac{1200 \times 10}{750} = 16 \text{ m}^2$$

Additional area required: $A_d - A_c = 16 - 10 = 6 \text{ m}^2$ (a 60% increase!).

> [!WARNING]
> Always include fouling resistance while designing — forgetting it is one of the most common causes of heat exchanger undersizing in KTU exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fouling Resistance Network (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Fluid] --> B[h_o - Outer Convection]
    B --> C[R_f,o - Outer Fouling]
    C --> D[x / k_w - Wall Conduction]
    D --> E[R_f,i - Inner Fouling]
    E --> F[h_i - Inner Convection]
    F --> G[Cold Fluid]
    H["1/U_d = 1/h_o + R_f,o + x/k_w + R_f,i + 1/h_i"] -.summation.- B
    H -.summation.- C
    H -.summation.- D
    H -.summation.- E
    H -.summation.- F
```

## 4.2 Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger — Temperature Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InletEnd
        H1["Hot fluid IN T_h,i"] --> H2["Hot fluid OUT T_h,o"]
        C1["Cold fluid OUT T_c,o"] --> C2["Cold fluid IN T_c,i"]
    end
    H1 --- C1
    H2 --- C2
    note1["ΔT_1 = T_h,i - T_c,o (at one end)"] -.annotates.- H1
    note2["ΔT_2 = T_h,o - T_c,i (at other end)"] -.annotates.- H2
    note3["ΔT_lm = (ΔT_1 - ΔT_2) / ln(ΔT_1/ΔT_2)"] -.computes.- note1
```

## 4.3 Heat Exchanger Selection Flowchart (LMTD vs ε–NTU)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Heat Exchanger Problem] --> B{Known Information?}
    B -->|Outlet Temperatures Known| C[Use LMTD Method]
    B -->|Outlet Temperatures Unknown| D[Use ε-NTU Method]
    C --> E[Compute ΔT_lm]
    E --> F{Configuration?}
    F -->|Counter-flow| G[Q = U*A*ΔT_lm]
    F -->|Multi-pass or Cross-flow| H[Read F from F-chart]
    H --> I[Q = U*A*F*ΔT_lm,CF]
    D --> J[Compute C* and NTU]
    J --> K[Read ε from ε-NTU chart]
    K --> L[Compute Q = ε * C_min * ΔT_inlet_max]
    G --> M[Final Answer: Q and Area A]
    I --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.4 Transfer Units — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet T_h,i] --> B[Transfer Unit 1]
    B --> C[Transfer Unit 2]
    C --> D[Transfer Unit 3]
    D --> E[...]
    E --> F[Outlet T_h,o]
    G[Inlet T_c,i] --> H[Counter-flow Cold Stream]
    H --> I[Outlet T_c,o]
    note1["Each Transfer Unit = One ΔT Step"] -.annotates.- B
    note2["NTU = Total number of such steps"] -.annotates.- E
    note3["Effectiveness ε = f(NTU, C*)"] -.annotates.- F
```

## 4.5 Fouling Build-Up Over Time (Block-Level Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clean Exchanger Start] --> B[Operating Period Increases]
    B --> C[Fouling Deposit Forms]
    C --> D[R_f Increases]
    D --> E[U_d Decreases]
    E --> F{Q same?}
    F -->|Yes, A same| G[Outlet T deviates from design]
    F -->|No, A increased during design| H[Exchanger meets design Q]
    G --> I[Cleaning Required]
    H --> J[Scheduled Maintenance]
    I --> K[Restored to U_c]
    J --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define *fouling factor* and explain why it must be included in heat exchanger design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fouling factor ($R_f$) is the additional thermal resistance offered by the deposit of unwanted material such as scale, sludge, rust, or biological growth on the heat transfer surfaces of an exchanger. It has units of $\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$ and is added to the overall resistance chain as:
$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$
It must be included in design because, over time, deposits accumulate and reduce the actual heat transfer coefficient below the clean value. Including $R_f$ in the design ensures the exchanger delivers the required heat duty even in its fouled condition, providing a *design margin*. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Define *Number of Transfer Units (NTU)*. What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NTU is a dimensionless number defined as:
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}$$
Physically, NTU represents the *thermal size* of a heat exchanger. A higher NTU means the exchanger is larger (more area) and can achieve a temperature change closer to the maximum possible. It is also the ratio of the actual temperature change of a fluid to the mean temperature driving force, and is used in the effectiveness–NTU method to rate heat exchangers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** (a) Derive the expression for the **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** for a counter-flow heat exchanger, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b) In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot fluid enters at 200°C and leaves at 100°C, while cold fluid enters at 30°C and leaves at 80°C. If $U = 800 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ and the heat duty is 80 kW, determine the required heat transfer area. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*(Write the full derivation as shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

Key points to be awarded marks:
- Correct heat balance equations for both fluids (1 mark)
- Correct identification of $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$ (1 mark)
- Setting up the differential equation $\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dA} = -U \Delta T \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$ (2 marks)
- Integration and substitution to obtain LMTD formula (2 marks)
- Statement of assumptions: constant $U$, constant $c_p$, no phase change, no heat loss to surroundings (1 mark)

**Final result to quote:**
$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_{h,i} = 200°C, T_{h,o} = 100°C, T_{c,i} = 30°C, T_{c,o} = 80°C$, $U = 800 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$, $Q = 80 \text{ kW} = 80,000 \text{ W}$

**Step 1 — Identify end temperature differences (counter-flow):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 200 - 80 = 120°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 100 - 30 = 70°C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate LMTD:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} \\
&= \frac{120 - 70}{\ln(120/70)} \\
&= \frac{50}{\ln(1.7143)} \\
&= \frac{50}{0.5390} \\
&\approx 92.77°C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{80{,}000}{800 \times 92.77} = \frac{80{,}000}{74{,}216} \approx 1.078 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $A \approx 1.08 \text{ m}^2$ **(7 marks distributed: 2 for ΔT identification, 3 for LMTD, 2 for area calculation)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** (a) Explain the concept of **fouling factor** in heat exchangers. With a neat resistance diagram, derive the relationship between the **clean** and **dirty** overall heat transfer coefficients. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A shell-and-tube heat exchanger has $U_c = 1500 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$. Inner and outer fouling factors are given as $R_{f,i} = 0.00018 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$ and $R_{f,o} = 0.00035 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$. Calculate: (i) the dirty overall heat transfer coefficient $U_d$, and (ii) the percentage overdesign in area required to maintain the same heat duty. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Concept + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** Define fouling factor. Discuss types of fouling (scaling, sedimentation, biological, chemical reaction, freezing). Mention TEMA standard values.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For a clean tube:

$$\frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

For a dirty (operating) exchanger, the fouling resistances $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ are added to the chain:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{x}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o} \\
\frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}
\end{aligned}$$

Draw a thermal resistance network diagram (as in Section 4.1) showing each resistance in series.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $U_c = 1500 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}, R_{f,i} = 0.00018, R_{f,o} = 0.00035 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$

**Step 1 — Total fouling resistance:**

$$R_f = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} = 0.00018 + 0.00035 = 0.00053 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $U_d$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{U_c} + R_f = \frac{1}{1500} + 0.00053 \\
&= 0.000667 + 0.000530 = 0.001197 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
U_d &= \frac{1}{0.001197} \approx 835.4 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage overdesign in area:**

Since $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ must remain constant, $U_c A_c = U_d A_d$:

$$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{U_c}{U_d} = \frac{1500}{835.4} \approx 1.796$$

**Percentage overdesign:**

$$\% \text{ overdesign} = (1.796 - 1) \times 100\% \approx 79.6\%$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $U_d \approx 835.4 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$, (ii) Area overdesign $\approx 79.6\%$ **(7 marks: 2 for fouling sum, 2 for $U_d$, 3 for overdesign calculation)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** of fouling factor — it is $\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$, not $\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$. This single error costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** in LMTD — for counter-flow, $\Delta T_1$ corresponds to the *inlet* end of the hot fluid. Forgetting to take the absolute value in $\ln$ calculation will lead to wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$** in NTU calculation. Always identify the smaller heat capacity rate correctly.
> 4. **Not using natural log** — students often use $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$. KTU strictly expects $\ln$ in the LMTD formula.
> 5. **Ignoring the F-factor** in multi-pass exchangers — many students write $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ for shell-and-tube exchangers, which is **incorrect** without $F$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> ✅ **Fouling Factor ($R_f$)**: Extra thermal resistance from deposits; units $\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$; added in series to the resistance chain.
>
> ✅ **Clean vs Dirty $U$**: $\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$; always $U_d < U_c$.
>
> ✅ **LMTD**: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$; used when outlet temperatures are known.
>
> ✅ **Correction Factor $F$**: Applied for multi-pass and cross-flow exchangers; depends on $P$ and $R$; read from F-charts.
>
> ✅ **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)**: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \dfrac{C_h(T_{h,i}-T_{h,o})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})}$.
>
> ✅ **NTU**: $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{UA}{C_{\min}}$; dimensionless; represents thermal size.
>
> ✅ **Heat Capacity Ratio**: $C^* = C_{\min}/C_{\max} \leq 1$.
>
> ✅ **ε–NTU Relations**: Differ for each flow configuration (counter, parallel, shell-and-tube, cross-flow). Counter-flow is the most efficient.
>
> ✅ **Limiting Case**: $C^* = 0 \Rightarrow \varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$; $C^* = 1 \Rightarrow \varepsilon = \text{NTU}/(1+\text{NTU})$.
>
> ✅ **Design Overdesign**: Always oversize by 10–25% to account for fouling.
>
> ✅ **Use of Methods**: LMTD for *design* problems; ε–NTU for *rating* and *selection* problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Transfer** is defined as the transport of a given species from one region to another, caused primarily by a **concentration gradient**, **temperature gradient**, or **pressure gradient** acting on the system. The transfer can occur by two principal mechanisms: **molecular diffusion** (microscopic, due to random molecular motion) and **convective (eddy) diffusion** (macroscopic, due to bulk fluid motion).

**Diffusion Mass Transfer** specifically refers to the transfer of mass at the **molecular level**, where individual molecules migrate from regions of higher chemical potential to regions of lower chemical potential, driven purely by **random molecular motion** (Brownian motion) in the absence of any bulk macroscopic flow.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Ink Drop in Water**: Imagine dropping a single drop of blue ink into a still glass of clear water. If you do not stir the water at all, you will still observe that the blue color slowly spreads throughout the entire glass over several hours. This spontaneous spreading, without any stirring or external force, is **molecular diffusion**. The ink molecules are constantly moving randomly (thermal motion), and over time, they uniformly distribute themselves throughout the water.
> 
> **Key Insight**: Diffusion is "nature's way of erasing concentration differences" — exactly analogous to how Fourier's law of heat conduction erases temperature differences, and Ohm's law erases voltage differences. All three are governed by a generalized **transport equation**: *Flux = − (Transport Property) × (Gradient of Potential)*.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Units

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{molecules/mol}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Standard Pressure:** $P_0 = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
- **Standard Temperature:** $T_0 = 273.15 \, \text{K}$

### 1.4 Classification of Mass Transfer Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Driving Force | Typical Scale |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Molecular Diffusion** | Concentration gradient | Microscopic ($\mu$m) |
| **Convective (Eddy) Diffusion** | Bulk fluid motion | Macroscopic (m) |
| **Thermal Diffusion (Soret Effect)** | Temperature gradient | Molecular |
| **Pressure Diffusion** | Pressure gradient | Molecular |
| **Forced Diffusion** | External electric/magnetic field | Molecular |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope**: In Module 4, the focus is strictly on **Molecular Diffusion** through Fick's Laws, including analysis in **gases**, **liquids**, and **solids**. Steady-state and unsteady-state diffusion problems form the core of this module.

### 1.5 The Mass Transfer Analogies (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The triumvirate of transport phenomena — *Heat, Momentum, Mass* — share mathematically identical governing equations. This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU exams:

| Phenomenon | Law | Flux Equation | Transport Property | Driving Gradient |
|------------|-----|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Heat Transfer | Fourier's Law | $q'' = -k \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Thermal conductivity $k$ | Temperature |
| Momentum Transfer | Newton's Law | $\tau = -\mu \, \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ | Velocity |
| Mass Transfer | Fick's First Law | $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ | Concentration |
