## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady-State)

**Fick's First Law** is the cornerstone equation of diffusion mass transfer, completely analogous to Fourier's Law for heat conduction. It states that the **molar flux of species A** by molecular diffusion is directly proportional to the **concentration gradient** of species A and occurs in the direction of **decreasing concentration**.

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

where:
- $J_A^*$ = molar flux of species A due to molecular diffusion $\left[ \text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = binary diffusivity (or mass diffusivity) of A in B $\left[ \text{m}^2/\text{s} \right]$
- $C_A$ = molar concentration of species A $\left[ \text{kmol/m}^3 \right]$
- $x$ = direction of diffusion $\left[ \text{m} \right]$
- The **negative sign** indicates that diffusion occurs from high to low concentration (gradient is negative when flux is positive in the $+x$ direction).

### 2.2 Mass Flux vs. Molar Flux — Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction**: Mass transfer problems often involve **bulk flow** in addition to molecular diffusion. Therefore, we must distinguish between:
> 
> 1. **Molar flux due to molecular diffusion alone** ($J_A^*$): The flux relative to the **molar average velocity**.
> 2. **Total molar flux** ($N_A$): The flux relative to a **stationary coordinate system**, which includes bulk convection.

For a **binary mixture** of A and B, the **total molar flux** $N_A$ is given by:

$$N_A = J_A^* + C_A \, v_m$$

where $v_m$ is the **molar average velocity** of the mixture:

$$v_m = \frac{C_A v_A + C_B v_B}{C_A + C_B} = \frac{C_A v_A + C_B v_B}{C}$$

Combining these:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A \, v_m$$

This is the most general form and is the basis for all subsequent derivations.

### 2.3 The Two Canonical Diffusion Problems

#### Case 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)

When species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** (e.g., in a binary distillation column at constant molar overflow), $N_A = -N_B$. In this case, the bulk flow term **vanishes** ($v_m = 0$), and the flux simplifies to:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Integrating across a distance $z$ from point 1 (concentration $C_{A1}$) to point 2 (concentration $C_{A2}$):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{z} \, (C_{A1} - C_{A2}) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T z} \, (y_{A1} - y_{A2})$$

#### Case 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

When species B is **non-diffusing** (stagnant), as in gas absorption where B is the carrier gas and is insoluble, then $N_B = 0$. The molar average velocity is no longer zero, and we get the **Stefan equation**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, z} \, \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$$

where $P_{B,lm}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B**:

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

### 2.4 Binary Diffusivity Correlations

#### (a) Diffusivity in Gases (Chapman-Enskog / Fuller-Schettler-Giddings)

For low-pressure binary gas mixtures, the **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings correlation** gives:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^{-2} \, T^{1.75}}{P \, \left[ \left( \Sigma v_A \right)^{1/3} + \left( \Sigma v_B \right)^{1/3} \right]^2} \, \sqrt{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, $T$ in K, $P$ in kPa, and $\Sigma v$ is the **sum of atomic diffusion volumes**.

#### (b) Diffusivity in Liquids (Wilke-Chang Correlation)

$$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

where $\phi$ is the **association factor** (2.6 for water, 1.0 for non-associated solvents), $V_A$ is the molar volume of solute A at its normal boiling point.

#### (c) Diffusivity in Solids

For steady-state diffusion through a solid (e.g., membrane), Fick's law applies directly:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note**: Solid-state diffusion problems are usually straightforward Fick's law applications. Liquid diffusion problems use Wilke-Chang. Gas diffusion is the most extensively tested.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU ESE — Diffusion Module**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (general) | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 2 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A v_m$ | Total molar flux (general) | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 3 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT z}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | EMCD in gas phase | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT z} \dfrac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$ | A through stagnant B | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 6 | $P_{B,lm} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ | Log-mean of B | kPa |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law | — |
| 8 | $D_{AB} = 0.001858 \, \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | Hirschfelder-Bird-Spotz (gases) | $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $D_{AB} \propto \dfrac{T^{1.75}}{P}$ | Gas diffusivity scaling | — |
| 10 | $\text{Schmidt No., } Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Analogous to Prandtl no. | dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:**
> - **Distillation columns**: Molecular diffusion in trays/packing determines mass transfer efficiency.
> - **Gas absorption** ($\text{CO}_2$ in MEA, $\text{SO}_2$ in water): Stefan diffusion model applies when carrier gas is insoluble.
> - **Membrane separation**: Hydrogen purification, oxygen enrichment, reverse osmosis.
> - **Catalytic converters**: Gaseous reactants diffusing through porous catalyst pellets.
> - **Drying operations**: Moisture diffusing through solid food/polymer materials.
> - **CVD/PVD in semiconductor manufacturing**: Dopant diffusion in silicon wafers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law from Kinetic Theory of Gases

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU**: Although not always asked as a full derivation, KTU examiners frequently test the **physical meaning of $D_{AB}$** and its dependence on temperature, pressure, and molecular properties. This derivation establishes that understanding.

#### Step 1: Statement of the Physical Premise

Consider a gas at rest, with species A having a **non-uniform concentration** along the $x$-direction. Molecules of A move randomly in all directions with an **average thermal speed** $\bar{u}_A$. Consider an imaginary plane at $x = 0$ perpendicular to the concentration gradient.

#### Step 2: Net Molecular Flux Across the Plane

By the kinetic theory of gases, molecules of A crossing the plane from above and below have, on average, traveled one **mean free path** $\lambda_A$ from their last collision. If the concentration at $x = -\lambda_A$ is $C_A(-\lambda_A)$ and at $x = +\lambda_A$ is $C_A(+\lambda_A)$, then the number of molecules crossing per unit area per unit time in the $+x$ direction is:

$$n_{A,+} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{u}_A \, C_A(-\lambda_A)$$

and in the $-x$ direction:

$$n_{A,-} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{u}_A \, C_A(+\lambda_A)$$

The **net flux** $J_A^*$ in the $+x$ direction is:

$$J_A^* = n_{A,+} - n_{A,-} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{u}_A \, \left[ C_A(-\lambda_A) - C_A(+\lambda_A) \right]$$

#### Step 3: Taylor Series Expansion of Concentration

For small $\lambda_A$, expand using Taylor series:

$$C_A(+\lambda_A) = C_A(0) + \lambda_A \left. \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right|_{x=0} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda_A^2)$$

$$C_A(-\lambda_A) = C_A(0) - \lambda_A \left. \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right|_{x=0} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda_A^2)$$

Subtracting:

$$C_A(-\lambda_A) - C_A(+\lambda_A) = -2 \lambda_A \left. \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right|_{x=0}$$

#### Step 4: Final Form of Fick's First Law

Substituting back:

$$J_A^* = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{u}_A \, \left( -2 \lambda_A \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right) = -\frac{1}{3} \, \bar{u}_A \, \lambda_A \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Comparing with $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$, we identify:

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \, \bar{u}_A \, \lambda_A}$$

This shows that the **diffusivity** is one-third of the product of average molecular speed and mean free path — a beautiful physical interpretation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation**: This derivation is one of the most frequently tested 14-mark problems. Master this thoroughly.

#### Setup

Consider ammonia (A) diffusing through stagnant air (B) in a vertical tube of length $z$. Ammonia is absorbed at the bottom by water; air is insoluble and remains stagnant. Steady state, isothermal, isobaric conditions. Total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ are constant.

#### Step 1: Governing Equation

The general molar flux equation for species A in a binary mixture A-B is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A \, v_m$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Stagnant-B Condition

Since air (B) is non-diffusing, $N_B = 0$. Using the same equation for B:

$$N_B = 0 = -D_{BA} \, \frac{dC_B}{dx} + C_B \, v_m$$

Solve for $v_m$:

$$v_m = \frac{D_{BA}}{C_B} \, \frac{dC_B}{dx}$$

Since $D_{AB} = D_{BA}$ in a binary system, and using $\dfrac{dC_B}{dx} = -\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ (from $C_A + C_B = C = \text{const}$):

$$v_m = -\frac{D_{AB}}{C_B} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Back into Flux Equation

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A \left( -\frac{D_{AB}}{C_B} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \right)$$

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \left( 1 + \frac{C_A}{C_B} \right)$$

Using $1 + \dfrac{C_A}{C_B} = \dfrac{C_B + C_A}{C_B} = \dfrac{C}{C_B}$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, C}{C_B} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

#### Step 4: Convert Concentrations to Partial Pressures

For an ideal gas, $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ and $C_B = \dfrac{P_B}{RT} = \dfrac{P - P_A}{RT}$. Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, (P/RT)}{(P - P_A)/RT} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{P - P_A} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Since $C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{RT}$, we have $dC_A = \dfrac{dP_A}{RT}$. Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{P - P_A} \, \frac{1}{RT} \, \frac{dP_A}{dx} = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT(P - P_A)} \, \frac{dP_A}{dx}$$

#### Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate

Under steady state, $N_A$ is constant. Separate variables:

$$N_A \, dx = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT(P - P_A)} \, dP_A$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ ($P_A = P_{A1}$) to $x = z$ ($P_A = P_{A2}$):

$$N_A \int_0^z dx = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}} \frac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

$$N_A \, z = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \left[ -\ln(P - P_A) \right]_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}}$$

$$N_A \, z = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln \left( \frac{P - P_{A1}}{P - P_{A2}} \right) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln \left( \frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}} \right)$$

#### Step 6: Final Form (Stefan Equation)

Rewriting the logarithm:

$$\ln \left( \frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}} \right) = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{P_{B,lm}}$$

where the **log-mean partial pressure** is:

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

Therefore, the final Stefan diffusion equation is:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Pattern Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of air (B) 1 mm thick at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $101.325\,\text{kPa}$. The partial pressure of ammonia at one side is $20\,\text{kPa}$ and at the other side is $5\,\text{kPa}$. The binary gas-phase diffusivity of $\text{NH}_3$-air at this condition is $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of ammonia through the air film.

#### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $z = 1\,\text{mm} = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298\,\text{K}$
- $P = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$
- $P_{A1} = 20\,\text{kPa}$, $P_{A2} = 5\,\text{kPa}$
- $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1**: Compute partial pressures of B:
$$P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 101.325 - 20 = 81.325\,\text{kPa}$$
$$P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 101.325 - 5 = 96.325\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2**: Compute log-mean partial pressure of B:

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{96.325 - 81.325}{\ln(96.325/81.325)}$$

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{15.0}{\ln(1.18443)} = \frac{15.0}{0.16930} = 88.602\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the Stefan diffusion equation:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$$

$$N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101.325)}{(8.314)(298)(10^{-3})} \cdot \frac{(20 - 5)}{88.602}$$

$$N_A = \frac{2.3305 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4776} \cdot \frac{15}{88.602}$$

$$N_A = (9.406 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.16930)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 1.592 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Step 4 (Verification)**: The flux is positive (in the direction of decreasing A concentration), confirming that A diffuses from high concentration (side 1) to low concentration (side 2). ✓

### 3.4 Derivative Reference — Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State)

For completeness (and KTU Part A questions):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

This is mathematically identical to the **transient heat conduction equation** $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$. Standard solutions include:
- Semi-infinite medium: $\dfrac{C_{A,s} - C_A(x,t)}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,i}} = \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\, t}}\right)$
- Thin film on substrate: $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{8}{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2}\exp\left[-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4 L^2}\right]$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Diffusion Mass Transfer Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Init[INITIAL CONDITIONS]
        direction TB
        nodeA1[Concentration C_A1 at x equals 0]
        nodeB1[Pressure P1 at x equals 0]
        nodeT1[Temperature T throughout]
        nodeZ1[Distance z between boundaries]
    end

    subgraph InputBoundary[BOUNDARY 1 - HIGH CONCENTRATION]
        direction TB
        nodeBA[Species A at high concentration]
        nodeBB[Species B stagnant or counter-diffusing]
        nodeBC[Concentration gradient dC_A/dx NEGATIVE]
    end

    subgraph MassTransport[MASS TRANSPORT MECHANISM]
        direction TB
        nodeMT1{Relative Motion of B}
        nodeMT2[Equimolar Counter-Diffusion if N_A equals minus N_B]
        nodeMT3[Stefan Diffusion if N_B equals 0]
        nodeMT4[Bulk flow term C_A v_m adjusts flux]
    end

    subgraph GoverningEq[GOVERNING EQUATIONS]
        direction TB
        nodeGE1[Ficks First Law J_A equals minus D_AB dC_A by dx]
        nodeGE2[Stefan Equation N_A equals D_AB P by RT z times delta P_A by P_B lm]
        nodeGE3[Ficks Second Law dC_A by dt equals D_AB d2C_A by dx2]
    end

    subgraph OutputBoundary[BOUNDARY 2 - LOW CONCENTRATION]
        direction TB
        nodeOB1[Species A absorbed or reacted]
        nodeOB2[Concentration C_A2 at x equals z]
        nodeOB3[Measured flux N_A out]
    end

    subgraph OutputCalc[ENGINEERING OUTPUTS]
        direction TB
        nodeOC1[Molar flux N_A in kmol per m2 per s]
        nodeOC2[Mass transfer rate W_A equals N_A times A]
        nodeOC3[Diffusivity D_AB from correlations]
        nodeOC4[Schmidt number Sc equals nu by D_AB]
    end

    Init --> InputBoundary
    InputBoundary --> MassTransport
    MassTransport --> GoverningEq
    GoverningEq --> OutputBoundary
    OutputBoundary --> OutputCalc

    nodeMT1 --> nodeMT2
    nodeMT1 --> nodeMT3
    nodeMT2 --> nodeMT4
    nodeMT3 --> nodeMT4
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Physical Picture of Stefan Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tube[VERTICAL DIFFUSION TUBE - LENGTH z]
        direction TB
        pt1[POINT 1: x equals 0<br/>P_A1 equals 20 kPa<br/>P_B1 equals 81.325 kPa<br/>AMMONIA SOURCE]
        pt2[POINT 2: x equals z equals 1 mm<br/>P_A2 equals 5 kPa<br/>P_B2 equals 96.325 kPa<br/>AMMONIA ABSORBED]
    end

    pt1 -- MolecDiff[MOLECULAR DIFFUSION<br/>Species A moves DOWN gradient] --> pt2

    pt1 -- StagB[STAGNANT B<br/>N_B equals 0<br/>No net motion] --- pt2

    subgraph FluxVec[FLUX VECTORS]
        direction LR
        fvA[ARROW: N_A in plus x direction]
        fvB[ARROW: v_m in plus x direction<br/>Bulk flow caused by A motion]
    end

    FluxVec --- Tube
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Identifying the Correct Diffusion Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([GIVEN DIFFUSION PROBLEM]) --> Q1{Is species B stagnant<br/>or counter-diffusing?}

    Q1 -- B stagnant N_B equals 0 --> StPath[USE STEFAN EQUATION]
    Q1 -- Counter diffusion N_A equals minus N_B --> EMCPath[USE EMCD EQUATION]
    Q1 -- General case --> GenPath[USE GENERAL FLUX EQUATION<br/>N_A equals minus D_AB dC_A by dx<br/>plus C_A v_m]

    StPath --> StForm[FORMULA<br/>N_A equals D_AB P by RT z<br/>times delta P_A by P_B lm]
    EMCPath --> EMCForm[FORMULA<br/>N_A equals D_AB P by RT z<br/>times y_A1 minus y_A2]

    GenPath --> GenForm[FORMULA<br/>N_A equals minus D_AB dC_A by dx<br/>plus C_A v_m<br/>Find v_m from N_B equation]

    StForm --> Out([ANSWER: Flux N_A])
    EMCForm --> Out
    GenForm --> Out
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the physical significance of each term with their SI units. Also write down Fick's Second Law.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Fick's First Law**: The molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion is directly proportional to the concentration gradient of that species and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

- $J_A^*$ = Molar flux of A $\left[ \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} \right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusivity of A in B $\left[ \text{m}^2/\text{s} \right]$
- $C_A$ = Molar concentration of A $\left[ \text{kmol/m}^3 \right]$
- $x$ = Distance in direction of diffusion $\left[ \text{m} \right]$
- Negative sign indicates flux from high to low concentration.

**Fick's Second Law** (for unsteady state):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

This is the **diffusion equation** that describes transient mass transfer, mathematically analogous to the transient heat conduction equation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with suitable units: (i) Molar flux, (ii) Binary diffusivity, (iii) Schmidt number.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Molar Flux** ($N_A$): The amount of substance (in moles) of species A passing through a unit area per unit time. Units: $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$.

**(ii) Binary Diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$): A transport property that quantifies the rate of molecular diffusion of species A through species B. It depends on temperature, pressure, and the molecular properties of both species. Units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**(iii) Schmidt Number** ($Sc$): A dimensionless number that represents the ratio of momentum diffusivity ($\nu$) to mass diffusivity ($D_{AB}$), analogous to the Prandtl number in heat transfer.

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{\mu}{\rho \, D_{AB}}$$

It is dimensionless. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**: As per the ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Module 4 is the relevant module here.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Starting from the general molar flux equation for a binary mixture, derive the equation for the **steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through a stagnant film of species B**. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Hydrogen sulfide ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, A) is absorbed into water from a stagnant gas mixture containing 30% $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and 70% nitrogen ($\text{N}_2$, B) by volume at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $1.013\,\text{bar}$. The diffusion takes place across a stagnant gas film of thickness $2\,\text{mm}$. At the gas-liquid interface, the partial pressure of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ is negligible. The binary gas-phase diffusivity is $D_{AB} = 1.45 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate:
  (i) The steady-state molar flux of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$.
  (ii) The molar flux if nitrogen were also diffusing in the opposite direction at an equal rate (equimolar counter-diffusion). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

*(See complete derivation in Section 3.2 above. Key steps:)*

1. Start with general flux equation: $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A v_m$
2. Apply stagnant-B condition $N_B = 0$ to find $v_m = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{C_B}\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$
3. Substitute back to get $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB} \, C}{C_B}\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$
4. Convert to partial pressures using ideal gas law
5. Separate variables and integrate to get the Stefan equation

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state
- Isothermal and isobaric conditions
- Ideal gas behavior
- One-dimensional diffusion in $z$-direction
- B is truly stagnant ($N_B = 0$)
- Constant total molar concentration $C$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{A1} = 0.30 \times 1.013 = 0.3039\,\text{bar} = 30.39\,\text{kPa}$
- $P_{A2} \approx 0$ (negligible at interface)
- $P_{B1} = 0.70 \times 1.013 = 0.7091\,\text{bar} = 70.91\,\text{kPa}$
- $P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 101.3\,\text{kPa}$
- $T = 298\,\text{K}$, $P = 101.3\,\text{kPa}$
- $z = 2\,\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $D_{AB} = 1.45 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1**: Log-mean partial pressure of B:

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{101.3 - 70.91}{\ln(101.3/70.91)} = \frac{30.39}{\ln(1.4285)}$$

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{30.39}{0.3567} = 85.20\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 (i)** — Stefan Diffusion:

$$N_A = \frac{(1.45 \times 10^{-5})(101.3)}{(8.314)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})} \cdot \frac{(30.39 - 0)}{85.20}$$

$$N_A = \frac{1.4689 \times 10^{-3}}{4.9552} \cdot \frac{30.39}{85.20}$$

$$N_A = (2.964 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.3567)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 1.057 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Step 3 (ii)** — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} (y_{A1} - y_{A2}) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{P}$$

$$N_A = \frac{(1.45 \times 10^{-5})(30.39 - 0)}{(8.314)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$N_A = \frac{4.407 \times 10^{-4}}{4.9552}$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 8.89 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Comparison**: Stefan flux $\approx 1.19 \times$ EMCD flux because bulk flow (induced by A's net movement) augments the diffusion of A when B is stagnant. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define molecular diffusion. Explain the two cases of equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B with neat sketches and write the corresponding flux equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gas mixture containing ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$, A) and nitrogen ($\text{N}_2$, B) is in contact with water at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $101.325\,\text{kPa}$. The gas phase contains 25% $\text{NH}_3$ by volume. Ammonia is absorbed rapidly by water, so its partial pressure at the water surface is essentially zero. The effective stagnant gas film thickness is $1.5\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the rate of absorption of $\text{NH}_3$ per $\text{m}^2$ of water surface if the diffusivity of $\text{NH}_3$ in $\text{N}_2$ at this condition is $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory:**

**Molecular Diffusion**: Mass transfer at the molecular level due to random thermal motion of molecules, driven by concentration gradients.

**Case 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion**
- Occurs when $N_A = -N_B$
- Examples: Binary distillation at constant molar overflow, two gases diffusing in opposite directions at equal molar rates
- Bulk molar velocity $v_m = 0$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2}) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT z}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$$

**Case 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B**
- Occurs when $N_B = 0$ (B is non-diffusing)
- Examples: Gas absorption of A in liquid with insoluble B
- Bulk molar velocity $v_m \neq 0$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$$

where $P_{B,lm} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$

*(Neat sketch should show concentration profiles: linear for EMCD, curved for Stefan diffusion due to bulk flow.)* **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $y_{A1} = 0.25$, $y_{A2} = 0$ (water absorbs ammonia)
- $T = 20 + 273 = 293\,\text{K}$
- $P = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$
- $z = 1.5\,\text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1**: Partial pressures:

$$P_{A1} = 0.25 \times 101.325 = 25.33\,\text{kPa}, \quad P_{A2} = 0$$

$$P_{B1} = 101.325 - 25.33 = 75.99\,\text{kPa}, \quad P_{B2} = 101.325 - 0 = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2**: Log-mean partial pressure of nitrogen (B):

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{101.325 - 75.99}{\ln(101.325/75.99)}$$

$$P_{B,lm} = \frac{25.335}{\ln(1.3334)} = \frac{25.335}{0.2877} = 88.06\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3**: Apply Stefan diffusion equation:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$$

$$N_A = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(101.325)}{(8.314)(293)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})} \cdot \frac{(25.33 - 0)}{88.06}$$

$$N_A = \frac{2.533 \times 10^{-3}}{3.654} \cdot 0.2876$$

$$N_A = (6.932 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.2876)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 1.994 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Step 4**: Rate of absorption per $\text{m}^2$ of water surface:
$$W_A = N_A \times A = 1.994 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 = 1.994 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kmol/s}$$

In mass terms: $\dot{m}_A = N_A \times M_A = 1.994 \times 10^{-4} \times 17 = 3.39 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}$ **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the log-mean correction**: When using Stefan diffusion (stagnant B), students often mistakenly use $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}P}{RTz}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$ instead of the correct $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}P}{RTz}\dfrac{(P_{A1}-P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$. This results in **losing 4 out of 7 marks** in Part B. Always check: *Is bulk flow present? If yes, use log-mean!*
> 
> 2. **Confusing $J_A^*$ and $N_A$**: $J_A^*$ is the **diffusive flux only**, while $N_A$ is the **total flux including bulk flow**. They are equal only when there is no net molar flow ($v_m = 0$, e.g., EMCD).
> 
> 3. **Unit inconsistency**: Always convert $z$ to **meters**, $D_{AB}$ to **m²/s**, and $P$ to **kPa or Pa** consistently. Mixing cm and m is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers.
> 
> 4. **Temperature in Kelvin**: Always convert $T$ to K (add 273.15) before plugging into $RT$. Using °C directly gives wrong answers by orders of magnitude.
> 
> 5. **Equimolar counter-diffusion assumption**: EMCD requires $N_A + N_B = 0$ (equal and opposite molar fluxes). Use only in distillation at constant molar overflow. In absorption with stagnant carrier gas, **always use Stefan diffusion**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Diffusion Mass Transfer:**
> 
> - [x] **Fick's First Law**: $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ — analogous to Fourier's law
> - [x] **Fick's Second Law**: $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ — analogous to transient heat conduction
> - [x] **EMCD flux**: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}P}{RTz}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ — no bulk flow correction
> - [x] **Stefan flux**: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}P}{RTz}\dfrac{(P_{A1}-P_{A2})}{P_{B,lm}}$ — bulk flow correction via log-mean
> - [x] **Log-mean**: $P_{B,lm} = \dfrac{P_{B2}-P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ — symmetric to log-mean temperature difference in heat exchangers
> - [x] **$D_{AB}$ scales as**: $T^{1.75}/P$ for gases (Chapman-Enskog theory)
> - [x] **$D_{AB}$ values**: gases $\sim 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, liquids $\sim 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, solids $\sim 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-14}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
> - [x] **Schmidt number**: $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ — analogous to Prandtl number $Pr$
> - [x] **Physical meaning of $D_{AB}$**: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{1}{3}\bar{u}_A \lambda_A$ — one-third of product of mean speed and mean free path
> - [x] **Stagnant B condition**: $N_B = 0$, hence $v_m \neq 0$ and bulk flow augments flux of A
> - [x] **Equimolar condition**: $N_A = -N_B$, hence $v_m = 0$ and bulk flow absent
> - [x] **Wilke-Chang correlation**: For liquid-phase diffusivity
> - [x] **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings**: For gas-phase diffusivity
> - [x] **Engineering applications**: Distillation, gas absorption, membrane separation, drying, catalysis
> - [x] **SI Units Checklist**: $D_{AB}$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $N_A$ in $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$, $T$ in K, $P$ in Pa or kPa, $z$ in m

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fick's Law of Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fick's Law of Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fick's Law of Diffusion** is the fundamental constitutive equation of mass transfer, analogous to Fourier's Law in heat conduction and Newton's Law in momentum transfer. It states that *the molar flux of a species due to molecular diffusion is proportional to the negative gradient of its concentration*.

Mathematically, **Fick's First Law** for species **A** diffusing through a stagnant medium is expressed as:

$$
\vec{J}_A = -D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A
$$

For one-dimensional diffusion along the $x$-axis:

$$
J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
$$

where:
- $J_{A,x}$ = molar flux of species **A** in the $x$-direction $\left[ \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}} \right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = **binary mass diffusivity** or **diffusion coefficient** of **A** through **B** $\left[ \dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \right]$
- $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ = concentration gradient of species **A** $\left[ \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^4} \right]$

The **negative sign** is the directional indicator: species diffuse *down* the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration), a process driven by the random thermal motion of molecules seeking thermodynamic equilibrium.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Perfume Spray in a Closed Room:** Imagine spraying perfume at one corner of a closed room. Initially, the air near the spray nozzle has an extremely high concentration of perfume molecules. After a few minutes, you can smell the fragrance at the opposite corner of the room. What happened? The perfume molecules did not fly across the room — they underwent random Brownian motion, gradually migrating from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration until the entire room was uniformly scented. This spontaneous, macroscopic mixing driven purely by concentration differences — without any bulk convection — is **molecular diffusion**, and **Fick's Law** is the mathematical statement of how fast this mixing proceeds.

**Other everyday examples:**
- **Sugar cube dissolving** in a stationary cup of tea (diffusion of sucrose molecules into water).
- **Ink drop** spreading in a glass of still water.
- **Oxygen absorption** across the alveolar membrane in human lungs.
- **Moisture migration** through porous building materials.

## 1.3 Order of Magnitude of Mass Diffusivity

| Physical State of System | Typical Range of $D_{AB}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gas in Gas (e.g., water vapor in air) | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ $\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$ |
| Liquid in Liquid (e.g., ethanol in water) | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ $\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$ |
| Solid in Solid (e.g., carbon in iron) | $10^{-14}$ to $10^{-12}$ $\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$ |

This enormous variation (over **10 orders of magnitude**) is why mass transfer rates are vastly different in gases, liquids, and solids.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Mechanism

Molecular diffusion arises from the **random thermal motion** of molecules. Although each individual molecule moves erratically in three-dimensional space, the *net statistical result* over a large population is a migration from regions of high concentration to low concentration. Fick's Law is essentially a *macroscopic statistical consequence* of microscopic Brownian motion.

## 2.2 Critical Assumptions Embedded in Fick's Law

1. The medium is **isothermal** and **isobaric** (no temperature or pressure gradients driving bulk flow).
2. The fluid is a **dilute solution** (species **A** is the minor component; the host **B** is the major component).
3. $D_{AB}$ is treated as **constant** (independent of position and concentration).
4. No chemical reaction occurs within the control volume.
5. Diffusion is governed by an **ideal dilute solution** behavior.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When $D_{AB}$ varies significantly with concentration (e.g., in concentrated liquid solutions), the **non-linear** form of Fick's Law $J_A = -D_{AB}(C_A) \, dC_A/dx$ must be used, requiring numerical or iterative integration.

## 2.3 Forms of Fick's Law (Comprehensive)

### (a) Molar Flux Form (with Bulk Motion Correction)

When bulk fluid motion occurs, the *absolute* molar flux $N_A$ differs from the *diffusive* flux $J_A$:

$$
N_A = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

where $x_A$ is the mole fraction of **A**, and $(N_A + N_B)$ is the total molar flux accounting for bulk convection.

### (b) Special Case 1 — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion

When $N_A + N_B = 0$ (e.g., in a binary distillation process where molecules of **A** moving one direction are exactly balanced by molecules of **B** moving the opposite direction):

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
$$

### (c) Special Case 2 — Diffusion of A through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

When $N_B = 0$ (component **B** is stationary, e.g., evaporation of water into still air):

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, C}{1 - x_A} \, \frac{dx_A}{dx}
$$

which integrates to give:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation / Formula | Physical Meaning / When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | **Fick's First Law** — general 1-D diffusive flux. |
| 2 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{C}{1-x_A} \, \dfrac{dx_A}{dx}$ | Diffusive flux in mole-fraction form (preferred for gas mixtures). |
| 3 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$ | **Total molar flux** including bulk motion. |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$ | Steady-state equimolar counter-diffusion through a slab of thickness $L$. |
| 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, C}{L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A,2}}{1-x_{A,1}}\right)$ | Steady-state diffusion of **A** through stagnant **B** (Stefan flow). |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | **Fick's Second Law** — unsteady-state 1-D diffusion. |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \nabla^2 C_A$ | Fick's Second Law in 3-D Cartesian coordinates. |
| 8 | $D_{AB} \approx D_{BA}$ | Reciprocity relation for binary gas diffusion (Graham's law). |
| 9 | $\dfrac{D_{AB} \, \rho}{T} = \text{const}$ | Chapman-Enskog scaling for gas diffusivity (high-Y in KTU derivations). |
| 10 | $N_A \, = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - P_{A,2}}{P - P_{A,1}}\right)$ | Stefan diffusion form expressed in partial pressures for ideal gases. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fick's Law is the cornerstone of:
- **Distillation column design** (vapor-liquid mass transfer).
- **Absorption and stripping** operations in chemical process industries.
- **Membrane separation** technology (pervaporation, reverse osmosis).
- **Drying operations** (moisture migration from solid interiors to surface).
- **Atmospheric pollution dispersion** modeling.
- **Drug delivery systems** (controlled release from polymer matrices).
- **Corrosion engineering** (oxygen diffusion through electrolyte films).
- **Food processing** (salt-curing, sugar infusion, dehydration).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Transport Analogies:** Fick's Law, Fourier's Law, and Newton's Law of viscosity are mathematically identical in structure — a flux proportional to the gradient of a conserved quantity. This **heat-mass-momentum analogy** is a recurring favorite in KTU examinations, often asked in Part A or as a sub-question in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's First Law from Kinetic Theory of Gases

We will rigorously derive $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ using the statistical mechanics of an ideal gas.

### Setup (Pre-Derivation Logic)

Consider species **A** molecules in an ideal gas mixture. Each molecule moves randomly with an average thermal speed $\bar{c}$. We define:
- $n_A(x)$ = number density (molecules per unit volume) at position $x$.
- $\lambda$ = mean free path (average distance a molecule travels between collisions).
- We assume the gas is in *dynamic equilibrium* — no net bulk motion.

### Step 1: Molecules Crossing an Imaginary Plane

Consider an imaginary plane perpendicular to the $x$-axis at $x = x_0$. Due to random thermal motion, molecules cross this plane in both directions.

By the principle of isotropy, exactly **1/6** of all molecules at any location move in the $+x$ direction (since random motion in 3-D space splits into $\pm x$, $\pm y$, $\pm z$ directions).

**Number of A molecules crossing per unit area per unit time from the left (position $x_0 - \lambda$):**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{left}} = \frac{1}{6} \, n_A(x_0 - \lambda) \, \bar{c}
$$

**Number of A molecules crossing per unit area per unit time from the right (position $x_0 + \lambda$):**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{right}} = \frac{1}{6} \, n_A(x_0 + \lambda) \, \bar{c}
$$

### Step 2: Net Molecular Flux

The net number of **A** molecules crossing per unit area per unit time in the $+x$ direction:

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{net}} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{c} \, \bigl[ n_A(x_0 - \lambda) - n_A(x_0 + \lambda) \bigr]
$$

### Step 3: Taylor Series Expansion

Expand both $n_A(x_0 - \lambda)$ and $n_A(x_0 + \lambda)$ about $x_0$ using Taylor series (assuming $\lambda$ is small):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_A(x_0 - \lambda) &= n_A(x_0) - \lambda \, \frac{dn_A}{dx} + \frac{\lambda^2}{2!} \, \frac{d^2 n_A}{dx^2} - \dots \\[6pt]
n_A(x_0 + \lambda) &= n_A(x_0) + \lambda \, \frac{dn_A}{dx} + \frac{\lambda^2}{2!} \, \frac{d^2 n_A}{dx^2} + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtract the second expansion from the first:

$$
n_A(x_0 - \lambda) - n_A(x_0 + \lambda) = -2\lambda \, \frac{dn_A}{dx} + O(\lambda^3)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back into the Flux Expression

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{net}} = \frac{1}{6} \, \bar{c} \, \left( -2\lambda \, \frac{dn_A}{dx} \right) = -\frac{1}{3} \, \bar{c} \, \lambda \, \frac{dn_A}{dx}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the Diffusion Coefficient

Define the **binary mass diffusivity** $D_{AB}$ as:

$$
D_{AB} \equiv \frac{1}{3} \, \bar{c} \, \lambda
$$

This yields the celebrated **Fick's First Law**:

$$
\boxed{\;J_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dn_A}{dx}\;}
$$

If we work in molar concentration units $C_A = n_A / N_A$ (where $N_A$ is Avogadro's number), the equation becomes:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The factor of $1/3$ in $D_{AB} = \bar{c}\lambda/3$ arises from the 3-D isotropic motion. A common student error is writing $D_{AB} = \bar{c}\lambda/6$ — remember that we used $1/6$ for *one direction*, and the cancellation between left-to-right and right-to-left halves yields the final factor of $1/3$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State)

### Step 1: Mass Balance on a Differential Element

Consider a slab of thickness $dx$, area $A$, with concentration $C_A(x,t)$ varying with both position and time. The mass balance within this elemental volume is:

$$
\left(\text{Rate of mass in}\right) - \left(\text{Rate of mass out}\right) + \left(\text{Rate of generation}\right) = \left(\text{Rate of accumulation}\right)
$$

Assuming **no chemical reaction** (generation = 0):

$$
\left. J_A \right|_x \cdot A - \left. J_A \right|_{x+dx} \cdot A = A \, dx \, \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}
$$

### Step 2: Taylor Expansion of Flux at $x + dx$

$$
J_A(x+dx) = J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} \, dx + \dots
$$

Substituting into the balance:

$$
-\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} \, dx = dx \, \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Fick's First Law

Insert $J_A = -D_{AB} \, \partial C_A / \partial x$:

$$
-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}
$$

For constant $D_{AB}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}\;}
$$

This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** — the direct mass-transfer analogue of the heat equation $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \, \partial^2 T/\partial x^2$.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example — Steady-State Diffusion Through a Slab

**Problem Statement:**
Hydrogen (H₂) at $25°C$ and 1 atm diffuses through a stagnant film of nitrogen (N₂) of thickness $L = 2$ mm. The mole fraction of H₂ on one side is $x_{A,1} = 0.10$ and on the other side is $x_{A,2} = 0.05$. The binary diffusion coefficient is $D_{AB} = 6.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of H₂.

### Solution Steps

**Step 1: Identify the physical scenario.**

Since N₂ is stagnant, this is a **Stefan diffusion** problem (A through stagnant B).

**Step 2: Apply the Stefan diffusion equation:**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Calculate total molar concentration using ideal gas law.**

$$
C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325 \, \text{Pa}}{(8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K})(298.15 \, \text{K})}
$$

$$
C = \frac{101325}{2478.8} \approx 40.87 \, \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
N_A = \frac{(6.85 \times 10^{-5})(40.87)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.10}\right)
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{2.80 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \, \ln(1.0556)
$$

$$
N_A = (1.400) \times (0.05407) = 7.57 \times 10^{-2} \, \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A \approx 7.57 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Check:** Because $x_{A,1} > x_{A,2}$, H₂ flows from side 1 to side 2. The logarithmic correction factor accounts for the fact that the effective diffusion path changes as the bulk motion (induced by H₂'s net flux) drags N₂ along — a hallmark of Stefan diffusion that pure linear Fick's Law would miss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Transport Phenomena — Universal Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H["HEAT TRANSFER"]
        HQ["Driving Potential: Temperature T"]
        HF["Transport Law: Fourier's Law"]
        HK["Transport Coefficient: Thermal Conductivity k"]
        HF1["q = -k * dT/dx"]
    end
    subgraph M["MOMENTUM TRANSFER"]
        MQ["Driving Potential: Velocity u"]
        MF["Transport Law: Newton's Law"]
        MK["Transport Coefficient: Viscosity mu"]
        MF1["tau = -mu * du/dx"]
    end
    subgraph MT["MASS TRANSFER"]
        MTQ["Driving Potential: Concentration C_A"]
        MTF["Transport Law: Fick's Law"]
        MTK["Transport Coefficient: Diffusivity D_AB"]
        MTF1["J_A = -D_AB * dC_A/dx"]
    end
    HQ --> MF
    MQ --> MF
    MQ --> MTF
    MTQ --> MTF
    MQ --> HF
    MTQ --> HF
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of the Mass Transfer System (Steady-State 1-D Diffusion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bulk Region: High Concentration C_A1"] --> B["Stagnant Boundary Layer or Membrane"]
    B --> C["Bulk Region: Low Concentration C_A2"]
    B --> D["Molecular Diffusion Zone - Thickness L"]
    D --> E["Concentration Gradient: dC_A/dx less than 0"]
    E --> F["Drives Diffusive Flux J_A in +x direction"]
    F --> G["Equation: J_A equals -D_AB times dC_A/dx"]
    G --> H["Conservation: Steady state implies constant flux"]
    H --> I["Integrate to obtain linear concentration profile"]
    I --> J["Final Form: N_A equals D_AB over L times C_A1 minus C_A2"]
```

## 4.3 Fick's First vs Second Law — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Mass Transfer Problem Identified"] --> S2{"Steady State?"}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3["Apply Fick's First Law"]
    S2 -- "No" --> S4["Apply Fick's Second Law"]
    S3 --> S5{"Bulk Motion Present?"}
    S5 -- "No, equimolar counter-diffusion" --> S6["N_A equals -D_AB times dC_A/dx"]
    S5 -- "Yes, A through stagnant B" --> S7["Stefan diffusion: Use logarithmic form"]
    S4 --> S8["Use dC_A/dt equals D_AB times d2C_A/dx2"]
    S8 --> S9["Apply IC and BC to solve PDE"]
    S9 --> S10["Use separation of variables or error function solutions"]
```

## 4.4 Physical Picture — Concentration Profile Across a Slab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["x equals 0: C_A equals C_A1 HIGH"] --> P2["Linear decrease through slab thickness L"]
    P2 --> P3["x equals L: C_A equals C_A2 LOW"]
    P1 -. "Slope equals minus N_A over D_AB" .-> P3
    Q1["x equals 0: x_A1 equals 0.10"] --> Q2["Stefan flow: NON-LINEAR profile"]
    Q2 --> Q3["x equals L: x_A2 equals 0.05"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram illustrates the powerful **heat-mass-momentum analogy** that KTU examiners love to test. The second and fourth diagrams distinguish between the *linear* concentration profile of equimolar counter-diffusion and the *non-linear* (logarithmic) profile of Stefan diffusion. The third diagram is your **decision-making flowchart** when confronted with a mass transfer problem in the exam hall.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Define all the terms involved and explain the physical significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer:**

Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the molar flux of a diffusing species **A** is directly proportional to the concentration gradient of that species and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.

Mathematically:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
$$

**Terms:**
- $J_A$: Molar flux of species **A** $\left[ \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}} \right]$
- $D_{AB}$: Binary mass diffusivity $\left[ \dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \right]$
- $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$: Concentration gradient of **A** in the $x$-direction

**Significance of the negative sign:** The negative sign ensures that mass flux always occurs in the direction of **decreasing** concentration, i.e., from high to low concentration regions, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy increases through mixing).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare and contrast Fick's Law of Diffusion with Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction, highlighting at least two similarities and one key difference.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fick's Law (Mass Transfer) | Fourier's Law (Heat Transfer) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mathematical form | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ |
| Driving potential | Concentration gradient $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Temperature gradient $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ |
| Transport property | Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ | Thermal conductivity $k$ |
| Sign convention | Flux is in the direction of decreasing $C_A$ | Heat flux is in the direction of decreasing $T$ |
| Result of integration | Linear concentration profile (steady state) | Linear temperature profile (steady state) |

**Similarities:**
1. Both are linear phenomenological laws stating that flux is proportional to a driving gradient.
2. Both produce linear steady-state profiles through a homogeneous medium.

**Key difference:** The driving force in Fick's Law is the concentration gradient of a *species*, while in Fourier's Law it is the temperature gradient — fundamentally different transport quantities, but mathematically isomorphic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's First Law of Diffusion from the kinetic theory of gases, clearly stating all assumptions. Explain the physical significance of the binary diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ and how it differs in gases, liquids, and solids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary gas mixture of methane (CH₄) and air is contained in a tube of length $L = 0.05$ m at $25°C$ and 1 atm. The mole fraction of CH₄ at one end is $x_{A,1} = 0.20$ and at the other end is $x_{A,2} = 0.05$. If the diffusion is equimolar counter-diffusion and $D_{AB} = 2.10 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, determine:
  (i) The molar flux of CH₄ in mol/(m²·s).
  (ii) The mass flux of CH₄ in kg/(m²·s).
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The gas is ideal and dilute.
2. Isothermal, isobaric conditions.
3. Random molecular motion with no net bulk flow in absence of concentration gradient.
4. Mean free path $\lambda$ is small compared to system dimensions.

**Derivation:** (Follow Section 3.1 of these notes)

Key result:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}, \quad \text{where} \quad D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c} \lambda
$$

**Difference in $D_{AB}$ across phases:**
- **Gases:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; dominated by molecular mean free path; increases with temperature as $T^{3/2}$ and decreases with pressure.
- **Liquids:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; governed by Stokes-Einstein relation $D = k_B T / (6 \pi \mu r)$; molecules must squeeze past neighbors.
- **Solids:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-12}$ m²/s or smaller; depends on crystal lattice vacancies, defects, and activated hopping; obeys Arrhenius behavior $D = D_0 \, e^{-E/RT}$.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given:** $L = 0.05$ m, $T = 298.15$ K, $P = 101325$ Pa, $x_{A,1} = 0.20$, $x_{A,2} = 0.05$, $D_{AB} = 2.10 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Total molar concentration (Ideal gas law):**

$$
C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{(8.314)(298.15)} = 40.87 \, \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**(i) Molar flux (equimolar counter-diffusion):**

Since equimolar counter-diffusion implies $N_A + N_B = 0$:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{L}(x_{A,1} - x_{A,2})
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.10 \times 10^{-5})(40.87)}{0.05}(0.20 - 0.05)
$$

$$
N_A = (1.717 \times 10^{-2})(0.15) = 2.575 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

**(ii) Mass flux:**

Molecular weight of CH₄ = $M_A = 16.04$ kg/kmol = $0.01604$ kg/mol.

$$
\dot{m}_A = N_A \cdot M_A = (2.575 \times 10^{-3})(0.01604)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}_A \approx 4.13 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is meant by **Stefan diffusion** (diffusion of A through stagnant B)? Derive the corresponding steady-state flux equation and contrast it with the equimolar counter-diffusion case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tube of length $L = 0.10$ m contains a stagnant mixture of air and water vapor at $30°C$ and 1 atm. The partial pressure of water vapor at the bottom is $P_{A,1} = 0.04$ atm and at the top is $P_{A,2} = 0.01$ atm. Given $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, determine:
  (i) The molar flux of water vapor at the bottom of the tube.
  (ii) The molar flux of water vapor at the top of the tube.
  Explain why the two values are equal in this scenario. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Stefan Diffusion:** When species **A** diffuses through a stagnant medium **B** (i.e., $N_B = 0$), bulk motion of the gas occurs in the direction of **A**'s diffusion to compensate for the zero flux of **B**. This bulk motion distorts the concentration profile, making it **non-linear**.

**Derivation:**

For 1-D steady state, $J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B) = N_A$, and $N_B = 0$:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, C}{1 - x_A} \, \frac{dx_A}{dx}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $x=0$ ($x_A = x_{A,1}$) to $x=L$ ($x_A = x_{A,2}$):

$$
N_A \int_0^L dx = -D_{AB} \, C \int_{x_{A,1}}^{x_{A,2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}
$$

$$
N_A \cdot L = D_{AB} \, C \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)\;}
$$

**Contrast with Equimolar Counter-Diffusion:**

| Feature | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion | Stefan Diffusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bulk motion | Absent ($N_A + N_B = 0$) | Present (in direction of **A**) |
| Concentration profile | **Linear** in $x$ | **Logarithmic (non-linear)** in $x$ |
| Flux equation | $N_A = (D_{AB}/L)(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$ | $N_A = (D_{AB}C/L) \ln[(1-x_{A,2})/(1-x_{A,1})]$ |
| Common applications | Distillation, equimolar evaporation | Liquid evaporation into stagnant air |

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:** $L = 0.10$ m, $T = 303.15$ K, $P = 1$ atm = $101325$ Pa, $P_{A,1} = 0.04$ atm, $P_{A,2} = 0.01$ atm, $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Total molar concentration:**

$$
C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{(8.314)(303.15)} = 40.18 \, \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Stefan diffusion flux equation:**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A,2}}{P - P_{A,1}}\right)
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(303.15)(0.10)} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.01}{1 - 0.04}\right)
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{2.6345}{252.04} \ln(1.03125)
$$

$$
N_A = (1.0453 \times 10^{-2})(0.03077)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}\;}
$$

**(i) Bottom flux:** $N_A \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m²·s)

**(ii) Top flux:** $N_A \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m²·s) — *same as the bottom.*

**Explanation of equality:** Under **steady-state** conditions, mass conservation requires that the molar flux of water vapor must be **identical at every cross-section** of the tube. Otherwise, mass would accumulate or deplete somewhere along the tube, contradicting the steady-state assumption. Hence $N_A$ at the bottom equals $N_A$ at the top.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** of $D_{AB}$, $L$, or $C$ — the most frequent reason for numerical errors (lose ~2 marks).
> 2. **Using equimolar counter-diffusion formula** when the problem clearly states *stagnant* or *stationary* B — this directly yields a wrong answer (lose ~3-4 marks).
> 3. **Missing the bulk motion correction** when working in mole fractions instead of concentrations — leads to errors of 5–10% in the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing molar flux** $N_A$ **with mass flux** $\dot{m}_A$ — remember $\dot{m}_A = N_A \cdot M_A$.
> 5. **Writing the negative sign incorrectly** in Fick's Law — the negative sign belongs to the *equation*, not just the *gradient* (lose ~1 mark if omitted).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fick's Law of Diffusion**

- **Fick's First Law (Steady State):** $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ — relates diffusive flux to concentration gradient.
- **Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State):** $\partial C_A / \partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A / \partial x^2$ — direct analogue of the heat equation.
- **Binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$** has units of **m²/s**; ranges from $10^{-5}$ (gases) to $10^{-14}$ (solids).
- **Equimolar counter-diffusion:** $N_A + N_B = 0 \Rightarrow$ linear concentration profile; $N_A = (D_{AB}/L)(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$.
- **Stefan diffusion (A through stagnant B):** $N_B = 0 \Rightarrow$ logarithmic concentration profile; $N_A = (D_{AB} C / L) \ln[(1-x_{A,2})/(1-x_{A,1})]$.
- **Kinetic theory expression:** $D_{AB} = \bar{c} \lambda / 3$ — derived from one-sixth of molecules moving in $+x$ direction and Taylor expansion.
- **Transport analogy (heat ↔ mass ↔ momentum):** All three are mathematically isomorphic; memorize the triplets (potential, law, coefficient): ($T$, Fourier, $k$), ($C_A$, Fick, $D_{AB}$), ($u$, Newton, $\mu$).
- **Gas-phase diffusivity scaling:** Chapman-Enskog relation gives $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ — increases with temperature, decreases with pressure.
- **Liquid-phase diffusivity:** Stokes-Einstein relation: $D_{AB} = k_B T / (6 \pi \mu r)$.
- **Stefan flow bulk correction:** When $N_B = 0$, bulk motion in the direction of $N_A$ reduces the effective driving force — captured by the $(1-x_A)$ denominator in the mole-fraction form of Fick's Law.
- **Sign convention:** Negative sign in Fick's Law always points mass flux from high to low concentration.
- **Reciprocity:** For binary ideal gases, $D_{AB} \approx D_{BA}$ (Graham's law basis).
- **Exam shortcut:** For $D_{AB}$ in problems with $T$ and $P$ given, scale reference values using $D_{AB,2}/D_{AB,1} = (T_2/T_1)^{3/2} (P_1/P_2)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
