<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady State Molecular Diffusion** is the mass transfer process in which molecules of a chemical species are transported from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration through a stationary (or co-moving) fluid medium under the influence of a **concentration gradient**, where the concentration profile at every point in the system remains **constant with respect to time** ($\partial C / \partial t = 0$).

The driving force is purely molecular in nature — there is **no bulk convective motion** contributing to the transport; only random molecular agitation (Brownian motion) causes the net displacement of species. The governing transport law is **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, which is mathematically analogous to Fourier's Law of heat conduction and Newton's Law of viscosity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumptions for Steady State Molecular Diffusion:**
> 1. Diffusion occurs through a **stationary plane of reference** (Stokes reference frame) OR a defined moving reference.
> 2. The total concentration $C_T$ (or total pressure $P$) remains constant throughout the medium.
> 3. The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ is assumed **constant** over the concentration range (valid for ideal gases and dilute solutions).
> 4. No chemical reaction occurs within the diffusion path.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Scented Perfume in a Closed Room"

Imagine you open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a still, closed room. You do not blow a fan; the room air is perfectly still. After some time, the perfume molecules travel to the opposite corner entirely on their own.

**Why does this happen?**
Each molecule is in constant random motion, colliding millions of times per second. Because the concentration of perfume molecules is high near the bottle and zero at the far wall, statistically, **more molecules leave the high-concentration zone than enter it**. This imbalance creates a **net molecular flux** from high to low concentration — this is molecular diffusion.

In *steady state*, if you kept the perfume bottle open continuously and the molecules that arrived at the far wall were constantly removed (vented), the concentration *profile* at every fixed point in the room would no longer change with time. A stable, linear (or curved) gradient of perfume concentration would exist between the two walls. This stable gradient is the **steady state**.

| Real World Element | Diffusion Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Perfume vapour | Species **A** |
| Still air | Species **B** (stagnant medium) |
| Difference in smell intensity | Concentration gradient $\Delta C_A$ |
| Smell propagation speed | Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ |
| Closed still room | No bulk convection, only molecular motion |

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition:** *Diffusion is the "democratic" transport mechanism — molecules vote with their motion, and the majority direction wins until equilibrium.*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities are used universally in KTU problems:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Molar Volume:** $22.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$ at **STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)**
- **Typical Gas Diffusivity $D_{AB}$:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Typical Liquid Diffusivity $D_{AB}$:** $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Typical Solid Diffusivity $D_{AB}$:** $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-14} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Knudsen Number Threshold:** $K_n = \lambda / d_p > 0.5$ → molecular diffusion breaks down
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's First Law — The Foundation

**Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)** states that the molar flux of species A relative to a stationary reference plane is directly proportional to its concentration gradient:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = Molar flux of species A $\left[\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})\right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusivity of A in B $\left[\text{m}^2/\text{s}\right]$
- $C_A$ = Concentration of A $\left[\text{kmol}/\text{m}^3\right]$
- $z$ = Direction of diffusion [m]
- **Negative sign** indicates flux is in the direction of decreasing concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> Fick's Law is a **linear, first-order differential equation** under constant $D_{AB}$, which makes steady-state problems analytically tractable.

---

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Steady-State Cases (KTU High-Yield)

### **Case 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)**
- Equal and opposite molar fluxes: $N_A = -N_B$
- Net molar flux = 0 (no net bulk flow)
- Example: Distillation of a binary mixture at constant molar overflow

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{z} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

### **Case 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B**
- Species B is non-diffusing (e.g., B reacts at the surface, or is absorbed elsewhere)
- Bulk flow contribution must be added
- Example: Sublimation of naphthalene into still air, water evaporation into air

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot z} \cdot \frac{1}{p_{BM}} \left( p_{A1} - p_{A2} \right)$$

Where the **log mean partial pressure of B** is:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

### **Case 3: Diffusion in Porous Solids (Effective Diffusivity)**
- Used in catalysis, drying, membrane separation
- Accounts for tortuosity $\tau$ and porosity $\varepsilon$:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{AB}$$

---

## 2.3 Reference Frame Correction (Stokes vs. Molar-Average)

The flux measured with respect to a **stationary** coordinate system must account for bulk motion. The relation is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

This is the most general form used in KTU derivations. Rearranging:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_T$$

where $N_T = N_A + N_B$ is the total molar flux.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dz$ | Fick's First Law (general) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $N_A = (D_{AB}/z)(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | Equimolar counter-diffusion | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 3 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T z \, p_{BM}}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ | A through stagnant B (gas) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $p_{BM} = (p_{B2} - p_{B1})/\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})$ | Log mean of B | kPa or atm |
| 5 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C_T}{z} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)$ | A through stagnant B (mole frac) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{z \, x_{BM}}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$ | Liquid: A through stagnant B | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 7 | $x_{BM} = (x_{B2} - x_{B1})/\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})$ | Log mean of B (liquid) | — |
| 8 | $D_{eff} = (\varepsilon/\tau) \, D_{AB}$ | Porous solid diffusion | m²/s |
| 9 | $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}, \propto 1/P$ | Gas diffusivity trend | — |
| 10 | $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu$ | Liquid diffusivity (Stokes-Einstein) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the problem states *"B is stagnant"* or *"diffusion of A through non-diffusing B"*, you **must** include the bulk flow correction. Using only the simplified Fick's law (Case 1) will give a **wrong answer and full mark deduction**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Chemical Process Industry:** Distillation columns, absorption towers, membrane separators
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug release from polymer matrices, transdermal patches
- **Food Industry:** Drying of grains, moisture migration in packaged foods
- **Petrochemical:** Catalyst pore diffusion, hydrogen purification
- **Environmental Engineering:** Pollutant dispersion in stagnant water bodies
- **Electronics:** Dopant diffusion in semiconductor wafer fabrication
- **Biomedical:** Oxygen transport in blood capillaries, controlled drug delivery

> [!TIP]
> The **stagnant B** model is the most-tested scenario in KTU numericals. Always verify the problem wording — "equimolar" vs. "stagnant" vs. "equal mass" — each leads to a different equation set.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady State Diffusion Through a Stagnant Gas Film

### **Problem Statement:**
A gas mixture containing species A and B is in a tube. Species A diffuses from point 1 ($z=0$) to point 2 ($z=L$). Species B is **non-diffusing** (stagnant). Total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ are constant.

### **Step 1: Start with the General Flux Equation (Stokes Reference)**

In a stationary reference frame, the total flux is the sum of molecular diffusion flux and bulk convective flux:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C_T} (N_A + N_B)$$

### **Step 2: Apply the Stagnant B Condition**

Since B is non-diffusing, $N_B = 0$. Therefore the total flux is $N_T = N_A + N_B = N_A$.

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C_T} \cdot N_A$$

### **Step 3: Isolate the Diffusion Term**

Subtract $(C_A/C_T)N_A$ from both sides:

$$N_A - \frac{C_A}{C_T} N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C_T}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### **Step 4: Recognize That $1 - C_A/C_T = C_B/C_T$**

$$N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C_T} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### **Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{C_T}{C_B} \, dC_A$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ (point 1) to $z = L$ (point 2), and noting $N_A$ and $D_{AB}$ are constants:

$$\int_{0}^{L} N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} C_T \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} \frac{dC_A}{C_B}$$

### **Step 6: Evaluate the Right-Hand Side Integral**

Since $C_B = C_T - C_A$, we have $dC_B = -dC_A$:

$$N_A L = D_{AB} C_T \int_{C_{B1}}^{C_{B2}} \frac{dC_B}{C_B} = D_{AB} C_T \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{B2}}{C_{B1}}\right)$$

### **Step 7: Convert Concentration to Partial Pressures Using Ideal Gas Law**

Using $C = P/(RT)$ and $C_A/P = p_A/P_T$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right)$$

### **Step 8: Express in Terms of A's Partial Pressures (log mean form)**

Using the identity $\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1}) = (p_{A1} - p_{A2})/p_{BM}$:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T L \, p_{BM}} \left( p_{A1} - p_{A2} \right)}$$

where the log-mean of B is:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fick's Law from Kinetic Theory (Physical Origin)

### **Step 1: Consider a Reference Plane at z = 0**
Molecules on either side cross this plane randomly with an average velocity $\bar{u}$ (from Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution).

### **Step 2: Number Density of A above the Plane**
At distance $\lambda$ (mean free path) above the plane, the number density is $C_A(-\lambda) = C_A(0) - \lambda \, (dC_A/dz)$.

### **Step 3: Molecules Crossing the Plane per Unit Time**
Molecules cross from a distance $\lambda$ within a time interval of $\lambda/\bar{u}$:

$$\text{Flux from above} = \frac{1}{6} \bar{u} \left[ C_A(0) - \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dz} \right]$$

### **Step 4: Net Flux Through the Plane**
Subtracting the downward flux from the upward flux (only the gradient terms survive):

$$N_A = -\frac{1}{3} \bar{u} \lambda \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### **Step 5: Define the Diffusivity**

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{u} \lambda}$$

This shows **$D_{AB}$ is a property of the medium** depending on molecular velocity and mean free path.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of nitrogen (B) of thickness 2 mm. Partial pressure of NH₃ at one side is 20 kPa and at the other is 5 kPa. Total pressure = 101.3 kPa, T = 298 K, $D_{AB} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state flux.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate partial pressures of B**
- $p_{B1} = 101.3 - 20 = 81.3$ kPa
- $p_{B2} = 101.3 - 5 = 96.3$ kPa

**Step 2: Log-mean of B**
$$p_{BM} = \frac{96.3 - 81.3}{\ln(96.3 / 81.3)} = \frac{15.0}{\ln(1.1845)} = \frac{15.0}{0.1694} = 88.55 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply the stagnant-B flux equation**
$$N_A = \frac{(2.3 \times 10^{-5})(101300)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)(88550)} (20000 - 5000)$$

**Step 4: Numerator**
$$(2.3 \times 10^{-5})(101300)(15000) = 34.948$$

**Step 5: Denominator**
$$(8.314)(298)(0.002)(88550) = 43{,}880$$

**Step 6: Result**
$$N_A = \frac{34.948}{43{,}880} = 7.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_A \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Steady State Molecular Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEADY STATE MOLECULAR DIFFUSION"] --> B["FLUID MEDIA GAS OR LIQUID"]
    A --> C["SOLID MEDIA POROUS AND NON POROUS"]
    B --> D["BINARY DIFFUSION TWO SPECIES A AND B"]
    B --> E["MULTICOMPONENT DIFFUSION THREE OR MORE SPECIES"]
    D --> F["EQUIMOLAR COUNTER DIFFUSION NA EQUALS MINUS NB"]
    D --> G["STAGNANT B DIFFUSION NB EQUALS ZERO BULK FLOW PRESENT"]
    D --> H["UNEQUAL COUNTER DIFFUSION NA PLUS NB NOT EQUAL ZERO"]
    G --> I["GAS PHASE USE PARTIAL PRESSURE FORM"]
    G --> J["LIQUID PHASE USE MOLE FRACTION FORM"]
    C --> K["KNUDSEN DIFFUSION PORE SIZE LESS THAN MEAN FREE PATH"]
    C --> L["EFFECTIVE DIFFUSIVITY DEFF EQUALS EPSILON OVER TAU TIMES DAB"]
    F --> M["FLUX FORMULA NA EQUALS DAB BY Z TIMES CA1 MINUS CA2"]
    G --> N["FLUX FORMULA NA EQUALS DAB P OVER RT Z PBM TIMES PA1 MINUS PA2"]
    L --> O["APPLICATION CATALYST PELLADS DRYING MEMBRANES"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Reference Frame Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["CONCENTRATION C_A1 AT Z EQUALS 0"] --> R1["REFERENCE PLANE STATIONARY STOKES FRAME"]
    R1 --> Q["MOLECULAR DIFFUSION FLUX EQUALS MINUS DAB DCA BY DZ"]
    R1 --> S["BULK FLOW FLUX EQUALS XA TIMES NT"]
    Q --> T["TOTAL FLUX NA EQUALS DIFFUSION PLUS BULK"]
    S --> T
    T --> P2["CONCENTRATION C_A2 AT Z EQUALS L"]
    P2 --> O1["OUTPUT STEADY STATE FLUX NA"]
    T --> O1
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Identifying the Correct Case in a KTU Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["READ PROBLEM CAREFULLY"] --> Q1{"IS B STAGNANT NB EQUALS ZERO"}
    Q1 -- YES --> R1["USE STAGNANT B EQUATION WITH LOG MEAN"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"IS NET FLUX ZERO NA EQUALS MINUS NB"}
    Q2 -- YES --> R2["USE EQUIMOLAR EQUATION"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"GIVEN PARTIAL PRESSURES OR MOLE FRACTIONS"}
    Q3 -- PARTIAL PRESSURES --> R3["USE PRESSURE FORM OF STAGNANT B"]
    Q3 -- MOLE FRACTIONS --> R4["USE MOLE FRACTION FORM"]
    R1 --> S["SOLVE AND REPORT NA IN KILOMOL PER METER SQUARE SECOND"]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams for the exam:** Start at the top block, follow the arrow corresponding to the *given condition* in your numerical, and exit at the bottom with the correct formula. This flowchart is your personal KTU problem-solving algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the significance of the negative sign with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a diffusing species A in a binary mixture is directly proportional to its concentration gradient in the direction of diffusion.

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Significance of the negative sign:** Since mass transport occurs from a region of **higher concentration to a region of lower concentration**, the gradient $dC_A/dz$ is negative in the direction of flux. To make $N_A$ come out as a positive value (in the direction of transport), the equation carries a negative sign. If we define $z$ in the flux direction, the result is always positive.

The proportionality constant $D_{AB}$ (binary diffusivity) has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and depends on temperature, pressure, and the molecular properties of the species involved.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion | A Through Stagnant B |
|---|---|---|
| Net molar flux $N_T$ | Zero ($N_A = -N_B$) | Non-zero ($N_B = 0$, $N_T = N_A$) |
| Bulk flow correction | Not required | Must be added |
| Governing equation | $N_A = (D_{AB}/z)(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $N_A = (D_{AB} P / R T z \, p_{BM})(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ |
| Physical example | Distillation column at constant molar overflow | Sublimation of naphthalene in still air |
| Driving species | Both A and B move symmetrically | Only A moves; B remains stationary |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through a stagnant gas film B. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mixture of oxygen (A) and nitrogen (B) is diffusing at steady state through a stagnant gas film of thickness 1.5 mm. The partial pressure of O₂ at the high-pressure side is 30 kPa and at the low-pressure side is 10 kPa. Total system pressure is 101.3 kPa and temperature is 25 °C. The binary diffusivity $D_{AB} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate: **(i)** the molar flux $N_A$, and **(ii)** the molar flux $N_B$ (comment on its value). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** above. The student must:
1. Start with the general Stokes-frame equation: $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz) + x_A N_T$ (1 mark)
2. Apply $N_B = 0$ so $N_T = N_A$ (1 mark)
3. Rearrange to get $N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$ (1 mark)
4. Substitute $1 - x_A = x_B$ and use $x_A = C_A/C_T$ (1 mark)
5. Separate variables: $N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} C_T (dC_A/C_B)$ (1 mark)
6. Integrate from $z = 0$ to $L$ (1 mark)
7. Convert to partial pressure form and introduce $p_{BM}$ (1 mark)

Final result:
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T L \, p_{BM}} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $p_{A1} = 30$ kPa, $p_{A2} = 10$ kPa
- $P = 101.3$ kPa
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $D_{AB} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Partial pressures of B** (1 mark)
- $p_{B1} = 101.3 - 30 = 71.3$ kPa
- $p_{B2} = 101.3 - 10 = 91.3$ kPa

**Step 2: Log-mean partial pressure of B** (1 mark)
$$p_{BM} = \frac{91.3 - 71.3}{\ln(91.3/71.3)} = \frac{20.0}{0.2478} = 80.71 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Substituting into flux equation** (1 mark)
$$N_A = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(101.3 \times 10^3)}{(8.314)(298)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(80.71 \times 10^3)} (30000 - 10000)$$

**Step 4: Numerator** (1 mark)
$$= (1.8 \times 10^{-5})(101300)(20000) = 36.47$$

**Step 5: Denominator** (1 mark)
$$= (8.314)(298)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(80710) = 299.69$$

**Step 6: Final flux** (1 mark)
$$N_A = \frac{36.47}{299.69} = 1.217 \times 10^{-1} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Part (ii) — Flux of B:** (1 mark)
By definition of the stagnant B condition, $\boxed{N_B = 0}$. Although B has zero net molar flux, it is *not* motionless at the molecular level — molecules still jiggle randomly. The bulk flow of A sweeps B along, but its net displacement across any plane is zero.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$. Discuss how $D_{AB}$ varies with temperature and pressure for gases and liquids. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an equimolar counter-diffusion of helium (A) and air (B) at 1 atm and 300 K, the partial pressures at the two ends of a 4 mm diffusion path are $p_{A1} = 40$ kPa, $p_{A2} = 10$ kPa (at the other end). If $D_{AB} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, determine: **(i)** the molar flux of A, **(ii)** the molar flux of B, and **(iii)** the net molar flux. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Physical Significance and Variation (7 marks)**

**Significance of $D_{AB}$** (3 marks):
The binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$ is a transport property that quantifies *how fast* species A can move through species B under a unit concentration gradient. It has units of m²/s and is a function of the molecular size, mass, and interaction potential between the two species. It is the mass transfer analogue of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ in heat conduction.

**For Gases** (2 marks):
From kinetic theory, $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$. Therefore:
- $D_{AB}$ **increases** with temperature (molecules move faster at higher $T$).
- $D_{AB}$ **decreases** with pressure (denser packing reduces mean free path).
- Heavier molecules → lower $D_{AB}$.

**For Liquids** (2 marks):
From the Stokes–Einstein equation, $D_{AB} \propto T/(\mu V_A^{1/3})$:
- $D_{AB}$ increases with temperature (viscosity $\mu$ drops sharply with $T$, dominating the trend).
- $D_{AB}$ is **essentially independent of pressure** for liquids.
- $D_{AB}$ typically lies in the range $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s, which is **four orders of magnitude smaller** than for gases.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $P = 101.3$ kPa, $T = 300$ K
- $p_{A1} = 40$ kPa, $p_{A2} = 10$ kPa
- $D_{AB} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Equimolar counter-diffusion flux formula** (2 marks)
For EMCD, the simple Fick's law applies without bulk flow correction:
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T L} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$

**Step 2: Substituting values** (1 mark)
$$N_A = \frac{(7.5 \times 10^{-5})(30000 - 10000)}{(8.314)(300)(4 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 3: Numerator** (1 mark)
$$= (7.5 \times 10^{-5})(20000) = 1.5$$

**Step 4: Denominator** (1 mark)
$$= (8.314)(300)(0.004) = 9.977$$

**Step 5: Result** (2 marks)
$$N_A = \frac{1.5}{9.977} = 0.1503 \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Part (ii) — Flux of B:** $N_B = -N_A = -0.1503$ kmol/(m²·s) (opposite direction)

**Part (iii) — Net molar flux:** $N_T = N_A + N_B = 0$ (definition of EMCD)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Steady State Molecular Diffusion:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong formula** — Students often apply the simple Fick's law ($N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/z$) for "A through stagnant B" problems. This **ignores bulk flow** and gives an under-prediction. The correct form uses the log-mean $p_{BM}$ in the denominator.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — Partial pressures in kPa must be converted to Pa before substituting into equations with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). A 1000× error loses all marks.
>
> 3. **Sign convention confusion** — In EMCD, $N_B$ is **negative** relative to $N_A$ (counter-direction). Forgetting the minus sign loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Log-mean arithmetic mistake** — When $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$ differ by less than ~10%, the arithmetic mean is acceptable as an approximation. Otherwise, the log mean is mandatory.
>
> 5. **Mixing gas and liquid equations** — Liquid diffusion uses mole fractions $x$ with log-mean $x_{BM}$ and total concentration $C$. Gas diffusion uses partial pressures $p$ with $p_{BM}$. The structures look similar but the variable substitutions are different.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the student doesn't list "steady state, constant $D_{AB}$, constant $T$ and $P$, no chemical reaction" at the start of the derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Fick's First Law** is the fundamental governing equation; remember the negative sign indicates transport down the concentration gradient.
- ☐ **Three canonical cases:** EMCD, Stagnant B, and Porous Solid — identify the case first, then choose the formula.
- ☐ **Stagnant B equation** includes the **log-mean $p_{BM}$** correction to account for bulk convective flow.
- ☐ **EMCD equation** is the simplest form; no bulk flow correction is needed.
- ☐ For **gases**: $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ | For **liquids**: $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu$.
- ☐ **Knudsen diffusion** dominates when pore size $d_p <$ mean free path $\lambda$ of the gas molecules.
- ☐ **Effective diffusivity** in porous solids: $D_{eff} = (\varepsilon/\tau) D_{AB}$ where $\varepsilon$ is porosity and $\tau$ is tortuosity.
- ☐ **Units check** — $D_{AB}$: m²/s | $N_A$: kmol/(m²·s) | $P$: Pa (use 101325 Pa for 1 atm) | $T$: K.
- ☐ Always **state assumptions** at the start of a derivation question — this alone can earn 1–2 marks.
- ☐ **Dilute solution approximation** is implicit in most KTU problems — treat total concentration as constant.
- ☐ **Steady state** means $\partial C/\partial t = 0$, NOT that concentrations are uniform — a stable gradient is allowed.
- ☐ In **EMCD**: $N_A = -N_B$ and net flux = 0. In **stagnant B**: $N_B = 0$ and $N_A \neq 0$.
- ☐ **Wilke–Chang equation** (for liquids) and **Chapman–Enskog** (for gases) are the standard empirical/theoretical correlations for $D_{AB}$ — be ready to identify which is being used.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Steady State Molecular Diffusion Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code: PCAGT503*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convective Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Convective Mass Transfer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Convective Mass Transfer** is the physical mechanism by which a chemical species (mass) is transported between a surface and a moving fluid (or between two relatively moving fluids) due to the combined action of **molecular diffusion** and the **bulk motion (advection)** of the fluid. When a fluid flows over a surface across which there exists a concentration gradient, mass is transported by convection in a manner analogous to convective heat transfer. The driving potential for convective mass transfer is the **concentration difference** $\Delta C_A$ (or partial pressure difference $\Delta P_A$), and the rate of transport is characterized by a **mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ (m/s), analogous to the heat transfer coefficient $h$ in convection. The convective mass flux from a surface to a fluid stream is mathematically expressed as: $$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$ where: - $N_A$ = mass flux of species A (kmol/m²·s) - $k_c$ = convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $C_{A,s}$ = concentration of A at the surface (kmol/m³) - $C_{A,\infty}$ = concentration of A in the bulk fluid (kmol/m³) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The convective mass transfer topic is the heart of Module 4. It unifies the theories of forced convection, free convection, and phase-change processes under a mass-transfer framework. Mastery of dimensionless numbers — **Sherwood (Sh)**, **Schmidt (Sc)**, **Reynolds (Re)**, and **Lewis (Le)** — is mandatory. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a room where someone is spray-painting near an open window. The strong smell of paint (volatile organic compounds) rapidly spreads throughout the room — not because individual molecules walk to your nose, but because the **moving air currents** carry them. This bulk transport of "mass" (smell molecules) by fluid motion is **convective mass transfer**. Now think of it another way: Place a wet towel on a windy clothesline. The water evaporates much faster than in still air. Why? Two effects combine: 1. **Molecular diffusion** of water vapor from the wet surface into adjacent stagnant air. 2. **Bulk air motion** sweeping the water-saturated air away and bringing drier air to the surface. The faster the wind (higher Reynolds number), the thinner the layer of stagnant saturated air hugging the towel, and the faster evaporation occurs. The same physics governs chemical absorption, drying, humidification, and catalysis. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Table — Heat vs. Mass Transfer (Heat–Mass Transfer Analogy is a KTU Favourite!)** > > | Heat Transfer | Mass Transfer | Driving Potential | > |---|---|---| > | Newton's Law of Cooling: $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective Mass Flux: $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Temperature / Concentration | > | Thermal Diffusivity: $\alpha$ | Mass Diffusivity: $D_{AB}$ | — | > | Prandtl Number: $\Pr = \nu/\alpha$ | Schmidt Number: $\Sc = \nu/D_{AB}$ | — | > | Nusselt Number: $\Nu = hL/k$ | Sherwood Number: $\Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ | — | > | Thermal Boundary Layer: $\delta_t$ | Concentration Boundary Layer: $\delta_c$ | — | ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ **kJ/kmol·K** - **Standard Conditions (STP):** $T = 273.15$ **K**, $P = 101.325$ **kPa** - **Mass Transfer Coefficient Range:** $k_c \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ **m/s** (typical for gas–liquid systems) - **Schmidt Number Range:** $\Sc \approx 0.6$ (gases) to $\approx 1000$ (highly viscous liquids) - **Sherwood Number Range:** $\Sh \approx 0.1$ (natural convection) to $\approx 10^4$ (high Re flow) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mechanism — Why and How Mass Transfer Occurs Convective mass transfer is governed by the **convection–diffusion equation**, which for steady, incompressible flow with constant properties reduces to a form directly analogous to the energy equation: $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$ **Interpretation of each term:** - $u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ — convective transport of A in the streamwise (x) direction by bulk flow. - $v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}$ — convective transport in the transverse (y) direction (often negligible for thin boundary layers). - $D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$ — molecular diffusion of A normal to the surface. **Why is this equation important?** It tells us that species concentration in a flowing fluid is shaped by a competition between **advection (sweeping species downstream)** and **diffusion (spreading species from high to low concentration)**. ### 2.1.1 The Concentration Boundary Layer Analogous to the thermal boundary layer, the **concentration boundary layer** $\delta_c(x)$ is defined as the region adjacent to the surface within which the concentration of species A changes from $C_{A,s}$ (at the wall) to $C_{A,\infty}$ (in the free stream). For flow over a flat plate with no blowing/suction, the ratio of the concentration to hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness is: $$\frac{\delta_c}{\delta} \approx \Sc^{-1/3}$$ > [!TIP] > **Physical meaning:** > - For **gases** ($\Sc \approx 0.6$ to $2$): $\delta_c > \delta$ → concentration boundary layer is thicker than the velocity boundary layer. > - For **liquids** ($\Sc \approx 100$ to $1000$): $\delta_c \ll \delta$ → concentration boundary layer is much thinner; diffusion is slow. ### 2.1.2 Local vs. Average Mass Transfer Coefficient - **Local** $k_{c,x}$: varies with position $x$ along the surface. - **Average** $\bar{k}_c$: integrated over the entire surface length $L$. $$\bar{k}_c = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L k_{c,x}\, dx$$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the single most important reference for Module 4 problem-solving. Memorize every entry.** | # | Formula | Description / Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Definition of mass transfer coefficient (concentration basis) | | 2 | $N_A = k_y(y_{A,s} - y_{A,\infty})$ | Mass flux on mole fraction basis | | 3 | $N_A = k_G(P_{A,s} - P_{A,\infty})$ | Mass flux on partial pressure basis (gases) | | 4 | $\Sh = \dfrac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | **Sherwood Number** (dimensionless mass transfer coefficient) | | 5 | $\Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | **Schmidt Number** (ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity) | | 6 | $\Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\Sc}{\Pr}$ | **Lewis Number** (ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity) | | 7 | $\Re_L = \dfrac{\rho u_\infty L}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** | | 8 | $\Nu_L = \dfrac{hL}{k_{th}}$ | **Nusselt Number** | | 9 | $\Sh_L = C \Re_L^m \Sc^{1/3}$ | General flat-plate mass transfer correlation | | 10 | $\dfrac{\delta_c}{\delta} \approx \Sc^{-1/3}$ | Ratio of concentration to momentum boundary layer | | 11 | $j_M = \St_M \Sc^{2/3} = \dfrac{\Sh}{\Re \Sc} \cdot \Sc^{2/3} = \dfrac{\Sh}{\Re \Sc^{1/3}}$ | **Chilton–Colburn j-factor for mass** | | 12 | $\St_M = \dfrac{k_c}{u_\infty} = \dfrac{\Sh}{\Re \Sc}$ | **Mass Transfer Stanton Number** | | 13 | $\dfrac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \dfrac{k_c}{u_\infty}$ | **Reynolds Analogy** (Pr = Sc = 1) | | 14 | $\dfrac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} \Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{k_c}{u_\infty} \Sc^{2/3}$ | **Chilton–Colburn Analogy** | | 15 | $k_y \approx \dfrac{c \cdot D_{AB}}{L}$ | Order-of-magnitude estimate for mass transfer coefficient | | 16 | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_{y,g}} + \dfrac{m}{k_{y,l}}$ | Two-film theory: overall resistance | | 17 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta_c}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Mass flux through concentration boundary layer | | 18 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla C_A = D_{AB}\nabla^2 C_A$ | General convection–diffusion equation (vector form) | > **Important Notation Note:** In the formulas above, $k_{th}$ denotes thermal conductivity (W/m·K) to avoid confusion with the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ (m/s). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Convective mass transfer underpins: - **Chemical absorption / scrubbing** (CO₂ capture, SO₂ removal in flue gas). - **Drying operations** in paper, textile, food, and pharmaceutical industries. - **Catalytic converters** in automobiles (NO_x, CO, hydrocarbon reactions on hot surfaces). - **Distillation and humidification** columns in process plants. - **Membrane separation** systems where boundary-layer polarization limits flux. - **CVD/PVD thin-film deposition** in semiconductor manufacturing. - **Atmospheric dispersion** of pollutants (boundary-layer meteorology). - **Drug delivery from skin patches** (transdermal pharmacokinetics). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Whenever mass transfer is limited by fluid motion (not by equilibrium or chemical reaction), the convective mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ — and hence the Sherwood number $\Sh$ — controls the rate. **Always identify whether the process is mass-transfer-limited, reaction-limited, or equilibrium-limited** before applying a correlation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Concentration Boundary Layer Thickness $\delta_c$ Over a Flat Plate ### Setup Consider steady, incompressible flow of a fluid (containing species A at bulk concentration $C_{A,\infty}$) over a flat plate at $y = 0$. The plate surface maintains a constant concentration $C_{A,s}$. The free-stream velocity is $u_\infty$. **Governing equation (convection–diffusion in 2-D, thin BL form):** $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$ **Boundary conditions:** - At $y = 0$: $C_A = C_{A,s}$ - As $y \to \infty$: $C_A \to C_{A,\infty}$ - At $x = 0$: $C_A = C_{A,\infty}$ (uniform free-stream upstream) **Approximations (Pohlhausen-type integral method):** **Step 1** — Non-dimensionalize concentration using a similarity variable: $$\theta_c(y) = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}}$$ So $\theta_c = 0$ at the wall and $\theta_c \to 1$ as $y \to \infty$. **Step 2** — Use the von Kármán integral approach. Integrate the convection–diffusion equation across the boundary layer thickness $\delta_c$: $$\int_0^{\delta_c} u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} dy = D_{AB} \left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$ **Step 3** — Substitute a polynomial profile (cubic), analogous to the thermal boundary layer derivation: $$\theta_c(\eta) = a + b\eta + c\eta^2 + d\eta^3$$ where $\eta = y/\delta_c$. Applying boundary conditions: - $\theta_c(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$ - $\theta_c(1) = 1$ - $\frac{\partial \theta_c}{\partial \eta}\bigg|_1 = 0$ (zero gradient at edge of BL) - $\frac{\partial^2 \theta_c}{\partial \eta^2}\bigg|_0 = 0$ (zero curvature at wall, matches wall BC of momentum equation) Solving yields: $$\theta_c = \tfrac{3}{2}\eta - \tfrac{1}{2}\eta^3$$ **Step 4** — Approximate $u(y)$ within $\delta_c$ using the momentum boundary layer cubic profile: $$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \tfrac{3}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) - \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$ **Step 5** — Substitute both profiles into the integral equation. After integration, the convective term gives: $$\int_0^{\delta_c} u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} dy = (C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}) \cdot \frac{3}{20}\left(u_\infty \frac{d\delta_c}{dx} - u_\infty \frac{\delta_c^3}{\delta^3}\frac{d\delta}{dx}\right)$$ **Step 6** — The diffusion term at the wall is: $$D_{AB}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_0 = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{3(C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s})}{2\delta_c}$$ **Step 7** — Substitute $\delta(x) \approx 5.0\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}$ (Blasius solution) and equate both sides. After algebraic simplification, we obtain: $$\delta_c \approx \delta \cdot \Sc^{-1/3}$$ $$\boxed{\;\delta_c(x) \approx \delta(x) \cdot \Sc^{-1/3}\;}$$ **Step 8** — Therefore: $$\delta_c(x) \approx 5.0\,\Sc^{-1/3}\,\sqrt{\frac{\nu x}{u_\infty}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU students often confuse thermal and mass boundary layer ratios.** > - Thermal: $\delta_t/\delta \approx \Pr^{-1/3}$ > - Concentration: $\delta_c/\delta \approx \Sc^{-1/3}$ > > The exponents are the same — both are **−1/3** — but the dimensionless number used differs. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Local Mass Transfer Coefficient $k_{c,x}$ Over a Flat Plate **Step 1** — Mass flux at the wall by Fick's law: $$N_{A,x}\big|_{y=0} = -D_{AB}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{3(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{2\delta_c}$$ **Step 2** — By definition of the local mass transfer coefficient: $$N_{A,x}\big|_{y=0} \equiv k_{c,x}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$ **Step 3** — Equate: $$k_{c,x}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{3(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{2\delta_c}$$ $$k_{c,x} = \frac{3 D_{AB}}{2 \delta_c}$$ **Step 4** — Substitute $\delta_c = 5.0\,\Sc^{-1/3}\,\sqrt{\nu x / u_\infty}$: $$k_{c,x} = \frac{3 D_{AB}}{2 \cdot 5.0\,\Sc^{-1/3}\,\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}}$$ $$k_{c,x} = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{D_{AB}}{\Sc^{-1/3}\,\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}}$$ **Step 5** — Recall $\Sc = \nu/D_{AB}$, so $D_{AB} = \nu/\Sc$. Substituting: $$k_{c,x} = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{\nu/\Sc}{\Sc^{-1/3}\,\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}} = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{\nu^{1/2}\, u_\infty^{1/2}}{x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{2/3}}$$ **Step 6** — Express in terms of local Sherwood number $\Sh_x = k_{c,x} x / D_{AB}$: $$\Sh_x = \frac{k_{c,x}\, x}{D_{AB}} = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{x}{D_{AB}}\cdot\frac{\nu^{1/2}u_\infty^{1/2}}{x^{1/2}\Sc^{2/3}}$$ $$= \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{(\nu u_\infty x)^{1/2}}{D_{AB}\,\Sc^{2/3}}$$ **Step 7** — Multiply and divide by $x$ inside the radical and rewrite: $$\Sh_x = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{x\,u_\infty}{\nu}\cdot\frac{\nu}{D_{AB}\,\Sc^{2/3}}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{x u_\infty/\nu}}$$ $$\Sh_x = \frac{3}{10}\cdot\frac{\Re_x^{1/2}}{\Sc^{1/3}}$$ $$\boxed{\;\Sh_x = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Heat vs. Mass — Direct Comparison:** > - $\Nu_x = 0.332\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$ (Pohlhausen exact = 0.332) > - $\Sh_x = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$ (Pohlhausen approximate = 0.3) > > The slight coefficient difference (0.3 vs 0.332) is due to the cubic-profile approximation for concentration. The exact Blasius-based value is $\Sh_x = 0.332\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$ — KTU examiners typically accept both 0.3 and 0.332. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Reynolds Analogy Between Heat and Mass Transfer **Premise:** If $\Pr = \Sc = 1$ (i.e., momentum, thermal, and mass diffusivities are all equal), then the velocity, thermal, and concentration profiles are identical. This is the **Reynolds Analogy**. **Step 1** — Recall the thermal Stanton number: $$\St = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \frac{\Nu}{\Re \Pr}$$ **Step 2** — Recall the mass Stanton number: $$\St_M = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} = \frac{\Sh}{\Re \Sc}$$ **Step 3** — If $\Pr = \Sc = 1$ and $\Nu = \Sh$ (from identical profiles), then: $$\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}$$ $$\boxed{\;h = \rho c_p\, k_c\;}\quad\text{(Reynolds Analogy)}$$ **Step 4** — This implies $\Nu = \Sh$ when $\Pr = \Sc = 1$. **Limitation:** Reynolds analogy is too restrictive (only valid for $\Pr = \Sc \approx 1$, typical of gases). --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Chilton–Colburn Analogy (Modified Reynolds Analogy) **Step 1** — For fluids where $\Pr$ or $\Sc \neq 1$, Chilton and Colburn (1934) empirically introduced the **j-factor**: $$j_H = \St\,\Pr^{2/3} \quad ; \quad j_M = \St_M\,\Sc^{2/3}$$ **Step 2** — The empirical observation (valid for $0.6 < \Pr < 60$ and $0.6 < \Sc < 3000$) is: $$j_H \approx j_M$$ $$\St\,\Pr^{2/3} \approx \St_M\,\Sc^{2/3}$$ **Step 3** — Rewriting: $$\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty}\Pr^{2/3} \approx \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}\Sc^{2/3}$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty}\Pr^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}\Sc^{2/3}\;}\quad\text{(Chilton–Colburn Analogy)}$$ **Step 4** — Cross-relating heat and mass coefficients: $$k_c = \frac{h}{\rho c_p}\left(\frac{\Pr}{\Sc}\right)^{2/3}$$ or in terms of dimensionless numbers: $$\Sh = \Nu \cdot \left(\frac{\Sc}{\Pr}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Engineering utility:** If you know $h$ from a heat-transfer correlation and $\Pr$, you can predict $k_c$ without an independent mass-transfer experiment — invaluable in process design. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map — Convective Mass Transfer System (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Fluid Free Stream
