State its physical significance and write its mathematical expression.** **Model Answer:** The **Sherwood Number** $(\Sh)$ is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of convective mass transfer to the rate of diffusive mass transport. It is the mass-transfer analogue of the Nusselt number in heat transfer. **Mathematical expression:** $$\Sh_L = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$$ where: - $k_c$ = convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $L$ = characteristic length (m) - $D_{AB}$ = mass diffusivity of A in B (m²/s) **Physical significance:** A high $\Sh$ indicates that convective mass transport dominates over pure molecular diffusion. It is used to develop correlations of the form $\Sh = f(\Re, \Sc)$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the concentration boundary layer? How does it compare with the hydrodynamic boundary layer for liquids and gases?** **Model Answer:** The **concentration boundary layer** $\delta_c$ is the region adjacent to a surface within which the concentration of a diffusing species varies from its value at the surface $C_{A,s}$ to its free-stream value $C_{A,\infty}$. **Comparison:** The ratio of the two boundary layers is governed by the Schmidt number: $$\frac{\delta_c}{\delta} \approx \Sc^{-1/3}$$ - **Gases** $(\Sc \approx 0.6\text{ to }2)$: $\delta_c$ is **larger** than $\delta$. - **Liquids** $(\Sc \approx 100\text{ to }1000)$: $\delta_c$ is **much smaller** than $\delta$ because molecular diffusion is very slow compared to momentum diffusion. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Each Part B question below carries internal choice — i.e., the student picks **either** Question A **or** Question B in the exam hall. Both are presented here for full preparation. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the local mass transfer coefficient over a flat plate using the integral method of boundary layer analysis. Show that $\Sh_x = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Air at $27\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $1\text{ atm}$ flows with a free-stream velocity of $5\text{ m/s}$ parallel to a flat plate of length $0.5\text{ m}$. The plate is covered with a thin film of water. Calculate: (i) The local mass transfer coefficient at $x = 0.25\text{ m}$. (ii) The average mass transfer coefficient over the entire plate. (iii) The total mass of water evaporated per metre width of plate per hour. Given: $D_{AB,\text{water-air}} = 0.26 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ at $27\,^\circ\text{C}$. Vapour pressure of water at $27\,^\circ\text{C} = 3.56\text{ kPa}$. Use kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.57 \times 10^{-5}\text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1** — Governing equation for concentration in the BL: $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$ **Step 2** — Non-dimensional concentration: $$\theta_c = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}}$$ **Step 3** — Integrate across BL (von Kármán integral): $$\int_0^{\delta_c} u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\, dy = D_{AB}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$ **Step 4** — Use cubic profiles: $$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3, \quad \theta_c = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^3, \quad \eta = \frac{y}{\delta_c}$$ **Step 5** — After integration (using momentum BL $\delta = 5.0\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}$): $$\delta_c \approx 5.0\,\Sc^{-1/3}\sqrt{\frac{\nu x}{u_\infty}}$$ **Step 6** — Wall mass flux from Fick's law: $$N_{A,x}\big|_{y=0} = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{3(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{2\delta_c} = k_{c,x}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$ $$\Rightarrow k_{c,x} = \frac{3 D_{AB}}{2 \delta_c}$$ **Step 7** — Substitute $\delta_c$: $$k_{c,x} = \frac{3 D_{AB}}{2 \cdot 5.0\,\Sc^{-1/3}\sqrt{\nu x/u_\infty}} = 0.3\,\frac{D_{AB}}{\Sc^{-1/3}}\sqrt{\frac{u_\infty}{\nu x}}$$ **Step 8** — Substitute $D_{AB} = \nu/\Sc$: $$k_{c,x} = 0.3\,\frac{u_\infty \cdot \nu^{1/2}}{x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{2/3}}$$ **Step 9** — Compute Sherwood number: $$\Sh_x = \frac{k_{c,x}\, x}{D_{AB}} = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$$ $$\boxed{\;\Sh_x = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}\;}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1** — Calculate Schmidt number: $$\Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.57 \times 10^{-5}}{0.26 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.604$$ **Step 2** — Calculate local Reynolds number at $x = 0.25\text{ m}$: $$\Re_x = \frac{u_\infty x}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 0.25}{1.57 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.96 \times 10^4$$ **Step 3** — Calculate local Sherwood number: $$\Sh_x = 0.3\,(7.96 \times 10^4)^{1/2}\,(0.604)^{1/3}$$ $$\Sh_x = 0.3 \times 282.1 \times 0.846 = 71.6$$ **Step 4** — Calculate $k_{c,x}$ at $x = 0.25\text{ m}$: $$k_{c,x} = \frac{\Sh_x \cdot D_{AB}}{x} = \frac{71.6 \times 0.26 \times 10^{-4}}{0.25} = 7.45 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{k_{c,x} = 7.45 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 5** — Average Sherwood number (integrate $k_{c,x} \propto x^{-1/2}$): $$\bar{\Sh}_L = 2\,\Sh_L = 2 \times 0.3\,\Re_L^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$$ Calculate $\Re_L$ at $L = 0.5\text{ m}$: $$\Re_L = \frac{5 \times 0.5}{1.57 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.59 \times 10^5$$ $$\bar{\Sh}_L = 2 \times 0.3 \times (1.59 \times 10^5)^{1/2} \times (0.604)^{1/3} = 2 \times 0.3 \times 399 \times 0.846 = 202.5$$ **Step 6** — Average mass transfer coefficient: $$\bar{k}_c = \frac{\bar{\Sh}_L \cdot D_{AB}}{L} = \frac{202.5 \times 0.26 \times 10^{-4}}{0.5} = 1.053 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{k}_c = 1.05 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 7** — Calculate concentration driving force (ideal gas at surface = saturation): $$C_{A,s} = \frac{P_{A,s}}{R_u T} = \frac{3560}{8.314 \times 300} = 1.428\text{ mol/m}^3$$ $$C_{A,\infty} \approx 0 \text{ (dry air)}$$ $$\Delta C_A = 1.428\text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 8** — Average mass flux: $$\bar{N}_A = \bar{k}_c \cdot \Delta C_A = 1.053 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.428 = 1.504 \times 10^{-2}\text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ **Step 9** — Total moles evaporated per metre width per hour: $$\dot{n}_A = \bar{N}_A \times L \times 1\text{ m} \times 3600\text{ s/h}$$ $$= 1.504 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.5 \times 3600 = 27.07\text{ mol/h}$$ **Step 10** — Mass per hour (molar mass of water = 18 g/mol): $$\dot{m}_A = 27.07 \times 18 = 487.3\text{ g/h}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_A \approx 0.487\text{ kg/h per metre width}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes costing marks (KTU Examiner Pitfalls):** > 1. Forgetting to convert temperature to **Kelvin** when computing $C_{A,s}$ — losing 1 mark. > 2. Using $C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}$ incorrectly (always surface minus bulk). > 3. Mixing **average** and **local** values: $k_{c,x}$ at $x = L/2 \neq \bar{k}_c$. > 4. Forgetting $\times 3600$ to convert seconds to hours — a classic 1-mark deduction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a)** State and explain the **Reynolds analogy** and the **Chilton–Colburn analogy** between heat and mass transfer. Show the limiting case in which the two analogies coincide. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A sphere of diameter $25\text{ mm}$ is suspended in air at $1\text{ atm}$ and $100\,^\circ\text{C}$. The free-stream air velocity is $10\text{ m/s}$. Using the Chilton–Colburn analogy, estimate the mass transfer coefficient for naphthalene sublimating from the sphere into the air. Given for naphthalene–air system: $D_{AB} = 6.1 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $\nu = 2.15 \times 10^{-5}\text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $\Pr = 0.71$. For the heat-transfer correlation over a sphere: $\Nu_D = 2.0 + 0.6\,\Re_D^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$. Vapour pressure of naphthalene at $100\,^\circ\text{C} = 0.27\text{ atm}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Reynolds Analogy:** When $\Pr = \Sc = 1$, the velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles are identical. Hence: $$\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{h = \rho c_p\, k_c}$$ This implies $\Nu = \Sh$ when $\Pr = \Sc = 1$. **Limitation:** Only valid for gases with $\Pr \approx \Sc \approx 0.7$. **Chilton–Colburn Analogy:** For $\Pr$ or $\Sc$ differing from unity: $$\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty}\Pr^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}\Sc^{2/3}$$ Defining j-factors: $$j_H = \St\,\Pr^{2/3} = j_M = \St_M\,\Sc^{2/3}$$ **Limiting case:** Setting $\Pr = \Sc = 1$ in the Chilton–Colburn expression recovers the Reynolds analogy exactly. **Why does the $2/3$ exponent work?** Empirically, for both heat and mass transfer, the same Pohlhausen-type cubic profile yields an exponent of $1/3$ for $\Pr$ (or $\Sc$) in the Nusselt/Sherwood correlation. By dividing out the $\Pr^{1/3}$ (or $\Sc^{1/3}$) that multiplies $\St$ (or $\St_M$), the $j$-factor becomes universal. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1** — Calculate Reynolds number based on sphere diameter: $$\Re_D = \frac{u_\infty D}{\nu} = \frac{10 \times 0.025}{2.15 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.163 \times 10^4$$ **Step 2** — Calculate Schmidt number for naphthalene–air: $$\Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{2.15 \times 10^{-5}}{6.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.525$$ **Step 3** — Apply heat-transfer correlation to get Nusselt number: $$\Nu_D = 2.0 + 0.6\,\Re_D^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$$ $$\Nu_D = 2.0 + 0.6 \times (1.163 \times 10^4)^{1/2} \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$ $$\Nu_D = 2.0 + 0.6 \times 107.85 \times 0.892 = 2.0 + 57.7 = 59.7$$ **Step 4** — Apply Chilton–Colburn analogy to get Sherwood number: $$\frac{\Nu}{\Re \Pr} \Pr^{2/3} = \frac{\Sh}{\Re \Sc}\Sc^{2/3}$$ $$\Sh = \Nu \cdot \left(\frac{\Sc}{\Pr}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$\Sh = 59.7 \times \left(\frac{3.525}{0.71}\right)^{1/3} = 59.7 \times (4.965)^{1/3} = 59.7 \times 1.704 = 101.7$$ **Step 5** — Calculate mass transfer coefficient: $$k_c = \frac{\Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{D} = \frac{101.7 \times 6.1 \times 10^{-6}}{0.025} = 2.482 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{k_c \approx 2.48 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly compute $\Sh = \Nu \times (\Pr/\Sc)^{1/3}$ — the ratio is **inverted**! It should be $(\Sc/\Pr)^{1/3}$ because $\Sc > \Pr$ in most gas systems, and mass diffusion is slower than thermal diffusion in gases, so the concentration boundary layer is thicker — yielding $\Sh > \Nu$. **Always double-check.** --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Convective Mass Transfer (Module 4)** - ✅ **Governing Equation:** $u\,\partial C_A/\partial x + v\,\partial C_A/\partial y = D_{AB}\,\partial^2 C_A/\partial y^2$ - ✅ **Mass flux definition:** $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ — always **surface minus bulk**. - ✅ **Sherwood number:** $\Sh = k_c L/D_{AB}$ — mass-transfer analogue of Nusselt number. - ✅ **Schmidt number:** $\Sc = \nu/D_{AB}$ — controls relative thickness of concentration BL. - ✅ **Boundary layer ratio:** $\delta_c/\delta = \Sc^{-1/3}$; thermal analogue: $\delta_t/\delta = \Pr^{-1/3}$. - ✅ **Flat-plate correlation:** $\Sh_x = 0.3\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$ (integral method) or $0.332\,\Re_x^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$ (Blasius). - ✅ **Reynolds Analogy** (when $\Pr = \Sc = 1$): $h = \rho c_p\, k_c$. - ✅ **Chilton–Colburn Analogy:** $\St\Pr^{2/3} = \St_M \Sc^{2/3}$, or equivalently $\Sh = \Nu(\Sc/\Pr)^{1/3}$. - ✅ **Sphere correlation:** $\Sh_D = 2.0 + 0.6\,\Re_D^{1/2}\,\Sc^{1/3}$ (Froessling/Ranz–Marshall). - ✅ **Two-film theory:** $1/K_y = 1/k_{y,g} + m/k_{y,l}$ — resistances are additive. - ✅ **Concentration at surface (ideal gas):** $C_{A,s} = P_{A,s}/(R_u T)$ — remember to use **Kelvin**! - ✅ **Lewis number:** $\Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = \Sc/\Pr$ — important in combined heat–mass transfer (psychrometrics). - ✅ **Physical meaning:** $\delta_c \gg \delta$ for gases; $\delta_c \ll \delta$ for liquids. - ✅ **Engineering applications:** drying, absorption, catalysis, humidification, atmospheric dispersion. - ✅ **KTU exam tip:** Always specify the **basis** (molar concentration, mole fraction, or partial pressure) for $k_c$, $k_y$, $k_G$ — these are NOT interchangeable without unit conversion!

topics.title: Momentum, Heat and Mass Transfer Analogy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Momentum, Heat and Mass Transfer Analogy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Transport Analogies** (Momentum, Heat, and Mass Transfer Analogies) are mathematical relations that establish equivalence between the dimensionless transport coefficients governing the three fundamental transport processes — **momentum transfer** (fluid friction), **heat transfer** (conduction/convection), and **mass transfer** (diffusion) — when expressed in terms of the dimensionless flow Reynolds number and the relevant transport property ratios (Prandtl number, Schmidt number). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *"Heat, mass and momentum transfer analogies are based on the premise that the mechanism of transfer of these three entities is identical at the molecular level. Empirical relations developed for one mode of transport can be extended to the other two using dimensionless groups."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) Imagine a crowded marketplace: - **Momentum transfer** is like a group of people (molecules) pushing each other — friction at the boundary is just molecules colliding with the wall. - **Heat transfer** is like a rumor spreading from one person to another — energy hops from hot molecules to cold molecules. - **Mass transfer** is like a perfume smell diffusing through the crowd — molecules of A wander into the space of B. The **brilliant insight** of the analogy is this: *all three processes are governed by the same microscopic random molecular motion*. Therefore, a mathematical description of one (say, friction) can be "translated" to describe another (say, heat transfer) by simply replacing the property ratio. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** "Friction drags, Heat diffuses, Mass diffuses — they all play by the same rulebook, just with different property booklets (μ, k, D_AB)." ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - **Prandtl Number (Pr):** ν/α — typically **0.7 – 1000** for common fluids - **Schmidt Number (Sc):** ν/D_AB — typically **0.6 – 3000** - **Lewis Number (Le):** α/D_AB = Sc/Pr - **Stanton Number (St):** h/(ρ·V·C_p) - **Skin Friction Coefficient (C_f):** τ_w / (½·ρ·V²) - **Reynolds Number (Re):** ρ·V·L / μ > [!IMPORTANT] > All three dimensionless groups (St, St_m, C_f/2) are essentially **dimensionless flux ratios** of the actual flux to the maximum possible convective flux — this is the foundation of all analogies. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Foundation: Why Do Analogies Exist? The analogies emerge from the **Reynolds' Transport Theorem** applied to a fluid element: 1. **Molecular Mechanism:** All three processes (momentum, heat, mass) at the molecular scale occur via **random molecular motion** and collisions. 2. **Continuum Assumption:** All three can be described by the **constitutive equations** (Newton's law of viscosity, Fourier's law, Fick's law). 3. **Boundary Layer Analogy:** Velocity, thermal, and concentration boundary layers grow in **geometrically similar** ways if the dimensionless numbers (Pr, Sc) are near unity. ### 2.2 Key Dimensionless Groups (KTU High-Yield Table) | Dimensionless Group | Symbol | Definition | Physical Meaning | KTU Usage | |---|---|---|---|---| | Reynolds Number | Re | ρ·V·L / μ | Inertial / Viscous forces | Universal | | Prandtl Number | Pr | ν / α = C_p·μ / k | Momentum diffusivity / Thermal diffusivity | Heat transfer | | Schmidt Number | Sc | ν / D_AB | Momentum diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | Mass transfer | | Lewis Number | Le | α / D_AB = Sc / Pr | Thermal diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | Combined HMT | | Nusselt Number | Nu | h·L / k | Convective / Conductive heat transfer | Heat transfer | | Sherwood Number | Sh | h_m·L / D_AB | Convective / Diffusive mass transfer | Mass transfer | | Stanton Number (Heat) | St | h / (ρ·V·C_p) | Heat transfer coefficient / ρVC_p | Heat transfer | | Stanton Number (Mass) | St_m | h_m / V | Mass transfer coefficient / V | Mass transfer | | Skin Friction Coeff. | C_f | 2·τ_w / (ρ·V²) | Wall shear / Dynamic pressure | Momentum | | Stanton Number (Momentum) | C_f / 2 | τ_w / (ρ·V²) | Same as wall-friction form | Momentum | | Colburn j-factor (Heat) | j_H | St · Pr^(2/3) | Empirical heat factor | Heat transfer | | Colburn j-factor (Mass) | j_M | St_m · Sc^(2/3) | Empirical mass factor | Mass transfer | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a derivation of an analogy, always verify it through **dimensional analysis** first — St, St_m, and C_f/2 are ALL dimensionless, which is precisely why they can be set equal. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why Bother? - **Predicting heat/mass transfer coefficients** from a single friction measurement (extremely useful in compact heat exchangers, fins). - **Designing chemical reactors** — knowing flow friction gives mass transfer rates. - **Scale-up of equipment** — empirical correlations can be transferred across systems. - **Reduced experimental effort** — once friction is measured, heat and mass transfer follow. ### 2.4 The Three Classical Analogies — A Bird's-Eye View The analogies evolved historically to overcome limitations: 1. **Reynolds Analogy (1874):** Valid only when **Pr = Sc = 1** (e.g., gases). Equates momentum and heat transfer at the wall. 2. **Prandtl Analogy (1910):** Accounts for the **laminar sublayer** — valid up to **Pr ≈ 1**. Includes a logarithmic correction. 3. **Von Kármán Analogy (1939):** Adds the **buffer layer** consideration — valid up to **Pr ≤ 10**. 4. **Colburn Analogy (1933):** Empirical — uses **Pr^(2/3)** correction. Valid for **0.6 < Pr < 100**. 5. **Chilton–Colburn Analogy (1934):** Extension of Colburn to mass transfer with **Sc^(2/3)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2024 Exams:** The **Reynolds, Prandtl, and Colburn analogies** are the most frequently asked. Know each derivation and the **limitation on Pr/Sc**. ### 2.5 Reynolds Analogy — Statement > *"In a turbulent flow where Pr = 1 and the fluid properties are constant, the dimensionless wall heat flux equals the dimensionless wall momentum flux (skin friction)."* $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = St = St_m $$ This implies: a single curve describes friction, heat transfer, and mass transfer at the wall. ### 2.6 Prandtl Analogy — Statement > *"For turbulent flow with a laminar sublayer, momentum and heat transfer both occur through the sublayer. The Prandtl number enters as a correction factor for the sublayer thickness."* $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = St \cdot \left[1 + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}} \left(Pr - 1\right)\right]^{-1} $$ Valid for **0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 10**. ### 2.7 Colburn Analogy — Statement > *"Empirically, the heat transfer j-factor and the mass transfer j-factor each equal half the friction factor."* $$ j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} \quad ; \quad j_M = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} $$ Valid for **0.6 < Pr < 100** and **0.6 < Sc < 3000** — covers most engineering fluids (water, air, oils). ### 2.8 Heat–Mass Transfer Analogy (Direct Equivalence) When **Sc ≈ Pr** (Le ≈ 1), one can directly relate: $$ \frac{St}{St_m} = \left(\frac{Pr}{Sc}\right)^{2/3} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{h}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot h_m} = \left(\frac{Pr}{Sc}\right)^{2/3} $$ This is the **Chilton–Colburn** heat–mass analogy. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reynolds Analogy (Full, Exhaustive) **Setup:** Consider a flat plate in turbulent flow. A small control volume adjacent to the wall transports momentum, heat, and mass simultaneously. **Step 1 — Wall Shear Stress (momentum flux):** By Newton's law of viscosity applied at the wall: $$ \tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 2 — Non-dimensionalize by Dynamic Pressure:** $$ C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{C_f}{2} = \frac{\mu}{\rho V^2}\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 3 — Wall Heat Flux (heat transfer by Fourier's law):** $$ q_w'' = -k \left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 4 — Non-dimensionalize by Maximum Possible Convective Flux (ρ·V·C_p·ΔT):** $$ St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho V C_p \Delta T} = \frac{k}{\rho V C_p \Delta T}\left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 5 — Wall Mass Flux (Fick's law):** $$ J_{A,w} = -D_{AB} \left(\frac{dC_A}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 6 — Non-dimensionalize (Stanton for mass):** $$ St_m = \frac{h_m}{V} = \frac{D_{AB}}{V \Delta C_A}\left(\frac{dC_A}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ **Step 7 — The Reynolds Hypothesis:** If **Pr = Sc = 1**, the dimensionless profiles of velocity, temperature, and concentration are identical: $$ \frac{u}{V} = \frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s} = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}} $$ **Step 8 — Differentiate each profile at the wall:** All three gradients evaluate to the same dimensionless quantity (let's call it K): $$ \frac{\mu}{\rho V^2} \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = \frac{k}{\rho V C_p \Delta T}\left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = \frac{D_{AB}}{V \Delta C_A}\left(\frac{dC_A}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = K $$ **Step 9 — Equate the three dimensionless fluxes:** $$ \boxed{\;\frac{C_f}{2} = St = St_m\;} $$ This is the **Reynolds Analogy**. **Limitation:** Strictly valid only when Pr = Sc = 1 (e.g., most gases at moderate conditions). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Prandtl Analogy **Step 1 — Acknowledge the Laminar Sublayer:** Turbulent flow has a thin **viscous sublayer** (y⁺ < 5) near the wall where transport is purely by molecular motion. Outside this layer, transport is by turbulent eddies. **Step 2 — Sublayer Thickness (η):** For a turbulent boundary layer, the sublayer thickness is: $$ \eta \approx \frac{y}{V}\sqrt{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho}} = \frac{u_\tau y}{\nu} $$ where u_τ = √(τ_w/ρ) is the **friction velocity**. **Step 3 — Wall Shear Stress in Sublayer:** By molecular transport in the sublayer: $$ \tau_w = \mu \frac{V}{\eta_1} $$ Solving for sublayer thickness: $$ \eta_1 = \frac{\mu V}{\tau_w} = \frac{\nu}{u_\tau} $$ **Step 4 — Heat Transfer in Sublayer (Fourier's law):** $$ q_w'' = k \frac{\Delta T}{\eta_1^{''}} $$ where η_1'' is the **thermal sublayer thickness**. The ratio of thermal to momentum sublayer is governed by Pr: $$ \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_1^{''}} \approx Pr^{1/2} $$ (Heavier fluids with larger Pr have thinner thermal sublayers.) **Step 5 — Substitute and Rearrange:** $$ St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho V C_p \Delta T} = \frac{k}{\rho V C_p \eta_1^{''}} = \frac{k}{\rho V C_p} \cdot \frac{Pr^{1/2}}{\eta_1} $$ Replacing η_1 from Step 3: $$ St = \frac{k \cdot Pr^{1/2}}{\rho V C_p} \cdot \frac{\tau_w}{\mu V} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho V^2} \cdot \frac{k \cdot Pr^{1/2}}{\mu C_p} $$ Using Pr = μC_p/k: $$ St = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho V^2} \cdot Pr^{-1/2} = \frac{C_f}{2} \cdot Pr^{-1/2} $$ **Step 6 — Correct for Pr ≠ 1 (empirical correction):** Add the Pr-dependent correction term: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{C_f}{2} = St \left[1 + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}(Pr - 1)\right]\;} $$ **Range of Validity:** 0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 10. For Pr = 1, this reduces to Reynolds analogy (as expected). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Von Kármán Analogy (Extension) **Step 1 — Three-Layer Model:** - **Layer 1 (y⁺ < 5):** Viscous sublayer — molecular transport dominates. - **Layer 2 (5 < y⁺ < 30):** Buffer layer — both molecular and turbulent transport. - **Layer 3 (y⁺ > 30):** Turbulent core — turbulent eddies dominate. **Step 2 — Write Flux in Each Layer:** Momentum flux in sublayer: $$ \frac{\tau}{\rho} = (\nu + \epsilon_M)\frac{du}{dy} $$ Heat flux: $$ \frac{q''}{\rho C_p} = (\alpha + \epsilon_H)\frac{dT}{dy} $$ where ε_M and ε_H are the **eddy diffusivities** of momentum and heat. **Step 3 — Integrate Across Sublayer (Momentum):** After integration (details in Kausthubharam and Bennett's text): $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{\kappa}\ln\left(\frac{V y}{\nu}\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}\right) + B - \frac{Pr\sqrt{C_f/2} - 1}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}(Pr - 1)} \cdot \ln(5 Pr) + \text{buffer terms}} $$ The **final form** of the Von Kármán analogy is complex but more accurate: $$ \boxed{\;St = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\{Pr - 1 + \ln\left[\frac{1 + 5 Pr}{6}\right]\}}\;} $$ **Range of Validity:** Pr up to ~10–100 (more accurate than Prandtl for liquid metals). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Colburn Analogy (Empirical) **Step 1 — Start from Reynolds Analogy:** $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = St \quad \text{(valid only for } Pr = 1\text{)} $$ **Step 2 — Observe the Failure for Pr ≠ 1:** For liquid water (Pr ≈ 7) or oil (Pr ≈ 100), Reynolds analogy over-predicts St by up to 4×. **Step 3 — Introduce Empirical Correction:** Colburn fitted experimental data and found that St·Pr^(2/3) (rather than St) collapses onto a single curve with C_f/2. **Step 4 — Heat Transfer j-factor:** $$ \boxed{\;j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}\;} $$ **Step 5 — Mass Transfer j-factor:** $$ \boxed{\;j_M = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}\;} $$ **Step 6 — Combined Heat-Mass Analogy (Chilton-Colburn):** $$ \frac{j_H}{j_M} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{St \cdot Pr^{2/3}}{St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}} = 1 $$ This yields: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{h}{h_m} = \rho C_p \left(\frac{Sc}{Pr}\right)^{2/3}\;} $$ This is **extremely useful in chemical engineering** to predict mass transfer from heat transfer (or vice versa). --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Heat–Mass Transfer Analogy from Scratch **Step 1 — Governing Equations:** Momentum: ν ∇²u = 0 Heat: α ∇²T = 0 Mass: D_AB ∇²C_A = 0 **Step 2 — Similarity Variables:** For a flat plate, introduce: $$ \eta = y \sqrt{\frac{V}{x \cdot \xi}} $$ where ξ is ν, α, or D_AB for momentum, heat, and mass respectively. **Step 3 — Boundary Conditions:** - At y = 0: u = 0, T = T_s, C_A = C_{A,s} - At y = ∞: u = V, T = T_∞, C_A = C_{A,∞} **Step 4 — Define Dimensionless Profiles:** $$ \phi = \frac{u}{V}, \quad \theta = \frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s}, \quad \theta_m = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}} $$ **Step 5 — Reduce the PDEs to ODEs:** The three ODEs become: $$ \frac{d^2\phi}{d\eta^2} = 0, \quad \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} = 0, \quad \frac{d^2\theta_m}{d\eta^2} = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Solve with BCs:** $$ \phi = \eta, \quad \theta = \eta, \quad \theta_m = \eta $$ **Step 7 — Differentiate at the Wall:** $$ \left(\frac{d\phi}{d\eta}\right)_0 = \left(\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\right)_0 = \left(\frac{d\theta_m}{d\eta}\right)_0 = 1 $$ **Step 8 — Convert Back to Physical Gradients:** $$ \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_0 = \frac{V}{\eta_1}, \quad \left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_0 = \frac{\Delta T}{\eta_1^{''}}, \quad \left(\frac{dC_A}{dy}\right)_0 = \frac{\Delta C_A}{\eta_1^{'''}} $$ **Step 9 — Relate Sublayer Thicknesses:** $$ \eta_1 : \eta_1^{''} : \eta_1^{'''} = 1 : Pr^{-1/2} : Sc^{-1/2} $$ **Step 10 — Compute Wall Fluxes and Non-dimensionalize:** After substitution, we get the **modified Reynolds analogy** for arbitrary Pr and Sc: $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = St \cdot Pr^{1/2} = St_m \cdot Sc^{1/2} $$ This is the **first-order** heat–mass analogy. The Colburn (2/3) exponent is an empirical refinement of the 1/2 exponent. --- ### 3.6 Summary of All Analogy Equations (Analytical Matrix) | Analogy | Equation | Valid Pr Range | Source | |---|---|---|---| | Reynolds | C_f/2 = St = St_m | Pr = Sc = 1 | First principles | | Prandtl | C_f/2 = St[1 + 5√(C_f/2)(Pr−1)] | 0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 10 | Laminar sublayer | | Von Kármán | Complex (see §3.3) | 0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 100 | Three-layer model | | Colburn (Heat) | j_H = St·Pr^(2/3) = C_f/2 | 0.6 < Pr < 100 | Empirical | | Colburn (Mass) | j_M = St_m·Sc^(2/3) = C_f/2 | 0.6 < Sc < 3000 | Empirical | | Chilton–Colburn | h/h_m = ρ·C_p·(Sc/Pr)^(2/3) | 0.6 < Pr, Sc < 3000 | Combined HMT | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Order of historical accuracy:** Reynolds (least accurate) → Prandtl → Von Kármán → Colburn (empirically best for moderate Pr). --- ### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Air at 300 K and 1 atm flows over a flat plate at 30 m/s. The plate is 1 m long. The friction coefficient is measured to be C_f = 0.004. Estimate the heat transfer coefficient using (a) Reynolds analogy, (b) Prandtl analogy, (c) Colburn analogy. **Given:** T = 300 K, P = 1 atm, V = 30 m/s, L = 1 m, C_f = 0.004 **Properties of air at 300 K:** Pr = 0.707, C_p = 1005 J/(kg·K), ρ = 1.177 kg/m³ **Solution (a) — Reynolds Analogy:** St = C_f / 2 = 0.004 / 2 = 0.002 h = St · ρ · V · C_p = 0.002 × 1.177 × 30 × 1005 h = **70.97 W/(m²·K)** **Solution (b) — Prandtl Analogy:** First compute: 5 × √(C_f/2) × (Pr − 1) = 5 × √(0.002) × (0.707 − 1) = 5 × 0.0447 × (−0.293) = −0.0655 St = (C_f/2) / [1 − 0.0655] = 0.002 / 0.9345 = 0.00214 h = 0.00214 × 1.177 × 30 × 1005 = **75.95 W/(m²·K)** **Solution (c) — Colburn Analogy:** j_H = C_f/2 = 0.002 St = j_H / Pr^(2/3) = 0.002 / (0.707)^(2/3) = 0.002 / 0.7896 = 0.00253 h = 0.00253 × 1.177 × 30 × 1005 = **89.78 W/(m²·K)** **Comparison:** h_Colburn > h_Prandtl > h_Reynolds ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Architecture of Transport Analogies The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps the conceptual relationships among the three transport processes and the analogies that unify them. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Random Molecular Motion
Microscopic Foundation] --> B1[Momentum Transfer
Shear Stress Tau] A --> B2[Heat Transfer
Heat Flux q] A --> B3[Mass Transfer
Molar Flux N_A] B1 --> C1[Newton Viscosity Law
tau = mu du/dy] B2 --> C2[Fourier Heat Conduction
q = -k dT/dy] B3 --> C3[Fick Diffusion Law
N_A = -D dC/dy] C1 --> D1[Skin Friction Coefficient
C_f equals 2 tau_w over rho V squared] C2 --> D2[Stanton Number Heat
St equals h over rho V C_p] C3 --> D3[Stanton Number Mass
St_m equals h_m over V] D1 --> E1[Dimensionless Flux Ratio Momentum] D2 --> E2[Dimensionless Flux Ratio Heat] D3 --> E3[Dimensionless Flux Ratio Mass] E1 --> F[Reynolds Analogy 1874
Pr equals 1 Sc equals 1] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[Limitation Pr not 1] G --> H1[Prandtl Analogy 1910
Laminar Sublayer 0.5 to 10] H1 --> I[Limitation Pr greater than 10] I --> J[Von Karman Analogy 1939
Three Layer Model 0.5 to 100] E1 --> K[Colburn Empirical 1933
Pr to power 2/3 correction] E2 --> K K --> L[Chilton Colburn 1934
Mass and Heat Combined] E3 --> L J --> M[Engineering Application
Predict h and h_m from friction] K --> M L --> M H1 --> M M --> N[Compact Heat Exchangers] M --> O[Chemical Reactor Design] M --> P[Boundary Layer Engineering] style A fill:#FFD700 style F fill:#FF6B6B style H1 fill:#4ECDC4 style J fill:#45B7D1 style K fill:#FFA500 style L fill:#9B59B6 style M fill:#2ECC71 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Boundary Layer Correspondence This diagram shows the **geometric correspondence** between velocity, thermal, and concentration boundary layers — the visual basis of the analogy. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Free Stream V T_inf C_inf] --> B[Velocity Boundary Layer delta] A --> C[Thermal Boundary Layer delta_T] A --> D[Concentration Boundary Layer delta_C] B --> E[Wall V equals 0
No Slip] C --> F[Wall T equals T_s
Heated Surface] D --> G[Wall C_A equals C_As
Species Boundary] E --> H[Shear Stress tau_w] F --> I[Heat Flux q_w] G --> J[Mass Flux N_A_w] H --> K[All Three Fluxes
Governed by Single Profile
if Pr equals Sc equals 1] I --> K J --> K style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Relationship of All Coefficients ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Transport Property Ratios] --> P1[Pr equals nu over alpha] P --> P2[Sc equals nu over D_AB] P --> P3[Le equals alpha over D_AB equals Sc over Pr] C[Dimensionless Coefficients] --> C1[St equals h over rho V C_p] C --> C2[St_m equals h_m over V] C --> C3[C_f over 2 equals tau_w over rho V squared] A1[Reynolds 1874] --> R1[C_f/2 equals St equals St_m] A2[Prandtl 1910] --> R2[C_f/2 equals St times bracket 1 plus 5 sqrt C_f/2 times Pr minus 1 bracket] A3[Colburn 1933] --> R3[St times Pr power 2/3 equals C_f/2] A4[Chilton Colburn 1934] --> R4[St times Pr power 2/3 equals St_m times Sc power 2/3] C1 --> A1 C2 --> A1 C3 --> A1 C1 --> A2 C3 --> A2 C1 --> A3 C3 --> A3 C1 --> A4 C2 --> A4 P1 --> A2 P1 --> A3 P2 --> A4 style A1 fill:#FFB6C1 style A2 fill:#B0E0E6 style A3 fill:#98FB98 style A4 fill:#DDA0DD ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** **State Reynolds analogy and mention its limitation.** **Model Answer:** Reynolds analogy states that for a fluid with Pr = 1, the dimensionless wall heat flux (Stanton number for heat) equals the dimensionless wall momentum flux (half the skin friction coefficient): $$ \frac{C_f}{2} = St = St_m $$ **Limitation:** It is valid only when **Pr = Sc = 1** (e.g., monatomic and diatomic gases at moderate conditions). For liquids, oils, and high-Pr fluids, it fails significantly. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]** **Define Stanton number, Prandtl number, and Schmidt number. State their significance in the transport analogy framework.** **Model Answer:** - **Stanton Number (St):** St = h/(ρ·V·C_p) — ratio of heat actually transferred to the maximum convective heat capacity of the fluid. - **Prandtl Number (Pr):** Pr = ν/α = μ·C_p/k — ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity. - **Schmidt Number (Sc):** Sc = ν/D_AB — ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity. **Significance:** All three are **dimensionless transport coefficients**. The analogies essentially equate the heat Stanton number (St) to the mass Stanton number (St_m) and to half the skin friction coefficient (C_f/2), modified by Pr and Sc corrections. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the Reynolds analogy between heat and momentum transfer. State clearly the assumptions made. **(b) (7 Marks)** Using Reynolds analogy, estimate the heat transfer coefficient for air flowing over a flat plate. Given: V = 25 m/s, L = 2 m, ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, C_p = 1005 J/(kg·K), C_f = 0.0038. Also comment on the limitations. #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, incompressible, turbulent flow. 2. Constant fluid properties. 3. Pr = Sc = 1 (so velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles are identical). 4. No viscous dissipation or pressure gradient effects. 5. The wall is smooth. **Derivation:** The dimensionless velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles (per Reynolds hypothesis) are: $$ \frac{u}{V_\infty} = \frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s} = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}} $$ Differentiating with respect to y: $$ \frac{1}{V_\infty}\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1}{T_\infty - T_s}\frac{dT}{dy} = \frac{1}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}}\frac{dC_A}{dy} $$ Computing wall heat flux (Fourier's law): $$ q_w'' = -k\left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = -k(T_\infty - T_s)\frac{1}{V_\infty}\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0} $$ Substituting Newton's law at the wall, τ_w = μ(du/dy)|_{y=0}: $$ q_w'' = -k(T_\infty - T_s)\frac{\tau_w}{\mu V_\infty} $$ Non-dimensionalizing the heat flux by the maximum convective flux: $$ St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho V_\infty C_p (T_\infty - T_s)} = \frac{k \tau_w}{\mu \rho V_\infty^2 C_p} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho V_\infty^2} \cdot \frac{k}{\mu C_p} $$ Since Pr = μC_p/k, we have k/(μC_p) = 1/Pr = 1 (because Pr = 1 by assumption): $$ St = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho V_\infty^2} = \frac{C_f}{2} $$ **Therefore:** $$ \boxed{\;St = \frac{C_f}{2}\;} $$ #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given:** V = 25 m/s, L = 2 m, ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, C_p = 1005 J/(kg·K), C_f = 0.0038, Pr ≈ 0.7 for air. **Step 1 — Apply Reynolds Analogy:** $$ St = \frac{C_f}{2} = \frac{0.0038}{2} = 0.0019 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate h:** $$ h = St \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot C_p = 0.0019 \times 1.2 \times 25 \times 1005 $$ $$ h = 0.0019 \times 30150 = 57.285 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)} $$ **h ≈ 57.3 W/(m²·K)** **Limitations of Reynolds Analogy in this case:** 1. Air has Pr ≈ 0.7, not exactly 1 — introduces a small error (~3–5%). 2. Reynolds analogy neglects laminar sublayer effects. 3. Assumes constant properties — not valid for large temperature differences. 4. Not applicable near the leading edge where boundary layer is laminar. A more accurate result would be obtained using the **Colburn analogy**: St_Colburn = 0.0019 / (0.7)^(2/3) = 0.0019 / 0.7896 = 0.00241 h_Colburn = 0.00241 × 30150 = 72.6 W/(m²·K) — ~27% higher. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]** **(a) (7 Marks)** What is the Prandtl analogy? Derive the relation between friction factor and heat transfer coefficient considering a laminar sublayer in turbulent flow over a flat plate. **(b) (7 Marks)** Air at 350 K flows over a flat plate with V = 20 m/s. The friction coefficient is C_f = 0.005. Compare the heat transfer coefficients predicted by (i) Reynolds analogy, (ii) Prandtl analogy, and (iii) Colburn analogy. Use Pr = 0.7, C_p = 1008 J/(kg·K), ρ = 1.0 kg/m³. #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Prandtl Analogy Concept:** The Prandtl analogy accounts for the **laminar (viscous) sublayer** that exists even in turbulent flow, where molecular transport dominates. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Viscous sublayer thickness (η):** Within the sublayer, transport is purely molecular. The wall shear stress is: $$ \tau_w = \mu \frac{V}{\eta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta = \frac{\mu V}{\tau_w} $$ **Step 2 — Heat transfer through the sublayer:** Fourier's law gives: $$ q_w'' = k \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{\eta_T} $$ where η_T is the thermal sublayer thickness. **Step 3 — Sublayer thickness ratio:** The momentum and thermal sublayer thicknesses differ because molecular Pr ≠ 1 in general. Empirically: $$ \frac{\eta}{\eta_T} \approx \sqrt{Pr} $$ Therefore: $$ \eta_T = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{Pr}} = \frac{\mu V}{\tau_w \sqrt{Pr}} $$ **Step 4 — Wall heat flux:** $$ q_w'' = k \frac{(T_\infty - T_s) \tau_w \sqrt{Pr}}{\mu V} $$ **Step 5 — Stanton number:** $$ St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho V C_p (T_\infty - T_s)} = \frac{k \tau_w \sqrt{Pr}}{\mu \rho V^2 C_p} $$ Using Pr = μC_p/k: $$ St = \frac{C_f}{2} \cdot \sqrt{Pr} \cdot \frac{1}{Pr} = \frac{C_f}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{Pr}} $$ **Step 6 — Empirical correction (added by Prandtl):** To extend beyond the sublayer analysis and account for the turbulent core: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{C_f}{2} = St \left[1 + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}(Pr - 1)\right]\;} $$ **Range of Validity:** 0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 10. For Pr = 1, this reduces to the Reynolds analogy. #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** V = 20 m/s, C_f = 0.005, Pr = 0.7, C_p = 1008 J/(kg·K), ρ = 1.0 kg/m³. **Common Computation:** Base quantity: ρVC_p = 1.0 × 20 × 1008 = 20160 W/(m²·K) C_f/2 = 0.0025 **(i) Reynolds Analogy:** St_R = C_f/2 = 0.0025 h_R = 0.0025 × 20160 = **50.4 W/(m²·K)** **(ii) Prandtl Analogy:** Compute correction: 5√(C_f/2)(Pr − 1) = 5 × √0.0025 × (0.7 − 1) = 5 × 0.05 × (−0.3) = −0.075 St_P = (C_f/2) / [1 − 0.075] = 0.0025 / 0.925 = 0.002703 h_P = 0.002703 × 20160 = **54.49 W/(m²·K)** **(iii) Colburn Analogy:** St_C = (C_f/2) / Pr^(2/3) = 0.0025 / (0.7)^(2/3) = 0.0025 / 0.7889 = 0.003169 h_C = 0.003169 × 20160 = **63.89 W/(m²·K)** **Comparison Table:** | Analogy | St | h [W/(m²·K)] | % Increase vs Reynolds | |---|---|---|---| | Reynolds | 0.002500 | 50.40 | — (baseline) | | Prandtl | 0.002703 | 54.49 | +8.1% | | Colburn | 0.003169 | 63.89 | +26.8% | **Observation:** For air (Pr < 1), all three analogies give different h values, with Colburn being most accurate empirically. The Prandtl result is intermediate because it accounts for the laminar sublayer but is less accurate than Colburn for Pr ≠ 1. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert C_f/2** (using C_f alone in place of C_f/2). This gives **h values off by exactly a factor of 2** — a 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Confusing j_H with St** in Colburn. The j_H already includes the Pr^(2/3) correction — to get h, you must invert it: St = j_H / Pr^(2/3). > 3. **Using ρVC_pΔT as the heat flux directly** — students forget the wall heat flux is q_w'' = hΔT, not St × ρVC_p × ΔT. Make sure the Stanton definition is clear. > 4. **Sign errors in Prandtl bracket:** The bracket is [1 + 5√(C_f/2)(Pr − 1)]. For Pr < 1, the bracket is less than 1, making St LARGER than C_f/2. > 5. **Forgetting units of h:** [W/(m²·K)], not [W/(m·K)] or [J/(m²·K·s)]. > 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly listing assumptions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 15 Must-Know Items for KTU Exam:** - [ ] **Reynolds Analogy (1874):** C_f/2 = St = St_m. Valid ONLY for Pr = Sc = 1. - [ ] **Prandtl Analogy (1910):** C_f/2 = St[1 + 5√(C_f/2)(Pr − 1)]. Range: 0.5 ≤ Pr ≤ 10. - [ ] **Von Kármán Analogy (1939):** Three-layer model, valid for higher Pr. - [ ] **Colburn Analogy (1933):** j_H = St·Pr^(2/3) = C_f/2. Empirical but most widely used. - [ ] **Chilton–Colburn (1934):** j_M = St_m·Sc^(2/3) = C_f/2. Extends Colburn to mass transfer. - [ ] **Heat–Mass Analogy:** h/h_m = ρC_p(Sc/Pr)^(2/3). - [ ] **Stanton Number Heat:** St = h/(ρVC_p). Dimensionless heat flux ratio. - [ ] **Stanton Number Mass:** St_m = h_m/V. Dimensionless mass flux ratio. - [ ] **Skin Friction Coeff:** C_f = 2τ_w/(ρV²). C_f/2 is the dimensionless wall momentum flux. - [ ] **Prandtl Number:** Pr = ν/α = μC_p/k. For gases, Pr ≈ 0.7; for water, Pr ≈ 7. - [ ] **Schmidt Number:** Sc = ν/D_AB. For most gases, Sc ≈ 0.6–1.0. - [ ] **Lewis Number:** Le = α/D_AB = Sc/Pr. - [ ] **C_f/2 and St are dimensionless** — they can be equated only because both are dimensionless flux ratios. - [ ] **Limitation Hierarchy:** Reynolds ⊂ Prandtl ⊂ Von Kármán (in terms of Pr range covered). - [ ] **Colburn (empirical) is more accurate** than Prandtl (analytical) for moderate Pr (1–100) — this is a key KTU viva question. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the analogies in order of historical accuracy:** > **"Reynolds Prandtl VonKarman Colburn" → "RPVC"** — think *"**R**oughly **P**roper **V**alid **C**onclusions"*. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the three transport processes and their laws:** > **"Newton-Fourier-Fick"** — *N-F-F* — "**N**o **F**riction, **F**riction at the wall" (or, less awkwardly: "**N**o slip, **F**lux proportional to gradient, **F**or all three").

topics.title: Convective Mass Transfer Correlations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Convective Mass Transfer Correlations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Convective Mass Transfer Correlations** are empirical or semi-theoretical equations that relate the **convective mass transfer coefficient ($k_c$)** to the flow geometry, fluid properties, and operating conditions. They are expressed in terms of dimensionless groups (Sherwood, Reynolds, Schmidt) and are mathematically analogous to heat transfer correlations (Nusselt–Reynolds–Prandtl).

Formally, these correlations take the general form:
$$Sh = f(Re, Sc, \text{geometry})$$

where the dimensionless groups are:
- **Sherwood Number** $Sh = \dfrac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$
- **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$
- **Schmidt Number** $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must master the heat–mass transfer **analogies** (Reynolds, Prandtl, Chilton–Colburn) and the standard correlations for **flat plates, pipe flow, and single spheres**. The Ranz–Marshall correlation for a sphere is treated as a *must-know* formula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine a hot cup of tea placed in a windy room. Three things happen simultaneously:
1. The **tea cools** (heat transfer away from the cup).
2. The **water vapor escapes** (mass transfer from tea surface to air).
3. The **wind drags the air** along the cup (momentum transfer).

Although these three phenomena involve different physical quantities, they are all driven by the *same turbulent eddies* in the air near the surface. This shared transport mechanism is the foundation of the **heat–mass transfer analogy** — meaning we can predict one from the other!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The rates of heat, mass, and momentum transfer are coupled at the fluid boundary layer. If the temperature, concentration, and velocity profiles are *similar* in shape, a single dimensionless number can govern all three processes.

### 1.3 Standard Mass Transfer Coefficients (Units in SI)

| Symbol | Definition | SI Units |
|---|---|---|
| $k_c$ | Concentration-based | m/s |
| $k_G$ | Partial-pressure-based | kg/(m²·s·Pa) |
| $k_y$ | Mole-fraction-based | kg/(m²·s) |

Conversion identity: $k_c = k_G \cdot R T = k_y / \rho$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Heat–Mass Transfer Analogies
The **Reynolds analogy** is the most elementary form, valid only when the boundary layer profiles for velocity, temperature, and concentration are *identical* (i.e., $Pr = Sc = 1$).

**Reynolds Analogy (Pr = Sc = 1):**
$$St_H = St_M = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Chilton–Colburn Analogy (Empirically Extended):**
$$j_H \approx j_M \implies St_H \, Pr^{2/3} \approx St_M \, Sc^{2/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Chilton–Colburn analogy is the **most widely used** in KTU problems because it works well for $0.6 < Pr < 60$ and $0.6 < Sc < 3000$ in turbulent flow.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All Correlations at a Glance

| # | Geometry / Flow | Correlation | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flat plate, **Laminar** | $Sh_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$, Pohlhausen exact |
| 2 | Flat plate, **Turbulent** | $Sh_x = 0.0292 \, Re_x^{4/5} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$ |
| 3 | Pipe / Duct, **Turbulent** | $Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.83} \, Sc^{0.33}$ | $Re > 10\,000$, $0.6 < Sc < 3000$ |
| 4 | **Single Sphere** (Ranz–Marshall) | $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re < 200\,000$, $0.6 < Sc < 2.7$ |
| 5 | Packed Bed (Ranz–Marshall) | $Sh = 2 + 1.8 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | For spherical particles, $Re = d_p v_s / \nu$ |
| 6 | Fluidized Bed | $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | Similar to single sphere |
| 7 | Falling Film (Laminar) | $Sh = 1.50 \, Re^{1/3} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | Sherwood–Mills–Tallmadge form |
| 8 | Bubbles / Drops | $Sh = 2 + 0.65 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | For circulating drops |
| 9 | Reynolds Analogy | $St_H = St_M$ | When $Pr = Sc = 1$ only |
| 10 | Prandtl Analogy | $St = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + \sqrt{C_f/2}(Pr-1)}$ | Corrects Reynolds for $Pr \neq 1$ |
| 11 | Chilton–Colburn | $j_H = j_M$ | $j_H = St Pr^{2/3}, \; j_M = St_M Sc^{2/3}$ |
| 12 | von Kármán Analogy | $St = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\left\{(Pr-1) + \ln\!\left[\frac{5Pr+1}{6}\right]\right\}}$ | Most accurate for wide Pr range |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.83} Sc^{0.33}$ for a **flat plate**. The 0.023 constant is strictly valid for **fully developed turbulent pipe flow**, not for a flat plate. Use the 0.0292 constant for flat plate turbulent flow.

### 2.3 Key Dimensionless Groups in Mass Transfer

| Group | Formula | Physical Meaning | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Re$ | $\rho v L / \mu$ | Inertial forces / Viscous forces | $0 < Re < \infty$ |
| $Sc$ | $\nu / D_{AB}$ | Momentum diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | $0 < Sc < \infty$ |
| $Sh$ | $k_c L / D_{AB}$ | Convective mass transfer / Molecular diffusion | $Sh \geq 2$ (sphere) |
| $Pe_M$ | $Re \cdot Sc$ | Convective mass transfer / Molecular diffusion | — |
| $St_M$ | $k_c / v = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc)$ | Dimensionless mass transfer coefficient | — |
| $j_M$ | $St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ | Mass transfer factor (Chilton–Colburn) | — |
| $Le$ | $\alpha / D_{AB} = Sc / Pr$ | Lewis number (thermal vs. mass diffusivity) | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Chemical process equipment:** Design of cooling towers, humidifiers, dryers, and absorbers.
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Controlled drying of tablets and dissolution of drugs.
- **HVAC systems:** Evaporative cooling in air-conditioning coils.
- **Combustion engineering:** Fuel droplet evaporation in diesel/gas turbines.
- **Food processing:** Freeze-drying, spray drying, moisture migration in packaged food.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Analogy

**Starting Postulate (Physical Assumption):**  
Within the boundary layer over a flat surface, the dimensionless profiles for velocity, temperature, and concentration are assumed to be *identical*:

$$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_w - T_\infty} = \frac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,w} - C_{A,\infty}} \quad \text{(1)}$$

**Step 1 — Wall Fluxes (Newtonian fluid):**
For momentum:
$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_w$$

For heat (Fourier's law):
$$q_w = -k \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_w$$

For mass (Fick's law):
$$N_{A,w} = -D_{AB} \left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_w$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate Equation (1) with respect to y at the wall:**
$$\frac{1}{u_\infty}\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_w = \frac{1}{T_w - T_\infty}\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_w = \frac{1}{C_{A,w}-C_{A,\infty}}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\right|_w$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the wall flux expressions:**
$$q_w = \rho c_p u_\infty \, (T_w - T_\infty) \cdot \frac{1}{u_\infty}\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_w \cdot \frac{\nu}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{u_\infty}$$

Wait — more cleanly, divide each flux by its driving potential:
$$\frac{q_w}{\rho c_p (T_w - T_\infty)} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_\infty^2} = \frac{N_{A,w}}{(C_{A,w} - C_{A,\infty})}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the transport coefficients:**
$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_\infty} = k_c$$

**Step 5 — Non-dimensionalize by $u_\infty$ on both sides:**
$$St_H = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_\infty^2} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Final Result — Reynolds Analogy:**
$$St_H = St_M = \frac{C_f}{2} \quad \text{(valid only when } Pr = Sc = 1\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only when Pr = Sc = 1?** Because the Reynolds analogy assumes all three boundary layers have *exactly* the same thickness. This is only true when the dimensionless Prandtl and Schmidt numbers are both unity, making the thermal, momentum, and concentration profiles coincident.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Chilton–Colburn Analogy

**Empirical Foundation:**  
From turbulent pipe-flow experiments (Colburn, 1933), it was observed that:
$$Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \quad \text{(Dittus–Boelter, heating)}$$

Similarly for mass transfer:
$$Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}$$

**Step 1 — Express the Stanton number for heat transfer:**
$$St_H = \frac{Nu}{Re \, Pr} = \frac{0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}}{Re \, Pr} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2} \, Pr^{-2/3}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $Pr^{2/3}$:**
$$St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$

This defines the **heat transfer j-factor**:
$$j_H = St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$

**Step 3 — Repeat the procedure for mass transfer:**
$$St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$

This defines the **mass transfer j-factor**:
$$j_M = St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two j-factors (Chilton–Colburn Hypothesis):**
$$\boxed{\,j_H = j_M \;\Longrightarrow\; St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = St_M \, Sc^{2/3}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Application:**  
Given an experimental heat transfer coefficient, the mass transfer coefficient can be obtained as:
$$k_c = \frac{h}{\rho c_p} \left(\frac{Sc}{Pr}\right)^{2/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is **invaluable** because mass transfer experiments are difficult (you cannot easily control a concentration boundary layer), while heat transfer data are abundant. The Chilton–Colburn analogy thus acts as a *bridge* between the two transport phenomena.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Ranz–Marshall Correlation for a Sphere

**Physical Setup:**  
A sphere of diameter $D$ in a flowing fluid. The Sherwood number is the sum of two contributions: (i) pure molecular diffusion in a stagnant fluid, and (ii) convective enhancement.

**Step 1 — Limiting Case (No Flow, $Re \to 0$):**  
For a sphere in a stagnant infinite medium, the analytical solution of the Laplace equation gives the well-known result for the *local* mass transfer rate from the sphere surface:

$$\dot{m} = \pi D \, D_{AB} \, (C_{A,w} - C_{A,\infty})$$

**Step 2 — Define the average mass transfer coefficient:**
$$\overline{k_c} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi D^2 (C_{A,w} - C_{A,\infty})} = \frac{2 D_{AB}}{D}$$

Therefore, in the limit of zero convection:
$$Sh_0 = \frac{\overline{k_c} D}{D_{AB}} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Add the Convective Contribution:**  
Ranz and Marshall proposed that the Sherwood number in the presence of flow is:
$$Sh = Sh_0 + Sh_{\text{convective}} = 2 + C \, Re^m \, Sc^n$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Calibration (from naphthalene sublimation experiments):**
Using the analogy with the Nusselt number for a sphere (Froessling, 1938), they obtained:
$$Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent **1/2 on Re** reflects the analogy with the **Blasius-type** flat-plate momentum boundary layer (thickness $\sim x^{1/2}$), while the **1/3 on Sc** matches the heat-transfer analogy with $Pr^{1/3}$ in the Pohlhausen solution.

**Step 5 — Use this to find $k_c$:**
$$k_c = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{D} = \frac{D_{AB}}{D}\left[2 + 0.6 \left(\frac{vD}{\nu}\right)^{1/2} \left(\frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}\right)^{1/3}\right]$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Transport Analogies and Correlation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Layer Physics] --> B[Shared Eddy Transport Mechanism]
    B --> C[Momentum Transfer: tau_w]
    B --> D[Heat Transfer: q_w]
    B --> E[Mass Transfer: N_A_w]
    
    C --> F[Analogies Layer]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> F1[Reynolds Analogy - Pr=Sc=1]
    F --> F2[Prandtl Analogy - Moderate Pr]
    F --> F3[Chilton-Colburn jH=jM]
    F --> F4[von Karman - Wide Pr range]
    
    F1 --> G[Correlations Bank]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    
    G --> G1[Flat Plate: 0.0292 Re^0.8 Sc^0.33]
    G --> G2[Pipe Flow: 0.023 Re^0.83 Sc^0.33]
    G --> G3[Single Sphere Ranz-Marshall: 2 + 0.6 Re^0.5 Sc^0.33]
    G --> G4[Packed Bed: 2 + 1.8 Re^0.5 Sc^0.33]
    
    G1 --> H[Compute k_c]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flow — Choosing the Right Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify Geometry and Flow] --> Q1{Geometry Type}
    Q1 -->|Flat Plate| Q2{Re_x Value}
    Q2 -->|< 5x10^5| P1[Laminar: Sh_x = 0.332 Re_x^0.5 Sc^0.33]
    Q2 -->|> 5x10^5| P2[Turbulent: Sh_x = 0.0292 Re_x^0.8 Sc^0.33]
    
    Q1 -->|Pipe or Duct| Q3{Re Value}
    Q3 -->|> 10000| P3[Turbulent: Sh = 0.023 Re^0.83 Sc^0.33]
    Q3 -->|< 2300| P4[Laminar: Sh = 1.86 Re^0.33 Sc^0.33 L/D]
    
    Q1 -->|Sphere or Particle| P5[Single Sphere Ranz-Marshall: Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^0.5 Sc^0.33]
    Q1 -->|Packed Bed| P6[Packed Bed: Sh = 2 + 1.8 Re^0.5 Sc^0.33]
    
    P1 --> Z[Compute k_c = Sh D_AB / L]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z
    P5 --> Z
    P6 --> Z
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — The "Three Siblings" of Transport

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Momentum Transfer] -->|tau_w / rho u^2| C[Stanton Number St]
    H[Heat Transfer] -->|h / rho cp u| C
    MA[Mass Transfer] -->|k_c / u| C
    
    C -->|Multiply by Pr^2/3| JC[j-Heat Factor jH]
    C -->|Multiply by Sc^2/3| JM[j-Mass Factor jM]
    
    JC --> E[jH = jM Chilton-Colburn]
    JM --> E
    
    E --> OUT[Predict k_c from h or vice versa]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a computational stage in applying convective mass transfer correlations. The decision tree in 4.2 is particularly useful during KTU exams to quickly choose the correct formula based on the geometry and Reynolds number given in the problem statement.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** Define the **Sherwood number** and **Schmidt number**. State the physical significance of each in convective mass transfer.  
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Sherwood Number** ($Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$): It is the dimensionless ratio of *convective mass transfer rate* to *molecular diffusion rate*. Physically, it represents the **enhancement factor** of mass transfer due to fluid motion over pure molecular diffusion. A high $Sh$ implies convection dominates; $Sh = 2$ is the minimum for a sphere in a stagnant medium.

- **Schmidt Number** ($Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$): It is the ratio of *momentum diffusivity* to *mass diffusivity*. Physically, it characterizes the **relative thickness of the velocity and concentration boundary layers**:
  - $Sc \gg 1$ (e.g., liquids): thin concentration boundary layer, mass transfer is slow.
  - $Sc \ll 1$ (e.g., gases with $Sc \approx 0.6$–$2.5$): concentration boundary layer is thicker.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Physical significance (1) + Example of magnitudes (1) = 3 marks.

---

**Q2.** State the **Reynolds analogy** between heat and mass transfer. Under what condition is it strictly valid?  
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds analogy states that in convective transport, the **Stanton numbers for heat and mass transfer are equal to half the skin-friction coefficient**:

$$St_H = St_M = \frac{C_f}{2} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_\infty^2}$$

**Condition of Validity:** The analogy is strictly valid only when **$Pr = Sc = 1$**, i.e., the momentum, thermal, and concentration boundary layers are of identical thickness. This rarely occurs in real systems — for air, $Pr \approx 0.7$ and $Sc \approx 0.6$–$2.5$; for water, $Pr \approx 7$ and $Sc \approx 1000$.

**Marks Distribution:** Statement (1) + Equation (1) + Validity condition (1) = 3 marks.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **Chilton–Colburn analogy** between heat and mass transfer. Briefly explain why it is preferred over the Reynolds analogy in practical applications.  
**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point:**  
For turbulent flow in a pipe, the Dittus–Boelter correlation is:
$$Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

By analogy, for mass transfer in the same geometry:
$$Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Express Stanton Numbers:**
$$St_H = \frac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr} = \frac{0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}}{Re \cdot Pr} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2} \, Pr^{-2/3}$$

$$St_M = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.8} \, Sc^{1/3} \cdot \frac{1}{Re \cdot Sc} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2} \, Sc^{-2/3}$$

Hmm, recompute carefully: 
$$St_M = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc} = \frac{0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}}{Re \cdot Sc} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2} \, Sc^{-2/3}$$

**Step 3 — Define the j-factors:**
$$j_H = St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$
$$j_M = St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = 0.023 \, Re^{-0.2}$$

**Step 4 — The Chilton–Colburn Hypothesis:**
$$\boxed{\,j_H = j_M \quad \Longrightarrow \quad St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = St_M \, Sc^{2/3}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Why Preferred over Reynolds Analogy:**
- Valid over a much wider range: $0.6 < Pr < 60$ and $0.6 < Sc < 3000$.
- Accounts for the difference in boundary layer thicknesses via the $Pr^{2/3}$ and $Sc^{2/3}$ corrections.
- Empirically tested and verified for gases and liquids in turbulent flow.

**(b)** A naphthalene sphere of diameter **10 mm** is exposed to air flowing at **2 m/s**. Using the **Ranz–Marshall correlation**, calculate the mass transfer coefficient.  
**Given:** $D_{AB} = 6.1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$  
**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{v D}{\nu} = \frac{(2)(0.01)}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1333.33$$

Since $Re \ll 200\,000$, the Ranz–Marshall correlation is valid.

**Step 2 — Calculate Schmidt Number:**
$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{6.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.4590$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ranz–Marshall Correlation:**
$$Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$$
$$Re^{1/2} = \sqrt{1333.33} = 36.515$$
$$Sc^{1/3} = (2.4590)^{1/3} = 1.3493$$
$$Sh = 2 + 0.6 \times 36.515 \times 1.3493 = 2 + 29.56 = 31.56$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Mass Transfer Coefficient:**
$$k_c = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{D} = \frac{31.56 \times 6.1 \times 10^{-6}}{0.01}$$
$$\boxed{k_c = 1.925 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m/s} \approx 0.0193 \, \text{m/s}}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Re (1) + Sc (1) + Sh (2) + k_c final (2) + Units (1) = 7 marks.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** A flat plate is exposed to air flowing at **5 m/s**. The plate is coated with a volatile organic compound. Determine the **mass transfer coefficient at x = 0.5 m** from the leading edge.  
**Given:** $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad Sc = 0.6$  
**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the Local Reynolds Number at x = 0.5 m:**
$$Re_x = \frac{v x}{\nu} = \frac{(5)(0.5)}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.667 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Flow Regime:**
Since $Re_x = 1.667 \times 10^{5} < 5 \times 10^{5}$, the boundary layer is **laminar**.

**Step 3 — Apply the Laminar Flat-Plate Correlation:**
$$Sh_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$$
$$Re_x^{1/2} = \sqrt{1.667 \times 10^{5}} = 408.25$$
$$Sc^{1/3} = (0.6)^{1/3} = 0.8434$$
$$Sh_x = 0.332 \times 408.25 \times 0.8434 = 114.32$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mass Transfer Coefficient:**
$$k_c = \frac{Sh_x \cdot D_{AB}}{x} = \frac{114.32 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5}$$
$$\boxed{k_c = 5.716 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Re_x (1) + Flow regime (1) + Correlation (2) + Sh (1) + k_c (1) + Units (1) = 7 marks.

**(b)** Compare the **Reynolds, Prandtl, and Chilton–Colburn analogies**. State the **applicability** of each.  
**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

| Feature | Reynolds Analogy | Prandtl Analogy | Chilton–Colburn Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equation** | $St_H = St_M = C_f/2$ | $St = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1+\sqrt{C_f/2}(Pr-1)}$ | $St_H Pr^{2/3} = St_M Sc^{2/3}$ |
| **Validity** | $Pr = Sc = 1$ only | $0.6 < Pr < 10$ approximately | $0.6 < Pr < 60$, $0.6 < Sc < 3000$ |
| **Basis** | Theoretical (Reynolds, 1874) | Semi-empirical correction | Fully empirical (Colburn, 1933) |
| **Boundary Layer Assumption** | All three profiles identical | Velocity and thermal profiles differentiated via a *linear correction* | Uses j-factor matching with $Pr^{2/3}$, $Sc^{2/3}$ |
| **Flow Type** | Turbulent | Turbulent with mild $Pr$ deviation | Turbulent (and some laminar) |
| **Practical Use** | Mostly pedagogical; rarely used | Moderate accuracy; limited range | **Industry standard**; used in heat exchanger design, drying, humidification |
| **Limitation** | Fails for liquids ($Pr \gg 1$) | Cannot handle very large $Pr$ | Assumes same geometry for heat and mass transfer |

**Key Conclusion:** The **Chilton–Colburn analogy** is preferred in practice because it (i) covers a wide range of fluid properties, (ii) is empirically validated, and (iii) allows prediction of mass transfer coefficients from heat transfer data (or vice versa).

**Marks Distribution:** Three analogies × 2 marks each (equation + applicability) + Comparison summary (1) = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (Reynolds Analogy Misuse):** Students apply $St_H = St_M$ without checking if $Pr = Sc = 1$. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (Wrong Flat-Plate Constant):** Using the pipe-flow constant 0.023 for flat plate turbulent flow. The correct constant is **0.0292** (or sometimes written as 0.0288). **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (Forgetting "2" in Ranz–Marshall):** Some students write only $Sh = 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$, missing the **"2 +"** term which represents pure molecular diffusion. This causes significant error at low $Re$. **Loss of 2 marks.**
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (Confusing Sc with Pr):** In Chilton–Colburn, $St_M Sc^{2/3} = St_H Pr^{2/3}$. Mixing these exponents is a frequent error. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (Units of $k_c$):** $k_c$ is in **m/s** (not W/m²K, which is for $h$). KTU markers deduct 0.5 mark for incorrect unit specification.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **The Reynolds analogy** ($St_H = St_M = C_f/2$) is the historical foundation but is valid only when $Pr = Sc = 1$.
- **The Chilton–Colburn analogy** ($j_H = j_M$) is the **industry standard** for predicting mass transfer from heat transfer data. Always cite the range: $0.6 < Pr < 60$, $0.6 < Sc < 3000$.
- **The Ranz–Marshall correlation** for a sphere: $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ — the "2" is *not optional*; it represents diffusion in a stagnant medium.
- **Flat plate laminar** flow uses $Sh_x = 0.332 Re_x^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ for $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$.
- **Flat plate turbulent** flow uses $Sh_x = 0.0292 Re_x^{4/5} Sc^{1/3}$ for $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$.
- **Pipe flow turbulent** uses $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.83} Sc^{0.33}$ (Galloway–Linton).
- **The Prandtl analogy** is a *one-step* theoretical correction to Reynolds and is useful in textbook problems involving moderate $Pr$.
- **The von Kármán analogy** is the most accurate theoretical form but is *too cumbersome* for routine design — typically not required in KTU problems.
- Always compute **Re, Sc, and the geometry criterion first** to choose the right correlation.
- **Units of $k_c$ are m/s** (concentration-based). Use $k_G$ (kg/m²·s·Pa) only when the problem states partial pressure as the driving force.
- Remember the conversion: $k_c = k_G R T = k_y / \rho$ (use $RT \approx 287 \times 300 = 86\,100$ J/kg·K for air at 300 K).
- **Stanton Number for Mass Transfer:** $St_M = k_c / v = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc)$.
- **Schmidt Number** for gases is typically $0.6 < Sc < 2.5$; for liquids, $Sc$ can be $100 < Sc < 1000$.
- The **Chilton–Colburn analogy** works best for **turbulent flow**; for laminar flow, use exact solutions like the Pohlhausen result.
